topics.title: Introduction to Big Data and Hadoop Distributed File System: Evolution of Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Big Data and Hadoop Distributed File System: Evolution of Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Big Data?

**Big Data** refers to extremely large, diverse, and complex datasets that cannot be efficiently captured, stored, processed, managed, or analyzed using traditional data processing tools and relational database management systems (RDBMS) within a tolerable elapsed time. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, Big Data is characterized by datasets whose **size**, **type**, and **speed of generation** exceed the storage and computational capabilities of conventional systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable):**
> Big Data is a term that describes the large volume of data – both structured and unstructured – that inundates a business on a day-to-day basis. It is characterized by the **5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value.

## 1.2 The Five V's of Big Data (KTU High-Yield Framework)

| Dimension | Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Scale of data — Terabytes, Petabytes, Exabytes | Facebook generates **4 Petabytes** of data per day |
| **Velocity** | Speed at which data is generated and processed | Stock market transactions: **millions per second** |
| **Variety** | Different data formats — structured, semi-structured, unstructured | Text, images, videos, sensor logs, JSON, XML |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness, quality, and accuracy of data | Filtering truthful tweets from spam/bots |
| **Value** | Business worth extracted from the data | Recommendations increasing revenue |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library That Grew Overnight"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a small-town library with 10,000 books catalogued neatly in a card-cabinet system (analogous to a traditional RDBMS). Suddenly, overnight, the library receives **500 million books** in 200 languages, including handwritten scrolls, video documentaries, and audio recordings — all arriving continuously. The old card catalogue (single-server RDBMS) cannot find, store, or organize this. Big Data technologies are like building a **distributed library system** spread across many buildings, with parallel librarians, automatic cataloguing, and fault tolerance — this is what Hadoop/HDFS does.

## 1.4 What is Hadoop?

**Apache Hadoop** is an open-source framework that allows for the **distributed storage** and **distributed processing** of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers. It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage.

The core of Hadoop consists of:
- **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — for storage
- **MapReduce** — for parallel processing
- **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** — for resource management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS** is the **primary storage system** of Hadoop. It is designed to store very large files across multiple machines in a cluster, providing **fault tolerance**, **high throughput**, and **streaming access** to data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Big Data — A Historical Perspective

The evolution follows a clear timeline of escalating data challenges:

1. **Era 1 (1960s–1990s): Traditional RDBMS Era**
   - Data in **Megabytes to Gigabytes**.
   - Used hierarchical and relational databases (IBM DB2, Oracle, MySQL).
   - ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) guaranteed.
   - **Limitation:** Vertically scalable only (scale-up by buying bigger machines).

2. **Era 2 (Late 1990s–2000s): The Web Era & Data Explosion**
   - Rise of the World Wide Web, e-commerce, and social media.
   - Data grew to **Terabytes**. Google's search index crossed billions of pages.
   - Vertical scaling hit economic and physical limits.
   - Birth of **parallel computing** and **distributed systems**.

3. **Era 3 (2003–2006): The Google Papers**
   - **Google File System (GFS)** paper published in **2003** by Ghemawat et al.
   - **MapReduce** paper published in **2004** by Dean and Ghemawat.
   - These papers defined the modern blueprint for handling petabyte-scale data.

4. **Era 4 (2006–Present): The Hadoop Era**
   - **Doug Cutting** and **Mike Cafarella** created Hadoop (named after Cutting's son's toy elephant) by open-source implementing Google's GFS and MapReduce.
   - Hadoop 1.0 released in **2006**.
   - YARN introduced in Hadoop 2.0 (2012).
   - Cloud-based Big Data platforms (AWS EMR, GCP Dataproc) emerged post-2014.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically credits the **Google File System (2003)** and **MapReduce (2004)** papers as the conceptual foundation of Hadoop.

## 2.2 Limitations of Traditional Data Processing (Why Hadoop?)

| Traditional System Limitation | How Hadoop Solves It |
|---|---|
| Single machine storage (Vertical scaling) | **Horizontal scaling** across commodity hardware |
| High cost of enterprise servers | Uses **low-cost commodity machines** |
| Poor fault tolerance (single point of failure) | **Replication** (default 3 copies) ensures fault tolerance |
| Cannot handle unstructured data | HDFS stores any data type (text, image, log) |
| Batch-only processing | Supports **streaming access** with high throughput |
| Limited to GBs–TBs | Scales to **Petabytes–Exabytes** seamlessly |

## 2.3 HDFS — Architecture Deep Dive

HDFS follows a **Master-Slave (NameNode–DataNode) architecture**.

### 2.3.1 NameNode (Master)
- Stores **metadata**: file names, directory structure, block locations, permissions.
- Manages the **file system namespace**.
- Does **not** store actual data — only pointers.
- Runs on high-memory, high-end hardware.
- **Single point of failure** in HDFS v1 (mitigated by Secondary NameNode; HA in HDFS v2).

### 2.3.2 DataNode (Slave)
- Stores the **actual data blocks** (default block size = **128 MB** in HDFS v2; was 64 MB in v1).
- Reports block status to NameNode via **heartbeat signals** (default every 3 seconds).
- Multiple DataNodes run per cluster (often hundreds/thousands).

### 2.3.3 Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)
- Periodically merges the **fsimage** (snapshot) and **edit logs** to prevent edit log explosion.
- **Not a backup** of NameNode despite the name.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Value / Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default HDFS Block Size (Hadoop 2.x+) | **128 MB** | Megabytes | Configurable via `dfs.blocksize` |
| Default Replication Factor | **3** | Copies | Configurable via `dfs.replication` |
| Number of Blocks | $\text{Number of Blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil$ | Integers | Ceiling function used |
| Total Storage Required | $\text{Total Storage} = \text{File Size} \times \text{Replication Factor}$ | Bytes | Includes replication overhead |
| Replication Overhead % | $\text{Overhead} = \left( \dfrac{R - 1}{1} \right) \times 100$ | Percent | $R$ = replication factor |
| Heartbeat Interval | **3 seconds** | Seconds | Tunable via `dfs.heartbeat.interval` |
| Rack Awareness Penalty | 1 extra hop for off-rack | Hops | Configurable via topology script |
| NameNode Memory Rule of Thumb | **1 GB RAM per 1 million blocks** | GB | Empirical estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When calculating number of blocks, ALWAYS apply the **ceiling function**. A 300 MB file with 128 MB block size requires $\lceil 300/128 \rceil = \lceil 2.34 \rceil = 3$ blocks, NOT 2.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

HDFS powers the data lakes behind **Facebook's photo storage** (over 300 PB), **Yahoo's search index**, **Netflix's recommendation engine**, and **Twitter's tweet analytics**. The "commodity hardware + horizontal scaling" philosophy democratized large-scale data processing — what once required million-dollar mainframes now runs on clusters of $500 commodity boxes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1: Block Calculation for a Given File Size

**Question:** A file of size **700 MB** is stored in HDFS with a default block size of **128 MB** and replication factor of **3**. Calculate:
(a) Number of blocks
(b) Total storage consumed in the cluster
(c) Replication overhead percentage

### Solution:

**(a) Number of Blocks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Blocks} &= \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{700 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil 5.46875 \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= 6 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Dividing 700 by 128 gives 5.46875, meaning the first 5 blocks are fully filled (5 × 128 = 640 MB), and the 6th block holds the remaining 60 MB. Since blocks cannot be partially used, we round up to 6.

**(b) Total Storage Consumed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Storage} &= \text{Number of Blocks} \times \text{Block Size} \times \text{Replication Factor} \\[6pt]
&= 6 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3 \\[6pt]
&= 2304 \text{ MB} \approx 2.25 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each of the 6 logical blocks is replicated 3 times. Note that the last block only stores 60 MB of actual data, but the storage accounting still treats it as 128 MB × 3 = 384 MB of cluster space.

**(c) Replication Overhead %**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Logical Data Size} &= 700 \text{ MB} \\[6pt]
\text{Physical Storage} &= 2304 \text{ MB} \\[6pt]
\text{Overhead \%} &= \left( \frac{\text{Physical} - \text{Logical}}{\text{Logical}} \right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \left( \frac{2304 - 700}{700} \right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \left( \frac{1604}{700} \right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
&\approx 229.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Problem 2: Cluster Sizing for a Known Data Volume

**Question:** A company needs to store **10 TB** of raw data in HDFS. Each DataNode has **2 TB** usable disk. If the replication factor is **3** and block size is **128 MB**, what is the **minimum number of DataNodes** required?

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Storage Required} &= \text{Logical Data} \times \text{Replication Factor} \\[6pt]
&= 10 \text{ TB} \times 3 = 30 \text{ TB} \\[6pt]
\text{Number of DataNodes} &= \left\lceil \frac{\text{Physical Storage}}{\text{Per-Node Capacity}} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{30 \text{ TB}}{2 \text{ TB}} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= 15 \text{ DataNodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** With 15 DataNodes, total cluster capacity = 15 × 2 TB = 30 TB, which exactly matches the 30 TB needed. This excludes reserved space for Hadoop daemons (typically 10% reserved).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Big Data Eras

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Parameter} & \textbf{RDBMS Era} & \textbf{Parallel DB} & \textbf{MapReduce (Hadoop 1)} & \textbf{Hadoop 2 + YARN} \\
\hline
\text{Data Scale} & \text{GB} & \text{TB} & \text{PB} & \text{PB--EB} \\
\hline
\text{Scaling} & \text{Vertical} & \text{Vertical} & \text{Horizontal} & \text{Horizontal} \\
\hline
\text{Fault Tolerance} & \text{Low} & \text{Medium} & \text{High} & \text{High} \\
\hline
\text{Data Type} & \text{Structured} & \text{Structured} & \text{Any} & \text{Any} \\
\hline
\text{Cost} & \text{Very High} & \text{High} & \text{Low} & \text{Low} \\
\hline
\text{Latency} & \text{Low} & \text{Low} & \text{High} & \text{Medium} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Master-Slave Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientApp[Client Application] -->|Read/Write Request| NN[NameNode Master]
    NN -->|Metadata Response| ClientApp
    NN -->|Block Location Info| ClientApp
    ClientApp -->|Direct Data Transfer| DN1[DataNode 1]
    ClientApp -->|Direct Data Transfer| DN2[DataNode 2]
    ClientApp -->|Direct Data Transfer| DN3[DataNode 3]
    NN -.->|Heartbeat Every 3s| DN1
    NN -.->|Heartbeat Every 3s| DN2
    NN -.->|Heartbeat Every 3s| DN3
    SNN[Secondary NameNode] -.->|Checkpoint Operation| NN
    DN1 <-->|Replication| DN2
    DN2 <-->|Replication| DN3
    DN1 <-->|Replication| DN3
```

## 4.2 Big Data Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1960s-1990s RDBMS Era GB Data] -->|Data Explosion| B[2000s Web Era TB Data]
    B -->|Google GFS Paper 2003| C[2004 MapReduce Paper]
    C -->|Doug Cutting implements| D[2006 Hadoop 1.0]
    D -->|YARN added| E[2012 Hadoop 2.0]
    E -->|Cloud Adoption| F[2014+ Cloud Big Data EMR Dataproc]
    F -->|Spark and Beyond| G[2015+ Real Time and AI Era]
```

## 4.3 HDFS Block Replication Strategy (3x Default)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    File[Input File 400 MB] --> Split1[Block 1 128 MB]
    File --> Split2[Block 2 128 MB]
    File --> Split3[Block 3 128 MB]
    File --> Split4[Block 4 16 MB]
    Split1 --> R1A[Replica on Rack A Node 1]
    Split1 --> R1B[Replica on Rack B Node 2]
    Split1 --> R1C[Replica on Rack B Node 3]
    Split2 --> R2A[Replica on Rack A Node 1]
    Split2 --> R2B[Replica on Rack B Node 2]
    Split2 --> R2C[Replica on Rack B Node 3]
    Split3 --> R3A[Replica on Rack A Node 4]
    Split3 --> R3B[Replica on Rack B Node 5]
    Split3 --> R3C[Replica on Rack B Node 6]
    Split4 --> R4A[Replica on Rack A Node 4]
    Split4 --> R4B[Replica on Rack B Node 5]
    Split4 --> R4C[Replica on Rack B Node 6]
```

## 4.4 HDFS Read Operation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant NameNode as NameNode
    participant DataNode as DataNode
    Client->>NameNode: open(file_name)
    NameNode-->>Client: block locations list
    Client->>DataNode: read(block_id)
    DataNode-->>Client: data stream
    Client->>NameNode: close()
```

## 4.5 HDFS Write Operation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant NameNode as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2
    participant DN3 as DataNode 3
    Client->>NameNode: create(file_name)
    NameNode-->>Client: allowed (block locations)
    Client->>DN1: write block
    DN1->>DN2: forward to next replica
    DN2->>DN3: forward to next replica
    DN3-->>DN2: ack
    DN2-->>DN1: ack
    DN1-->>Client: write success ack
    Client->>NameNode: close()
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the 5 V's of Big Data with an example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The 5 V's of Big Data characterize modern datasets and distinguish them from traditional data:
- **Volume:** Refers to the massive size of data. Example: Facebook generates over **4 PB** of data daily.
- **Velocity:** Refers to the speed at which data is generated and must be processed. Example: High-frequency stock trading generates millions of transactions per second.
- **Variety:** Refers to the different forms of data — structured (RDBMS tables), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (videos, images). Example: YouTube hosts petabytes of unstructured video data.
- **Veracity:** Refers to the trustworthiness and quality of data. Example: Filtering spam or fake reviews from genuine ones on e-commerce platforms.
- **Value:** Refers to the meaningful insights extracted from data. Example: Amazon's recommendation engine converts browsing history into revenue.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Differentiate between traditional RDBMS and Hadoop.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Traditional RDBMS | Hadoop |
|---|---|---|
| Data Size | GB to low TB | TB to PB and beyond |
| Scaling | Vertical (scale up) | Horizontal (scale out) |
| Hardware | High-end servers | Commodity hardware |
| Data Type | Structured only | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured |
| Fault Tolerance | Low | High (replication-based) |
| Cost | Very high | Low |
| Processing | Real-time OLTP | Batch-oriented MapReduce |
| Schema | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-read |

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Discuss the evolution of Big Data, highlighting the role of the Google File System (GFS) and MapReduce papers. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The evolution of Big Data is a journey through escalating data volumes that traditional systems could not handle:

1. **1960s–1990s — RDBMS Era:** Data was limited to MB–GB scale. Systems like Oracle and DB2 dominated. They were vertically scalable, structured, and expensive. As data grew, vertical scaling hit physical and economic limits.

2. **Late 1990s–Early 2000s — Data Explosion:** The rise of the internet, e-commerce, and early social media pushed data into the TB range. Single machines could no longer store or process this. The industry needed a new paradigm.

3. **2003 — Google File System (GFS) Paper:** Published by Ghemawat, Gobioff, and Leung at Google. GFS proposed a **distributed file system** running on commodity hardware with:
   - Large block sizes (64 MB)
   - Master-slave architecture (single Master, many Chunkservers)
   - Built-in replication for fault tolerance
   - Optimized for large, streaming reads/writes

   This paper became the **conceptual blueprint** for HDFS.

4. **2004 — MapReduce Paper:** Published by Dean and Ghemawat. MapReduce provided a **programming model** for parallel processing of large datasets, abstracting away distributed system complexities. The two functions — **Map** (filter/sort) and **Reduce** (aggregate/summarize) — became the foundation for Hadoop's processing engine.

5. **2006 — Birth of Hadoop:** Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella implemented the GFS and MapReduce ideas as open-source Apache Hadoop (named after Cutting's son's toy elephant). Yahoo! adopted it, contributing to rapid development.

6. **2012 — Hadoop 2.0 / YARN:** Resource management decoupled from MapReduce, enabling multiple processing engines (Spark, Tez) on the same cluster.

7. **Post-2014 — Cloud Era:** AWS EMR, GCP Dataproc, Azure HDInsight made Hadoop accessible without owning hardware.

**The combined impact of the GFS and MapReduce papers** cannot be overstated — they together solved the "how to store" and "how to process" problems of Big Data simultaneously, defining the modern data engineering landscape.

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the HDFS architecture. List any four features of HDFS. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**HDFS Architecture** (Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1):

HDFS follows a **Master-Slave architecture**:
- **NameNode (Master):** Maintains file system metadata including filenames, directory tree, block-to-DataNode mapping, and permissions. It does NOT store user data. It runs on a high-memory machine.
- **DataNodes (Slaves):** Store the actual data blocks. Each block is **replicated** (default 3 times) across different DataNodes, often across different racks for fault tolerance. DataNodes send **heartbeat signals** to the NameNode every 3 seconds to confirm they are alive.
- **Secondary NameNode:** Periodically merges the **fsimage** (file system snapshot) and **edit logs** to prevent the edit log from growing unbounded. It is NOT a hot standby of the NameNode.
- **Client:** The user application that interacts with HDFS. It contacts the NameNode for metadata, then reads/writes directly to DataNodes.

**Data Flow:** A file is split into blocks of 128 MB (default). Each block is replicated 3 times across DataNodes. The NameNode decides the placement; the Client transfers data directly to DataNodes (not through NameNode), reducing the master's load.

**Four Key Features of HDFS:**

1. **Fault Tolerance:** Default replication factor of 3 ensures data is available even if 2 DataNodes fail.
2. **Scalability:** Horizontally scalable — add more DataNodes to increase storage linearly.
3. **High Throughput:** Large block size (128 MB) optimizes for streaming reads/writes rather than low-latency access.
4. **Cost Effective:** Runs on commodity hardware, drastically lowering infrastructure costs.
5. **Portability:** Written in Java, portable across heterogeneous hardware.
6. **Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM):** Optimized for workloads where files are written once and read many times.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — Alternative Choice

**(a) A 1.2 GB file is stored in HDFS with a default block size of 128 MB and replication factor 3. Calculate: (i) the number of blocks, (ii) the total cluster storage consumed, and (iii) the percentage of wasted (overhead) storage. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Number of Blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Blocks} &= \left\lceil \frac{1200 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil 9.375 \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= 10 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** 9 full blocks occupy 9 × 128 = 1152 MB. The remaining 48 MB forms the 10th block. Ceiling gives 10.

**(ii) Total Cluster Storage Consumed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Storage} &= 10 \text{ blocks} \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3 \\[6pt]
&= 3840 \text{ MB} \approx 3.75 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Overhead Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead \%} &= \left( \frac{3840 - 1200}{1200} \right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \left( \frac{2640}{1200} \right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 220\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** The 220% overhead comes from replication (200% from 3× replication) and partial last-block wastage (additional 20%).

**(b) Explain the working of HDFS Read and Write operations with sequence diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**HDFS Read Operation:**
1. The **Client** calls `FileSystem.open(path)` which contacts the **NameNode**.
2. The **NameNode** returns the list of block locations (DataNode IPs) for the file.
3. The **Client** then calls `FSDataInputStream.read()` on the nearest DataNode.
4. The **DataNode** streams the block data back to the Client.
5. Once a block is fully read, the connection to that DataNode closes, and the Client moves to the next block (possibly a different DataNode).
6. After all blocks are read, the Client calls `close()`.

**Key Point:** Data flows **directly** between Client and DataNode — not through the NameNode, which prevents the NameNode from being a bottleneck.

**HDFS Write Operation:**
1. The **Client** calls `FileSystem.create(path)` to create a new file in HDFS. The NameNode checks for duplicates and creates a new record.
2. The **Client** calls `FSDataOutputStream.write(data)`. The output stream splits data into packets.
3. Packets are sent to the **first DataNode** in a pipeline.
4. Each DataNode forwards packets to the next DataNode in the pipeline (replication factor 3 → 3-node pipeline).
5. Acknowledgments (ACKs) flow back from the last DataNode to the first, then to the Client.
6. After all blocks are written, the Client calls `close()`. The NameNode finalizes the file metadata.

**Key Points:** Pipeline replication ensures all replicas are written before success is reported. If any DataNode fails mid-pipeline, the pipeline is reconstructed excluding the failed node, and remaining replicas maintain the desired replication factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Block Calculation:** Forgetting the **ceiling function** in $\lceil \text{File}/\text{Block} \rceil$ — a 700 MB file needs 6 blocks, not 5.
> 2. **Replication Mistake:** Multiplying logical size by replication factor correctly for total physical storage, but forgetting that the last block may not be full.
> 3. **HDFS Confusion:** Writing "Secondary NameNode is a backup of NameNode" — INCORRECT. It is a checkpoint helper, not a hot standby.
> 4. **Data Flow:** Saying "Client reads/writes through the NameNode" — INCORRECT. Data flows directly between Client and DataNode; NameNode only handles metadata.
> 5. **5 V's:** Missing any one of Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value will cost 1 mark out of 3 in Part A.
> 6. **Block Size:** Using 64 MB (Hadoop 1.x default) instead of 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+ default) loses marks in calculation questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Big Data is defined by the **5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- ✅ **Apache Hadoop** consists of HDFS (storage), MapReduce (processing), and YARN (resource management).
- ✅ The **GFS (2003)** and **MapReduce (2004)** papers from Google are the conceptual foundation of Hadoop.
- ✅ **HDFS follows Master-Slave architecture** — NameNode (master), DataNodes (slaves), Secondary NameNode (checkpoint helper).
- ✅ **Default HDFS block size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+); **Default replication factor = 3**.
- ✅ Number of blocks formula: $\lceil \text{File Size} / \text{Block Size} \rceil$ — always use **ceiling**.
- ✅ Total cluster storage = Number of Blocks × Block Size × Replication Factor.
- ✅ **NameNode stores METADATA only** — never the actual data.
- ✅ **Data flows directly** between Client and DataNode — not through NameNode.
- ✅ Heartbeat interval between NameNode and DataNode = **3 seconds**.
- ✅ HDFS is optimized for **Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM)** workloads — NOT for low-latency random access.
- ✅ Hadoop uses **horizontal scaling** (add more commodity machines) vs. RDBMS's vertical scaling (bigger machines).
- ✅ Secondary NameNode is **NOT a hot standby/backup** for NameNode — it merges fsimage + edit logs.
- ✅ Hadoop is written in **Java** and supports heterogeneous hardware platforms.
- ✅ **Rack Awareness** policy places replicas across different racks to survive rack-level failures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Big Data Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Big Data Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data Characteristics** refer to the defining qualitative and quantitative attributes that distinguish large-scale, high-complexity datasets from traditional structured data. These characteristics describe **how data is generated, captured, stored, processed, and analyzed** when it exceeds the capability of conventional database systems.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) curriculum, the canonical model used to describe Big Data is the **"5 V's Framework"**, which serves as the foundation for all subsequent modules covering Hadoop, MapReduce, NoSQL, and Spark.

The **Five V's of Big Data** are:

| # | Characteristic | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volume** | Scale / Size of data |
| 2 | **Velocity** | Speed of data generation & movement |
| 3 | **Variety** | Heterogeneity of data types & sources |
| 4 | **Veracity** | Trustworthiness / quality of data |
| 5 | **Value** | Business / analytical worth extracted from data |

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mega-Library" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine a small neighborhood library with **10,000 books** — each cataloged, indexed, and easy to find. This is **traditional data**.
>
> Now imagine the **entire internet** as one library: 50 billion web pages, 500 hours of video uploaded every minute, 500 million tweets per day, and 1 trillion sensor readings. There is no central card catalog. Books are in different languages, formats (PDF, video, audio), and many are duplicates, broken, or unreliable. **This is Big Data.**
>
> The **5 V's** are simply the **five ways** this mega-library is different from your local one.

---

### 1.3 Why the 5 V's Matter in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Every Module 1 question on Big Data Characteristics is mapped to at least one of the 5 V's. Students who memorize the V's *without* understanding their engineering implications lose marks on application-based sub-questions.

The 5 V's are not just descriptive labels — they directly dictate:
- **Storage architecture** (HDFS, distributed file systems)
- **Processing paradigm** (batch vs. stream)
- **Schema design** (schema-on-read vs. schema-on-write)
- **Data quality pipelines** (cleansing, validation)
- **Business intelligence strategy** (ROI of analytics)

---

### 1.4 Extended Characteristics (Beyond the Core 5)

Modern data engineering literature (and KTU reference material) also recognizes additional V's. These are **higher-order derivatives** of the core 5:

- **Variability** — Change in data rate / meaning over time
- **Validity** — Correctness of data for intended use
- **Vulnerability** — Security and privacy concerns
- **Viscosity** — Resistance to flow / processing bottlenecks
- **Virality** — Speed and extent of data spread across networks

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristic 1 — VOLUME

**Definition:** The sheer *quantity* of data generated, stored, and processed, typically measured in Petabytes (PB), Exabytes (EB), or Zettabytes (ZB).

> **KTU Benchmark Metrics (Memorize for ESE):**
> - **1 PB = 10⁶ GB = 10¹⁵ Bytes**
> - **1 EB = 10⁹ GB = 10¹⁸ Bytes**
> - **1 ZB = 10¹² GB = 10²¹ Bytes**
> - Global data sphere projected to reach **~175 ZB by 2025** (IDC Report — KTU reference data).

**Sources of Volume:**
- Social media platforms (Facebook, X/Twitter, Instagram)
- IoT sensors (smart devices, industrial SCADA)
- Scientific instruments (LHC at CERN — ~1 PB/s raw, ~25 PB/year stored)
- E-commerce transactions (Amazon, Flipkart)
- Healthcare records (EHR, genomics)

**Engineering Implication:** Traditional RDBMS cannot handle PB+ scale → Requires **distributed storage** (HDFS, S3, GFS) and **horizontal scaling** (commodity hardware clusters).

---

### 2.2 Characteristic 2 — VELOCITY

**Definition:** The *speed* at which data is generated, moved, processed, and acted upon.

**Two Sub-Dimensions:**

1. **Data In-Velocity** — Rate of arrival
2. **Data Out-Velocity** — Rate at which insights must be delivered

> **KTU Benchmark Metrics:**
> - **Stock trading:** decisions required in **microseconds (μs)**
> - **Credit card fraud detection:** real-time, **< 100 ms latency**
> - **Twitter/X firehose:** ~500 million tweets/day ≈ **5,786 tweets/second**
> - **IoT sensor data:** can stream at **millions of events/second**

**Engineering Implication:** Velocity drives the choice between **batch processing** (Hadoop MapReduce) and **stream processing** (Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, Apache Storm).

---

### 2.3 Characteristic 3 — VARIETY

**Definition:** The *heterogeneity* of data types, formats, and sources.

**Three Major Data Categories:**

| Type | Description | Examples | Storage Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structured** | Tabular, schema-defined | RDBMS tables, OLAP cubes | Row/Column (Parquet, ORC) |
| **Semi-Structured** | Self-describing, partial schema | JSON, XML, YAML, Log files | Key-Value, Document |
| **Unstructured** | No predefined schema | Images, video, audio, text | Object stores (S3, HDFS blobs) |

> **KTU Typical Question:** *“Give two examples each for structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data.”* (3-mark standard)

**Engineering Implication:** Requires **polyglot persistence** — using multiple storage technologies (RDBMS + NoSQL + Object Store) suited to each data type.

---

### 2.4 Characteristic 4 — VERACITY

**Definition:** The *trustworthiness, accuracy, and reliability* of data — i.e., how "dirty" or "clean" it is.

**Components of Veracity:**
- **Consistency** — Same data across systems
- **Completeness** — No missing values
- **Conformity** — Adheres to defined formats
- **Integrity** — Relationships between data are valid
- **Timeliness** — Data is up-to-date

> **KTU Insight:** Veracity is often the *hardest* V to engineer for, because it is **probabilistic** and **context-dependent**.

**Engineering Implication:** Requires **data quality frameworks** (Apache Griffin, Deequ, Great Expectations) and **data lineage** tracking.

---

### 2.5 Characteristic 5 — VALUE

**Definition:** The *business, scientific, or operational worth* extracted from Big Data after processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle:** *"Data without value is just cost."*
> Big Data is only meaningful when it produces **actionable insights** — predictions, recommendations, optimizations, or automations.

**Value Extraction Techniques:**
- Descriptive analytics (dashboards, reports)
- Diagnostic analytics (root cause analysis)
- Predictive analytics (ML models)
- Prescriptive analytics (decision optimization)

**Engineering Implication:** Drives investment in **data analytics platforms** (Hive, Presto, Spark SQL, MLlib) and **BI tools** (Tableau, Power BI, Superset).

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Relationships for Big Data Characteristics**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B} \right\rceil$$ | Number of HDFS blocks = ceiling of Data size / Block size | HDFS storage planning |
| 2 | $$T_{transfer} = \frac{D}{BW}$$ | Transfer time = Data size / Bandwidth | Network capacity planning |
| 3 | $$S_{total} = N_{blocks} \times B \times R$$ | Total storage with replication (R = replication factor) | HDFS capacity calculation |
| 4 | $$T_{hadoop} = T_{map} + T_{shuffle} + T_{reduce}$$ | Hadoop job total time | Performance tuning |
| 5 | $$T_{parallel} = \frac{T_{seq}}{N_{nodes}} + T_{overhead}$$ | Amdahl's law — speedup with N nodes | Cluster sizing |
| 6 | $$\text{Throughput} = \frac{D_{processed}}{T_{total}}$$ | Data processed per unit time | Velocity metric |
| 7 | $$\text{Latency} = T_{event} - T_{result}$$ | Time from event to insight | Real-time SLAs |
| 8 | $$\text{Data Quality Score} = \frac{N_{valid}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$ | Veracity metric | Data governance |
| 9 | $$\text{Storage Growth} = D_0 \times 2^{t}$$ | Doubling period (Kryder's Law) | Volume forecasting |
| 10 | $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{serial}}{T_{parallel}}$$ | Parallel efficiency | Cluster performance |

> **Where:**
> - $D$ = Data size (Bytes)
> - $B$ = HDFS block size (default **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+)
> - $R$ = HDFS replication factor (default **3**)
> - $BW$ = Network bandwidth (Bytes/sec)
> - $N_{nodes}$ = Number of worker nodes
> - $T_{overhead}$ = Coordination, I/O, serialization overhead

---

### 2.7 Real-World Utility of the 5 V's

| Industry | Dominant V's | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Banking / FinTech** | Velocity, Veracity | Fraud detection, algorithmic trading |
| **Healthcare** | Volume, Variety, Value | Genomics, EHR analytics, drug discovery |
| **Retail / E-Commerce** | Variety, Value, Velocity | Recommendation engines, dynamic pricing |
| **Smart Cities / IoT** | Volume, Velocity | Traffic mgmt, pollution monitoring |
| **Social Media** | Volume, Variety, Virality | Sentiment analysis, trend detection |
| **Astronomy / Physics** | Volume, Velocity | LHC data analysis, telescope feeds |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — HDFS Block Count from Data Size

**Problem Statement:** Compute the number of HDFS blocks required to store **2.5 TB** of data, given a default HDFS block size of **128 MB**.

**Step 1: Convert units to a common base (Bytes).**

$$1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB} = 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB}$$

$$D = 2.5 \text{ TB} = 2.5 \times 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB} = 2{,}621{,}440 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2: Apply the block-count formula.**

$$N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2{,}621{,}440 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3: Perform the division.**

$$2{,}621{,}440 \div 128 = 20{,}480$$

**Step 4: Since 20,480 is already an integer, the ceiling operation does not change the value.**

$$N_{blocks} = 20{,}480 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**
> Each of the 20,480 blocks will be replicated **3 times** (default), occupying **60,960 physical blocks** across the cluster.

**Final Answer:** 20,480 logical blocks (60,960 physical replicas).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total HDFS Storage with Replication

**Problem Statement:** If 2.5 TB of data is stored in HDFS with a replication factor of **R = 3** and a block size of **128 MB**, what is the **total physical storage** consumed?

**Step 1: Compute logical storage (data only).**

$$S_{logical} = D = 2.5 \text{ TB} = 2560 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 2: Apply replication multiplier.**

$$S_{total} = S_{logical} \times R = 2560 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 7680 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3: Express in TB.**

$$S_{total} = \frac{7680}{1024} \text{ TB} = 7.5 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 4: Verify via block-level calculation.**

$$S_{total} = N_{blocks} \times B \times R = 20{,}480 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3$$

$$S_{total} = 20{,}480 \times 384 \text{ MB} = 7{,}864{,}320 \text{ MB}$$

$$S_{total} = \frac{7{,}864{,}320}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ TB} = 7.5 \text{ TB} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** 7.5 TB of total physical storage.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Data Transfer Time over Network

**Problem Statement:** Transfer **1 PB** of data over a **10 Gbps** link. Compute the transfer time.

**Step 1: Convert data size to bits.**

$$D = 1 \text{ PB} = 10^{15} \text{ Bytes} = 8 \times 10^{15} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Express bandwidth in bits per second.**

$$BW = 10 \text{ Gbps} = 10 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/sec} = 10^{10} \text{ bits/sec}$$

**Step 3: Apply transfer time formula.**

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{D}{BW} = \frac{8 \times 10^{15}}{10^{10}} \text{ sec} = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 4: Convert to hours.**

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{8 \times 10^5}{3600} \text{ hours} \approx 222.22 \text{ hours} \approx 9.26 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5: Engineering insight.**
> A 10 Gbps link is insufficient for PB-scale data movement. Real systems use **parallel multi-Gbps links**, **dedicated dark fiber**, or **physical shipment of disks** (the famous "sneakernet" used by AWS Snowmobile for 100 PB transfers).

**Final Answer:** ~9.26 days on a single 10 Gbps link.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Speedup via Parallelism (Amdahl's Law Simplified)

**Problem Statement:** A MapReduce job takes **10 hours** on a single node. **90%** of the job is parallelizable; the remaining **10%** is inherently sequential. Compute the theoretical speedup and time when run on **N = 8** nodes.

**Step 1: State the simplified Amdahl's Law.**

$$T_{parallel} = (1 - P) \times T_{seq} + \frac{P \times T_{seq}}{N}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**
- $P = 0.90$ (parallel fraction)
- $1 - P = 0.10$ (sequential fraction)
- $T_{seq} = 10$ hours
- $N = 8$

$$T_{parallel} = (0.10 \times 10) + \frac{0.90 \times 10}{8}$$

**Step 3: Compute each term.**

$$T_{parallel} = 1.0 + \frac{9.0}{8} = 1.0 + 1.125 = 2.125 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4: Compute speedup.**

$$S = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{parallel}} = \frac{10}{2.125} \approx 4.71\times$$

**Step 5: Maximum theoretical speedup (N → ∞).**

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10\times$$

> **Insight:** Even with infinite nodes, the sequential 10% caps speedup at 10×. This is the **fundamental limit of parallel computing**.

**Final Answer:** 2.125 hours (4.71× speedup) on 8 nodes; theoretical max 10×.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Characteristic vs. Engineering Solution

| Characteristic | Challenge | Typical Big Data Tool | Storage / Processing Layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | TB–PB scale storage | HDFS, Amazon S3, GFS | Distributed file system |
| **Velocity** | Real-time ingestion | Kafka, Flume, Pulsar | Stream processing layer |
| **Variety** | Multiple data formats | Avro, Parquet, JSON parsers | Schema-on-read engines |
| **Veracity** | Noisy / inconsistent data | Apache Griffin, Deequ | Data quality layer |
| **Value** | Extracting insights | Spark MLlib, Hive, Presto | Analytics / ML layer |
