```mermaid
    dn1 -.->|Heartbeat Every 3 sec| nnMaster
    dn2 -.->|Heartbeat Every 3 sec| nnMaster
    dn3 -.->|Heartbeat Every 3 sec| nnMaster
    dn4 -.->|Heartbeat Every 3 sec| nnMaster
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Client** first asks the **NameNode** for metadata (step 1–2), then directly streams data blocks to or from **DataNodes** (step 3). This is called the **"metadata-then-data"** pattern, which is fundamental to HDFS scalability.

## 4.2 MapReduce Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputHDFS[Input Data on HDFS]
    splitA[Input Split A]
    splitB[Input Split B]
    splitC[Input Split C]
    mapA[Mapper Task 1]
    mapB[Mapper Task 2]
    mapC[Mapper Task 3]
    combA[Combiner Optional]
    combB[Combiner Optional]
    partA[Partitioner]
    partB[Partitioner]
    shuffleA[Shuffle and Sort Phase]
    shuffleB[Shuffle and Sort Phase]
    reduceA[Reducer Task 1]
    reduceB[Reducer Task 2]
    outputFinal[Final Output to HDFS]
    inputHDFS --> splitA
    inputHDFS --> splitB
    inputHDFS --> splitC
    splitA --> mapA
    splitB --> mapB
    splitC --> mapC
    mapA --> combA
    mapB --> combB
    combA --> partA
    combB --> partB
    partA --> shuffleA
    partB --> shuffleB
    shuffleA --> reduceA
    shuffleB --> reduceB
    reduceA --> outputFinal
    reduceB --> outputFinal
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The **Shuffle and Sort** phase is the most **I/O-intensive** stage of MapReduce. It involves **disk writes, network transfer, merging, and sorting**. The **Combiner** drastically reduces shuffle data volume.

## 4.3 YARN Architecture — Detailed Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    appClient[Client Application]
    rmMaster[ResourceManager]
    sched[Scheduler]
    appsMgr[ApplicationsManager]
    nm1[NodeManager 1]
    nm2[NodeManager 2]
    amApp1[ApplicationMaster 1]
    amApp2[ApplicationMaster 2]
    contA1[Container 1 - Map Task]
    contA2[Container 2 - Reduce Task]
    contB1[Container 3 - Spark Executor]
    rmMaster --> sched
    rmMaster --> appsMgr
    appClient -->|1 Submit Job| rmMaster
    appsMgr -->|2 Allocate Container| nm1
    appsMgr -->|2 Allocate Container| nm2
    nm1 -->|3 Launch| amApp1
    nm2 -->|3 Launch| amApp2
    amApp1 -->|4 Request Resources| rmMaster
    amApp2 -->|4 Request Resources| rmMaster
    amApp1 -->|5 Launch Container| contA1
    amApp1 -->|5 Launch Container| contA2
    amApp2 -->|5 Launch Container| contB1
    nm1 -.->|Heartbeat and Resource Report| rmMaster
    nm2 -.->|Heartbeat and Resource Report| rmMaster
```

> [!NOTE]
> **YARN allows multi-tenancy:** A single cluster can simultaneously run **MapReduce, Spark, Tez, and Storm** jobs. Each application has its own **ApplicationMaster**, ensuring isolation.

## 4.4 Hadoop Ecosystem — High-Level Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    coreLayer[Hadoop Core]
    hdfsNode[HDFS - Storage]
    mrNode[MapReduce - Processing]
    yarnNode[YARN - Resource Management]
    ingestLayer[Data Ingestion Layer]
    sqoopNode[Sqoop - RDBMS Bridge]
    flumeNode[Flume - Log Streaming]
    kafkaNode[Kafka - Message Queue]
    queryLayer[Query and Analytics Layer]
    hiveNode[Hive - SQL on HDFS]
    pigNode[Pig - Data Flow Scripting]
    sparkNode[Spark - In-Memory Engine]
    impalaNode[Impala - Real-Time SQL]
    storeLayer[Storage Extension Layer]
    hbaseNode[HBase - NoSQL DB]
    accumuloNode[Accumulo - Secure NoSQL]
    coordLayer[Coordination Layer]
    zkNode[ZooKeeper - Coordinator]
    oozieNode[Oozie - Workflow Scheduler]
    monitorLayer[Monitoring Layer]
    ambariNode[Ambari - Cluster Manager]
    hueNode[Hue - Web UI]
    coreLayer --> hdfsNode
    coreLayer --> mrNode
    coreLayer --> yarnNode
    ingestLayer --> hdfsNode
    sqoopNode --> ingestLayer
    flumeNode --> ingestLayer
    kafkaNode --> ingestLayer
    queryLayer --> hdfsNode
    hiveNode --> queryLayer
    pigNode --> queryLayer
    sparkNode --> queryLayer
    impalaNode --> queryLayer
    storeLayer --> hdfsNode
    hbaseNode --> storeLayer
    accumuloNode --> storeLayer
    coordLayer --> yarnNode
    zkNode --> coordLayer
    oozieNode --> coordLayer
    monitorLayer --> yarnNode
    ambariNode --> monitorLayer
    hueNode --> monitorLayer
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This diagram is a high-yield KTU visual.** In 14-mark questions, drawing this layered ecosystem architecture scores full marks when accompanied by short tool descriptions.

## 4.5 Hadoop Block Replication — Rack-Aware Placement

```mermaid
graph TD
    rack1[Rack 1]
    rack2[Rack 2]
    node1R1[DataNode A1]
    node2R1[DataNode A2]
    node1R2[DataNode B1]
    node2R2[DataNode B2]
    rack1 --> node1R1
    rack1 --> node2R1
    rack2 --> node1R2
    rack2 --> node2R2
    blockX[Block X - 3 Replicas]
    blockX -->|Replica 1 Same Node| node1R1
    blockX -->|Replica 2 Same Rack| node2R1
    blockX -->|Replica 3 Different Rack| node1R2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Rack Awareness Strategy (default):**
> - 1st replica: on the **local DataNode** (where the writer is).
> - 2nd replica: on a **different node in the same rack**.
> - 3rd replica: on a **different node in a different rack**.
> This balances **reliability** (rack failure protection) with **network bandwidth** (intra-rack writes are faster).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Hadoop. List and explain any four characteristics of Hadoop.

> **Model Answer:**
> **Hadoop** is an open-source, distributed computing framework based on Java that enables the storage and processing of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers using the **MapReduce** programming model.
>
> The **four key characteristics** are:
> 1. **Scalability** – Hadoop scales horizontally by adding new nodes to the cluster. It supports thousands of nodes linearly.
> 2. **Fault Tolerance** – Data is replicated across multiple nodes (default replication factor = 3), so failure of a node does not cause data loss.
> 3. **Cost-Effectiveness** – It uses inexpensive commodity hardware instead of high-end enterprise servers, drastically reducing infrastructure cost.
> 4. **Flexibility** – It can store and process structured, semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (images, videos, logs) data.
>
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 2: What is the role of the NameNode in HDFS? Why is it called the master node?

> **Model Answer:**
> The **NameNode** is the master daemon in HDFS that manages the **file system namespace** and stores **metadata** such as file names, directory structures, permissions, block-to-DataNode mapping, and replication factor.
>
> It is called the **master node** because:
> 1. It **coordinates** all read/write requests from clients.
> 2. It **instructs** DataNodes to create, replicate, or delete blocks.
> 3. It **receives heartbeats** (every 3 seconds) and block reports (every 6 hours) from all DataNodes.
> 4. It is the **single source of truth** for the cluster's metadata, making it a single point of failure (mitigated by Secondary NameNode or HA).
>
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain HDFS architecture with a neat diagram. Discuss the read and write operations in detail. (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **HDFS Architecture:**
> HDFS follows a **master-slave architecture**. The **NameNode** (master) runs on a high-RAM machine, while multiple **DataNodes** (slaves) run on commodity machines across one or more racks.
>
> - **NameNode** stores metadata in two files: `FsImage` (snapshot) and `EditLog` (transaction log). The **Secondary NameNode** periodically merges these to prevent EditLog from growing unbounded.
> - **DataNodes** store actual data blocks. Each block is **128 MB** (default) and replicated **3 times** across the cluster using **rack-aware placement**.
>
> **Read Operation Steps:**
> 1. The **Client** opens a file using `FileSystem.open()` and contacts the **NameNode**.
> 2. The **NameNode** returns the list of **DataNodes** holding each block (sorted by network proximity).
> 3. The **Client** directly connects to the **DataNodes** via `FSDataInputStream` and reads blocks in parallel.
> 4. If a DataNode fails mid-read, the client transparently connects to the next replica.
> 5. After reading, the client calls `close()`.
>
> **Write Operation Steps:**
> 1. The **Client** calls `create()` on NameNode, which allocates new blocks on suitable DataNodes.
> 2. Data is broken into **128 MB packets** and written to the first DataNode.
> 3. The first DataNode **pipelines** the packet to the second, then the third.
> 4. After successful replication, an **ack packet** flows back upstream.
> 5. Once all blocks are written, the NameNode commits the metadata.
>
> **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 in these notes for a full HDFS architecture diagram.)
>
> **(7 Marks: Architecture 2 + Read 2.5 + Write 2.5)**
>
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### (b) With a suitable example (e.g., Word Count), explain the MapReduce programming model. Discuss the role of the Combiner and Partitioner. (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **MapReduce Model:**
> MapReduce processes data in two main phases: **Map** and **Reduce**, with an intermediate **Shuffle and Sort** phase.
>
> **Example: Word Count**
>
> **Input Data (HDFS):**
> ```
> Hello World Hello Hadoop
> Hello World
> ```
>
> **Map Phase:** Each line is processed by a Mapper. The output is `(word, 1)` pairs.
> ```
> (Hello, 1), (World, 1), (Hello, 1), (Hadoop, 1)
> (Hello, 1), (World, 1)
> ```
>
> **Shuffle and Sort Phase:** Keys are sorted and grouped:
> ```
> (Hadoop, [1])
> (Hello, [1, 1, 1])
> (World, [1, 1])
> ```
>
> **Reduce Phase:** Each Reducer sums the values:
> ```
> (Hadoop, 1)
> (Hello, 3)
> (World, 2)
> ```
>
> **Role of the Combiner:**
> - The **Combiner** is a local reducer that runs on the **mapper node** itself, **before the shuffle phase**.
> - It aggregates intermediate output **on the mapper side** to reduce network bandwidth.
> - **Example:** In Word Count, the Combiner sums local `(Hello, 1)` pairs to `(Hello, 3)` before sending to the Reducer.
> - **Constraint:** It can be used **only when the reduce function is commutative and associative**.
>
> **Role of the Partitioner:**
> - The Partitioner decides which **Reducer** will process a given key.
> - Default Partitioner uses: $\text{hash}(key) \bmod R$, where $R$ = number of reducers.
> - **Example:** If we have 2 reducers and the hash of `"Hello"` is 47, then $47 \bmod 2 = 1$, so it goes to **Reducer 1**.
>
> **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 in these notes for a MapReduce data flow diagram.)
>
> **(7 Marks: MapReduce model 2 + Word Count example 2.5 + Combiner 1.5 + Partitioner 1)**
>
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Discuss the Hadoop ecosystem with a neat diagram. Explain the functions of HBase, Hive, Pig, Sqoop, Flume, and Oozie. (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The **Hadoop Ecosystem** is a suite of open-source tools that complement Hadoop's core (HDFS, MapReduce, YARN) to provide a complete big data platform. The ecosystem is organized in **layers**:
>
> **1. Storage Layer:**
> - **HBase** – A NoSQL, column-oriented, distributed database running on HDFS. It provides **real-time, random read/write** access to large tables. Inspired by Google's BigTable. Used for sparse datasets.
>
> **2. Ingestion Layer:**
> - **Sqoop** – A tool for transferring bulk data between **Hadoop and RDBMS** (e.g., MySQL, Oracle). Uses JDBC and parallel import/export.
> - **Flume** – A distributed service for collecting, aggregating, and moving **streaming log data** into HDFS/HBase. Architecture: **Source → Channel → Sink**.
>
> **3. Query/Analytics Layer:**
> - **Hive** – A **data warehouse** system that provides **SQL-like queries** via HQL. Translates queries into MapReduce/Tez/Spark jobs. Best for batch analytics.
> - **Pig** – A **data flow scripting language** (Pig Latin) for ETL pipelines. Pig Engine compiles Pig Latin scripts into MapReduce jobs.
>
> **4. Coordination/Workflow Layer:**
> - **Oozie** – A **workflow scheduler** for Hadoop jobs. Supports DAG-based workflows and time-based coordinators.
>
> **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.4 in these notes for the layered ecosystem architecture.)
>
> **(7 Marks: Diagram 2 + HBase 1 + Hive 1 + Pig 0.75 + Sqoop 0.75 + Flume 0.75 + Oozie 0.75)**
>
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### (b) Explain YARN architecture in detail. Compare YARN with the Hadoop 1.x JobTracker architecture. (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator):**
> Introduced in **Hadoop 2.x**, YARN decouples **resource management** from the **processing model**, enabling multi-tenancy (MapReduce, Spark, Tez, etc. on the same cluster).
>
> **YARN Key Components:**
> 1. **ResourceManager (RM):** Global master daemon that arbitrates resources among all applications. Has two sub-components:
>    - **Scheduler:** Allocates resources based on capacity or fairness policies.
>    - **ApplicationsManager (AsM):** Accepts job submissions and launches the ApplicationMaster.
> 2. **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node agent responsible for **container management**, resource monitoring (CPU, memory, disk, network), and log management.
> 3. **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-application; negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs to execute and monitor tasks.
> 4. **Container:** A logical bundle of resources (RAM + vCores) where a task runs. Allocated by RM and managed by NM.
>
> **YARN Workflow:**
> 1. Client submits an application to RM.
> 2. RM allocates a container and launches the **ApplicationMaster**.
> 3. AM requests more containers from RM (as needed).
> 4. AM works with NMs to launch task containers.
> 5. Tasks report progress back to AM; AM reports to RM.
>
> **Comparison Table:**
>
> | Feature | Hadoop 1.x (JobTracker/TaskTracker) | Hadoop 2.x+ (YARN) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Architecture | Monolithic (tightly coupled) | **Decoupled** (ResourceManager + per-app ApplicationMaster) |
> | Scalability | Limited (~4,000 nodes) | **Scales to 10,000+ nodes** |
> | Multi-Tenancy | Only MapReduce | **Multiple engines** (Spark, Tez, MapReduce, Storm) |
> | Failure Recovery | JobTracker restart = long downtime | **AM restart** is per-application and fast |
> | Resource Awareness | Slot-based (fixed Map/Reduce slots) | **Container-based** (flexible RAM + vCore allocation) |
> | API Support | MapReduce only | **Generic YARN API** for any distributed app |
> | High Availability | No HA for JobTracker | **RM HA** via Zookeeper |
>
> **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 in these notes for a full YARN architecture diagram.)
>
> **(7 Marks: YARN components 2.5 + Workflow 1.5 + Comparison table 3)**
>
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HDFS and HBase** – HDFS is a **file system** (storage of files as blocks). HBase is a **NoSQL database** (real-time read/write on top of HDFS). Do not interchange them.
>
> 2. **Wrong block size** – In KTU 2024 scheme, examiners expect **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+ default). Writing **64 MB** is now considered outdated and may lose partial marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting replication in storage calculation** – If a question asks for **physical storage used**, you **must multiply by replication factor (3)**. Writing only the logical file size is a common error.
>
> 4. **Missing the ceiling function** – When calculating number of blocks, always use the **ceiling function** for non-divisible file sizes. Forgetting the ceiling leads to undercounting.
>
> 5. **Confusing JobTracker with YARN** – Hadoop 1.x used JobTracker/TaskTracker. Hadoop 2.x+ uses ResourceManager/NodeManager. Examiners penalize mixed-up terminology.
>
> 6. **Writing the MapReduce diagram without Shuffle & Sort phase** – This phase is **mandatory** in the diagram for full marks. Many students skip it.
>
> 7. **Not explaining Combiner constraints** – The Combiner works **only for commutative and associative** functions. Writing just "Combiner reduces data" loses 1 mark.
>
> 8. **Mixing up Hive and Pig** – Hive is **SQL-based (HQL)** and best for analysts. Pig is **script-based (Pig Latin)** and best for ETL developers. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Hadoop Ecosystem and Core Components**
>
> - **Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based, distributed framework for **storage and processing** of big data using the **MapReduce** programming model.
> - The **three core components** are **HDFS** (storage), **MapReduce** (processing), and **YARN** (resource management).
> - HDFS follows a **master-slave architecture** with **NameNode (master)** and **DataNodes (slaves)**.
> - **HDFS default block size = 128 MB**; **default replication factor = 3** (rack-aware placement).
> - **Heartbeat interval = 3 seconds**; **block report interval = 6 hours**.
> - **MapReduce phases:** Input Split → Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce → Output.
> - **Combiner** is a local reducer used **only for commutative and associative** functions.
> - **Partitioner** uses the formula $\text{hash}(key) \bmod R$ to route keys to reducers.
> - **Number of mappers** = $\lceil \text{Input Size} / \text{Block Size} \rceil$.
> - **YARN components:** ResourceManager (Scheduler + ApplicationsManager), NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, Container.
> - YARN enables **multi-tenancy** (MapReduce, Spark, Tez, etc. on the same cluster).
> - **Hadoop 1.x** had JobTracker/TaskTracker with limited scalability; **Hadoop 2.x+** introduced YARN.
> - **Hadoop 3.x** adds **Erasure Coding** (saves ~50% storage), HA NameNode, and Federation.
> - **Ecosystem tools to memorize:** HBase (NoSQL), Hive (SQL/HQL), Pig (Pig Latin), Sqoop (RDBMS bridge), Flume (log streaming), Oozie (workflow), ZooKeeper (coordination), Spark (in-memory), Ambari (monitoring), Avro (serialization), Mahout (ML), HCatalog (schema).
> - **Rack-aware replica placement:** 1st replica on local node, 2nd on same rack, 3rd on different rack.
> - **NameNode RAM ≈ 150 bytes per block** (used for capacity planning).
> - **Hadoop advantages:** Scalability, fault tolerance, cost-effectiveness, flexibility, schema-on-read, data locality, high throughput.
> - **Hadoop limitations:** Not suitable for low-latency real-time queries, small file problem, no native encryption (added in 3.x), iterative algorithms are slow (solved by Spark).
> - **Hadoop 3.x default ports:** NameNode UI = **9870**, ResourceManager UI = **8088**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HDFS Architecture: Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** HDFS Architecture: Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is the primary storage layer of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem. It is a **highly fault-tolerant, scalable, and distributed file system** designed to store very large files (terabytes to petabytes) reliably across commodity hardware machines in a cluster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block (in HDFS):** A **block** is the smallest contiguous unit of data that HDFS can read from or write to. Every file stored in HDFS is split into one or more fixed-size **blocks**, each of which is stored as an independent file on a DataNode. The **default block size in Hadoop 2.x and later is 128 MB** (which can be raised to **256 MB** or more for very large clusters). The **default replication factor is 3** (i.e., three independent copies of every block are maintained across different DataNodes).

In other words, HDFS does not see a file as a single whole — it sees a sequence of **logical blocks** of equal size (except the last one, which may be smaller), and each block is replicated and stored on different machines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The National Library System

Imagine a librarian who is asked to preserve a 1,000-page encyclopedia.

- Instead of keeping the entire encyclopedia in a single shelf (where one fire or one theft can destroy everything), the librarian **tears the book into 100-page volumes**.
- Each volume is **photocopied three times** and the three copies are placed in **three different library branches** located in three different cities.
- A **central catalog** (the index) at the headquarters maintains a record of which volume is stored in which branch.

| Library Analogy | HDFS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Encyclopedia | The full file (e.g., a 1 GB log file) |
| 100-page volume | An HDFS **block** (e.g., 128 MB) |
| Three branches storing the same volume | **Replication** across DataNodes |
| Central catalog of volumes | **NameNode metadata** |
| Branch librarian who hands the volume to a reader | **DataNode** |
| Reader who requests the book | **HDFS Client** |

This block-level abstraction is the cornerstone of HDFS reliability and parallelism.

### 1.3 Why Blocks? — The Three Engineering Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> The block abstraction delivers three engineering advantages that justify its existence:
> 1. **Fault tolerance via replication** — losing a single DataNode does not lose data.
> 2. **Parallel I/O** — different blocks of the same file can be read simultaneously from multiple DataNodes.
> 3. **Storage efficiency** — HDFS can store files larger than the disk of any single machine, and the last partial block does not waste a full block's worth of disk.

### 1.4 Key Default Metrics (must memorize)

| Metric | Default Value | Configurable Property |
|---|---|---|
| Block size (Hadoop 2.x / 3.x) | **128 MB** | `dfs.blocksize` |
| Replication factor | **3** | `dfs.replication` |
| Heartbeat interval (DataNode → NameNode) | **3 seconds** | `dfs.heartbeat.interval` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the HDFS Block Architecture

HDFS follows a **master–worker (NameNode–DataNode) architecture**. The block concept ties every component together.

#### 2.1.1 The Three Logical Tiers

**Tier 1 — NameNode (Master):**
- Stores **metadata only**, never user data.
- Maintains two critical files: `fsimage` (checkpoint) and `edit logs` (transactional record).
- Holds the **file-to-block mapping** and the **block-to-DataNode mapping** in RAM.
- Acts as the *central catalog* from our library analogy.

**Tier 2 — DataNodes (Workers):**
- Store the **actual blocks** on their local disks.
- Send a **heartbeat** every 3 seconds to the NameNode to declare they are alive.
- Serve read/write requests directly from HDFS clients.

**Tier 3 — Client:**
- Talks to the NameNode first to discover which DataNode holds a particular block.
- Then talks **directly** to the DataNode(s) to transfer data. The NameNode is **never** in the data path.

#### 2.1.2 What Exactly is Stored Where?

| Information Category | Stored On | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| File name, path, permissions, owner | NameNode | File-level metadata |
| File → list of block IDs | NameNode | Block mapping |
| Block ID → list of DataNode IPs | NameNode | Replica locations |
| Actual block bytes | DataNode local disk | User data |
| Block checksums (CRC32) | DataNode | Integrity verification |
| Generation stamp of a block | NameNode + DataNode | Versioning |

### 2.2 Block Lifecycle — Step by Step

1. **File creation:** Client calls `create()` on `FileSystem`; NameNode allocates a new file entry in its metadata.
2. **Block split (client side):** The client splits the file stream into block-sized chunks (default **128 MB**).
3. **Pipeline replication:** The first DataNode writes the block locally and forwards it to a second DataNode, which forwards it to a third — a **replication pipeline** of length 3.
4. **Acknowledgement:** Once all replicas acknowledge, the client is told the block is safely written.
5. **Block report:** DataNodes periodically send a full **block report** to the NameNode so the master can reconcile its in-memory map with reality.

### 2.3 Block Placement Policy (Rack Awareness)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HDFS uses a **rack-aware placement strategy** for the default replication factor of 3:
> - **Replica 1** → on the **local rack** of the writer (same DataNode, or another DataNode on the same rack).
> - **Replica 2** → on a **different rack** (off-rack).
> - **Replica 3** → on the **same different rack** as Replica 2, but on a distinct DataNode.
>
> This guarantees that the loss of an entire rack does not lose all copies of a block, while keeping one replica "local" for read performance.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical HDFS problems in KTU exams reduce to the following four formulas. Memorize the table below.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of blocks per file | $N_b = \left\lceil \dfrac{S_f}{S_b} \right\rceil$ | dimensionless | $S_f$ = file size, $S_b$ = block size. The **ceiling** function is critical — a 129 MB file with a 128 MB block still creates **2** blocks. |
| 2 | Last block actual size | $S_{\text{last}} = S_f \bmod S_b$ | MB / GB | If $S_f$ is an exact multiple, the last block is exactly $S_b$. |
| 3 | Total physical storage consumed | $S_{\text{total}} = N_b \times S_b \times R$ | MB / GB / TB | $R$ = replication factor. This counts the disk space **including the wasted tail of the last block**. |
| 4 | Usable (logical) file size | $S_{\text{logical}} = S_f$ | MB / GB / TB | The client never sees the wasted tail; it is internal overhead. |
| 5 | Storage overhead | $\Delta S = S_{\text{total}} - S_{\text{logical}}$ | MB / GB | Wasted bytes = replicas of the partial tail of the last block. |
| 6 | Throughput scaling | $T_{\text{read}} \approx \min\!\left(N_{\text{disks}}, \, N_{\text{blocks}} \times \frac{1}{t_{\text{disk}}}\right)$ | MB/s | More blocks on more disks = higher aggregate read bandwidth. |

> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** In the formula sheet above, the absolute-value style barrier $\min(\,\cdot\,)$ is rendered using the LaTeX command `\min` (with parentheses) to avoid the markdown table-breaking vertical bar.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

HDFS blocks are not a theoretical curiosity — they are the foundation of every modern data lake:

- **Facebook (Meta):** Stores hundreds of petabytes of user-content and log data in HDFS clusters whose default block size was raised to **256 MB** precisely because their files are massive and replication is expensive.
- **Yahoo:** Was the original stress-tester of HDFS with the famous 4,000-node clusters.
- **Hadoop MapReduce / Spark:** Both engines read and process data **one block at a time** and try to schedule the computation **on the DataNode that holds the block** (data locality), which is only possible because blocks are discrete units.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Block-Count Formula

Let a file have size $S_f$ bytes, and let the configured HDFS block size be $S_b$ bytes.

The file is split into a sequence of full-size blocks followed by zero or one partial block.

$$
N_b = \underbrace{\left\lfloor \frac{S_f}{S_b} \right\rfloor}_{\text{full blocks}} \;+\; \underbrace{\mathbf{1}\!\left[\, S_f \bmod S_b \neq 0 \,\right]}_{\text{partial block indicator}}
$$

The indicator function $\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$ equals 1 when its argument is true and 0 otherwise. The two cases can be unified as:

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{S_f}{S_b} \right\rceil
$$

This identity is the standard KTU form and should be used in the answer script.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Total-Storage Formula

Each of the $N_b$ blocks is replicated $R$ times across distinct DataNodes. Hence the physical disk consumption in the cluster is:

$$
S_{\text{total}} \;=\; N_b \times S_b \times R
$$

Substituting $N_b$ from Section 3.1:

$$
S_{\text{total}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{S_f}{S_b} \right\rceil \times S_b \times R
$$

The **logical file size** perceived by the user is $S_f$, so the **waste overhead** is:

$$
\Delta S \;=\; S_{\text{total}} - S_f \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{S_f}{S_b} \right\rceil \times S_b \times R \;-\; S_f
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example 1 — Exact Multiple

> **Given:** File size $S_f = 512$ MB, block size $S_b = 128$ MB, replication $R = 3$.

**Step 1 — Number of blocks.**

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{512}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.000 \rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Each block is full** (because 512 is an exact multiple of 128), so each block occupies $128$ MB on disk.

**Step 3 — Total physical storage.**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 128 \times 3 = 1536 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4 — Storage overhead.**

$$
\Delta S = 1536 - 512 = 1024 \text{ MB} \;(\text{all of it is intentional replication, not waste})
$$

#### Example 2 — Non-Exact Multiple (the classic KTU trap)

> **Given:** File size $S_f = 500$ MB, block size $S_b = 128$ MB, replication $R = 3$.

**Step 1 — Number of blocks.**

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{500}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.906 \rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Sizes of individual blocks.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 1} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 2} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 3} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 4 (last)} &= 500 - (3 \times 128) = 500 - 384 = 116 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total physical storage consumed on the cluster.** Each block — including the partially filled last one — is replicated three times and reserves a full $128$ MB slot on disk.

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 128 \times 3 = 1536 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4 — True wasted bytes (the partial tail that is "empty" but still replicated).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_{\text{waste}} &= (S_b - S_{\text{last}}) \times R \\
&= (128 - 116) \times 3 \\
&= 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ MB of empty space on each replica set}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Useful logical storage vs. physical storage.**

$$
S_{\text{logical}} = 500 \text{ MB}, \quad S_{\text{total}} = 1536 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** A common mistake is to compute $N_b = \lfloor 500/128 \rfloor = 3$ and forget the partial last block. The ceiling function is **mandatory**.

#### Example 3 — Mixed-Size Files (Analytical Matrix)

> **Given:** Block size $S_b = 256$ MB, replication $R = 3$. Compute $N_b$, $S_{\text{total}}$, and $\Delta S_{\text{waste}}$ for a batch of uploaded files.

| File | $S_f$ (MB) | $N_b = \lceil S_f / 256 \rceil$ | Last block (MB) | $S_{\text{total}}$ (MB) | $\Delta S_{\text{waste}}$ (MB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 256 | 1 | 256 | $1 \times 256 \times 3 = 768$ | 0 |
| B | 300 | 2 | 44 | $2 \times 256 \times 3 = 1536$ | $(256-44) \times 3 = 636$ |
| C | 700 | 3 | 188 | $3 \times 256 \times 3 = 2304$ | $(256-188) \times 3 = 204$ |
| D | 1024 | 4 | 256 | $4 \times 256 \times 3 = 3072$ | 0 |
| E | 1 | 1 | 1 | $1 \times 256 \times 3 = 768$ | $(256-1) \times 3 = 765$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** A single **1 MB file** consumes **768 MB** of physical storage under the default 3× replication! This is why HDFS is **catastrophically bad for many tiny files** — each small file still occupies one full block. This is one of the highest-yield KTU questions on this topic.

#### Example 4 — Throughput Calculation

> **Given:** A 4 GB file stored in 128 MB blocks, replicated 3× across 6 DataNodes. Each disk sustains **120 MB/s** sequential read.

**Step 1 — Number of blocks.**

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{4096}{128} \right\rceil = 32 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — With 6 DataNodes and 32 blocks, on average:**

$$
\text{Blocks per DataNode} = \frac{32 \times 3}{6} = 16 \text{ replica blocks per DataNode}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical aggregate read bandwidth if all 6 disks are used in parallel:**

$$
T_{\text{read}} = 6 \times 120 = 720 \text{ MB/s}
$$

This is precisely why a large block size and many DataNodes deliver high throughput: more blocks mean more parallel disks can contribute to a single read.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HDFS Master–Worker Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[CLIENT APPLICATION]
    nn[NAMENODE MASTER]
    fsimg[FSimage and EditLogs Metadata]
    dn1[DataNode 1 Rack A]
    dn2[DataNode 2 Rack A]
    dn3[DataNode 3 Rack B]
    dn4[DataNode 4 Rack B]
    b1[Block A 128 MB]
    b2[Block B 128 MB]
    b3[Block C 128 MB]
    b4[Block D 100 MB Partial]
    hb1[Heartbeat every 3 s]
    hb2[Heartbeat every 3 s]
    hb3[Heartbeat every 3 s]
    hb4[Heartbeat every 3 s]
    br1[Block Report]
    br2[Block Report]
    br3[Block Report]
    br4[Block Report]

    client1 -- Metadata Request File to Block Mapping --> nn
    nn -- File to Blocks List and Replica Locations --> client1
    nn --- fsimg

    client1 -- Write Block A Replica 1 --> dn1
    client1 -- Pipeline Replica 2 --> dn2
    client1 -- Pipeline Replica 3 --> dn3

    dn1 --- b1
    dn1 --- b2
    dn2 --- b1
    dn2 --- b3
    dn3 --- b1
    dn3 --- b4
    dn4 --- b2
    dn4 --- b3
    dn4 --- b4

    dn1 --> hb1 --> nn
    dn2 --> hb2 --> nn
    dn3 --> hb3 --> nn
    dn4 --> hb4 --> nn
    dn1 --> br1 --> nn
    dn2 --> br2 --> nn
    dn3 --> br3 --> nn
    dn4 --> br4 --> nn
```

> **How to read this diagram for the exam:**
> - The **Client** asks the **NameNode** only for *metadata* (which blocks exist, where their replicas live).
> - The **Client** then talks *directly* to the **DataNodes** in a **replication pipeline** to write the data.
> - The **NameNode never touches the data bytes** — this is the single most important property of HDFS and is a guaranteed KTU question.
> - DataNodes continuously push **heartbeats** and **block reports** upward so the NameNode can detect failures.

### 4.2 Block-Replication Pipeline (Time-Sequential View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant N as NameNode
    participant D1 as DataNode 1 Local Rack
    participant D2 as DataNode 2 Off Rack
    participant D3 as DataNode 3 Off Rack
    C->>N: create file and request block allocation
    N-->>C: returns list of 3 DataNodes for replica pipeline
    C->>D1: write block chunk 1
    D1->>D2: forward chunk 1
    D2->>D3: forward chunk 1
    D3-->>D2: ack chunk 1
    D2-->>D1: ack chunk 1
    D1-->>C: ack chunk 1
    C->>D1: write block chunk 2
    D1->>D2: forward chunk 2
    D2->>D3: forward chunk 2
    D3-->>D2: ack chunk 2
    D2-->>D1: ack chunk 2
    D1-->>C: ack chunk 2
    C->>N: close file
```

> **Exam-Ready Phrasing:** "HDFS writes use a **replication pipeline** of length equal to the replication factor. Each DataNode forwards data to the next in the chain and sends an acknowledgement back up the chain, ensuring end-to-end durability with a single round-trip per chunk."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an HDFS block. Why is data stored in blocks rather than as a single contiguous file? State the default block size and replication factor in Hadoop 2.x and later.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **HDFS block** is the smallest unit of data that HDFS reads from or writes to. Every file in HDFS is divided into fixed-size blocks, each of which is stored as an independent unit and replicated across multiple DataNodes. The **default block size in Hadoop 2.x and later is 128 MB**, and the **default replication factor is 3**.

> Data is stored in blocks (rather than as a single file) for three reasons:
> 1. **Fault tolerance** — replication of blocks across DataNodes ensures data survives hardware failure.
> 2. **Parallel I/O** — multiple blocks of the same file can be read/written in parallel from different disks, increasing throughput.
> 3. **Scalability across machines** — a file larger than any single disk can still be stored, since the blocks are distributed across the cluster.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three differences between a block in a local Linux file system and an HDFS block.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Local File-System Block | HDFS Block |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Typical size is **4 KB** | Default size is **128 MB** |
| 2 | Transparent to user applications; the OS hides block boundaries | Exposed as a **first-class abstraction**; the application/client knows about blocks |
| 3 | Fixed disk geometry — block = sector cluster | Logical abstraction decoupled from disk — a "block" may span DataNodes and racks |
| 4 | No in-built replication | **Default replication factor of 3** managed by NameNode |
| 5 | Read/write unit at the OS layer | Read/write unit also used by **MapReduce/Spark** for task scheduling (data locality) |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture**. Clearly label the role of the **NameNode**, **DataNodes**, and **client**. Mention the default block size and replication factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A file of size **750 MB** is uploaded to HDFS with a block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Calculate:
  1. The number of blocks created.
  2. The size of the last block.
  3. The total physical storage consumed in the cluster.
  4. The amount of storage "wasted" due to the partial last block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HDFS Architecture (7 marks):**

HDFS follows a **master–worker architecture** consisting of three logical tiers:

1. **NameNode (Master):** Runs on a high-memory machine. Stores all **metadata** — file name, path, permissions, file-to-block mapping, and block-to-DataNode mapping. Stores its persistent state in `fsimage` and `edit logs`. It is the **single point of metadata contact** for every client request.

2. **DataNodes (Workers):** Commodity machines that store the **actual blocks** on their local disks. They send a **heartbeat every 3 seconds** to the NameNode, and a full **block report** periodically. They serve all read/write traffic directly to clients.

3. **Client:** Splits the file into block-sized chunks (default **128 MB**), asks the NameNode for replica locations, and then writes/reads **directly** to/from the DataNodes through a **replication pipeline**. The NameNode is **never** in the data path.

**Default settings:** Block size = **128 MB**, Replication factor = **3**.

**Rack-aware block placement for replication factor 3:**
- Replica 1 → local rack of the writer.
- Replica 2 → a different rack (off-rack).
- Replica 3 → same different rack as Replica 2, on a distinct DataNode.

```
   +-----------+        +-----------+
   |  CLIENT   | <----> | NAMENODE  |   (metadata only)
   +-----------+        +-----------+
        |                ^     ^     ^
        |  data path     | hb  | hb  | hb
        v                |     |     |
   +----------+       +----+ +----+ +----+
   | DataNode |  <--->| DN1| | DN2| | DN3|   (blocks stored)
   |  ...     |       +----+ +----+ +----+
   +----------+
```

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $S_f = 750$ MB, $S_b = 128$ MB, $R = 3$.

**1. Number of blocks:**

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{750}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.859 \rceil = 6 \text{ blocks}
$$

**2. Size of the last block:**

$$
S_{\text{last}} = 750 - (5 \times 128) = 750 - 640 = 110 \text{ MB}
$$

**3. Total physical storage consumed:**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 128 \times 3 = 2304 \text{ MB} \approx 2.25 \text{ GB}
$$

**4. Storage wasted due to partial last block:**

Each of the three replicas of the last block reserves a full 128 MB slot but holds only 110 MB.

$$
\Delta S_{\text{waste}} = (128 - 110) \times 3 = 18 \times 3 = 54 \text{ MB}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of blocks $N_b$ | **6** |
| Last block size $S_{\text{last}}$ | **110 MB** |
| Total cluster storage $S_{\text{total}}$ | **2304 MB (≈ 2.25 GB)** |
| Wasted storage $\Delta S$ | **54 MB** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS block placement policy** (rack awareness) used when the replication factor is 3. Why does HDFS not place all three replicas on the same rack or on the same DataNode? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hadoop cluster has **8 DataNodes**, each with a **1 TB disk**. The default block size is **256 MB** and the default replication factor is **3**. A user uploads **500 files**, each of size **64 MB**. Calculate:
  1. Total number of blocks created.
  2. Total logical storage required (raw user data).
  3. Total physical storage consumed on the cluster.
  4. Comment on whether the cluster can store this data. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rack-Aware Block Placement (7 marks):**

HDFS uses a **rack-aware replica placement policy** to balance **fault tolerance** and **read performance**. For the default replication factor of 3, the placement is:

| Replica | Location |
|---|---|
| **Replica 1** | On the **same rack** as the writer (preferably the same DataNode; otherwise another DataNode on the same rack). |
| **Replica 2** | On a **different rack** (off-rack) from Replica 1. |
| **Replica 3** | On the **same different rack** as Replica 2, but on a **different DataNode**. |

**Why not all on the same rack?** If the entire rack fails (power, switch, cooling), all replicas would be lost simultaneously. Spreading replicas across racks protects against rack-level failures.

**Why not all on the same DataNode?** A single disk or machine failure would destroy the data. Multiple replicas on independent machines provide the basic fault-tolerance guarantee.

**Trade-off:** HDFS keeps at least one replica **local to the writer's rack** so that reads served by the same rack get high throughput (low inter-switch latency). The off-rack replicas act as insurance against rack-wide disasters.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $S_b = 256$ MB, $R = 3$, 500 files each of size $S_f = 64$ MB, 8 DataNodes × 1 TB each.

**1. Total number of blocks:**

For each 64 MB file:

$$
N_b \text{ per file} = \left\lceil \frac{64}{256} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block per file}
$$

Across 500 files:

$$
N_b^{\text{total}} = 500 \times 1 = 500 \text{ blocks}
$$

**2. Total logical storage (raw user data):**

$$
S_{\text{logical}} = 500 \times 64 = 32\,000 \text{ MB} = 32 \text{ GB}
$$

**3. Total physical storage consumed:**

Each block reserves a full 256 MB on disk (even though the file is only 64 MB), and is replicated 3 times:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 500 \times 256 \times 3 = 384\,000 \text{ MB} \approx 375 \text{ GB}
$$

**4. Capacity check:**

Total cluster raw capacity:

$$
C_{\text{cluster}} = 8 \times 1 \text{ TB} = 8 \text{ TB} = 8192 \text{ GB}
$$

Since $375 \text{ GB} \;<\; 8192 \text{ GB}$, the cluster has enough capacity. However, in practice, HDFS reserves some space (default ~10%) and the actual usable capacity is lower. Still, the upload **fits comfortably** from a pure storage perspective.

**Insight to write in the answer script:** *"Although the raw user data is only 32 GB, the file-count overhead is enormous — the cluster consumes 375 GB, roughly 12× the logical size, because each tiny 64 MB file occupies a full 256 MB block. This is the classic 'small-files problem' of HDFS."*

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Blocks per file | **1** |
| Total blocks | **500** |
| Logical storage | **32 GB** |
| Physical storage consumed | **≈ 375 GB** |
| Cluster can store it? | **Yes (375 GB < 8 TB)** |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on HDFS Blocks questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** — writing $N_b = \lfloor S_f / S_b \rfloor$ instead of $\lceil S_f / S_b \rceil$. This silently drops the partial last block and loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by replication factor** when computing $S_{\text{total}}$. The block count is not the same as the physical storage; always multiply by $R$.
> 3. **Confusing logical and physical storage** in the final answer. The user-visible file size never changes; it is the *cluster's* disk consumption that grows by $R$.
> 4. **Drawing the architecture without arrows** — KTU examiners specifically award 1 mark for a *labelled diagram* in 7-mark sub-parts. Always include the diagram, even a simple one.
> 5. **Ignoring the rack-awareness policy** in (a) parts — naming the default factor (3) without explaining *why* the policy exists loses 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Block** = smallest unit of read/write in HDFS. Default size = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x/3.x), configurable to 256 MB or higher.
- [ ] **Replication factor** = default **3**, configurable. Loss of any 2 of 3 replicas is recoverable.
- [ ] **NameNode** stores *metadata only*; never user data. Single point of failure (mitigated by Secondary NameNode / HA in newer versions).
- [ ] **DataNodes** store *blocks* and send heartbeats (3 s) and block reports to the NameNode.
- [ ] **Client** writes/reads directly to/from DataNodes through a **replication pipeline**; the NameNode is never in the data path.
- [ ] **Rack-aware placement for R = 3:** Local rack, off-rack, same off-rack (different node).
- [ ] **Block count formula (must use ceiling):** $N_b = \lceil S_f / S_b \rceil$.
- [ ] **Physical storage formula:** $S_{\text{total}} = N_b \times S_b \times R$.
- [ ] **Small-files problem:** a 1 MB file still consumes 128 MB of physical storage per replica — HDFS is unsuitable for huge numbers of tiny files.
- [ ] **Block reports** (full) and **heartbeats** (incremental) are how the NameNode maintains a truthful in-memory map of where every block lives.
- [ ] **Default Hadoop 3.x block size in production** is often 256 MB to reduce NameNode memory pressure and improve throughput on very large files.
- [ ] A block that is partially filled still reserves a full $S_b$ slot on every replica — the only "waste" in HDFS.
- [ ] HDFS is **write-once, read-many** — files cannot be modified at arbitrary byte offsets; only appended to.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Name Nodes and Data Nodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Name Nodes and Data Nodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is HDFS?

**Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** is the primary storage layer of the Apache Hadoop framework. It is designed to store **very large files** reliably across multiple commodity machines in a cluster. HDFS follows a **Master–Slave (Leader–Follower) architecture**, where the master is the **NameNode** and the slaves are the **DataNodes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> HDFS is the foundational component of Big Data processing. Every concept of Hadoop — MapReduce, YARN, Spark — depends on how HDFS stores and manages data using **NameNodes** and **DataNodes**.

---

## 1.2 NameNode — The Master / Brain of HDFS

### Formal Definition
The **NameNode** is the master server in the HDFS cluster that manages the **file system namespace** (directory tree) and stores all the **metadata** about the files stored in HDFS. It does **NOT** store the actual data of the files. It only knows *where* the data blocks live and *how* they are organized.

### Metadata Stored by NameNode
The NameNode maintains the following in memory (RAM) and on disk:

- **File names** and directory structure
- **File-to-block mapping** (which file is split into which blocks)
- **Block-to-DataNode mapping** (on which DataNodes each block resides)
- **Replication factor** for each file
- **File permissions**, ownership, and timestamps
- **Transaction logs / Edit logs** for changes

### Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Receptionist

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a huge hospital with thousands of patients (data) spread across many wards (DataNodes). The **receptionist (NameNode)** does not treat patients or know their medical details. She only maintains a **register** that says: *"Patient ABC is admitted in Ward 7, Bed 12, file stored in Cabinet 3."* When a doctor needs a patient's file, the receptionist directs them to the exact location.

Just like the receptionist:
- The NameNode **never touches actual data** (it is metadata-only).
- The NameNode answers the question: *"Where is Block #57 of file `/data/sales.csv`?"*
- If the receptionist (NameNode) goes home (fails), the hospital (HDFS) collapses — hence NameNode is the **Single Point of Failure** in classic HDFS.

### Key Metrics for NameNode
- **Metadata size per file:** approximately **~150 bytes** (typical average in Hadoop).
- **Default RAM requirement:** 1 GB of heap per **1 million blocks** (production rule of thumb).
- **Namespace image file:** `fsimage`
- **Edit log file:** `edits`

---

## 1.3 DataNode — The Worker / Storage Engine of HDFS

### Formal Definition
A **DataNode** is a slave/worker node in the HDFS cluster that stores the **actual data blocks** of files on its local disk. A typical HDFS cluster has **tens to thousands of DataNodes**, and the data is distributed across them according to the replication policy.

### Responsibilities of DataNode
- **Store** data blocks on its local file system (under `${dfs.datanode.data.dir}`).
- **Serve** read/write requests from clients (after being authorized by the NameNode).
- **Periodically send Heartbeats** to the NameNode (default: every **3 seconds**).
- **Send Block Reports** listing all blocks it stores (default: every **6 hours** or **100 minutes** depending on configuration).
- **Replicate** blocks to other DataNodes when instructed by the NameNode.
- **Handle pipeline failures** during write operations.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Ward Boy in the Hospital

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Continuing the hospital analogy, **DataNodes are like ward boys / storage cabinets** that physically hold the patient files (data). They:
> - Keep the files safe on their shelves.
> - Report back to the receptionist (NameNode) every few seconds: *"Sir, I am alive, and I am holding 200 files."* (Heartbeat)
> - Periodically send a full inventory: *"Here is the complete list of files I hold."* (Block Report)
> - When a new patient arrives, they make **copies** of the file and hand them to other ward boys (Replication).

### Key Metrics for DataNode
- **Default block size:** **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+ (64 MB in Hadoop 1.x).
- **Replication factor (default):** **3** copies of every block.
- **Heartbeat interval:** **3 seconds** (configurable via `dfs.heartbeat.interval`).
- **Block report interval:** default **21600000 ms = 6 hours**.
- **Storage volume:** Each DataNode can have multiple disks mounted.

---

## 1.4 Quick Comparison Table

| Feature | **NameNode** | **DataNode** |
|---|---|---|
| **Role** | Master / Coordinator | Slave / Worker |
| **Stores** | Metadata only | Actual data blocks |
| **Count in cluster** | Usually 1 (or 2 with HA) | Many (10s to 1000s) |
| **Critical data** | `fsimage`, `edits` log | Block files on local disk |
| **Communication** | Receives heartbeats from DataNodes | Sends heartbeats to NameNode |
| **Failure impact** | **Catastrophic** (SPOF) | Tolerable — replication handles it |
| **Memory demand** | High (must hold all metadata) | Moderate (block data + caches) |
| **Analogy** | Hospital receptionist | Ward boy / storage cabinet |

---

## 1.5 Why Master–Slave Architecture?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centralized metadata** allows a single NameNode to **quickly answer** any client query about file location, while storage and I/O are **distributed** across DataNodes for **parallelism and scalability**. This separation is what enables HDFS to handle **petabytes** of data efficiently.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The HDFS File Write Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

When a client wants to write a file to HDFS, the following sequence occurs between the **Client**, **NameNode**, and **DataNodes**:

1. **Client → NameNode:** Client calls `create()` on `DistributedFileSystem`. It contacts the NameNode requesting to create a new file.
2. **NameNode checks:** NameNode verifies the file does not exist, the client has write permissions, and the directory tree is valid.
3. **NameNode → Client:** Returns a list of **DataNode addresses** that will host the block replicas (a *pipeline* of DataNodes).
4. **Client → DataNode pipeline:** Client splits the file into blocks (default **128 MB**) and starts writing the first block to the first DataNode.
5. **Pipeline replication:** The first DataNode passes the block to the second, which passes it to the third — forming a **replication pipeline** (replication factor = 3 by default).
6. **Acknowledge:** Once all replicas confirm receipt, an ack packet travels back through the pipeline to the client.
7. **Block report update:** DataNodes send a **blockReceived** notification to the NameNode via a heartbeat update.
8. **Repeat:** Steps 4–7 repeat for the next block until the entire file is written.
9. **Final commit:** Client calls `close()`. NameNode updates the edit log and confirms file creation.

---

## 2.2 The HDFS File Read Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Client → NameNode:** Client calls `open()` on `DistributedFileSystem` for a file path.
2. **NameNode → Client:** Returns the list of block locations — for each block, the **closest DataNode** (based on network topology) is prioritized.
3. **Client → DataNode (closest):** Client connects directly to the chosen DataNode and reads the block via TCP.
4. **Repeat for next block:** If the next block is on a different DataNode, the client connects to that one.
5. **Client assembles blocks** in order to reconstruct the original file.
6. **Client → NameNode (optional):** If checksums fail, client reports corruption to NameNode, which marks the block as *corrupt* and triggers re-replication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that the NameNode is **only consulted at the start and for metadata**. The actual data transfer happens **directly between Client and DataNode** — this is called the **"Data Locality"** principle and is critical for HDFS performance.

---

## 2.3 Heartbeat Protocol — The Lifeline of HDFS

The **Heartbeat Protocol** is the mechanism by which a DataNode informs the NameNode of its liveness and status.

### Heartbeat Mechanics
- A DataNode sends a **heartbeat message** to the NameNode every **3 seconds** (configurable).
- Each heartbeat also carries information about:
  - **Total storage capacity** of the DataNode
  - **Used storage**
  - **Free storage**
  - **Number of data transfers in progress**
- If the NameNode does **not receive a heartbeat within 10 minutes** (default `dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval` × a multiplier), it **declares the DataNode dead**.
- The NameNode then **schedules re-replication** of the dead DataNode's blocks to other live DataNodes.

### Heartbeat vs Block Report
- **Heartbeat:** Lightweight message sent every 3 sec — just to say "I am alive."
- **Block Report:** Heavier message sent every ~6 hours — full inventory of all blocks stored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The heartbeat interval (3 sec) and the timeout (10 min) are **favourite KTU viva and 2-mark questions**. Memorize: **Heartbeat = 3 s, Recheck = 10 min**.

---

## 2.4 Block Management and Replication Strategy

### Block Anatomy
- HDFS splits every file into **fixed-size blocks** (default **128 MB**).
- Each block is stored as a **separate file** on the DataNode's local file system.
- The NameNode stores the mapping: `filename → list of block IDs → list of DataNode locations per block`.

### Rack-Aware Replication Policy
HDFS uses a **rack-aware replica placement strategy** to balance **reliability** and **bandwidth utilization**:

| Replica # | Placement Rule (default RF=3) |
|---|---|
| **Replica 1** | On a local DataNode (same node as the client, if client is a DataNode) — otherwise a random DataNode on the same rack |
| **Replica 2** | On a **different rack**, but a randomly chosen DataNode |
| **Replica 3** | On the **same rack as Replica 2**, but a different DataNode |

This policy ensures that:
- **Rack failure** does not lose all copies.
- **Network traffic** is minimized (most reads are within a rack).

### Re-replication Triggers
The NameNode triggers **re-replication** of a block in these cases:
1. A DataNode becomes **dead** (no heartbeat).
2. A block is **corrupted** (checksum mismatch reported by client).
3. A **replication factor** is increased on a file.
4. A DataNode is **decommissioned** (taken out of service gracefully).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU university exams. Memorize them.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of blocks per file** | $\displaystyle N = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil$ | $F$ = file size, $B$ = block size, $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ = ceiling function |
| 2 | **Total storage consumed (with replication)** | $\displaystyle S = N \times B \times R$ | $N$ = number of blocks, $B$ = block size, $R$ = replication factor |
| 3 | **Memory for metadata (NameNode)** | $\displaystyle M = N \times 150 \text{ bytes (approx.)}$ | ~150 bytes metadata per block in NameNode RAM |
| 4 | **Block size in bytes** | $\displaystyle 1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \text{ KB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$ | $128 \text{ MB} = 128 \times 2^{20} = 134{,}217{,}728 \text{ bytes}$ |
| 5 | **Effective storage per node** | $\displaystyle E = D \times R_{\text{node}}$ | $D$ = disk capacity per DataNode, $R_{\text{node}}$ = number of blocks that can be stored |
| 6 | **Heartbeat Timeout (missed beats)** | $\displaystyle T = 2 \times \frac{\text{Heartbeat Recheck Interval}}{\text{Heartbeat Interval}}$ | Default: $2 \times \frac{10 \text{ min}}{3 \text{ s}} = 200$ missed beats |
| 7 | **Number of DataNodes needed (minimum)** | $\displaystyle D_{\min} = R$ | At least replication factor DataNodes to host one block |
| 8 | **Disk space per DataNode** | $\displaystyle S_{\text{node}} = C \times (1 - \text{reserved} \,\vert\, \text{fraction})$ | $C$ = raw capacity; default 10% reserved for non-DFS use |

> **Notation Note:** $\vert$ here denotes the standard vertical bar separator (e.g., for "reserved fraction" in formula 8). It is *not* a markdown table pipe.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

The NameNode–DataNode architecture is the backbone of:
- **Facebook's HDFS cluster** — stores over **300+ PB** of photos and videos.
- **Yahoo's Hadoop cluster** — 100,000+ cores, 500+ PB.
- **Netflix, Twitter, LinkedIn** — all use this architecture for log analytics, recommendation engines, and data warehousing.
- **Cloud platforms** — AWS EMR, Google Dataproc, and Azure HDInsight all use the NameNode–DataNode model under the hood.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Blocks in a File

### Problem Setup
A file of size $F$ is stored in HDFS with block size $B$. The HDFS file is split into multiple blocks. Determine the formula for the number of blocks $N$ and apply it to a numerical example.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the relationship.**
A block of size $B$ stores $B$ bytes. So for a file of size $F$, the *ideal* number of blocks is the ratio $F / B$. However, the *last block* may be partially filled, hence the **ceiling function** is used.

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Edge case verification.**
- If $F = B$, then $N = 1$ (single full block).
- If $F = B + 1$ byte, then $N = 2$ (one full block + one block of 1 byte).
- If $F < B$, then $N = 1$ (a single partial block).

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**
Let $F = 1000$ MB, $B = 128$ MB.

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 7.8125 \right\rceil = 8 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 4 — Last block size calculation.**

$$
\text{Last block size} = F - (N-1) \times B = 1000 - 7 \times 128 = 1000 - 896 = 104 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**
Total storage used (logical) $= 7 \times 128 + 104 = 896 + 104 = 1000$ MB $= F$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total HDFS Storage Consumption with Replication

### Problem Setup
Given a file of size $F$ MB, block size $B$ MB, and replication factor $R$, derive the **total physical storage** consumed across the cluster.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Logical size of file.**
The logical size is just $F$ (the actual file content size).

**Step 2 — Number of blocks.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Storage per block (with replication).**
Each block is replicated $R$ times across the cluster. So each block consumes $B \times R$ MB physically.

**Step 4 — Total physical storage.**

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = N \times B \times R
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
$F = 1000$ MB, $B = 128$ MB, $R = 3$.

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{128} \right\rceil = 8 \text{ blocks}
$$

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = 8 \times 128 \times 3 = 3072 \text{ MB} \approx 3 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 6 — Replication overhead.**

$$
\text{Overhead} = S_{\text{physical}} - F = 3072 - 1000 = 2072 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
\text{Overhead percentage} = \frac{2072}{3072} \times 100 \approx 67.45\%
$$

This means **~2/3 of the cluster's storage** is consumed by replica copies in default configuration. This is a classic KTU short-answer question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: NameNode Memory Estimation

### Problem Setup
A cluster stores **10,000 files**, each of average size **640 MB**, with block size **128 MB** and replication factor **3**. Estimate the **NameNode RAM requirement**.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Blocks per file.**

$$
N_{\text{per file}} = \left\lceil \frac{640}{128} \right\rceil = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Total blocks in the cluster (logical).**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 10{,}000 \times 5 = 50{,}000 \text{ blocks}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: The replication factor $R = 3$ does **NOT** multiply the block count for NameNode memory — because the NameNode tracks *each block by ID* and lists its $R$ replicas, but it does not store the actual data 3 times. The 150-byte estimate per block is **per block ID**, not per replica.

**Step 3 — NameNode memory estimate.**

$$
M = N_{\text{total}} \times 150 \text{ bytes} = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 7.15 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply production rule of thumb (1 GB heap per 1 million blocks).**

$$
M_{\text{production}} = \frac{50{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 1 \text{ GB} = 0.05 \text{ GB} = 50 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
Although the mathematical estimate is 7.15 MB, production deployments use the **1 GB per 1 million blocks** rule because the NameNode also holds file/directory entries, RPC handlers, thread stacks, and caches. A safe production allocation would be **at least 1 GB** of heap for this cluster size.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Heartbeat Math — Timeout Threshold

### Problem Setup
The NameNode uses the heartbeat mechanism to detect DataNode failures. Given the **Heartbeat Interval** $H$ and the **Heartbeat Recheck Interval** $T_r$, determine the **number of missed heartbeats** before a DataNode is declared dead.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Default values from `hdfs-site.xml`:**
- `dfs.heartbeat.interval` $H = 3$ seconds
- `dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval` $T_r = 10$ minutes $= 600$ seconds

**Step 2 — NameNode recheck logic.**
The NameNode only rechecks the DataNode status every $T_r$ seconds, not on every heartbeat. So the timeout is computed in units of $T_r$:

$$
\text{Timeout} = 2 \times T_r
$$

The factor of 2 ensures that **at least one full recheck cycle** of missed heartbeats is required.

**Step 3 — Number of missed heartbeats before timeout.**

$$
\text{Missed beats} = \frac{2 \times T_r}{H} = \frac{2 \times 600}{3} = 400 \text{ beats}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Hadoop's actual implementation uses `2 * recheck + 10 * heartbeat` (in milliseconds) as the precise formula, but the simplified answer above (200–400 missed beats) is what KTU expects.

**Step 4 — Implication.**
If a DataNode crashes, the cluster can take **up to 10 minutes** to detect the failure and start re-replication. This is why HDFS is suitable for **batch processing**, not low-latency systems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does HDFS Use What?

| **Scenario** | **HDFS Behaviour** | **Component Involved** |
|---|---|---|
| Client wants to **read** a file | NameNode returns block locations; Client reads from nearest DataNode | NameNode (metadata) + DataNode (data) |
| Client wants to **write** a file | NameNode allocates block IDs; DataNode pipeline replicates block | NameNode + multiple DataNodes |
| DataNode **disk fails** | DataNode marks block as corrupt, NameNode schedules re-replication | DataNode → NameNode → other DataNodes |
| DataNode **crashes** (network/power) | Heartbeat missed → NameNode re-replicates its blocks | NameNode (after ~10 min) |
| NameNode **crashes** (Hadoop 1.x) | **Cluster is unavailable** — manual recovery from SecondaryNameNode needed | SecondaryNameNode / Standby NameNode (HA) |
| **Block report** mismatch | NameNode marks missing blocks and triggers re-replication | NameNode |
| **Rack failure** (entire rack down) | Blocks on that rack still have replicas on other racks → no data loss | NameNode (rack-aware policy) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level HDFS Architecture

The diagram below shows the master–slave topology of HDFS with one NameNode and multiple DataNodes, along with the heartbeat and block report flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[Client Application]
    client2[Client Application 2]
    namenodeA[NameNode Master]
    fsimageA[(fsimage Metadata File)]
    editsA[(edits Transaction Log)]
    dnode1[DataNode 1]
    dnode2[DataNode 2]
    dnode3[DataNode 3]
    dnode4[DataNode 4]
    blockA[(Block A 128MB)]
    blockB[(Block B 128MB)]
    blockC[(Block C 128MB)]

    client1 -->|1. Read/Write Request Metadata| namenodeA
    client2 -->|1. Read/Write Request Metadata| namenodeA
    namenodeA -->|2. Block Locations| client1
    namenodeA -->|2. Block Locations| client2
    namenodeA -.->|Persists| fsimageA
    namenodeA -.->|Persists| editsA

    client1 -->|3. Direct Data Transfer| dnode1
    client2 -->|3. Direct Data Transfer| dnode3

    dnode1 -->|Heartbeat every 3 sec| namenodeA
    dnode2 -->|Heartbeat every 3 sec| namenodeA
    dnode3 -->|Heartbeat every 3 sec| namenodeA
    dnode4 -->|Heartbeat every 3 sec| namenodeA

    dnode1 -->|Block Report every 6 hr| namenodeA
    dnode2 -->|Block Report every 6 hr| namenodeA
    dnode3 -->|Block Report every 6 hr| namenodeA
    dnode4 -->|Block Report every 6 hr| namenodeA

    dnode1 -.->|Stores| blockA
    dnode2 -.->|Stores| blockA
    dnode3 -.->|Stores| blockA
    dnode1 -.->|Stores| blockB
    dnode3 -.->|Stores| blockB
    dnode4 -.->|Stores| blockB
    dnode2 -.->|Stores| blockC
    dnode3 -.->|Stores| blockC
    dnode4 -.->|Stores| blockC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent **active communication** (client requests, heartbeats). Dotted arrows represent **storage relationships** (NameNode persists metadata to disk; DataNodes store block replicas).

---

## 4.2 Block Replication Pipeline (Write Path)

The diagram below illustrates how a single block is replicated across 3 DataNodes using a pipeline during a write operation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientW[Client]
    dnode1W[DataNode 1]
    dnode2W[DataNode 2]
    dnode3W[DataNode 3]

    clientW -->|Step 1: Send Block Data| dnode1W
    dnode1W -->|Step 2: Forward to Next in Pipeline| dnode2W
    dnode2W -->|Step 3: Forward to Next in Pipeline| dnode3W
    dnode3W -->|Step 4: Ack Success| dnode2W
    dnode2W -->|Step 5: Ack Success| dnode1W
    dnode1W -->|Step 6: Ack Success to Client| clientW
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Concept:** The block is *not* sent 3 times by the client. The client sends it **once** to DataNode 1, which acts as a relay to DataNode 2, which relays to DataNode 3. This is a **fan-out pipeline** that minimizes network bandwidth.

---

## 4.3 Rack-Aware Replica Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph rack1[RACK 1]
        r1dn1[DataNode 11]
        r1dn2[DataNode 12]
        r1dn3[DataNode 13]
    end
    subgraph rack2[RACK 2]
        r2dn1[DataNode 21]
        r2dn2[DataNode 22]
        r2dn3[DataNode 23]
    end
    subgraph rack3[RACK 3]
        r3dn1[DataNode 31]
        r3dn2[DataNode 32]
        r3dn3[DataNode 33]
    end

    blockX[(Block X with RF=3)]
    blockX -.->|Replica 1 same rack| r1dn1
    blockX -.->|Replica 2 different rack| r2dn1
    blockX -.->|Replica 3 same rack as Replica 2| r2dn2
```

**Replication Policy Summary (default RF=3):**
- **Replica 1:** Local DataNode (or same rack).
- **Replica 2:** Different rack (for fault tolerance).
- **Replica 3:** Same rack as Replica 2 (to reduce cross-rack bandwidth).

---

## 4.4 Failure Recovery Flow — DataNode Crash

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[DataNode DN1 Operating Normally]
    hb1[Heartbeat Sent Every 3 sec]
    crashA[DN1 Crashes or Network Partition]
    missA[NameNode Stops Receiving Heartbeats]
    waitA[Wait for Recheck Interval approx 10 min]
    detectA[NameNode Declares DN1 Dead]
    reReplA[Identify Blocks That Were on DN1]
    schedA[Schedule Re-Replication on Live DataNodes]
    balA[Balance Replicas Across Racks]
    doneA[Cluster Restored to RF=3 State]

    startA --> hb1 --> crashA --> missA --> waitA --> detectA --> reReplA --> schedA --> balA --> doneA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Recovery Insight:** The 10-minute detection window is the **trade-off HDFS makes** for high throughput. Real-time systems cannot tolerate this; that is why HDFS is used for **batch analytics**, not transactional workloads.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define NameNode and DataNode. Differentiate between them.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | CO1 | Bloom: Remember/Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

**NameNode:** It is the **master daemon** of HDFS that manages the file system namespace and stores **metadata** such as file-to-block mapping, block locations, file permissions, and replication information. It does not store actual file data. It runs on a dedicated high-RAM machine.

**DataNode:** It is the **slave daemon** of HDFS that runs on every worker machine in the cluster. It stores the **actual data blocks** on its local disk and serves read/write requests from clients. It periodically sends heartbeats and block reports to the NameNode.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | NameNode | DataNode |
|---|---|---|
| Role | Master | Slave / Worker |
| Count | Usually 1 (or 2 with HA) | Many |
| Stores | Metadata | Data blocks |
| Failure impact | Single point of failure | Tolerated via replication |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the two and write that *NameNode stores data and DataNode stores metadata*. This is the **most common 1-mark loss** in KTU exams. Memorize: **NameNode = Metadata, DataNode = Data.**

---

### Question 2: What is the Heartbeat Protocol in HDFS? Why is it important?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 | CO2 | Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Heartbeat Protocol** is the mechanism by which a **DataNode periodically informs the NameNode of its liveness** and status. A DataNode sends a heartbeat message to the NameNode every **3 seconds** (default). Each heartbeat also carries information about storage capacity, used space, and number of data transfers in progress.

**Importance:**
1. **Fault Detection:** If the NameNode does not receive a heartbeat from a DataNode within **~10 minutes** (the recheck interval), it declares the DataNode **dead** and triggers **re-replication** of the dead node's blocks.
2. **Resource Awareness:** NameNode uses heartbeat data (free space, load) to make smart decisions about **where to place new replicas**.
3. **Cluster Health:** Provides a continuous health snapshot of the entire cluster.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention the **3-second interval** and the **10-minute timeout**. These specific numerical values are **expected answers** in KTU marking schemes.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module Internal Choice: Select **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: HDFS Architecture and Data Flow

> **Question:** Explain the HDFS architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Describe how a file of size **1024 MB** is stored in HDFS with a default block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Calculate the total number of blocks, total physical storage consumed, and the NameNode memory estimate.
>
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | CO1, CO2 | Bloom: Understand/Apply]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. HDFS Architecture (3 marks):**
HDFS follows a **Master–Slave architecture**.
- **NameNode (Master):** Runs on a dedicated machine. Manages the file system namespace, stores metadata (file-to-block mapping, block locations, replication info) in memory. Does NOT store actual file data. Persists metadata to disk via two files: `fsimage` (snapshot) and `edits` (transaction log).
- **DataNodes (Slaves):** Run on every worker machine. Store actual data blocks on local disk. Serve read/write requests from clients. Send heartbeats (every 3 sec) and block reports (every 6 hours) to the NameNode.
- **Client:** The user application that interacts with HDFS via `DistributedFileSystem` API.

**2. File Write Flow (2 marks):**
When a client writes a file:
- It first requests the NameNode to create a file.
- NameNode returns a list of DataNode addresses (a pipeline).
- Client splits the file into 128 MB blocks and writes the first block to the first DataNode in the pipeline.
- The DataNodes replicate the block along the pipeline.
- Acknowledgments flow back to the client.
- This repeats for all blocks.

**3. Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Number of blocks:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1024}{128} \right\rceil = 8 \text{ blocks}
$$

Total physical storage (with replication factor $R = 3$):

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = N \times B \times R = 8 \times 128 \times 3 = 3072 \text{ MB} = 3 \text{ GB}
$$

NameNode memory (using ~150 bytes per block rule):

$$
M = 8 \times 150 = 1200 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.17 \text{ KB}
$$

*(In practice, NameNode heap would be allocated at least 1 GB regardless of this small size, due to overheads.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Heartbeat and Block Report Mechanism

> **Question:** Describe the **Heartbeat Protocol** and **Block Report** mechanism in HDFS. Explain how the NameNode detects a DataNode failure and what actions it takes. Mention the default values of heartbeat interval, recheck interval, and timeout.
>
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | CO2 | Bloom: Understand/Apply]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Heartbeat Protocol (3 marks):**
The **Heartbeat Protocol** is a lightweight message that each DataNode sends to the NameNode at regular intervals to signal its liveness.
- **Default Interval:** Every **3 seconds** (configurable via `dfs.heartbeat.interval`).
- **Recheck Interval:** **10 minutes** (configurable via `dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval`).
- **Contents of heartbeat:** DataNode ID, total capacity, used space, free space, number of active transfers.

**2. Block Report (2 marks):**
A **Block Report** is a heavier message sent by each DataNode to the NameNode, listing **all block IDs** stored on that DataNode.
- **Default Interval:** Every **6 hours** (or `dfs.blockreport.intervalMsec` = 21600000 ms).
- **Purpose:** Allows the NameNode to **verify the cluster's actual data layout** against its metadata and detect **missing or corrupt blocks**.

**3. Failure Detection and Recovery (2 marks):**
- If the NameNode does not receive a heartbeat from a DataNode for **more than 10 minutes** (2 × recheck interval), it declares the DataNode **dead**.
- The NameNode identifies all **blocks that were stored on the dead DataNode**.
- It **schedules re-replication** of these blocks on other live DataNodes to restore the replication factor of 3.
- The process is also called **over-replication / under-replication balancing**.
- The cluster continues to operate normally during this process, as the data is still accessible from the remaining replicas.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the **numerical defaults** (3 sec, 10 min, 6 hr). Examiners specifically test whether you remember these values. Also, do NOT say *"NameNode sends heartbeat to DataNode"* — the direction is **DataNode → NameNode**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Rack-Aware Replication and Data Locality

> **Question:** What is the **Rack-Aware Replication Policy** in HDFS? Explain the default replica placement strategy for a replication factor of 3. How does this strategy improve **fault tolerance** and **bandwidth efficiency**?
>
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | CO2, CO3 | Bloom: Understand/Analyze]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Rack Awareness (2 marks):**
HDFS uses a **rack-aware replica placement strategy** to store block replicas intelligently across **physical racks** of machines. A **rack** is a group of machines that share a common network switch. Hadoop uses the `topology.script.file.name` configuration to map DataNodes to racks.

**2. Default Replica Placement for RF=3 (3 marks):**

| Replica # | Placement Rule |
|---|---|
| **Replica 1** | Placed on the **local DataNode** (or a randomly chosen DataNode on the same rack as the client). |
| **Replica 2** | Placed on a **different rack** (for fault tolerance against rack failure). |
| **Replica 3** | Placed on the **same rack as Replica 2**, but on a **different DataNode** (to reduce cross-rack traffic). |

**3. Benefits (2 marks):**

**Fault Tolerance:**
- If an **entire rack fails** (e.g., switch failure, power outage), at least **1 replica** (Replica 1) is still available from another rack.
- Probability of data loss is drastically reduced.

**Bandwidth Efficiency:**
- Most reads happen from **Replicas 1 and 3** (both on local or nearby rack) — minimizes cross-rack network traffic.
- Write pipeline goes through only **2 racks** (local + remote), saving bandwidth.

**4. Diagram (Reference):**
- Refer to **Section 4.3** of these notes for a visual representation of rack-aware placement.

---
