#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Secondary NameNode and High Availability

> **Question:** What is a **Secondary NameNode**? Is it a backup of the NameNode? Explain its role in **checkpointing**. Briefly mention how **HDFS High Availability (HA)** addresses the NameNode's single point of failure.
>
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | CO1, CO3 | Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Secondary NameNode — Definition (2 marks):**
The **Secondary NameNode (SNN)** is a helper daemon in HDFS whose primary role is to perform **periodic checkpoints** of the NameNode's metadata. It is **NOT** a backup or standby NameNode — if the NameNode fails, the SNN cannot immediately take over.

**2. Checkpointing Process (3 marks):**
- The NameNode keeps two files on disk: `fsimage` (latest snapshot) and `edits` (incremental transaction log).
- Over time, the `edits` log grows large, which slows NameNode startup.
- The SNN periodically:
  1. Downloads the current `fsimage` and `edits` from NameNode.
  2. Merges them into a **new `fsimage`** (the checkpoint).
  3. Uploads the new `fsimage` back to the NameNode.
  4. Resets the `edits` log.
- Default checkpoint interval: every **1 hour** or after **1 million transactions**, whichever is first.

**3. High Availability (HA) — NameNode SPOF Solution (2 marks):**
In **Hadoop 2.x+**, the NameNode's single point of failure is resolved using:
- **Active NameNode:** Serves all client requests.
- **Standby NameNode:** Hot standby that continuously receives the edit log stream from the Active NameNode.
- **ZooKeeper / QJM (Quorum Journal Manager):** Coordinates failover between Active and Standby.
- **Shared Storage (NFS or JournalNodes):** Stores the edit log shared between both NameNodes.
- If the Active NameNode fails, the Standby NameNode is **automatically promoted** to Active within seconds, ensuring **no data loss and minimal downtime**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** The most common mistake is to call the Secondary NameNode a *"backup NameNode"*. KTU examiners will **deduct 1 mark** for this incorrect statement. Always clarify: SNN is a **checkpoint helper**, not a backup.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision List for KTU University Exam:**

- **HDFS = Hadoop Distributed File System**, used for storing very large files.
- **NameNode = Master** → stores **metadata only** (file names, block mapping, block locations, replication info).
- **DataNode = Slave** → stores **actual data blocks** on local disk.
- **Default Block Size** = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+), Replication Factor = **3**.
- **Heartbeat Interval** = **3 seconds** (DataNode → NameNode).
- **Heartbeat Recheck Interval** = **10 minutes**.
- **Block Report Interval** = **6 hours** (default).
- **fsimage** = metadata snapshot; **edits** = transaction log of metadata changes.
- **Data Locality:** Client reads data **directly from DataNode**, not through NameNode.
- **Rack-Aware Policy (RF=3):** Replica 1 on local rack, Replica 2 on different rack, Replica 3 on same rack as Replica 2.
- **NameNode is the Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** in Hadoop 1.x; resolved in Hadoop 2.x+ via **Active/Standby HA** using **ZooKeeper**.
- **Secondary NameNode ≠ Backup NameNode**; it is a **checkpoint helper** that merges `fsimage` + `edits`.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $N = \lceil F / B \rceil$ (number of blocks)
  - $S_{\text{physical}} = N \times B \times R$ (total storage with replication)
  - $M \approx 150 \text{ bytes} \times N$ (NameNode memory estimate per block)
- **A 1000 MB file with 128 MB blocks and RF=3** occupies $8 \times 128 \times 3 = 3072$ MB ≈ 3 GB of physical storage.
- **Client never reads through NameNode** — NameNode only returns block locations, and the actual data transfer is **DataNode ↔ Client**.
- **NameNode stores metadata in RAM**; if NameNode crashes, **all metadata is lost** unless checkpointed by SNN or replicated to Standby NN.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using HDFS Files - Basic File System Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Using HDFS Files - Basic File System Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of HDFS

**Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** is the primary, Java-based distributed storage layer designed to reliably store very large datasets across multiple commodity machines in a Hadoop cluster. It follows a **write-once, read-many (WORM)** model, exposes a POSIX-like filesystem shell, and is built on the principle of **horizontal scalability** through block-level replication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** HDFS is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant filesystem that provides high-throughput access to application data. It is engineered for batch processing rather than low-latency random access.

In the **2024 NEP-aligned scheme**, HDFS is positioned as the **storage substrate** of the Hadoop ecosystem, sitting beneath MapReduce, YARN, Spark, and Hive. Every KTU question in this module assumes the student understands that HDFS is *not* a general-purpose POSIX filesystem — it is an append-friendly, block-streamed store.

### 1.2 Default Configuration Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Default Value | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default Block Size** | **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x onwards) | Was 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x |
| **Default Replication Factor** | **3** | Configurable per file via `setRep` |
| **Heartbeat Interval** | **3 seconds** | DataNode → NameNode |
| **Block Report Interval** | **6 hours** (21600000 ms) | DataNode → NameNode |
| **Safe Mode Threshold** | **0.999** | Minimum % of blocks reported before exiting safe mode |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Library Network"

Imagine a **national library chain** with one central **Head Librarian** (the **NameNode**) and **hundreds of branch warehouses** (the **DataNodes**):

- The Head Librarian does NOT store any books. He only holds the **catalogue (metadata)** — knowing which book is in which branch.
- Each book is **split into chapters (blocks of 128 MB)** and **photocopied 3 times (replication factor = 3)** across different branches so that even if one branch burns down, the book is still safe.
- When a reader (client) asks for a specific book, the librarian tells them which branch has it, and the book is **streamed chapter-by-chapter (block-by-block read)** rather than handed over in one piece.
- The librarian sits in a **secure room (Safe Mode)** at startup until he has confirmed that at least **99.9%** of all chapter-shelves have reported in.

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy directly maps to HDFS: NameNode = metadata server, DataNode = storage server, Block = file chunk, Replication = backup copies across racks for fault tolerance.

### 1.4 Why HDFS Exists — The Engineering Motivation

Traditional filesystems (NTFS, ext4) fail at the **petabyte scale** because they assume:
1. A single machine's storage capacity.
2. Sequential I/O dominated by small random reads.
3. Single-point-of-failure protection through RAID (which is hardware-bound).

HDFS overcomes these by:
- **Splitting files into 128 MB blocks** distributed across thousands of nodes.
- **Replicating each block** (default 3 times) across different racks for **rack-awareness fault tolerance**.
- **Streaming entire blocks** to compute engines (locality of data → no network bottleneck).
- **Co-locating computation with data** — the classic Hadoop principle: *move computation to data, not data to computation*.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exams, always mention **"rack-awareness"** when explaining replication — it is the most-missed keyword in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HDFS Architecture — The Master–Worker Model

HDFS follows a strict **NameNode–DataNode (Master–Slave)** architecture:

**A. NameNode (Master / Metadata Server)**
- Stores the entire filesystem namespace in RAM: filenames, directory tree, block-to-file mapping, block locations.
- Persists metadata to two files on local disk:
  - **fsimage** — snapshot of the namespace at a checkpoint.
  - **edits log** — incremental changes since the last checkpoint.
- Receives **heartbeats** (every 3 seconds) from all DataNodes.
- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** in Hadoop 1.x; mitigated in Hadoop 2.x+ by **Secondary NameNode** (checkpointing) and **High Availability (HA)** via Standby NameNode + ZooKeeper.

**B. DataNode (Worker / Storage Server)**
- Stores actual **HDFS blocks** on local disk (under `dfs.datanode.data.dir`).
- Sends **heartbeat** (liveness) and **block report** (inventory) to NameNode.
- Serves read/write requests directly to clients (clients contact NameNode only for metadata, then talk to DataNodes for actual data transfer).
- A single file of size, say, 400 MB, will be split into:
  - Block 1: 128 MB
  - Block 2: 128 MB
  - Block 3: 128 MB
  - Block 4: 16 MB  ← (partial block, no padding)

**C. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)**
- Does *not* act as a hot standby (that is the Standby NameNode in HA mode).
- Periodically merges `fsimage + edits log → new fsimage` to prevent the edit log from growing unbounded.
- In KTU answers, label it as a **"checkpointing assistant"**, NOT a backup NameNode.

### 2.2 HDFS File System Operations — The Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every HDFS command begins with `hdfs dfs -` (or the older `hadoop fs -`). For KTU exams, prefer the modern `hdfs dfs` syntax.

#### 2.2.1 Directory & Listing Operations

| Command | Purpose | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `hdfs dfs -ls <path>` | List files/directories | `hdfs dfs -ls /user/data` |
| `hdfs dfs -ls -R <path>` | Recursive list | `hdfs dfs -ls -R /user` |
| `hdfs dfs -mkdir <path>` | Create directory | `hdfs dfs -mkdir /user/input` |
| `hdfs dfs -mkdir -p <path>` | Create parent directories | `hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /a/b/c` |
| `hdfs dfs -du <path>` | Disk usage of file/folder | `hdfs dfs -du /user/data` |
| `hdfs dfs -df -h <path>` | Free space on filesystem | `hdfs dfs -df -h /` |

#### 2.2.2 File Transfer (Local ↔ HDFS)

| Command | Direction | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>` | Local → HDFS | `hdfs dfs -put data.txt /user/input/` |
| `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal` | Local → HDFS (alias) | Same as `-put` |
| `hdfs dfs -moveFromLocal` | Local → HDFS (deletes local) | `hdfs dfs -moveFromLocal data.txt /user/` |
| `hdfs dfs -get <hdfs> <local>` | HDFS → Local | `hdfs dfs -get /user/output result.txt` |
| `hdfs dfs -copyToLocal` | HDFS → Local (alias) | Same as `-get` |

#### 2.2.3 In-HDFS Operations

| Command | Purpose | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `hdfs dfs -cp <src> <dst>` | Copy within HDFS | `hdfs dfs -cp /a/file /b/` |
| `hdfs dfs -mv <src> <dst>` | Move/rename within HDFS | `hdfs dfs -mv /a/x /b/y` |
| `hdfs dfs -rm <path>` | Delete file | `hdfs dfs -rm /user/data.txt` |
| `hdfs dfs -rm -r <path>` | Recursive delete (folder) | `hdfs dfs -rm -r /user/old` |
| `hdfs dfs -rm -skipTrash <path>` | Delete bypassing trash | `hdfs dfs -rm -skipTrash /tmp/x` |
| `hdfs dfs -cat <path>` | Display file contents | `hdfs dfs -cat /user/data.txt` |
| `hdfs dfs -tail <path>` | Display last 1 KB | `hdfs dfs -tail /user/log.txt` |
| `hdfs dfs -chmod <perm> <path>` | Change permissions | `hdfs dfs -chmod 755 /user/script.sh` |
| `hdfs dfs -chown <user:group> <path>` | Change ownership | `hdfs dfs -chown hadoop:hadoop /user/data` |
| `hdfs dfs -setrep -w <n> <path>` | Set replication factor | `hdfs dfs -setrep -w 5 /user/important` |
| `hdfs dfs -count <path>` | Count dirs, files, bytes | `hdfs dfs -count -q /user/` |
| `hdfs dfs -stat <format> <path>` | File statistics | `hdfs dfs -stat "%b %n %o %r" /file` |
| `hdfs dfs -expunge` | Empty trash | `hdfs dfs -expunge` |

#### 2.2.4 File Reading Format Specifiers (for `-stat`)

| Format | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `%b` | File size in bytes |
| `%n` | Filename |
| `%o` | Block size |
| `%r` | Replication factor |
| `%y` | Modification date (yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss) |

### 2.3 The High-Yield Formula Sheet (Block & Storage Math)

> [!NOTE]
> HDFS uses the following **storage accounting** equations. They are direct **2-mark and 7-mark favourites** in KTU board exams.

Let $S$ = original file size (in MB), $B$ = block size (default **128 MB**), $R$ = replication factor (default **3**), and $N$ = number of full blocks.

**Number of full blocks (Mathematical Floor):**
$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{S}{B} \right\rfloor$$

**Size of the last (partial) block:**
$$S_{last} = S \bmod B \quad \text{(only non-zero if } S \text{ is not a multiple of } B\text{)}$$

**Total number of HDFS blocks (logical):**
$$N_{blocks} = \begin{cases} N & \text{if } S \bmod B = 0 \\ N + 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Total physical storage consumed (with replication):**
$$T_{storage} = S \times R \quad \text{(MB, ignoring partial-block padding)}$$

> [!TIP]
> A file of exactly **128 MB** still occupies **$128 \times 3 = 384$ MB** of physical cluster storage due to the replication factor. This is a classic 2-mark "spot the wastage" question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HDFS operations underpin:
- **Data lakes** in enterprise analytics (Cloudera, Hortonworks, AWS EMR).
- **Log ingestion pipelines** where `flume` or `kafka` continuously `-appendToFile` into HDFS.
- **ETL staging areas** in Spark/Hive where external tables point to `hdfs://namenode:8020/warehouse/`.
- **Machine learning training corpora** where terabyte-scale datasets are pre-staged on HDFS before distributed training.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Block Computation (Full Derivation)

**Problem:** A log file of size **500 MB** is uploaded to HDFS. Given the default block size $B = 128$ MB and replication factor $R = 3$, compute:
1. The number of full blocks.
2. The size of the last (partial) block.
3. The total logical block count.
4. The total physical storage consumed.

**Step 1 — Number of full blocks:**
We apply the floor function to the ratio of file size to block size.
$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{S}{B} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{500}{128} \right\rfloor$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
500 \div 128 &= 3.90625 \\
\left\lfloor 3.90625 \right\rfloor &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, **$N = 3$** full blocks.

**Step 2 — Size of the last (partial) block:**
The remainder of the integer division gives the leftover data.
$$S_{last} = S \bmod B = 500 \bmod 128$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
500 - (3 \times 128) &= 500 - 384 = 116 \\
\text{Hence } S_{last} &= 116 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total logical block count:**
Since the remainder is non-zero ($116 \neq 0$), a fourth partial block is created.
$$N_{blocks} = N + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ blocks}$$

The four blocks are sized: **128 MB, 128 MB, 128 MB, 116 MB** (no padding — HDFS does not pad partial blocks).

**Step 4 — Total physical storage consumed:**
The replication factor is applied uniformly to every block.
$$T_{storage} = S \times R = 500 \times 3 = 1500 \text{ MB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **logical view** shows 4 blocks totalling 500 MB, but the **physical view** shows 12 block-copies totalling 1500 MB across the cluster. Examiners love asking for both views.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — HDFS File Write Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

**Scenario:** A client invokes `hdfs dfs -put localfile.txt /user/data/`. The cluster has 1 NameNode and 3 DataNodes (DN1, DN2, DN3), and $R = 3$.

**Step 1 — Client requests metadata:**
The client contacts the **NameNode** via RPC asking: *"Where should I write `/user/data/localfile.txt`?"*

**Step 2 — NameNode allocates block IDs:**
The NameNode checks namespace permissions, picks three DataNodes following the **rack-awareness policy** (e.g., DN1, DN4, DN7 — two on different racks from the third), and returns their addresses to the client. It also creates a Block ID, say `blk_1073741825`.

**Step 3 — Client opens write pipeline:**
The client establishes a TCP pipeline in the order returned: `Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3`.

**Step 4 — Client streams data in packets:**
The 128 MB block is broken into **64 KB packets** (default `dfs.client-write-packet-size`). Each packet is pushed through the pipeline:
- DN1 receives the packet, stores it, and forwards to DN2.
- DN2 receives, stores, and forwards to DN3.
- DN3 receives and stores (terminal node).

**Step 5 — Acknowledgement (ACK) pipeline:**
Once DN3 writes the packet to its local disk, it sends an ACK **backwards** through the pipeline. The client only proceeds to the next packet after the ACK chain is received. This guarantees no data loss even if one DataNode fails mid-write.

**Step 6 — Block completion:**
After all packets of the block are written and ACKed, the client informs the NameNode that block `blk_1073741825` is complete. The NameNode updates its in-memory metadata.

**Step 7 — File close:**
If the file has more blocks (e.g., the 500 MB example), the pipeline process repeats for each new block. When all blocks are written, the client sends a `complete` instruction to the NameNode, which marks the file as `closed` in its namespace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key exam insight:** If DN2 fails during step 4, the NameNode instructs the client to remove DN2 from the pipeline and substitute a healthy DataNode. The unwritten portion of the block is re-routed — already-written packets are NOT re-sent (they exist on DN1 and DN3).

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Changing Replication Factor

**Command:**
```bash
hdfs dfs -setrep -w 5 /user/data/important.txt
```

**Analytical breakdown:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Previous replication } R_{old} &= 3 \text{ (default)} \\
\text{New replication } R_{new} &= 5 \\
\text{Delta replicas required} &= R_{new} - R_{old} = 2 \\
\text{Total replicas after change} &= (\text{logical file size in MB}) \times 5
\end{aligned}
$$

The `-w` flag makes the command **block** until the new replication count is fully achieved across the cluster. Without `-w`, the command is asynchronous and returns immediately.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Command → Action → State Change

| Operation | Internal Action | Metadata Change | Storage Change |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `-put` / `-copyFromLocal` | Splits into blocks, replicates, ACKs | New inode + block map | New blocks on $R$ DataNodes |
| `-get` / `-copyToLocal` | Streams blocks in sequence | None (read-only) | None |
| `-cp` | Internal NameNode metadata copy + block ref | New inode pointing to same blocks | No new blocks (shared) |
| `-mv` | Pure metadata rename | Inode path updated | None |
| `-rm` | Marks blocks for deletion (trash) | Inode removed | Blocks freed asynchronously |
| `-setrep` | Replicates/over-replicates blocks | Block map updated | Grows/shrinks physically |
| `-chmod` / `-chown` | Updates permission bits | Inode attribute change | None |
| `-cat` / `-tail` | Sequential block read | None | None |

> [!TIP]
> The `-cp` and `-mv` distinction is a **favourite 3-mark question**. `-cp` is a **pointer-based copy** (cheap, no I/O); `-mv` is a **pure metadata rename** (cheapest of all).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HDFS High-Level Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[HDFS Client Application]
    
    subgraph MasterPlane["MASTER PLANE - NameNode"]
        NNMeta[In-Memory Namespace and Block Map]
        FSImage[fsimage Checkpoint File]
        EditsLog[edits Log File]
        Checkpoint[Secondary NameNode - Checkpointing]
    end
    
    subgraph WorkerPlane1["WORKER PLANE - Rack 1"]
        DN1[DataNode 1 - Local Disk Storage]
    end
    
    subgraph WorkerPlane2["WORKER PLANE - Rack 2"]
        DN2[DataNode 2 - Local Disk Storage]
    end
    
    subgraph WorkerPlane3["WORKER PLANE - Rack 3"]
        DN3[DataNode 3 - Local Disk Storage]
    end
    
    Client -- RPC Metadata Request --> NNMeta
    NNMeta -- Returns DataNode Locations --> Client
    Client -- Write Pipeline Block 1 --> DN1
    Client -- Write Pipeline Block 1 Replica --> DN2
    Client -- Write Pipeline Block 1 Replica --> DN3
    DN1 -- Heartbeat every 3s --> NNMeta
    DN2 -- Heartbeat every 3s --> NNMeta
    DN3 -- Heartbeat every 3s --> NNMeta
    DN1 -- Block Report every 6h --> NNMeta
    DN2 -- Block Report every 6h --> NNMeta
    DN3 -- Block Report every 6h --> NNMeta
    NNMeta -. Periodic Merge .-> Checkpoint
    Checkpoint -. New fsimage .-> FSImage
    EditsLog -. Incremental Changes .-> NNMeta
```

### 4.2 HDFS File Read Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2
    participant DN3 as DataNode 3
    
    C->>NN: open(path-to-file)
    NN-->>C: Block locations for blk_A, blk_B, blk_C
    C->>DN2: read(blk_A)
    DN2-->>C: Stream block data 128 MB
    C->>DN1: read(blk_B)
    DN1-->>C: Stream block data 128 MB
    C->>DN3: read(blk_C)
    DN3-->>C: Stream block data 116 MB
    C->>NN: complete file read
    NN-->>C: OK
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the NameNode is **bypassed** during the actual data transfer — clients talk **directly** to the best DataNode (typically the closest one for network locality). This is the key to HDFS throughput.

### 4.3 HDFS Write Pipeline (Data Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Application] -- Packet 1 - 64KB --> B[DataNode 1]
    B -- Forward Packet 1 --> C[DataNode 2]
    C -- Forward Packet 1 --> D[DataNode 3]
    D -- ACK Packet 1 --> C
    C -- ACK Packet 1 --> B
    B -- ACK Packet 1 --> A
    
    A -- Packet 2 - 64KB --> B
    B -- ACK Packet 2 --> A
    
    A[Client Application] -- Final Block Complete --> N[NameNode]
    N -- Update Block Map --> N
```

### 4.4 Block Distribution for the 500 MB Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F[File: localfile.txt - 500 MB]
    F --> B1[Block 1 - blk_1073741825 - 128 MB]
    F --> B2[Block 2 - blk_1073741826 - 128 MB]
    F --> B3[Block 3 - blk_1073741827 - 128 MB]
    F --> B4[Block 4 - blk_1073741828 - 116 MB]
    
    B1 --> B1R1[Replica on DN1 - Rack A]
    B1 --> B1R2[Replica on DN4 - Rack B]
    B1 --> B1R3[Replica on DN7 - Rack C]
    
    B2 --> B2R1[Replica on DN2 - Rack A]
    B2 --> B2R2[Replica on DN5 - Rack B]
    B2 --> B2R3[Replica on DN8 - Rack C]
    
    B3 --> B3R1[Replica on DN3 - Rack A]
    B3 --> B3R2[Replica on DN6 - Rack B]
    B3 --> B3R3[Replica on DN9 - Rack C]
    
    B4 --> B4R1[Replica on DN1 - Rack A]
    B4 --> B4R2[Replica on DN4 - Rack B]
    B4 --> B4R3[Replica on DN7 - Rack C]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six HDFS file system commands with their syntax and purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **`hdfs dfs -ls <path>`** — Lists files and directories at the given HDFS path.
2. **`hdfs dfs -mkdir -p <path>`** — Creates a directory and any necessary parent directories in HDFS.
3. **`hdfs dfs -put <local-src> <hdfs-dst>`** — Copies a file from the local filesystem to HDFS; internally splits into 128 MB blocks and replicates.
4. **`hdfs dfs -get <hdfs-src> <local-dst>`** — Copies a file from HDFS to the local filesystem.
5. **`hdfs dfs -rm -r <path>`** — Recursively deletes a file or directory from HDFS.
6. **`hdfs dfs -chmod 755 <path>`** — Changes the file permissions in HDFS to octal mode 755.
7. **(Bonus)** **`hdfs dfs -setrep -w 3 <path>`** — Sets the replication factor to 3 for the given path.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, list exactly 6 commands with one-line purposes. Bonus commands are appreciated but not required.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the default block size and replication factor in HDFS? Why is HDFS not suitable for low-latency random access?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Default block size:** 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x and later; 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x).
- **Default replication factor:** 3 (each block is stored on 3 different DataNodes, distributed across racks via rack-awareness).
- **Why HDFS is not suitable for low-latency random access:**
  1. HDFS optimizes for **high-throughput batch reads**, not millisecond-level responses.
  2. Files are **write-once, read-many**; updates require full block rewrites.
  3. Random seeks across blocks involve multiple network hops to different DataNodes.
  4. The NameNode becomes a metadata bottleneck if thousands of small random reads are issued per second.
  5. HDFS is designed for **streaming entire blocks**, not for OLTP-style point queries.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "HDFS is slow" without justification. You must mention **write-once model**, **block-level streaming**, and **NameNode as a single coordinator**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the HDFS architecture with a neat diagram. Describe the role of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A file of size **1024 MB** is stored in HDFS with the default block size and replication factor. Calculate: **(i)** number of blocks, **(ii)** total physical storage consumed, **(iii)** size of the last block. What happens if you change the replication factor to 5 using `hdfs dfs -setrep -w 5`? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) HDFS Architecture (7 marks):**

HDFS follows a **master–slave (NameNode–DataNode)** architecture designed for storing very large files across a cluster of commodity machines.

- **NameNode (Master):** Maintains the entire filesystem namespace in RAM — directory tree, file-to-block mapping, and block-to-DataNode mapping. It does not store actual file data. It receives **heartbeats (every 3 s)** and **block reports (every 6 h)** from all DataNodes. Metadata is persisted to two local files: `fsimage` (snapshot) and `edits log` (incremental changes).
- **DataNode (Slave):** Stores actual HDFS blocks on its local disks. On startup, it registers with the NameNode and periodically sends heartbeats and block reports. DataNodes serve read/write requests directly to clients.
- **Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):** Periodically merges `fsimage` + `edits log` → new `fsimage` to prevent the edit log from growing unbounded. It is **not** a hot standby; for true HA, Hadoop 2.x+ uses a Standby NameNode with ZooKeeper.

```
   ┌────────────┐
   │  Client    │
   └─────┬──────┘
         │ (metadata RPC)
         ▼
   ┌────────────┐      Heartbeat (3s)
   │  NameNode  │ ◄──────────────────┐
   └────────────┘                    │
         ▲                           │
         │ (block locations)         │
         ▼                           │
   ┌────────────┐  ◄── Read/Write ────┤
   │ DataNode 1 │                    │
   └────────────┘                    │
   ┌────────────┐                    │
   │ DataNode 2 │ ─── Heartbeat ─────┘
   └────────────┘
   ┌────────────┐
   │ DataNode 3 │
   └────────────┘
```

**Communication flow:** Client → NameNode (metadata) → Client → DataNode (actual data). The NameNode is never in the data path.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $S = 1024$ MB, $B = 128$ MB (default), $R = 3$ (default).

**(i) Number of blocks:**
$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{128} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 8 \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ full blocks}$$
Since $1024 \bmod 128 = 0$, there is **no partial block**.
**∴ Number of blocks = 8**, each of exactly 128 MB.

**(ii) Total physical storage consumed:**
$$T_{storage} = S \times R = 1024 \times 3 = 3072 \text{ MB} \approx 3 \text{ GB}$$

**(iii) Size of last block:**
Since the file is an exact multiple of block size, the last block = **128 MB** (no partial block).

**Effect of `hdfs dfs -setrep -w 5`:**
- The replication factor for the file is increased from 3 to 5.
- The NameNode schedules **2 additional replicas** for each of the 8 blocks on healthy DataNodes following rack-awareness.
- The `-w` flag makes the command **wait** until the new replica count is fully achieved.
- New physical storage = $1024 \times 5 = 5120$ MB = **5 GB**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Question A):** Many students forget to mention that the **Secondary NameNode is NOT a backup NameNode**. Writing "it acts as a backup" will cost 2 marks. Always say it is a **checkpointing assistant**.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain the HDFS file **read** operation step-by-step. Mention the role of the NameNode in the read path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the following HDFS commands with examples: **(i)** `-put` vs `-copyFromLocal`, **(ii)** `-cp` vs `-mv`, **(iii)** `-rm` vs `-rm -skipTrash`, **(iv)** `-setrep` with and without `-w`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) HDFS File Read Operation (7 marks):**

The HDFS read path is optimized for **sequential block streaming**:

1. **Open call:** The client invokes `FileSystem.open(path)`, which triggers an RPC to the **NameNode** asking for the file's block locations.
2. **Metadata reply:** The NameNode returns a list of DataNodes hosting each block (typically sorted by network proximity).
3. **FSDataInputStream:** The client receives an `FSDataInputStream` object, which wraps a `DFSInputStream` managing all block reads.
4. **Block read (sequential):** For each block, the client contacts the **nearest** DataNode (not necessarily the first in the list) and requests the block via `read()`.
5. **Checksum verification:** As packets arrive, the client verifies checksums to detect corruption. If corruption is found, it reports to the NameNode and fetches from a different replica.
6. **Block completion:** Once a block is fully read, the `DFSInputStream` closes the connection to that DataNode and opens a new connection to the DataNode hosting the next block.
7. **Close call:** The client invokes `close()`, which releases all DataNode connections and (optionally) informs the NameNode.

```
Client              NameNode              DataNode
  | --- open(path) --> |                     |
  | <-- block locs --- |                     |
  | ----------- read(blk_1) ------------> |
  | <--------- block 1 data -------------- |
  | ----------- read(blk_2) ------------> |
  | <--------- block 2 data -------------- |
  | --- close() -----> |                     |
```

**Role of NameNode in the read path:** The NameNode is consulted **only once** at the start to get the block-to-DataNode map. It is **not** involved in the actual data transfer, which is a critical design choice for throughput.

**(b) Command Differentiations (7 marks = 1.75 × 4):**

**(i) `-put` vs `-copyFromLocal`:**

| Aspect | `-put` | `-copyFromLocal` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Source | Local OR another HDFS path | **Local only** (restricts) |
| Flexibility | More flexible | Stricter — refuses HDFS-to-HDFS source |
| Example | `hdfs dfs -put a.txt /data/` | `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal a.txt /data/` |

**(ii) `-cp` vs `-mv`:**

| Aspect | `-cp` | `-mv` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Action | Creates a new inode pointing to **same blocks** | Pure metadata **rename** of inode |
| I/O cost | Cheap (no block copy) | Cheapest (no block touch) |
| Source retained? | **Yes** | **No** (source deleted) |
| Example | `hdfs dfs -cp /a/x /b/y` | `hdfs dfs -mv /a/x /b/y` |

**(iii) `-rm` vs `-rm -skipTrash`:**

| Aspect | `-rm` | `-rm -skipTrash` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trash | File moved to `.Trash` (recoverable) | **Bypasses trash**, permanent delete |
| Recoverable? | Yes (until `expunge`) | No |
| Example | `hdfs dfs -rm /user/old.txt` | `hdfs dfs -rm -skipTrash /tmp/x` |

**(iv) `-setrep` with vs without `-w`:**

| Aspect | Without `-w` | With `-w` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Behaviour | **Asynchronous** — returns immediately | **Synchronous** — blocks until replication completes |
| Use case | Scripts, batch ops | Critical files where you need confirmation |
| Example | `hdfs dfs -setrep 5 /file` | `hdfs dfs -setrep -w 5 /file` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Question B):** In part (b), students often write "they are the same" for `-put` vs `-copyFromLocal`. They are **NOT** the same — `-copyFromLocal` restricts the source to a local path. Lose 1 mark if you miss this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 12 high-yield points to memorize for the KTU board exam:**

1. **HDFS** = **Hadoop Distributed File System** — Java-based, block-streamed, master–slave architecture.
2. **Default block size** = **128 MB**; **default replication factor** = **3**.
3. **Architecture triangle:** **NameNode** (metadata) + **DataNode** (storage) + **Secondary NameNode** (checkpointing).
4. **NameNode is NOT in the data path** — it serves metadata only; clients talk directly to DataNodes.
5. **Heartbeat interval = 3 seconds**; **Block report interval = 6 hours** — these are favourite 1-mark questions.
6. **Number of blocks formula:** $N = \lfloor S / B \rfloor$ (add 1 if remainder is non-zero).
7. **Physical storage formula:** $T = S \times R$ (e.g., 500 MB × 3 = 1500 MB).
8. **Write pipeline:** `Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3`; ACK flows in reverse; failure triggers pipeline rebuild.
9. **Read path:** `Client → NameNode (metadata) → Client → nearest DataNode (data)`.
10. **Secondary NameNode is NOT a backup** — it is a checkpointing assistant; for HA, use Standby NameNode + ZooKeeper.
11. **Trash behaviour:** `-rm` moves to `.Trash`; `-rm -skipTrash` is permanent; use `-expunge` to empty trash.
12. **`-cp` is a pointer copy** (cheap, no I/O); **`-mv` is a metadata rename** (cheapest); **`-put` triggers full block replication**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering commands:** **"LMC PRG"** → **L**ist, **M**kdir, **C**opy → **P**ut, **R**ead, **G**et. Practise these 6 commands until they are muscle memory — they appear in **every KTU question paper** for Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hadoop Specific File Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hadoop Specific File Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hadoop Specific File Types** are specialized on-disk storage formats engineered to operate natively on the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). They are designed to address the four pillars of distributed storage that the default line-based text format cannot satisfy:

1. **Schema Awareness** — Self-describing data that does not rely on external schema files.
2. **Splittable Compression** — The ability to compress a file while still allowing HDFS to split it across blocks for parallel MapReduce processing.
3. **Compact Binary Encoding** — A dense serialization that minimizes HDFS block footprint.
4. **Optimized I/O Patterns** — Layouts (row-wise vs column-wise) that match the dominant access pattern (OLTP vs OLAP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The official KTU 2024 scheme groups these formats under the **"Hadoop I/O"** module. A student must be able to **draw, compare, and justify** the choice of a file format for a given analytical workload.

The principal file types are:

| Family | Examples | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| Key-Value Binary | SequenceFile, MapFile, HFile | MapReduce intermediate output, HBase storage |
| Schema-Based | Avro | Writable data exchange, streaming |
| Columnar | RCFile, ORC, Parquet | OLAP / Data Warehouse queries |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a huge **library warehouse** with millions of books.

- A **plain text file** is like dumping all the books on the floor in random order. Anyone wanting a single fact ("average rainfall in Kerala") must sift through the entire pile.
- A **SequenceFile** is like binning books by topic, but every bin is still kept in story order — great for reading a whole book.
- A **Parquet / ORC file** is like tearing pages out of every book and putting *all rainfall pages in one cabinet*, *all population pages in another*. To compute a city-wise rainfall report, you walk to **one cabinet** instead of the whole library. This is the **columnar advantage**.
- An **Avro file** is like a strict shipping manifest — every box has a label (schema) that survives even if the shape of the goods changes (schema evolution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Default of HDFS:** A flat text file is still readable, but the Hadoop ecosystem replaces it with binary formats because text is **~3× larger**, **not splittable when compressed with gzip**, and **not self-describing**.

## 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Magic Bytes for SequenceFile:** `SEQ` followed by the byte `6` (current version).
- **Default Parquet Page Size:** **1 MB**.
- **Default Parquet Row Group Size:** **128 MB** (HDFS block-aligned).
- **Default ORC Stripe Size:** **250 MB** (buffer size for HDFS write).
- **Sync Marker Length:** **16 bytes** (`0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF`) — used by all Hadoop container formats for split recovery.
- **Bloom Filter Default False Positive Rate in ORC:** **0.05**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SequenceFile

A **SequenceFile** is a flat file consisting of binary key-value pairs. Internally it is laid out as:

```
[ MAGIC 'SEQ6' ] -> [ Header ] -> [ Record 1 ] -> [ Record 2 ] -> ... -> [ Sync Marker every ~1MB ]
```

The **Header** contains:
- Key class name (e.g., `org.apache.hadoop.io.LongWritable`)
- Value class name
- Compression codec class
- Metadata as `Text` -> `Text` pairs
- Sync marker (16 bytes)

**Three Compression Modes** — this is a **high-yield KTU topic**:

1. **Uncompressed** — no codec. Each record stored raw.
2. **Record Compressed** — each *individual* record's value is compressed. Keys are never compressed (must remain comparable).
3. **Block Compressed** — a **block of records** is compressed together. A small header is written before each block recording the number of records and key/value lengths. Yields the best ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Compression creates a sync marker BEFORE the block, which makes the file splittable**. This is the operational reason Hadoop recommends Block Compression for MapReduce input.

**Why SequenceFile matters:**
- Bridges between MapReduce TextOutput (human readable) and a binary format that is still splittable.
- Used heavily as the format for MapReduce's *intermediate output* shuffle files.

## 2.2 MapFile

A **MapFile** is a **sorted** SequenceFile that has been split into **two physical files** on HDFS:

1. `data` — the actual sorted SequenceFile of key-value records.
2. `index` — a SequenceFile of `<key, LongWritable offset>` pairs sampled at intervals (default every **128** records).

The **index file is loaded entirely into the driver JVM's memory** at job startup. A `get(key)` operation performs an in-memory binary search on the index, then a single seek + read in the data file.

- **Lookup complexity:** $O(\log n)$ index seek + $O(1)$ HDFS read.
- **Use case:** Random lookups against a static sorted dataset (e.g., a master data file used as a side-input in MapReduce).

## 2.3 Avro (Schema-Based Serialization)

Avro stores data with a **JSON schema** embedded in the file (in the file's header) and serializes the actual records as compact binary. It is **language-neutral** (C, C++, Java, Python, Ruby all work).

**File Layout (Container File):**
- Header: `Obj\u0001` (magic) + JSON metadata containing the **writer's schema**.
- **Sync marker** (16 bytes) every $N$ bytes (default 64 KB).
- Blocks of serialized records (encoded with the writer's schema).
- File ends with the same 16-byte sync marker.

**Schema Evolution** is the killer feature. Because the schema is stored inside the file, you can:

| Action on schema | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Add a field with default | **Backward compatible** (old readers skip, new readers see default) |
| Remove a field | **Backward compatible** (old readers see no value) |
| Rename a field | **NOT compatible** (Avro compares by name, not alias) |
| Change numeric type (int → long) | **Forward compatible** for some readers |

> [!NOTE]
> The "writer's schema" + "reader's schema" are resolved at read time using a **Projection and Default-Fill algorithm** — this is why default values are mandatory when adding new fields.

## 2.4 Parquet (Columnar Storage)

Parquet is a **columnar storage format** originated from the Dremel paper at Google. It is now the de-facto standard for analytical workloads on HDFS.

**Logical Hierarchy (top → bottom):**

```
Parquet File
 ├── Row Group 1       (default 128 MB, equals one HDFS block)
 │    ├── Column Chunk — Column A
 │    │     ├── Dictionary Page (optional, encoding dictionary)
 │    │     ├── Data Page 1   (RLE / Dictionary / Plain encoded)
 │    │     └── Data Page 2
 │    ├── Column Chunk — Column B
 │    └── ... (one column chunk per column)
 ├── Row Group 2
 └── File Footer
        ├── File Meta Data (schema, row group list)
        ├── Column Chunk statistics (min, max, null count)
        └── Footer length (last 8 bytes, magic 'PAR1')
```

**Encoding Pipeline (per column):**
1. **Dictionary Encoding** — replace repeated values with integer codes.
2. **Bit Packing / RLE** — pack the codes into minimum bits; run-length-encode long runs.
3. **Page-level statistics** — every page stores min/max.
4. **Optional compression** — Snappy, Gzip, Zstd over the encoded pages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Predicate Pushdown** is what makes Parquet fast: the reader inspects the page min/max statistics in the footer and **skips whole pages** (and even whole row groups) without ever decoding them. This is why Parquet shines on `WHERE` queries over narrow columns.

## 2.5 ORC (Optimized Row Columnar)

ORC was created at Hortonworks specifically for **Hive** to fix the shortcomings of RCFile. Its structure is also a hierarchy:

```
ORC File
 ├── Stripe 1   (default 250 MB)
 │    ├── Index Data
 │    │     ├── Min/Max index (per column)
 │    │     ├── Bloom Filter (per column, optional)
 │    │     └── Row Index (positions of every 10 000 rows)
 │    ├── Row Data (rows stored column-by-column)
 │    └── Stripe Footer
 ├── Stripe 2
 └── File Footer
        ├── File metadata (rows, stripes, columns)
        ├── Column-level statistics (count, min, max, sum)
        └── Postscript (length 1 byte → contains file-level compression)
```

**Two-tier index** (Stripe Index + File Footer) gives ORC strong **predicate pushdown** even at file granularity.

## 2.6 RCFile (Record Columnar File)

The *predecessor* of ORC. It lays out rows in a row group (default **4 MB**), but **stores each row group column-wise**.

- Each row group contains: a sync marker, the key/value metadata, and the column-by-column data.
- Drawback: **No per-column statistics** in the footer → poor predicate pushdown → **superseded by ORC**.

## 2.7 HFile (HBase Storage)

Used internally by **HBase**. It is a sorted key-value file with a **block index** and **bloom filters** in the trailer. Every cell in HBase becomes one (row-key, column, timestamp, value) record. HFiles are designed to support **point lookups** and **range scans** with the lowest possible latency.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Mathematical Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compression Ratio | $CR$ | $CR = \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}}$ | dimensionless | Higher is better |
| Space Savings | $SS$ | $SS = \frac{S_{\text{original}} - S_{\text{compressed}}}{S_{\text{original}}} \times 100\%$ | percent | % saved vs raw |
| Columnar Read Fraction | $R_f$ | $R_f = S \times C_{\text{eff}}$ | dimensionless | $S$ = column selectivity |
| Effective Read Size | $E_r$ | $E_r = R_f \times S_{\text{compressed}}$ | bytes | What is actually scanned |
| Query Speedup (col vs row) | $Q_{sp}$ | $Q_{sp} = \frac{1}{S}$ | dimensionless | For uncompressed read |
| Page Skip Rate | $P_{skip}$ | $P_{skip} = \frac{N_{skipped}}{N_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Pushdown effectiveness |
| Stripe Count | $N_{stripe}$ | $N_{stripe} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{S_{stripe}} \right\rceil$ | count | Round up |
| Row Group Count | $N_{rg}$ | $N_{rg} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{S_{rg}} \right\rceil$ | count | Round up |

**Boundary Conditions / Rules of Thumb:**
- $CR_{Snappy} \approx 2\text{ to }3$ on text, $\approx 4\text{ to }6$ on Parquet-encoded columnar data.
- A file with $n$ columns queried for $k$ columns gives $S = k/n$. Columnar formats win when $S \ll 1$.
- SequenceFile sync marker frequency is **~1 MB** in Block Compression.
- Parquet footer is read **first** during query planning; if a row group's stats exclude the predicate, the entire row group is skipped.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Lake (HDFS / S3):** All modern data lakes (e.g., Netflix, Uber) standardize on **Parquet + Snappy** for the gold layer.
- **Streaming (Kafka → HDFS):** **Avro** + Schema Registry is the dominant pipeline because the schema travels with the bytes.
- **OLAP on Hive / Impala / Presto / Spark SQL:** ORC and Parquet are the two supported file formats — knowing the difference is essential for query tuning.
- **HBase Backups:** HFile is the unit of bulk import (`BulkLoad`) and snapshot restore.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Compression Ratio and Space Savings

A **1 TB** raw CSV log file is uploaded to HDFS and converted to a **Parquet file with Snappy** compression. The compression ratio on log data is observed to be **5 : 1**.

**Step 1.** Convert TB to GB.

$$
S_{\text{original}} = 1 \text{ TB} = 1 \times 1024 \text{ GB} = 1024 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2.** Apply the Compression Ratio formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}} \\
5 &= \frac{1024}{S_{\text{compressed}}} \\
S_{\text{compressed}} &= \frac{1024}{5} = 204.8 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Compute the Space Savings percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS &= \frac{S_{\text{original}} - S_{\text{compressed}}}{S_{\text{original}}} \times 100 \\
   &= \frac{1024 - 204.8}{1024} \times 100 \\
   &= \frac{819.2}{1024} \times 100 \\
   &= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The HDFS NameNode sees **5× fewer blocks** and the cluster's storage bill is reduced by **80%** for this dataset.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Columnar Query Speedup

A Parquet table has **20 columns**. A typical analyst query reads only **3 columns**. The compressed file is **204.8 GB**.

**Step 1.** Compute column selectivity.

$$
S = \frac{k}{n} = \frac{3}{20} = 0.15
$$

**Step 2.** Compute effective read size.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_r &= S \times S_{\text{compressed}} \\
    &= 0.15 \times 204.8 \text{ GB} \\
    &= 30.72 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Compare against a row-based file that has the **same** compressed size but reads the whole file (since column data is interleaved).

$$
Q_{sp} = \frac{1}{S} = \frac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.67
$$

**Step 4.** Account for **Page-Level Pushdown**. Suppose $10\%$ of pages in those 3 columns fall outside the query's `WHERE` range. Then only $90\%$ of the $30.72$ GB must be decoded.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{r,\text{actual}} &= E_r \times (1 - P_{skip}) \\
                    &= 30.72 \times (1 - 0.10) \\
                    &= 27.648 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total speedup vs row-based format:** $\frac{204.8}{27.648} \approx 7.4\times$ — and that is *after* compression. This is the central KTU exam justification for **"why columnar formats"**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Stripe and Row Group Sizing

A company stores a **900 GB** fact table in ORC with the default stripe size of **250 MB**, and a copy in Parquet with the default row group size of **128 MB**.

**ORC stripes count:**

$$
N_{stripe} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{S_{stripe}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{900 \times 1024}{250} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3686.4 \right\rceil = 3687
$$

**Parquet row group count:**

$$
N_{rg} = \left\lceil \frac{900 \times 1024}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 7200 \right\rceil = 7200
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Implication:** ORC has **roughly half** the number of independent units of parallelism. A Spark SQL reader can therefore process ORC with **fewer, larger tasks** — better sequential throughput, marginally worse load balancing on a small cluster.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Bloom Filter and False Positives in ORC

Suppose a 250 MB ORC stripe contains **5 million rows**. The Bloom filter is sized to keep the false positive probability at $p = 0.05$.

**Step 1.** Approximate the number of bits per element using the optimal Bloom filter formula:

$$
m = -\frac{n \ln p}{(\ln 2)^2}
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $n = 5 \times 10^{6}$ and $p = 0.05$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= -\frac{5 \times 10^{6} \times \ln(0.05)}{(\ln 2)^{2}} \\
  &= -\frac{5 \times 10^{6} \times (-2.9957)}{0.4805} \\
  &= \frac{14{,}978{,}500}{0.4805} \\
  &\approx 31{,}173{,}000 \text{ bits} \\
  &\approx 3.72 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Add this to the stripe size. Bloom filters add about $1.5\%$ overhead in this scenario.

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{3.72}{250} \times 100 \approx 1.49\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational note:** The ORC writer computes $m$ automatically using a target FPP, so the engineer only configures the FPP, never the raw bit count.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Schema Evolution Cost

A Parquet file was written with schema V1 (10 columns, 1 GB). One year later the schema is changed to V2 (12 columns, 1.4 GB), then V3 (8 columns, 0.9 GB).

**Step 1.** Compute the average Parquet row group size.

$$
S_{rg} = 128 \text{ MB}, \quad N_{rg,V1} = \left\lceil \frac{1024}{128} \right\rceil = 8
$$

**Step 2.** Predicate pushdown efficiency on a query selecting only 2 columns from the V1 file.

$$
S = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2, \quad Q_{sp} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5\times
$$

**Step 3.** On V3 (8 columns), the same query:

$$
S = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25, \quad Q_{sp} = 4\times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Removing columns **reduces** the speedup for a fixed query. A schema-design decision made at ingestion directly affects performance **years later** — a key reason data engineers plan schemas carefully.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Hadoop File Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Hadoop File Types]
    rootA --> rootB[Row Based]
    rootA --> rootC[Columnar]
    rootA --> rootD[Key Value Binary]
    rootA --> rootE[Schema Based]
    rootB --> rootF[TextFile Default]
    rootB --> rootG[SequenceFile]
    rootC --> rootH[RCFile Legacy]
    rootC --> rootI[ORC Hive Optimized]
    rootC --> rootJ[Parquet Dremel Inspired]
    rootD --> rootK[MapFile Sorted SeqFile]
    rootD --> rootL[HFile HBase]
    rootE --> rootM[Avro JSON Schema]
```

## 4.2 Internal Layout of a Parquet File

```mermaid
graph TD
    fileA[Parquet File]
    fileA --> rg1[Row Group 1 Size 128 MB]
    fileA --> rg2[Row Group 2]
    fileA --> rgN[Row Group N]
    fileA --> footA[File Footer]
    footA --> footB[Schema Definition]
    footA --> footC[Column Chunk Stats]
    footA --> footD[Footer Length 8 Bytes]
    footA --> footE[Magic PAR1]
    rg1 --> cc1A[Column Chunk Col A]
    rg1 --> cc1B[Column Chunk Col B]
    rg1 --> cc1C[Column Chunk Col C]
    cc1A --> pg1A[Data Page 1 MB]
    cc1A --> pg1B[Data Page]
    cc1A --> pg1C[Dictionary Page Optional]
    pg1A --> statA[Min Max Sum NullCount Stats]
```

## 4.3 Internal Layout of an ORC File

```mermaid
graph TD
    orcA[ORC File]
    orcA --> st1[Stripe 1 Size 250 MB]
    orcA --> st2[Stripe 2]
    orcA --> ffA[File Footer]
    orcA --> psA[Postscript 1 Byte]
    st1 --> idxA[Index Data]
    st1 --> rdA[Row Data Columnar]
    st1 --> sfA[Stripe Footer]
    idxA --> mmA[Min Max Index Per Column]
    idxA --> bfA[Bloom Filter Optional]
    idxA --> riA[Row Index Every 10K Rows]
    ffA --> cmA[Column Level Statistics]
    ffA --> sMetaA[Stripe Metadata]
    psA --> compA[Compression Codec Info]
```

## 4.4 Schema Evolution Flow in Avro

```mermaid
graph LR
    avA[Schema V1 Fields A B C]
    avA --> avB[Add Field D with Default]
    avA --> avC[Remove Field C]
    avA --> avD[Rename Field B to Beta]
    avB --> avE[Schema V2 Backward Compatible]
    avC --> avF[Schema V2 Alternative Backward Compatible]
    avD --> avG[Schema V2 NOT Compatible]
```

## 4.5 Read Path Comparison — Row vs Columnar

```mermaid
graph TD
    qA[Analyst Query SELECT col3 col7 WHERE col3 GT 100]
    qA --> rowA[Row Based Format Reads Entire File 1 GB]
    qA --> colA[Columnar Format Reads Only col3 and col7]
    colA --> statA[Reads Page Min Max Stats in Footer]
    statA --> skipA[Skips Pages Outside Predicate]
    skipA --> finalA[Decodes Only ~30 GB Pages]
    rowA --> finalB[Decodes Entire 1 GB]
    finalA --> resA[Fast]
    finalB --> resB[Slow]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow:** All five diagrams above are constructed as Mermaid **flow-charts** so they compile deterministically in any Markdown renderer. The data labels are intentionally raw uppercase alphanumeric text to comply with the KTU 2024 engine rule that forbids inline markdown formatting inside Mermaid nodes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define SequenceFile. Briefly explain its three compression modes. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **SequenceFile** is a flat binary file consisting of serialized key-value pairs, used as a binary replacement for text in MapReduce. The three compression modes are:

1. **Uncompressed** — no compression; records are written as raw `Writables`. (1 mark)
2. **Record Compressed** — only the *value* of each record is compressed; the key remains plain so that sorting is preserved. (1 mark)
3. **Block Compressed** — a *block of records* is compressed together, preceded by a small header containing record count and key/value lengths. A sync marker before each block makes the file **splittable**. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. Differentiate between row-based and column-based file formats with an example. `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Row-based (e.g., SequenceFile, Avro):** All columns of a row are stored contiguously. Suitable for **OLTP** and write-heavy workloads. (1 mark)
- **Column-based (e.g., Parquet, ORC):** Values of the *same* column are stored contiguously. Best for **OLAP** queries that touch few columns. (1 mark)
- **Example:** For a 20-column table queried for 3 columns, row-based reads **100%** of data, whereas column-based reads only **15%** — giving a **~6.7× speedup**. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the internal structure of a Parquet file with a neat diagram. Why is Parquet preferred for analytical workloads? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Top-level structure:** A Parquet file is wrapped between two `PAR1` magic bytes. The layout is **Row Group → Column Chunk → Page**. (1 mark)
2. **Row Group:** Default size **128 MB**, aligned with HDFS block size for one-to-one locality. (1 mark)
3. **Column Chunk:** One per column per row group. Contains **Page Header + Pages**. (1 mark)
4. **Page:** Default **1 MB**. Stores data using Dictionary encoding + RLE/Bit-packing, optionally compressed with Snappy/Gzip/Zstd. Each page stores **min/max/null-count statistics**. (1 mark)
5. **File Footer:** Contains schema, list of row groups, and column chunk statistics. Read first by the query engine. (1 mark)
6. **Why preferred for analytics:**
   - Columnar layout means only relevant columns are read. (1 mark)
   - Page min/max enables **predicate pushdown** — skip whole pages. (1 mark)
   - Built-in encoding (Dictionary + RLE) yields **3–6× compression** on typical data. (1 mark)

**(b) Compare ORC and RCFile. Why did ORC replace RCFile in Hive? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | RCFile | ORC |
|---|---|---|
| Row group size | 4 MB | 250 MB |
| Index per row group | None | Min/Max, Bloom, Row index |
| File-level statistics | No | Yes (per column) |
| Compression | Limited | Zlib, Snappy, LZO |
| Predicate pushdown | Row group only | Stripe + file + page |
| ACID support | No | Yes (Hive 3) |

1. RCFile stored rows column-wise but **lacked any per-column statistics**, forcing readers to decode every row group. (2 marks)
2. ORC introduces a **two-tier index** (file footer + stripe index) so the reader can skip whole stripes before opening them. (2 marks)
3. ORC uses **run-length and dictionary encoding** before compression, achieving better ratios. (1 mark)
4. ORC has built-in **Bloom filters** for point lookups, useful in `WHERE x =` predicates. (1 mark)
5. Conclusion: ORC is **strictly superior** in analytical workloads, which is why Hive dropped RCFile as the default after Hive 0.14. (1 mark)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Avro data serialization system. What is schema evolution and how does Avro support it? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Avro** is a language-independent data serialization system that stores data in compact binary form together with a **JSON schema** in the file header. (1 mark)
2. **Container file layout:** `Obj\u0001` magic + JSON metadata + sync markers (every 64 KB) + blocks of binary records + closing sync marker. (1 mark)
3. **Self-describing:** The schema travels with the data, so no external schema file is required at read time. (1 mark)
4. **Schema Evolution:** Avro allows the schema to change over time while keeping old data readable. Two schemas are merged using a *projection-and-default-fill* algorithm at read time. (1 mark)
5. **Rules of compatibility:**
   - Adding a field with a default = **backward compatible**. (1 mark)
   - Removing a field = **backward compatible**. (0.5 mark)
   - Renaming a field = **NOT compatible** (name-based matching). (0.5 mark)
6. **Why it matters in engineering:** Kafka pipelines use Avro + Confluent Schema Registry to evolve event formats without downtime. (1 mark)

**(b) With a comparison table, explain the differences between SequenceFile, Avro, Parquet, and ORC. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | SequenceFile | Avro | Parquet | ORC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data layout | Row | Row | Columnar | Columnar |
| Splittable when compressed | Yes (Block mode) | Yes (sync) | Yes | Yes |
| Schema | Writable class names | JSON schema | Thrift-like in footer | Embedded in file |
| Compression | None/Record/Block | Snappy, Deflate, Bzip | Snappy, Gzip, Zstd | Zlib, Snappy, LZO |
| Predicate pushdown | No | No | Yes (page stats) | Yes (stripe + file) |
| Best for | MapReduce shuffle | Streaming / RPC | OLAP general purpose | Hive/Impala OLAP |
| Schema evolution | No | Yes (strong) | Limited | Limited |

1. **SequenceFile** is the legacy format for MapReduce intermediate data — no schema evolution. (1 mark)
2. **Avro** is the format of choice for streaming and write-heavy pipelines due to its schema evolution. (1.5 marks)
3. **Parquet** is the **columnar generalist**, used by Spark, Impala, Presto, and Athena. (1.5 marks)
4. **ORC** is **columnar with a Hive bias** — its indexes (min/max + Bloom) are designed specifically for Hive-style predicate queries. (1.5 marks)
5. **Selection rule:** Row-based & streaming → Avro; Analytics on HDFS → Parquet; Hive warehouse → ORC. (1.5 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Row Group with Stripe.** Many students treat Parquet Row Group and ORC Stripe as identical. They are **logically similar** but differ in *default size* (128 MB vs 250 MB) and *index granularity*.
> 2. **Forgetting that SequenceFile Block Compression is what makes the file splittable** after compression. Compressed SequenceFile in *Record* mode is **not** splittable.
> 3. **Stating "Avro is a columnar format."** It is **row-based** with a schema. The schema is JSON, not columnar storage.
> 4. **Omitting the magic bytes** in the Parquet diagram. Examiners deduct 1 mark when the `PAR1` magic and footer-length pointer are not shown.
> 5. **Reporting compression ratios without units.** Always write $CR = 5$ or $5{:}1$, never just "5".
> 6. **In Predicate Pushdown questions**, students often write "uses index" but forget to mention the *min/max per page* or *per stripe*. That keyword carries marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SequenceFile:** Binary `<key, value>` flat file. Three compression modes: **None / Record / Block**. **Block mode is splittable.**
- **MapFile:** Sorted SequenceFile + in-memory **index file** for $O(\log n)$ lookups.
- **Avro:** JSON schema embedded, compact binary records, **schema evolution** with default-fill projection, **language-neutral**.
- **Parquet:** Columnar, **Row Group (128 MB) → Column Chunk → Page (1 MB)**, page-level min/max enables **predicate pushdown**, encoded with **Dictionary + RLE**.
- **ORC:** Columnar, **Stripe (250 MB) + 2-tier index** (file footer + per-stripe min/max + Bloom filter), optimized for Hive.
- **RCFile:** Columnar predecessor of ORC; **no per-column statistics**; effectively deprecated.
- **HFile:** HBase's sorted KV file with block index and Bloom filter in trailer.
- **Compression Ratio:** $CR = S_{\text{original}} / S_{\text{compressed}}$.
- **Space Savings:** $SS = (S_{\text{original}} - S_{\text{compressed}}) / S_{\text{original}} \times 100\%$.
- **Columnar Speedup vs Row-based:** $Q_{sp} = 1 / S$ where $S$ is the fraction of columns accessed.
- **Parquet Magic:** `PAR1` at start and end; **footer read first**.
- **ORC Magic:** `ORC` at the start of the file; **Postscript 1 byte** holds compression info.
- **Default Bloom FPP in ORC:** 0.05.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Row-write heavy / streaming → **Avro**; General analytical → **Parquet**; Hive-centric → **ORC**; MapReduce shuffle → **SequenceFile**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anatomy of a File Read and Write
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anatomy of a File Read and Write

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anatomy of a File Read and Write** in the context of Big Data Processing refers to the detailed sequence of low-level operations, network interactions, and metadata exchanges that occur when a client application performs `read` or `write` operations on a distributed file system, most notably the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)**.

In HDFS, every file is logically broken into fixed-size **blocks** (default **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+, **64 MB** in Hadoop 1.x) and physically stored across multiple **DataNodes** in a cluster. The mapping between file-to-blocks and blocks-to-DataNodes is maintained centrally by a master server called the **NameNode**. A file read/write operation is therefore not a single local `read()` or `write()` system call — it is a **distributed, multi-stage protocol** involving a client library, the NameNode, and a chain of DataNodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mandated by KTU 2024 Syllabus (PBCDT504 – Module 1):**
> - **NameNode (NN):** Master daemon storing filesystem metadata (namespace image + edit log).
> - **DataNode (DN):** Slave daemon storing actual HDFS data blocks on local disk.
> - **Block:** Smallest unit of read/write, default size **128 MB**.
> - **Replication Factor:** Default **3** copies of every block.
> - **Client:** User application linked with the `org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem` API.
> - **Packet:** Default **64 KB** unit transferred in the data pipeline.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Central Library with Branch Warehouses"**

Imagine you want to **borrow a 1000-page encyclopaedia** (your file) from a national library.

- The **Chief Librarian** is the **NameNode**. She does NOT keep the books. She only maintains a **catalogue card** (metadata) that says: *"Pages 1–500 are in Warehouse A, Pages 501–1000 are in Warehouse B."*
- The **Branch Warehouses** are the **DataNodes**. They physically store the book chapters (blocks).
- **You** are the **Client** application.
- When you **read** the book, you don't ask the warehouses directly — you first ask the Chief Librarian for the locations (metadata lookup), then walk to the correct warehouse to borrow the chapters (data transfer).
- When you **write** (donate) a new book, the Chief Librarian decides which warehouses should hold copies, the first warehouse makes a copy, passes it to the next, and so on — this chain is the **replication pipeline**.

This two-step indirection (metadata → data) is the soul of the **"Anatomy of a File Read and Write"** in HDFS.

---

## 1.3 The Two Architectural Pillars

| Pillar | Role in Read | Role in Write |
|---|---|---|
| **NameNode** | Returns the list of DataNodes holding each block of the requested file | Allocates fresh block IDs, chooses the DataNode pipeline for replicas |
| **DataNode** | Streams actual block bytes to the client over a TCP socket | Receives data from the client and forwards to the next DataNode in the pipeline |
| **Client** | Opens `InputStream`, reads sequentially, handles failures | Opens `OutputStream`, pushes packets into the pipeline, manages ack queue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** The NameNode **never** moves data. It only moves **metadata** (small JSON-like responses). All heavy data transfer happens **directly** between Client and DataNodes — this is the core of HDFS's scalability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Phases of a HDFS File READ

A read operation unfolds in **four distinct phases**, and examiners frequently test these.

### Phase 1 — Client Library Initialisation
- The application calls `FileSystem.open(Path)`.
- The client library instantiates an `FSDataInputStream`, which wraps a `DFSInputStream`.
- `DFSInputStream` internally holds an object called the **"BlockReader" pool**, initially empty.

### Phase 2 — Metadata RPC to NameNode
- Client invokes a **Remote Procedure Call (RPC)** to the NameNode: `getBlockLocations(fileName, startOffset, length)`.
- NameNode consults its in-memory **inode map** and the **blocks map**, and returns an ordered list of block locations. Each entry contains:
  - `blockID` (128 MB chunk identifier),
  - list of `DataNode` addresses (sorted by **network proximity** — the closest DN is placed first),
  - offset within the block,
  - length of data.

### Phase 3 — Direct Data Streaming from DataNode
- The client connects via TCP to the **first** (closest) DataNode in the list.
- A `BlockReader` is created for that block.
- Bytes are streamed sequentially via repeated reads of a buffer of size `io.file.buffer.size` (default **4 KB** in Java, **128 KB** in HDFS for efficiency).
- When one block is exhausted, the `DFSInputStream` automatically issues a new RPC for the next block's locations — **without closing the stream** to the user.

### Phase 4 — Failure Handling & Checksum Verification
- Each packet on the wire carries a **32-bit CRC-32C checksum**.
- The client verifies the checksum on receipt. On corruption, it:
  1. Closes the current DataNode connection.
  2. Skips to the next DataNode in the location list.
  3. Asks NameNode to mark the bad replica for deletion.
  4. Continues reading seamlessly.

---

## 2.2 Anatomical Phases of a HDFS File WRITE

A write operation is symmetric but more complex due to **replication pipelining**.

### Phase 1 — Create RPC
- Application calls `FileSystem.create(Path)`.
- Client sends RPC `create(filename, replicationFactor, blockSize)` to NameNode.
- NameNode performs **path validation** (no duplicates, quota checks, permissions) and inserts an entry in the namespace. The file currently exists **only in NameNode's metadata** — it is **not visible** to readers yet.

### Phase 2 — Add Block RPC & Pipeline Setup
- When the client begins writing bytes, the output stream (`DFSOutputStream`) fills an internal packet buffer (default **64 KB** packet).
- Once full, the client sends RPC `addBlock(filename, clientName, previousBlock)` to NameNode.
- NameNode allocates a **fresh block ID** and returns a list of DataNodes forming a **replication pipeline** (typically 3 nodes, chosen using the **rack-aware replica placement policy**).

### Phase 3 — Data Pipelining
- Data is pushed to the **first** DataNode in the pipeline.
- That DataNode stores the packet locally and **immediately forwards** it to the second DataNode.
- The second forwards to the third.
- Each DataNode sends back an **acknowledgement (ack)** up the chain only after the packet is written to its local disk and forwarded downstream.
- A packet is considered successful only when the **entire pipeline acknowledges**.

### Phase 4 — Finalisation (Close & Commit)
- Client calls `close()`.
- The remaining buffer is flushed.
- Client sends RPC `complete(fileName, previousBlock)` to NameNode.
- NameNode commits the file; it is now **readable** by the world.

---

## 2.3 The Replica Placement Policy (Rack Awareness)

> [!NOTE]
> **Default HDFS Rack-Aware Policy (for Replication Factor = 3):**
> - **Replica 1:** On a DataNode in the **local rack** of the writer client.
> - **Replica 2:** On a **different rack** but a different DataNode.
> - **Replica 3:** On the **same rack as Replica 2** but a different DataNode.
>
> **Goal:** Trade-off between *write performance* (one replica is local) and *fault tolerance* (loss of an entire rack does not destroy the file).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of blocks per file** | $N_{blocks} = \lceil \dfrac{FileSize}{BlockSize} \rceil$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | **HDFS Block size (default, Hadoop 2.x+)** | $B = 128$ | MB |
| 3 | **Replication factor (default)** | $R = 3$ | copies |
| 4 | **Total physical storage used** | $Storage = N_{blocks} \times B \times R$ | MB |
| 5 | **Data packet size** | $P = 64$ | KB |
| 6 | **Client read buffer** | $C_{read} = 4$ (Java default); $128$ (HDFS tuned) | KB |
| 7 | **Checksum algorithm** | **CRC-32C** (Castagnoli polynomial) | 32 bits per chunk |
| 8 | **Network RTT tolerance** | $T_{write} = T_{RTT} \times (R - 1)$ for pipeline | ms |
| 9 | **Read throughput (ideal)** | $T = \dfrac{B \times 8}{t_{transfer}}$ | Mbps |
| 10 | **Pipeline write latency (sequential)** | $L = T_{disk} + (R-1) \cdot T_{RTT}$ | ms |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never** confuse the *block size* (128 MB) with the *packet size* (64 KB). They differ by a factor of **2048**. Examiners love this trap.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hadoop MapReduce / YARN** uses exactly this read/write anatomy when shuffle/sort moves intermediate data between mapper and reducer.
- **Apache Spark** talks to HDFS via the same `FileSystem` API, hence the same anatomy governs every `spark.read.text()` and `df.write.parquet()`.
- **Cloud analogues:** Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ABFS), and Google Cloud Storage (GCS) — all implement object-store variants of this read/write anatomy with weaker consistency guarantees but identical conceptual stages (metadata lookup → direct data plane transfer → checksum/ETag verification).
- **Disaster Recovery:** Understanding the write pipeline explains why *rack diversity* matters — entire DC outages do not lose data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Computing Number of Blocks

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A file of size **450 MB** is stored in HDFS with a block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Calculate the (a) number of blocks, (b) total logical storage, (c) total physical storage consumed.

**Given:**
- $F = 450$ MB
- $B = 128$ MB
- $R = 3$

### Step 1 — Compute the number of blocks

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{450}{128} \right\rceil
$$

Performing the division:
$$
\frac{450}{128} = 3.515625
$$

Since we always round **up** (the last block can be smaller than the block size):
$$
N_{blocks} = \lceil 3.515625 \rceil = 4
$$

> **Logic:** Even though 3 blocks could theoretically hold $3 \times 128 = 384$ MB, the remaining $450 - 384 = 66$ MB still requires a 4th block. HDFS never merges tail data with the previous block.

### Step 2 — Total logical storage

Logical storage is what the user *thinks* they have:
$$
S_{logical} = F = 450 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 3 — Total physical storage

Every block is replicated 3 times:
$$
S_{physical} = N_{blocks} \times B_{avg} \times R
$$

But careful — the **last block** is only $66$ MB, not $128$ MB. So we must use the *actual* per-block sizes:

$$
S_{physical} = (128 \times 3) + (128 \times 3) + (128 \times 3) + (66 \times 3)
$$

$$
S_{physical} = 3 \times (128 + 128 + 128 + 66)
$$

$$
S_{physical} = 3 \times 450 = 1350 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Shortcut:** $S_{physical} = S_{logical} \times R = 450 \times 3 = 1350$ MB. This shortcut works because the replication factor multiplies the *total content* uniformly, regardless of how it is split into blocks.

### Final Answer Block

$$
\boxed{N_{blocks} = 4, \quad S_{logical} = 450 \text{ MB}, \quad S_{physical} = 1350 \text{ MB}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Pipeline Write Latency

**Problem Statement:** A client writes a packet of size **64 KB** to HDFS. The disk write time per DataNode is **10 ms**, and the inter-DataNode RTT is **2 ms**. The replication factor is **3**. Calculate the **total time** for the packet to be fully acknowledged.

### Step 1 — Identify variables

- $T_{disk} = 10$ ms (write latency per DataNode)
- $T_{RTT} = 2$ ms (network hop between consecutive DataNodes)
- $R = 3$ (DataNodes in the pipeline: DN1 → DN2 → DN3)

### Step 2 — Pipeline stage analysis

In a 3-node pipeline, the data flows as:
- **t = 0 ms:** Client → DN1 (transfer starts)
- **t = T_{RTT} = 2 ms:** DN1 → DN2 (transfer starts)
- **t = 2 \times T_{RTT} = 4 ms:** DN2 → DN3 (transfer starts)
- **t = 4 ms + T_{disk}:** DN3 finishes writing to its local disk
- **t = 4 ms + 2 \times T_{disk}:** DN2 finishes writing and forwards ack
- **t = 4 ms + 3 \times T_{disk}:** DN1 finishes writing and forwards ack
- **t = 4 ms + 3 \times T_{disk} + T_{RTT}:** Client receives final ack

### Step 3 — General formula derivation

For a pipeline of length $R$, the **last packet's total latency** is:

$$
L_{total} = (R - 1) \cdot T_{RTT} + R \cdot T_{disk} + T_{RTT}
$$

Simplifying:
$$
L_{total} = R \cdot T_{RTT} + R \cdot T_{disk} = R \cdot (T_{RTT} + T_{disk})
$$

### Step 4 — Plug in the values

$$
L_{total} = 3 \times (2 + 10) = 3 \times 12 = 36 \text{ ms}
$$

### Final Answer Block

$$
\boxed{L_{total} = 36 \text{ ms}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the network transfer is only 4 ms, the disk write dominates. This is why **HDFS prefers fewer, larger blocks** — the per-pipeline overhead is amortised over a larger block size.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Read vs Write Comparison

| Parameter | READ Operation | WRITE Operation |
|---|---|---|
| **Master RPC** | `getBlockLocations()` | `create()` + `addBlock()` + `complete()` |
| **Direction of data** | DataNode → Client | Client → DataNode (piped) |
| **Pipeline used?** | No (direct from nearest DN) | Yes (DN1 → DN2 → DN3) |
| **Number of RPCs per file** | 1 per block (lazy) | 3 (create, addBlock, complete) |
| **Checksum verification** | Client-side, post-read | Per-packet at each DN, ack-based |
| **Failure recovery** | Switch to next replica in DN list | Pipeline rebuild, restart from last acked packet |
| **Visibility to readers** | Immediate (file is closed + committed) | Only after `complete()` succeeds |
| **Default buffer size** | 4 KB (Java) / 128 KB (HDFS tuned) | 64 KB (packet) |
| **Default block size** | 128 MB | 128 MB |
| **Consistency model** | Read sees latest committed | Strictly consistent (single writer lease) |

---

## 3.4 Block Size Trade-off Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Q: Why is the default HDFS block size 128 MB and not 4 KB (like Linux ext4)?**

Let the cluster store $D$ TB of data. Number of blocks:
$$
N = \frac{D \times 1024 \times 1024}{B_{size} \text{ in MB}}
$$

The NameNode keeps ~$250$ bytes of metadata per block in RAM. So RAM needed:
$$
RAM = N \times 250 \text{ bytes} = \frac{D \times 1024^2 \times 250}{B_{size}}
$$

For $D = 100$ PB and $B_{size} = 128$ MB:
$$
RAM = \frac{100 \times 10^6 \times 1024 \times 250}{128} \approx 200 \text{ GB}
$$

For $B_{size} = 4$ KB:
$$
RAM \approx 6.25 \text{ TB} \quad \text{(impractical for a single NameNode)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Larger blocks → fewer metadata entries → NameNode memory fits a petabyte-scale cluster. This is the *physical* reason HDFS is "big-data friendly."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Read Flow — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2
    participant DN3 as DataNode 3

    Client->>NameNode: RPC: open(filePath)
    NameNode-->>Client: Block locations [B1: [DN1,DN2,DN3], B2: [DN2,DN3,DN1]]
    Client->>DN1: TCP: read block B1 (closest replica)
    DN1-->>Client: Stream packet 1 (64KB + CRC32C)
    DN1-->>Client: Stream packet 2 (64KB + CRC32C)
    DN1-->>Client: Stream packet N (end of B1)
    Client->>NameNode: RPC: getBlockLocations(B2)
    NameNode-->>Client: Block locations for B2
    Client->>DN2: TCP: read block B2
    DN2-->>Client: Stream packets of B2
    Client->>Client: Verify CRC32C on every packet
    Client->>Client: close() - read complete
```

---

## 4.2 HDFS Write Flow — Replication Pipeline Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Application] -->|Step 1: create RPC| B[NameNode]
    B -->|Step 2: blockID + DN list| A
    A -->|Step 3: Packet P1 64KB| C[DataNode 1]
    C -->|Forward P1| D[DataNode 2]
    D -->|Forward P1| E[DataNode 3]
    E -->|Ack P1| D
    D -->|Ack P1| C
    C -->|Ack P1| A
    A -->|Packet P2 64KB| C
    C -->|Forward P2| D
    D -->|Forward P2| E
    E -->|Ack P2| D
    D -->|Ack P2| C
    C -->|Ack P2| A
    A -->|Step 4: complete RPC| B
    B -->|File is now committed| A
```

---

## 4.3 HDFS Architecture — Master/Slave Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MasterNode[NameNode Master]
        N1[Namespace Image fsimage]
        N2[Edit Log]
        N3[Block Map: file to block to DN list]
    end
    subgraph SlaveRack1[Slave Rack 1]
        D1[DataNode 1 - blocks B1 B2 B3]
        D2[DataNode 2 - blocks B1 B2 B4]
    end
    subgraph SlaveRack2[Slave Rack 2]
        D3[DataNode 3 - blocks B1 B3 B4]
        D4[DataNode 4 - blocks B2 B3 B4]
```
