```mermaid
    end
    Client[Client App] -->|Metadata RPC only| MasterNode
    Client -->|Direct data TCP| D1
    Client -->|Direct data TCP| D3
    MasterNode -->|Heartbeat every 3 sec| D1
    MasterNode -->|Heartbeat every 3 sec| D2
    MasterNode -->|Heartbeat every 3 sec| D3
    MasterNode -->|Heartbeat every 3 sec| D4
    D1 -.->|Block Report every 6 hr| MasterNode
    D2 -.->|Block Report every 6 hr| MasterNode
    D3 -.->|Block Report every 6 hr| MasterNode
    D4 -.->|Block Report every 6 hr| MasterNode
```

---

## 4.4 Replica Placement — Rack-Aware Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New block to be written Replication Factor 3] --> Q1{Writer is on which rack?}
    Q1 -->|Local Rack R1| P1[Replica 1: choose a DN on local rack R1]
    Q1 -->|Remote Rack R2| P2[Replica 1: choose a DN on remote rack R2]
    P1 --> P3[Replica 2: choose a DN on a DIFFERENT rack R2]
    P2 --> P3B[Replica 2: choose a DN on a DIFFERENT rack R1]
    P3 --> P4[Replica 3: choose a different DN on the same rack as Replica 2]
    P3B --> P4B[Replica 3: choose a different DN on the same rack as Replica 2]
    P4 --> Done[Block stored across 2 racks with 3 replicas]
    P4B --> Done
```

---

## 4.5 Packet Lifecycle During a Write

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PacketCreated: Client builds 64KB packet
    PacketCreated --> InPipeline: Send to DN1 via TCP
    InPipeline --> AtDN1: DN1 receives and writes to local disk
    AtDN1 --> AtDN2: DN1 forwards packet down pipeline
    AtDN2 --> AtDN3: DN2 forwards packet down pipeline
    AtDN3 --> AckedByDN3: DN3 writes to local disk
    AckedByDN3 --> AckedByDN2: DN3 sends ack up
    AckedByDN2 --> AckedByDN1: DN2 sends ack up
    AckedByDN1 --> AckedByClient: DN1 sends ack up
    AckedByClient --> [*]: Client dequeues packet from ack queue
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Explain the role of the NameNode and DataNode in HDFS. Why is the NameNode considered the single point of failure, and how is high availability addressed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **NameNode (NN)** is the master daemon that maintains the **filesystem namespace**, the mapping of files to blocks, and the location of every block replica. It stores all metadata in RAM for fast access and persists it via the `fsimage` and `edit log` files. It does **not** store any user data.

The **DataNode (DN)** is the slave daemon that runs on every commodity server in the cluster. It stores the actual HDFS data blocks on its local disk and serves read/write requests from clients. It periodically sends a **heartbeat** (every 3 seconds) and a **block report** (every 6 hours) to the NameNode.

The NameNode is a single point of failure because if it crashes, the metadata becomes inaccessible and the entire cluster becomes unreadable. Hadoop 2.x+ addresses this via **HDFS High Availability (HA)**: a **Standby NameNode** runs in a hot-standby configuration using a **Quorum Journal Manager (QJM)** or **NFS** to share the edit log, with **ZooKeeper** for leader election. *(3 marks: 1 for NN role, 1 for DN role, 1 for HA explanation.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the significance of the block size in HDFS? Why is it kept large (128 MB) compared to traditional filesystems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In HDFS, a file is split into fixed-size **blocks** (default **128 MB**) and distributed across the cluster. A large block size is significant for three reasons:

1. **Reduces NameNode memory footprint:** The NameNode stores ~250 bytes of metadata per block. With 128 MB blocks, a 100 PB cluster has only ~$2 \times 10^8$ blocks (≈ 50 GB metadata) versus $2.6 \times 10^{10}$ blocks for 4 KB blocks (≈ 6.5 TB metadata — impractical).

2. **Reduces seek time overhead:** HDFS is designed for **sequential reads** of large files (think TB-scale logs, genomics). A larger block means the per-block **disk seek overhead** is amortised over more contiguous data.

3. **Reduces RPC chatter:** The client contacts the NameNode only once per block, not per 4 KB chunk. Larger blocks ⇒ fewer metadata RPCs ⇒ higher throughput.

The trade-off is poor performance for **many small files**, because each file consumes at least one block on the NameNode regardless of its actual size. *(3 marks: 1 per point.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you are given an internal choice. Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, S7 B.Tech CSE]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **anatomy of a file read operation in HDFS** with a neat sequence diagram. Explain how HDFS handles DataNode failures and checksum mismatches during a read.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A file of size **1024 MB** is stored in HDFS with a default block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**.
   (i) Calculate the number of blocks, total logical size, and total physical storage.
   (ii) If the cluster has **5 DataNodes across 2 racks**, draw a possible **rack-aware replica placement** for the first block.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — HDFS File Read Anatomy [7 marks]**

A read in HDFS is a **two-stage** operation: **metadata lookup** followed by **direct data transfer**.

**Step 1 — Client calls `FileSystem.open(path)` [1 mark]**
The Hadoop client library creates a `FSDataInputStream` wrapping a `DFSInputStream` object. The client does not yet contact any DataNode.

**Step 2 — RPC to NameNode [1 mark]**
The `DFSInputStream` invokes a remote procedure call `getBlockLocations(file, offset, length)` on the NameNode. The NameNode consults its in-memory inode and block maps, then returns an ordered list of block locations, sorted by **network proximity** (the closest DataNode appears first). Each entry contains `blockID`, list of `DataNode` addresses, offset, and length.

**Step 3 — Direct streaming from nearest DataNode [2 marks]**
The client opens a TCP socket directly to the **first** DataNode in the list (shortest distance). A `BlockReader` is initialised. Data is streamed as **64 KB packets**, each carrying a **32-bit CRC-32C checksum**. The user application calls `read()` on the `FSDataInputStream`, which buffers data internally.

**Step 4 — Cross-block handling [1 mark]**
When the current block is fully consumed, `DFSInputStream` issues a *new* RPC to the NameNode for the next block's locations. The user's stream is **not** closed — this is transparent.

**Failure handling [2 marks]**
- **Checksum mismatch:** The client discards the corrupted packet, closes the current DataNode connection, skips to the **next** DataNode in the location list, and asks the NameNode to delete the bad replica. Reading continues seamlessly.
- **DataNode failure:** The client transparently fails over to the next replica. After the read, the NameNode schedules a new replica to restore the replication factor to 3.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical & Replica Placement [7 marks]**

**(i) Numerical Computation [3 marks]**

Given: $F = 1024$ MB, $B = 128$ MB, $R = 3$.

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{1024}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 8 \rceil = 8 \text{ blocks}
$$

$$
S_{logical} = 1024 \text{ MB} = 1 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
S_{physical} = N_{blocks} \times B \times R = 8 \times 128 \times 3 = 3072 \text{ MB} = 3 \text{ GB}
$$

(Or using the shortcut: $S_{physical} = S_{logical} \times R = 1024 \times 3 = 3072$ MB ✓.)

**(ii) Rack-Aware Replica Placement [4 marks]**

Assuming the writer client is on **Rack 1**:

| Replica | Rack | DataNode | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Replica 1** | Rack 1 (local) | DN1 | Minimises write latency |
| **Replica 2** | Rack 2 (remote) | DN3 | Ensures rack-level fault tolerance |
| **Replica 3** | Rack 2 (same as Replica 2) | DN4 | Spreads replicas on different nodes |

**Diagram (text representation):**
```
Rack 1                       Rack 2
[DN1] -- holds Replica 1     [DN3] -- holds Replica 2
[DN2]                        [DN4] -- holds Replica 3
                             [DN5]
```

This placement guarantees that the loss of an entire rack still preserves at least one replica of the block.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **anatomy of a file write operation in HDFS** with emphasis on the **replication pipeline** and the **ack mechanism**. Include a neat block diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **default rack-aware replica placement policy** of HDFS. Why is it preferred over placing all replicas on the same rack? What happens if a DataNode in the middle of the pipeline fails mid-write?

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — HDFS File Write Anatomy [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — `FileSystem.create(path)` [1 mark]**
The client library invokes RPC `create()` on the NameNode. The NameNode validates the path (no duplicates, quota, permissions) and inserts a new file entry in the namespace. **No data has moved yet** — the file is not visible to readers.

**Step 2 — Client begins writing to a buffer [1 mark]**
The `DFSOutputStream` accumulates data into an internal packet buffer of **64 KB**. When full, the packet is ready for transmission.

**Step 3 — `addBlock` RPC and pipeline setup [1 mark]**
Client invokes `addBlock()` on the NameNode, which allocates a fresh **block ID** and returns a list of **DataNodes** to form a pipeline (e.g., DN1 → DN2 → DN3), chosen per the **rack-aware policy**.

**Step 4 — Pipeline data transfer [2 marks]**
- Client pushes Packet P1 to **DN1**.
- DN1 writes P1 to its local disk and **immediately forwards** P1 to **DN2**.
- DN2 does the same and forwards to **DN3**.
- DN3 writes P1 to its local disk.
- Acks flow **back up the chain**: DN3 → DN2 → DN1 → Client.
- The client removes P1 from its **ack queue** only when the final ack arrives. Until then, the packet is *unacked* and can be retransmitted on failure.

**Step 5 — `complete()` finalisation [1 mark]**
After all data is written, the client calls `close()`. Any remaining buffered data is flushed, then the client sends RPC `complete()` to the NameNode. The NameNode commits the file — it is now visible cluster-wide.

**Ack mechanism explained [1 mark]**
Each DataNode sends an ack only after **(a)** writing the packet to disk **and** **(b)** forwarding to the next DN. This guarantees *at-least-once* semantics and survives pipeline failures because the client retains unacked packets for retransmission.

---

**Part (b) — Replica Placement & Failure Recovery [7 marks]**

**Default Rack-Aware Policy [3 marks]**

For a replication factor of 3:
- **Replica 1:** Placed on the **local rack** of the writer client (minimises write latency).
- **Replica 2:** Placed on a **different rack** (ensures rack-level fault tolerance).
- **Replica 3:** Placed on the **same rack as Replica 2** but on a **different DataNode** (spreads replicas on different physical machines).

**Why not all replicas on the same rack? [2 marks]**
If all replicas were on the same rack, a single **rack switch failure** or **rack-wide power outage** would destroy all copies → **data loss**. Spreading replicas across **at least 2 racks** guarantees the cluster can survive an entire rack failure, while still keeping one replica *local* to the writer for performance.

**What if a middle DataNode fails? [2 marks]**
Suppose **DN2** fails mid-write (after receiving P1, before forwarding). The pipeline breaks. The NameNode detects the failure via missing **heartbeats** and removes DN2 from the cluster. The client is notified of the broken pipeline. It then:
1. Stops the old pipeline.
2. Asks the NameNode for a **new pipeline** (e.g., DN1 → DN4 → DN5, choosing fresh nodes that have a copy of the latest block state).
3. **Replays all unacked packets** from its ack queue into the new pipeline.
4. Once acked, the write continues transparently to the user.

The block's replication factor may dip temporarily below 3 during the recovery window, but is restored by the NameNode's background **re-replication** process.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse block size with packet size.** Block = 128 MB (storage unit); Packet = 64 KB (network transfer unit). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this slip.
> 2. **Always mention the NameNode does NOT transfer data** in a read/write — it only transfers *metadata*. This is the single most-tested conceptual point.
> 3. **Never claim the NameNode has multiple replicas of data.** It has *no user data* — only metadata. The **Standby NameNode** in HA architecture has *its own* metadata, not user data.
> 4. **In rack-aware placement**, the **first replica is local**, *not* random. Examiners deduct marks if you say "all replicas are placed randomly."
> 5. **Failure recovery formula:** Always specify that the client maintains an **ack queue** of unacked packets. Without this, the answer on write recovery is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ HDFS read/write is a **two-stage protocol**: **metadata RPC** → **direct data transfer**. The NameNode **never** moves data bytes.
- ✅ Default **block size = 128 MB**; default **replication factor = 3**; default **packet size = 64 KB**.
- ✅ Total physical storage = Logical size × Replication factor.
- ✅ Number of blocks = $\lceil$ FileSize / BlockSize $\rceil$. Always round **up**.
- ✅ Each block is replicated across **at least 2 racks** for fault tolerance.
- ✅ The **first replica is on the local rack**; the **second is on a different rack**; the **third is on the same rack as the second but a different DN**.
- ✅ The client maintains an **ack queue** for unacknowledged packets — this is the basis of write failure recovery.
- ✅ On **read failure**, the client transparently fails over to the next replica in the location list returned by the NameNode.
- ✅ On **write failure** in the pipeline, the client reconstructs the pipeline with new DataNodes and **replays unacked packets**.
- ✅ HDFS uses **CRC-32C** checksums per 512-byte chunk (not per packet) for fine-grained corruption detection.
- ✅ The NameNode keeps all metadata **in RAM** for speed — which is why **block size matters so much** for scalability.
- ✅ **Heartbeats** (every 3 sec) and **Block Reports** (every 6 hours) flow from DataNode → NameNode.
- ✅ High Availability is achieved via **Standby NameNode + Quorum Journal Manager + ZooKeeper**.
- ✅ The "anatomy" specifically refers to the **ordered sequence of RPCs and TCP data transfers**, not the storage layout alone.
- ✅ HDFS write consistency is **strict** (single-writer lease model), unlike S3 or Cassandra.
- ✅ Last block in a file **can be smaller** than the configured block size — HDFS does not pad.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MapReduce Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** MapReduce Programming # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of MapReduce Programming ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) **MapReduce Programming** is a distributed, parallel-processing programming model designed by Google for processing and generating large-scale datasets in a fault-tolerant manner across commodity hardware clusters. In the Hadoop ecosystem, MapReduce provides a structured framework where user-defined **Mapper** and **Reducer** functions process data as **key-value pairs** in two principal phases — the *Map* phase and the *Reduce* phase — coordinated by the **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** resource manager and the **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** storage layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** > *"MapReduce is a programming paradigm that expresses large distributed computations as a sequence of operations on datasets of key-value pairs. It is the core data processing engine of the Apache Hadoop framework."* The complete API is encapsulated under the `org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce` package, exposing abstract classes `Mapper` and `Reducer`. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a **library with 10 million books**, and you are asked to count the frequency of every unique word appearing in the first chapter of each book. | Real-World Step | MapReduce Counterpart | |---|---| | Hire 1,000 librarians and split the 10M books among them | **Map Tasks** distributed across DataNodes | | Each librarian reads their share and writes word counts on index cards | **Mapper.emit(word, 1)** for each occurrence | | Collect all index cards and group them by word | **Shuffle & Sort Phase** (intermediate) | | Hand each grouped pile to a different librarian to add up counts | **Reduce Phase** | | Write the final dictionary of word counts | **Output written to HDFS** | The brilliance is that **no single librarian ever has to touch every book** — and if one librarian falls sick (node failure), another can be assigned his/her stack (fault tolerance via *speculative execution* and *task re-launch*). > [!NOTE] > **Core Data Types in MapReduce:** > Every input and output is a `(key, value)` pair. The framework is **typed**, but Hadoop's `Writable` interface lets Java primitive-like types (`IntWritable`, `Text`, `LongWritable`, `FloatWritable`) be serialized efficiently across the network. ## 1.3 Physical/Default Metrics in MapReduce | Default Metric | Value (Hadoop 3.x) | Significance | |---|---|---| | Default Block Size (HDFS) | **128 MB** (or **256 MB** on newer versions) | Splits = approx 1 split per block | | `mapreduce.job.maps` default | **2** | Maps = 1 per input split | | `mapreduce.job.reduces` default | **1** | Single reducer = bottleneck | | Replication Factor (HDFS) | **3** | Fault tolerance | | Default `Sort` behavior | **True** | Keys sorted in natural order before reducer | | Combiner Class | **Optional** | Local aggregation optimization | | Partitioner Class | **HashPartitioner** | Default partitioning by `key.hashCode() mod R` | ## 1.4 The Big Picture — Where MapReduce Fits in Hadoop ``` HDFS (Storage) + YARN (Cluster Resource Mgmt) + MapReduce (Processing) ↓ ↓ ↓ Stores data in Allocates containers Executes user-defined blocks/replicas for Map & Reduce tasks Mapper & Reducer logic ``` > [!NOTE] > MapReduce **cannot run without YARN** in modern Hadoop. YARN's `ResourceManager` and `NodeManager` schedule the tasks; MapReduce defines *what* those tasks compute. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Anatomy of a MapReduce Job — Step-by-Step A MapReduce job executes in the following ordered phases: ### Phase 1: Input Splitting - The **`InputFormat`** class (`TextInputFormat` is the default) divides the input file on HDFS into logical **InputSplits** — typically one split per HDFS block. - Each split is processed by exactly one **Map task**. ### Phase 2: Mapping - The `RecordReader` (default: `LineRecordReader`) parses the split into `(K1, V1)` pairs. - The user-defined **`map()`** method receives each pair and emits a *list* of intermediate `(K2, V2)` pairs: $$ \text{map}(K1, V1) \rightarrow \text{List} < (K2, V2) > $$ - All outputs of a single map task are written to a **circular buffer in memory** (default 100 MB). ### Phase 3: Spill, Partition, Combine (Local Optimization) - When the buffer fills to a threshold (default **80%**), a **spill** to disk begins. - Before spilling, three operations occur on the buffer: 1. **Partitioning** — Each `(K2, V2)` is assigned a reducer index using: $$ \text{partitioner}(K2, V2) \rightarrow r \in [0, R-1] $$ 2. **Sorting** — Pairs sorted by partition number, then by key. 3. **Combining** *(optional)* — A mini-reducer (`Combiner`) performs local aggregation to reduce data shuffled to reducers. - Spill files are merged into a single **partitioned, sorted** output file per map task. ### Phase 4: Shuffle & Sort (Network-Heavy Phase) - All map task outputs for a given partition `r` are **fetched over HTTP** by the reducer responsible for `r`. - All fetched segments are **merged-sorted** into a single ordered stream — this is the **most network-intensive** step. ### Phase 5: Reducing - The user-defined **`reduce()`** method processes the grouped pairs: $$ \text{reduce}(K2, \text{List}) \rightarrow \text{List} < (K3, V3) > $$ ### Phase 6: Output Write - The `OutputFormat` (default: `TextOutputFormat`) writes `(K3, V3)` pairs to HDFS. For each reducer output, a separate file is produced: `part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001`, ... ## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number of Map Tasks | $M \approx \lceil \text{Total Input Size} \;/\; \text{Block Size} \rceil$ | Controls parallelism in Map phase | | 2 | Number of Reducers | $R$ — user-defined (default 1) | Controls partitioning granularity | | 3 | Hash Partitioning | $r = (K2.\text{hashCode}()\ \&\ \text{Integer.MAX\_VALUE}) \mod R$ | Default key distribution | | 4 | Writable Serialization Cost | $\text{Size}_{\text{serialized}} = f(\text{type})$ | Smaller types → faster shuffle | | 5 | Data Locality | Ideal map = **node-local**; else **rack-local**; else **off-rack** | Determines network traffic | | 6 | Speculative Execution | Run duplicate slow task on another node | Fault tolerance + straggler mitigation | | 7 | Combiner Constraint | $f(f(K, V)) = f(K, V)$ — must be **idempotent & associative** | E.g., `SUM`, `MAX` ✅; `AVG` ❌ | | 8 | Inverted Index Quality | $I = \{(t_i, [d_{i1}, d_{i2}, \dots]) \;\vert\; t_i \in D\}$ | Standard MapReduce example | | 9 | MapReduce Sort Cost | $O\!\left(\sum_i M_i \log M_i + R \cdot \log R \cdot N_{\text{keys}}\right)$ | Theoretical time complexity | | 10 | Job Counters | `MAP_INPUT_RECORDS`, `REDUCE_INPUT_RECORDS`, `HDFS_BYTES_WRITTEN` | Used for debugging & monitoring | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitfall — Partitioner Output Cardinality:** > The partitioner function must return values in the range $[0, R-1]$ where $R$ = number of reducers. Returning a value $\geq R$ causes a `IOException: Illegal partition for ...`. ## 2.3 Why MapReduce? — Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Use Case | |---|---| | **Search Engines** | Build inverted indexes of web pages | | **Log Analytics** | Aggregate petabytes of server logs (Facebook, Twitter) | | **ETL Pipelines** | Transform raw clickstream → user sessions | | **Bioinformatics** | Map DNA reads against reference genome | | **Recommender Systems** | Co-occurrence matrix computation | | **Fraud Detection** | Real-time batch risk scoring | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From `map()` to `reduce()` — A Worked Word-Count Example > [!NOTE] > This is the **canonical KTU exam example**. Mastering this is essential. ### Step 1: Input & Logical Splitting Consider a file `input.txt` on HDFS containing: $$ \text{``hello world hello hadoop''} $$ HDFS block size = 128 MB; suppose the file is small and forms **one block → one input split → one map task**. ### Step 2: RecordReader converts the split into pairs $$ (K1, V1) = (\text{offset}, \text{line}) $$ For the above line: $$ (K1, V1) = (0, \text{``hello world hello hadoop''}) $$ ### Step 3: `map()` is invoked line-by-line The user-defined `TokenizerMapper` tokenizes the line and emits one `(word, 1)` per token. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{map}(0, \text{``hello world hello hadoop''}) & \rightarrow \text{emit}(\text{``hello''}, 1) \\ & \rightarrow \text{emit}(\text{``world''}, 1) \\ & \rightarrow \text{emit}(\text{``hello''}, 1) \\ & \rightarrow \text{emit}(\text{``hadoop''}, 1) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Intermediate Buffer & Partitioner Default Partitioner: `HashPartitioner`. With $R=1$ (one reducer): $$ r = (\text{key.hashCode()} \;\&\; \text{MAX\_VALUE}) \mod 1 = 0 $$ → All keys go to **partition 0**. ### Step 5: Sorting & Combining (Local) After sorting and combining locally: $$ (\text{``hadoop''}, [1]),\quad (\text{``hello''}, [1, 1]),\quad (\text{``world''}, [1]) $$ ### Step 6: Shuffle (Network Transfer) The single reducer pulls all map outputs (in this case, no network transfer is needed since both are on the same node; in a cluster, it crosses nodes). ### Step 7: `reduce()` Aggregates The `IntSumReducer` sums the values per key: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{reduce}(\text{``hello''}, [1, 1]) & \rightarrow \text{emit}(\text{``hello''}, 2) \\ \text{reduce}(\text{``world''}, [1]) & \rightarrow \text{emit}(\text{``world''}, 1) \\ \text{reduce}(\text{``hadoop''}, [1]) & \rightarrow \text{emit}(\text{``hadoop''}, 1) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8: Final Output (HDFS) Written to `/output/part-r-00000`: ``` hadoop 1 hello 2 world 1 ``` ## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Map Tasks for a Given Job Let $S$ = total size of input (bytes), $B$ = HDFS block size, $L$ = input split size, and let $S$ contain files of sizes $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n$ where each $s_i \geq B$. $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{file } i} & = \max\!\left(1,\; \left\lceil \frac{s_i}{L} \right\rceil\right) \\ M_{\text{total}} & = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{\text{file } i} \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** Let $L = 128\,\text{MB}$ and input files = $\{256\,\text{MB}, 100\,\text{MB}, 1\,\text{GB}\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} M_1 & = \lceil 256 / 128 \rceil = 2 \\ M_2 & = \lceil 100 / 128 \rceil = 1 \\ M_3 & = \lceil 1024 / 128 \rceil = 8 \\ M_{\text{total}} & = 2 + 1 + 8 = 11 \text{ map tasks} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapper vs. Reducer vs. Combiner vs. Partitioner | Property | Mapper | Reducer | Combiner | Partitioner | |---|---|---|---|---| | **User-defined?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Optional | Optional | | **Runs on** | Map slot | Reduce slot | Same node as map | Same JVM as map | | **Number** | $= M$ (splits) | $= R$ (configured) | $= M$ (1 per map) | $= 1$ per JVM (instantiated) | | **Input type** | $(K1, V1)$ | $(K2, \text{List})$ | $(K2, \text{List})$ | $(K2, V2)$ | | **Output type** | $(K2, V2)$ | $(K3, V3)$ | $(K2, V2)$ | partition id $\in [0, R-1]$ | | **Phases involved** | Map, Spill | Shuffle, Reduce, Output | Spill (local) | Spill (during write) | | **Default class** | `Mapper` (abstract) | `Reducer` (abstract) | None | `HashPartitioner` | | **Optimization goal** | Per-record parsing | Final aggregation | Reduce shuffle volume | Load balance across reducers | | **Idempotency required?** | No | No | **Mandatory** | No | | **Network access?** | None (data-local) | Yes (pulls from mappers) | None | None | ## 3.4 Derivation: Data Flow in a Multi-Node Cluster Consider **3 input blocks** on HDFS, **2 map tasks** for Block-1 (e.g., compressed split), and **2 reducers**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Node A:} & \quad \text{Block-1, Block-2} \\ \text{Node B:} & \quad \text{Block-3} \end{aligned} $$ Suppose Mappers $M_1, M_2$ run on Node A (data-local), and $M_3$ on Node B. After mapping, intermediate data is partitioned and shipped: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Partition } 0 & \rightarrow \text{Reducer } R_0 \\ \text{Partition } 1 & \rightarrow \text{Reducer } R_1 \end{aligned} $$ If $R_0$ is launched on Node A (any node can host any reducer), the fetch is **node-local** for $M_1, M_2$ but **cross-node** for $M_3$. Hadoop uses **HTTP-based fetches** with compression enabled by default (`mapreduce.map.output.compress = true`). > [!NOTE] > **Data Locality Levels** (from best to worst): > 1. **Node-local** (same node) — fastest > 2. **Rack-local** (same rack) — moderate > 3. **Off-rack** (different rack) — slowest, uses core switch bandwidth # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 MapReduce End-to-End Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HDFS Input File
Block 1 Block 2 Block 3] --> B[InputFormat
TextInputFormat] B --> C[InputSplit 1] B --> D[InputSplit 2] B --> E[InputSplit 3] C --> F[Map Task M1
Mapper.map] D --> G[Map Task M2
Mapper.map] E --> H[Map Task M3
Mapper.map] F --> I[Partition Sort Combine
on local node] G --> I H --> I I --> J{Partitioner
HashPartitioner} J --> K[Partition 0] J --> L[Partition 1] K --> M[Reduce Task R0
Shuffle Sort reduce] L --> N[Reduce Task R1
Shuffle Sort reduce] M --> O[OutputFormat
part-r-00000] N --> P[OutputFormat
part-r-00001] O --> Q[HDFS Output Dir] P --> Q ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Job Lifecycle Under YARN ```mermaid flowchart LR Client[Client Program
Job.waitForCompletion] --> Submit[hadoop jar submission] Submit --> RM[ResourceManager] RM --> AM[ApplicationMaster] AM --> NM1[NodeManager 1] AM --> NM2[NodeManager 2] NM1 --> C1[Container Map Task] NM1 --> C2[Container Reduce Task] NM2 --> C3[Container Map Task] NM2 --> C4[Container Reduce Task] C1 -.local data.-> HDFS1[HDFS DataNode 1] C2 -.shuffle.-> C3 C3 -.local data.-> HDFS2[HDFS DataNode 2] C4 --> FinalOut[HDFS Output
part-r-00000 etc] ``` ## 4.3 Job State Machine (KTU High-Yield) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> JobPrep JobPrep --> JobInit: Job.submit JobInit --> JobRunning: AM registered JobRunning --> JobSucceeded: all tasks done JobRunning --> JobFailed: task failure threshold exceeded JobRunning --> JobKilled: client cancels JobSucceeded --> [*] JobFailed --> [*] JobKilled --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Data Flow Inside a Single Map Task (Detailed View) ```mermaid flowchart TD Input[InputSplit
block data] --> Reader[RecordReader
LineRecordReader] Reader --> M[Mapper.map K1 V1] M --> Buf[Circular Buffer
100 MB] Buf --> P[Partitioner] P --> S[QuickSort by partition then key] S --> Comb[Combiner optional local aggregate] Comb --> Spill[Spill to local disk
spill00 spill01] Spill --> Merge[Merge phase single output per map] Merge --> Ready[Awaiting Reducer fetch] ``` ## 4.5 Sequence Diagram: Client → YARN → MapReduce ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Client participant R as ResourceManager participant A as ApplicationMaster participant N as NodeManager participant D as HDFS U->>R: Submit Job JAR + config R->>A: Allocate container launch AM A->>D: Read input split locations A->>N: Request containers for Map tasks N-->>A: Containers allocated A->>N: Launch Map task in container N->>D: Read data node-local N-->>A: Map task complete A->>N: Request containers for Reduce tasks N-->>A: Containers allocated A->>N: Launch Reduce task N->>D: Shuffle fetch map outputs N->>D: Write final output to HDFS A-->>U: Job completion notification ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Model questions following KTU 2024 scheme cognitive levels (Remember / Understand).** ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Explain the **Mapper** and **Reducer** classes in Hadoop MapReduce. State the four type parameters of each class. **Model Answer:** The `Mapper` abstract class converts input key-value pairs into intermediate key-value pairs. Its four type parameters represent the input key, input value, output key, and output value types. $$ \text{map}(K_{IN}, V_{IN}, \text{Context}) \rightarrow \text{List}<(K_{OUT}, V_{OUT})> $$ The `Reducer` class aggregates intermediate values associated with the same key. It receives a key and an iterator over all values for that key, then emits zero or more final output pairs. $$ \text{reduce}(K, \text{Iterable}, \text{Context}) \rightarrow \text{void (writes via Context.write)} $$ **Type-parameter examples for WordCount:** | Class | Type Parameters | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Mapper | `LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable` | `(offset, line) → (word, 1)` | | Reducer | `Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable` | `(word, [1,1,1]) → (word, total)` | --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Differentiate between **Combiner** and **Partitioner** in MapReduce. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Combiner | Partitioner | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Local aggregation to reduce shuffle volume | Decides which reducer receives each key | | **Where it runs** | In the same JVM as the map task | In the same JVM as the map task | | **Operates on** | $(K2, \text{List})$ after sort | $(K2, V2)$ before sort | | **Default** | None (must be set) | `HashPartitioner` | | **Constraint** | Must be **idempotent and associative** (e.g., SUM, MAX) | Must return an integer in $[0, R-1]$ | | **Count** | One per map task | One per JVM (logic is per-record) | | **Example use** | Local sum of word counts before shuffle | Distribute keys evenly across 10 reducers | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **Question A (Choice 1) — Compulsory OR Question B (Choice 2).** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *(a)* Explain the **complete architecture and execution flow of a MapReduce job** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of `JobTracker`, `ApplicationMaster`, `ResourceManager`, and `NodeManager` in the YARN-based execution. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Write the **Mapper and Reducer pseudo-code (Java)** for a Word Count program. Show the complete data flow from input to output, assuming the input is `text1.txt` containing the line *"hadoop mapreduce hadoop yarn hadoop"*. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution for (a) — MapReduce Architecture (7 marks):** The **YARN-based MapReduce architecture** consists of the following components: 1. **Client** — Submits the job (JAR + configuration) to the ResourceManager. 2. **ResourceManager (RM)** — Global arbiter of cluster resources. Allocates the first container for the ApplicationMaster. 3. **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — Per-job orchestrator. Negotiates additional containers with RM and tracks task progress. 4. **NodeManager (NM)** — Per-node agent that launches and monitors containers. 5. **Map Task & Reduce Task** — User logic running inside containers. 6. **HDFS** — Stores input and output data; provides data-locality hints. **Execution Flow:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{RM}: \text{submitJob} \\ \text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{RM} \rightarrow \text{NM}: \text{launch AM container} \\ \text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{AM} \rightarrow \text{HDFS NameNode}: \text{getInputSplits} \\ \text{Step 4:} & \quad \text{AM} \rightarrow \text{RM}: \text{request containers for Map tasks} \\ \text{Step 5:} & \quad \text{AM} \rightarrow \text{NM}: \text{launch Map task in container} \\ \text{Step 6:} & \quad \text{Map task} \rightarrow \text{HDFS DataNode}: \text{read split} \\ \text{Step 7:} & \quad \text{Map task writes intermediate to local FS} \\ \text{Step 8:} & \quad \text{AM} \rightarrow \text{RM}: \text{request containers for Reduce tasks} \\ \text{Step 9:} & \quad \text{Reduce task} \rightarrow \text{Map tasks (HTTP fetch)}: \text{shuffle} \\ \text{Step 10:} & \quad \text{Reduce task writes final output} \rightarrow \text{HDFS} \end{aligned} $$ > **Note:** In **Hadoop 1.x**, `JobTracker` and `TaskTracker` were the legacy equivalents of `ResourceManager + ApplicationMaster` and `NodeManager`. Hadoop 2.x+ moved to YARN for scalability. #### **Model Solution for (b) — Word Count Code (7 marks):** **Mapper pseudo-code:** ```java public class WordCountMapper extends Mapper { private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1); private Text word = new Text(); public void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context) throws IOException, InterruptedException { StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString()); while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) { word.set(itr.nextToken()); context.write(word, one); } } } ``` **Reducer pseudo-code:** ```java public class WordCountReducer extends Reducer { public void reduce(Text key, Iterable values, Context context) throws IOException, InterruptedException { int sum = 0; for (IntWritable val : values) sum += val.get(); context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum)); } } ``` **Data flow for `text1.txt` = *"hadoop mapreduce hadoop yarn hadoop"*:** | Stage | Output | |---|---| | After `map()` | `(hadoop,1), (mapreduce,1), (hadoop,1), (yarn,1), (hadoop,1)` | | After Partition+Sort | `(hadoop,[1,1,1]), (mapreduce,[1]), (yarn,[1])` | | After `reduce()` | `(hadoop,3), (mapreduce,1), (yarn,1)` | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *(a)* Explain the **shuffle and sort phase** in MapReduce. Why is this phase considered the most network-intensive? How does the **Combiner** function help optimize it? *(7 marks)* *(b)* Explain the concept of **Data Locality** in Hadoop MapReduce. Discuss the three levels of locality — node-local, rack-local, and off-rack. Describe how **speculative execution** improves job performance. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution for (a) — Shuffle, Sort & Combiner (7 marks):** **Shuffle and Sort Phase:** The **shuffle** is the process by which intermediate output from all map tasks is transferred to the reducers. The **sort** ensures that all values for the same key arrive at a reducer in sorted order. This phase is **network-intensive** because: 1. Every map output must cross the network (or stay local if a reducer is co-located). 2. With $M$ map tasks and $R$ reducers, the total shuffle volume is: $$ \text{Bytes Shuffled} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \text{Size}(\text{Output of } M_i) $$ 3. If the data is skewed, some reducers fetch disproportionately more data. **Combiner Function:** A Combiner is a mini-reducer that runs **on the same node as the map task**, just before the data is spilled to disk. It performs **local aggregation** to reduce the volume sent to reducers. **Example:** For word count, the map emits: $$ (\text{``hello''}, 1), (\text{``hello''}, 1), (\text{``hello''}, 1) $$ After combiner: $$ (\text{``hello''}, 3) $$ → Reduces 3 entries to 1, cutting shuffle volume. **Constraints:** A combiner must be **idempotent** and **associative**, since it may be applied zero, one, or multiple times during the merge phase. #### **Model Solution for (b) — Data Locality & Speculative Execution (7 marks):** **Data Locality** is Hadoop's strategy of moving **computation to data** rather than data to computation, exploiting HDFS's distributed storage. | Locality Level | Description | Network Hops | Speed | |---|---|---|---| | **Node-local** | Task runs on the same node holding the data | 0 | **Fastest** | | **Rack-local** | Task runs on a different node in the same rack | 1 (rack switch) | Moderate | | **Off-rack** | Task runs on a node in a different rack | 2+ (core switch) | Slowest | **Hadoop's preference order:** `node-local > rack-local > off-rack`. **Speculative Execution:** When a task runs much slower than the average (a *straggler*), Hadoop may launch a **duplicate copy** of that task on a different node. Whichever finishes first wins, and the slower one is killed. $$ \text{Slow Task Time} = \max(T_{\text{straggler}}, T_{\text{speculative}}) $$ Instead of waiting for the slow straggler, the job benefits from the faster one. This is especially useful in heterogeneous clusters where node performance varies. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing `JobTracker` (Hadoop 1.x) with `ResourceManager` (Hadoop 2.x+):** YARN splits responsibilities. Always state the **AM is per-job**, RM is per-cluster. > 2. **Forgetting the four type parameters of `Mapper`/`Reducer`:** Examiners expect all four: ``. > 3. **Saying "Combiner always runs":** It runs *only if set* in `Job.setCombinerClass(...)` and *only if the data is suitable* (idempotent). > 4. **Missing the difference between `OutputCollector` (old API) and `Context.write` (new API):** The new API (`org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce`) is what KTU expects. > 5. **Writing shuffle as "sorting only":** It includes both **fetching over HTTP** AND **merge-sorting**. > 6. **Forgetting the role of `RecordReader`:** It converts a `split` into records — students often skip this and lose 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for MapReduce Programming:** - ⭐ **MapReduce = Map phase + Shuffle & Sort + Reduce phase**, executed inside YARN containers. - ⭐ **Mapper input** = `(K1, V1)` from `RecordReader`; **Mapper output** = intermediate `(K2, V2)`. - ⭐ **Reducer input** = `(K2, Iterable)`; **Reducer output** = final `(K3, V3)` written to HDFS. - ⭐ The **shuffle & sort phase is the most expensive** (network-heavy). - ⭐ The **Partitioner** decides reducer routing using `HashPartitioner` by default. - ⭐ The **Combiner** is an optional local-aggregation optimizer (must be **idempotent** & **associative**). - ⭐ **Data locality hierarchy:** `node-local > rack-local > off-rack`. - ⭐ **Speculative execution** mitigates stragglers by launching duplicate slow tasks. - ⭐ Number of **Map tasks ≈ ⌈Input Size / Split Size⌉**; number of **Reducers = user-set**. - ⭐ **Hadoop 2.x+ uses YARN:** `ResourceManager` (cluster-level) + `ApplicationMaster` (job-level) + `NodeManager` (node-level). - ⭐ **Default block size = 128 MB**; **replication factor = 3** in HDFS. - ⭐ The **new API** lives in `org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce`; avoid the deprecated `mapred` package. - ⭐ **`Context.write(K, V)`** is used (not the legacy `OutputCollector.collect(...)`). - ⭐ A **Writable** is Hadoop's serializable data type (`IntWritable`, `Text`, `LongWritable`, `FloatWritable`). - ⭐ A **WritableComparable** is required for keys (since keys are sorted and partitioned). - ⭐ **Combiner ≠ Reducer:** Combiner runs *per map node*; Reducer runs *per partition* globally. - ⭐ For skewed data, a **custom Partitioner** may be required to balance reducer load.

topics.title: Data Processing with MapReduce: Execution Pipeline
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Processing with MapReduce: Execution Pipeline

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MapReduce Execution Pipeline** is the well-defined sequence of internal operational stages through which a MapReduce job is decomposed, scheduled, executed, and monitored across a distributed Hadoop cluster. It defines the data flow from raw HDFS input blocks through the **Map → Shuffle & Sort → Combine → Reduce → Output** stages, orchestrated by the master–worker architecture (`ResourceManager` ↔ `NodeManager` in YARN / `JobTracker` ↔ `TaskTracker` in Hadoop 1.x).

The execution pipeline is a **data-parallel, fault-tolerant distributed processing framework** that abstracts the complexity of distributed computing into two programmer-defined functions:

$$\text{Map} : (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{List}\langle (k_2, v_2) \rangle$$
$$\text{Reduce} : (k_2, \text{List}\langle v_2 \rangle) \rightarrow \text{List}\langle (k_3, v_3) \rangle$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The execution pipeline is the *spine* of the Hadoop distributed processing model. Questions frequently test the sequence, responsibilities, and data flow at each stage. Memorizing the pipeline order is non-negotiable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sorting Library" Intuition

Imagine a **massive library** with **10 million unsorted books** that must be re-shelved by genre. You cannot do it alone.

- **The Librarian-in-Chief (JobTracker / ResourceManager):** Reads the task list, decides *how many workers to hire*, and *assigns each worker a specific cart of books* (input split).
- **The Worker-Librarians (TaskTrackers / NodeManagers):** Each picks up a cart, **reads a book, and writes a sticky note** with the genre and a short summary → this is the **MAP** stage.
- **The Conveyor Belt (Shuffle & Sort Phase):** All sticky notes of the same genre are grouped onto the same moving belt, sorted alphabetically by genre, and pushed toward a specific sorting station → this is the **SHUFFLE & SORT**.
- **The Senior Cataloger (Reducer):** At each station, all notes for a single genre are combined into a final, consolidated catalogue entry → this is the **REDUCE** stage.
- **The Bookkeeper (Output Writer):** Writes the final catalogues to the library's permanent archive (HDFS).

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner to remember:** *Map = "extract," Shuffle = "group," Reduce = "aggregate."* If you can recite this in the exam, you will earn the first mark of any pipeline question.

## 1.3 Pipeline Stages — A Bird's-Eye View

| Stage | Who Executes | Input | Output | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Split** | Client → `InputFormat` | HDFS file | Logical splits (128 MB) | Yes |
| **Map** | User-defined `Mapper` | $(k_1, v_1)$ | $\text{List}\langle (k_2, v_2) \rangle$ | Yes |
| **Combine** *(optional)* | Mini-reducer on mapper | Local mapper output | Compressed $(k_2, v_2)$ | No |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | Hadoop framework | All mapper outputs | Grouped by key | Yes (implicit) |
| **Reduce** | User-defined `Reducer` | $(k_2, \text{List}\langle v_2 \rangle)$ | $\text{List}\langle (k_3, v_3) \rangle$ | Yes |
| **Output Write** | `OutputFormat` | Final KV pairs | HDFS blocks | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Execution Pipeline (Stage-by-Stage)

### **Stage 1: Job Submission & Initialization**
- The client invokes `Job.submit()` (or `waitForCompletion()`), packaging the job configuration (XML), the compiled JAR, and input/output paths.
- A **YARN ApplicationMaster (AM)** is launched on a container in the cluster. The AM is the *per-job master* — it negotiates resources from the `ResourceManager` and tracks all tasks of *that one job*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key difference between Hadoop 1.x and 2.x+ (YARN):**
> - Hadoop 1.x: `JobTracker` is a **global master** (handles scheduling + monitoring for *all* jobs) → single point of failure.
> - Hadoop 2.x+ (YARN): `ResourceManager` is global, but **each job gets its own `ApplicationMaster`** for scheduling and monitoring. This decouples resource management from job monitoring, enabling massive scalability (clusters of 10,000+ nodes).

### **Stage 2: Input Splitting**
- The `InputFormat` (commonly `TextInputFormat`) computes logical splits.
- Default split size = **HDFS block size (128 MB)** to maximize **data locality**.
- For an $N$-byte file:

$$N_{\text{splits}} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{S_{\text{block}}} \right\rceil$$

where $S_{\text{block}}$ is the HDFS block size.

### **Stage 3: Map Task Execution**
- Each map task processes one split via `RecordReader` (default: `LineRecordReader` → one line per record, key = byte offset, value = line content).
- The user's `map()` is invoked per $(k_1, v_1)$ pair.
- Output is buffered in a **circular in-memory buffer** of size:

$$B_{\text{buf}} = \text{mapreduce.task.io.sort.mb} = 100 \text{ MB (default)}$$

When the buffer reaches a threshold (default **80%**), it spills to local disk.

### **Stage 4: Combiner (Local Mini-Reducer)**
- An **optional, programmer-supplied** function that performs local aggregation *on the mapper's output* *before* the shuffle.
- Reduces the volume of data transferred across the network.
- Must be **idempotent and commutative** (safe to apply repeatedly).

### **Stage 5: Shuffle & Sort — The Heart of the Pipeline**
- **Shuffle:** Each reducer fetches its partition of the map outputs from *all* completed mappers via HTTP.
- **Sort:** Hadoop guarantees that reducer inputs are presented in **ascending key order**, achieved via an external merge sort on the spilled files.
- The number of reducer partitions:

$$N_{\text{partitions}} = N_{\text{reducers}}$$

The partitioner (default: `HashPartitioner`) assigns a key $k$ to reducer $r$ via:

$$r(k) \;=\; \big(\text{hash}(k)\big) \;\bmod\; N_{\text{reducers}}$$

### **Stage 6: Reduce Task Execution**
- The reducer iterates over the sorted, grouped input.
- For each unique key, the framework calls `reduce(key, Iterable<values>)`.
- Output is written to HDFS via `OutputFormat` (default: `TextOutputFormat`).

### **Stage 7: Job Completion & Cleanup**
- AM unregisters from RM, releases the container, and reports status to the client.
- Intermediate data on local disk and HDFS working directory is **deleted**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for MapReduce Calculations** (commonly asked as 3-mark or 7-mark sub-parts)

| # | Concept | Formula | Default / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of input splits | $N_{\text{splits}} = \lceil N / S_{\text{blk}} \rceil$ | $S_{\text{blk}} = 128$ MB | $N$ = file size |
| 2 | Partition assignment | $r(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod R$ | $R$ = #reducers | HashPartitioner default |
| 3 | Map-side sort buffer | $B_{\text{buf}} = \text{mapreduce.task.io.sort.mb}$ | $100$ MB | Triggers spill at threshold |
| 4 | Spill threshold | $B_{\text{spill}} = B_{\text{buf}} \times f$ | $f = 0.80$ | Fraction of buffer |
| 5 | HDFS replication factor | $R_{\text{rep}}$ | $3$ | Configurable in `hdfs-site.xml` |
| 6 | Data-local task ratio | $\eta_{\text{local}} = T_{\text{local}} / T_{\text{total}}$ | Ideal: $1.0$ | Higher = better locality |
| 7 | Job makespan (ideal) | $T_{\text{job}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}}$ | seconds | Sequential critical path |
| 8 | Speculative execution cap | $N_{\text{spec}} \leq N_{\text{tasks}} \times \alpha$ | $\alpha = 0.10$ | Defaults vary by Hadoop version |
| 9 | Combine input size limit | $B_{\text{combine}} \leq B_{\text{buf}}$ | MB | Bounded by sort buffer |
| 10 | Heartbeat interval | $\Delta t_{\text{hb}}$ | $3$ s | TaskTracker → JobTracker |

> **Caution on table symbols:** All absolute-value / modulus operators in the table above are written as `\bmod` or `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search engines** (Google's original use case): index billions of web pages.
- **Log analytics** at scale (e.g., Cloudera, Uber, Facebook).
- **ETL pipelines** in data warehouses (e.g., Hive on MapReduce).
- **Genomics & bioinformatics**: BLAST alignments over petabytes of DNA sequences.
- **Clickstream analysis** in e-commerce recommendation systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Map Tasks from a Dataset

### **Problem Statement**
A HDFS-resident log file is **480 MB** in size. The HDFS block size is configured to **128 MB**, and `mapreduce.input.fileinputformat.split.minsize` = 1 byte. Compute the number of map tasks that will be launched.

### **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Identify the governing formula:**

$$N_{\text{splits}} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{S_{\text{blk}}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$N_{\text{splits}} = \left\lceil \frac{480 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division:**

$$\frac{480}{128} = 3.75$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ceiling operator:**

$$\left\lceil 3.75 \right\rceil = 4$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:** A total of **4 map tasks** will be spawned, with the *last split being partial* (only $480 - 3 \times 128 = 96$ MB).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The last split is *not* padded to 128 MB. Hadoop only creates splits that are ≤ `S_blk`. KTU examiners love this distinction.

### **Variation 1 — Account for HDFS Block Boundaries**
If the file is 480 MB and HDFS has already laid out exactly 4 blocks (each ≈ 120 MB on average, but stored as 128 MB blocks with the last one being partial), the splits **align with HDFS block boundaries** because of the `FileInputFormat` default:

$$\text{Split size} = \max\left(S_{\min},\; \min\left(S_{\max},\; S_{\text{blk}}\right)\right)$$

With $S_{\min}=1$ byte and $S_{\max}=$ `Long.MAX_VALUE`, this simplifies to $S_{\text{blk}}=128$ MB.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hash Partitioner Reducer Assignment

### **Problem Statement**
A job is configured with **$R = 4$ reducers**. The `HashPartitioner` is the default. Given a key $k$ whose `hashCode()` returns **37**, which reducer will process it?

### **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Recall the partitioner formula:**

$$r(k) = \big(\text{hash}(k)\big) \bmod R$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$r(k) = 37 \bmod 4$$

**Step 3 — Perform the modulo operation:**

$$37 = 9 \times 4 + 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 37 \bmod 4 = 1$$

**Step 4 — State the conclusion:** All $(k, v)$ pairs with `hashCode() = 37` are routed to **Reducer #1** (zero-indexed).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Estimate of Job Makespan

### **Problem Statement**
A cluster has **8 mapper slots** and **4 reducer slots**. There are **12 map tasks**, each taking 60 s. The shuffle phase takes 25 s. Each of the 4 reduce tasks takes 80 s. The job has no failures. Compute the ideal makespan.

### **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Compute map-stage wall-clock time:**
Mappers run in parallel across 8 slots. Two waves are required: 8 in parallel, then 4.

$$T_{\text{map}} = 2 \times 60 \text{ s} = 120 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Add shuffle time:**

$$T_{\text{map+shuffle}} = 120 + 25 = 145 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute reduce-stage wall-clock time:**
All 4 reducers run in parallel.

$$T_{\text{reduce}} = 80 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total makespan:**

$$T_{\text{job}} = T_{\text{map+shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}} = 145 + 80 = 225 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:** The end-to-end job takes **225 seconds (3 min 45 s)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** This is the exact structure of a 7-mark derivation question. The examiner expects: formula → substitution → step-by-step arithmetic → boxed final answer.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Combiner, Partitioner, or Custom Components

| Scenario | Recommendation | Justification (with KTU-style rigor) |
|---|---|---|
| Sum / Count / Max operations | **Use Combiner** | Reduces network I/O by $\sim 30{-}50\%$ in word-count workloads. |
| Non-associative / non-commutative (e.g., median) | **Avoid Combiner** | Wrong result; reducer requires the *full* unsorted list. |
| Skewed key distribution | **Custom Partitioner** | HashPartitioner lumps hot keys to one reducer → straggler. Spread them. |
| File is compressed (e.g., `.gz`) | **Splittable?** No for gzip; yes for bzip2, LZO | A non-splittable compressed file = exactly 1 mapper → bottleneck. |
| Small files (< 1 MB each) | **CombineFileInputFormat** | Avoids 1-mapper-per-file anti-pattern. |
| Iterative jobs (ML, PageRank) | **Avoid raw MapReduce** | Use Spark / Giraph; MapReduce re-launches AM per iteration. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End MapReduce Execution Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend:** Each oval is a pipeline stage; arrows show data flow direction. `AM` = ApplicationMaster, `RM` = ResourceManager, `NM` = NodeManager.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Submits Job JAR and Config] --> B[ResourceManager Allocates Container]
    B --> C[ApplicationMaster Bootstraps on Node]
    C --> D[InputFormat Splits HDFS File]
    D --> E1[Mapper 1 on NM 1]
    D --> E2[Mapper 2 on NM 2]
    D --> E3[Mapper 3 on NM 3]
    E1 --> F1[Local Disk Spill Files]
    E2 --> F2[Local Disk Spill Files]
    E3 --> F3[Local Disk Spill Files]
    F1 --> G[Shuffle Phase via HTTP]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Sort by Key Externally]
    H --> I1[Reducer 1]
    H --> I2[Reducer 2]
    I1 --> J1[Write HDFS Block]
    I2 --> J2[Write HDFS Block]
    J1 --> K[ApplicationMaster Reports SUCCESS]
    J2 --> K
    K --> L[ResourceManager Releases Container]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Master–Worker Interaction in YARN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client Program] -->|submitApplication| RM[ResourceManager]
    RM -->|allocate container| AM[ApplicationMaster]
    AM -->|registerApplicationMaster| RM
    AM -->|request containers| RM
    RM -->|allocate containers| AM
    AM -->|launch container| NM1[NodeManager 1]
    AM -->|launch container| NM2[NodeManager 2]
    NM1 -->|heartbeat status| AM
    NM2 -->|heartbeat status| AM
    AM -->|progress and status| Client
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Data Locality Tiers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Map Task Scheduler] --> B{Is Data on Same Node?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Node-Local Read — Fastest]
    B -->|No| D{Is Data on Same Rack?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Rack-Local Read — Medium]
    D -->|No| F[Off-Rack Read — Slowest]
    C --> G[Execute Map Task]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Emit to Local Spill]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Point:** Hadoop 1.x used a *slot-based* model (fixed map/reduce slots per node). YARN introduced a *container-based* model where any container can run any task type. This is a **favourite 3-mark comparison question**.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Failure and Recovery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Reports Failure] --> B{Which Component Failed?}
    B -->|Map Task| C[AM Re-Schedules Map on Another Node]
    B -->|Reduce Task| D[AM Re-Schedules Reduce on Another Node]
    B -->|ApplicationMaster| E[RM Re-Launches AM in New Container]
    B -->|NodeManager| F[RM Marks Node UNHEALTHY]
    B -->|ResourceManager| G[JobTracker HA Standby Takes Over]
    C --> H[Re-Execute from Input Split]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[Job Resumes]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Define the term "Shuffle and Sort" phase in the MapReduce execution pipeline. Why is it considered the most expensive stage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Shuffle and Sort** phase is the framework-managed stage between the mapper and reducer in which (a) each reducer fetches its assigned partitions of output from *all* completed mappers over HTTP, and (b) the framework merges and sorts these records by key using an external merge sort. It is the most expensive stage because:
1. It involves **disk I/O** (spilled files are read back).
2. It involves **network I/O** (cross-node HTTP transfers).
3. It involves **serialization/deserialization** of every key-value pair.
4. It is a **synchronization barrier** — reducers cannot start until *all* mappers finish.

*(1 mark: definition; 2 marks: three of the four reasons above.)*

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` What is the role of the `ApplicationMaster` in the YARN-based MapReduce execution pipeline? How is it different from the `ResourceManager`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `ApplicationMaster` (AM) is a **per-job master process** that:
- Negotiates containers (CPU + memory) from the `ResourceManager`.
- Schedules and monitors all map and reduce tasks of its job.
- Handles task failures and speculation.

The `ResourceManager` (RM) is a **global, cluster-wide daemon** that:
- Allocates resources to *all* running applications.
- Does *not* know about individual map/reduce task status.
- Acts as the ultimate authority for container allocation.

**Key difference:** RM = *cluster-level resource arbiter*; AM = *job-level task scheduler*.

*(1 mark each for AM role, RM role, and clear contrast.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

### **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

> **Question A (14 marks):**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the complete **MapReduce Execution Pipeline** in detail, covering all stages from job submission to job completion. Mention the role of `ResourceManager`, `ApplicationMaster`, and `NodeManager` in YARN. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Consider a HDFS file of size **1.2 GB** with a block size of **128 MB**. The job is configured with **6 reducers** and the `HashPartitioner` is the default. A key has `hashCode() = 1735`. Compute:
>     (i) the number of map tasks,
>     (ii) the reducer number to which the key is routed, and
>     (iii) the ideal makespan, given: each map task = 50 s, shuffle = 30 s, each reduce task = 70 s, with 8 mapper slots. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

The MapReduce execution pipeline consists of the following stages:

**1. Job Submission (1 mark):** The client submits the job via `Job.waitForCompletion()`. The JAR, configuration XML, and input/output paths are uploaded to HDFS.

**2. ApplicationMaster Initialization (1 mark):** The `ResourceManager` allocates a container and launches the `ApplicationMaster` (AM). The AM is the per-job coordinator.

**3. Input Split Computation (1 mark):** The `InputFormat` (e.g., `TextInputFormat`) calculates logical splits aligned with HDFS block boundaries (128 MB default).

**4. Map Task Execution (1 mark):** The AM requests containers from RM; mappers run on `NodeManager`s, ideally on nodes holding the data block (data locality). The user-defined `map()` emits intermediate $(k_2, v_2)$ pairs.

**5. Shuffle and Sort (1 mark):** Each reducer fetches its partition from all mappers via HTTP and merges them in sorted key order.

**6. Reduce Task Execution (1 mark):** For each unique key, the user's `reduce()` is invoked with the grouped values. Output is written back to HDFS.

**7. Job Completion (1 mark):** The AM reports `SUCCESS` to the RM, releases containers, and cleans up intermediate data.

**Neat Diagram:** *(Use the Mermaid diagram from SECTION 4.1 — re-draw or describe stages.)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Number of map tasks (2 marks):**

$$N_{\text{splits}} = \left\lceil \frac{1.2 \text{ GB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1200 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \lceil 9.375 \rceil = 10 \text{ mappers}$$

**(ii) Reducer routing (2 marks):**

$$r(k) = 1735 \bmod 6$$

Compute: $1735 = 289 \times 6 + 1$, so $1735 \bmod 6 = 1$. The key goes to **Reducer #1**.

**(iii) Ideal makespan (3 marks):**
- 10 mappers on 8 slots → 2 waves: $T_{\text{map}} = 2 \times 50 = 100$ s.
- Shuffle: $T_{\text{shuffle}} = 30$ s.
- 6 reducers in parallel: $T_{\text{reduce}} = 70$ s.
- Total: $T_{\text{job}} = 100 + 30 + 70 = \mathbf{200 \text{ s}}$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

### **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`**

> **Question B (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **fault tolerance mechanisms** of the MapReduce execution pipeline. Explain how the framework handles (i) task failure, (ii) ApplicationMaster failure, and (iii) speculative execution. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A cluster has **16 mapper slots**. A job has **20 map tasks** each taking 40 s, **4 reduce tasks** each taking 90 s, and a shuffle phase of 20 s. Calculate the makespan. If 2 map tasks fail and need 50 s for re-execution on a free slot, recompute the makespan. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**(i) Task Failure Handling (3 marks):**
When a map or reduce task fails, the `ApplicationMaster` detects the failure via missed heartbeats and re-schedules the task on a different `NodeManager`. Map tasks are re-executed from scratch (intermediate output is **not** preserved). Reduce tasks can resume from the last completed *shuffle*, as completed map outputs are re-fetched.

**(ii) ApplicationMaster Failure (2 marks):**
If the AM itself crashes, the `ResourceManager` detects the lost heartbeat and spawns a *new* AM in a fresh container. The new AM reconstructs its state by querying the completed `TaskAttempt`s from the `HistoryServer` (or, in YARN, the AM can persist state to HDFS).

**(iii) Speculative Execution (2 marks):**
Hadoop identifies *straggler* tasks (those running significantly slower than the median). It launches **duplicate** attempts of slow tasks on other nodes. Whichever finishes *first* wins; the other is killed. This trades extra resource consumption for reduced job latency.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Base makespan (4 marks):**
- 20 mappers on 16 slots → 2 waves: $2 \times 40 = 80$ s.
- Shuffle: $20$ s.
- 4 reducers in parallel: $90$ s.
- Total: $T = 80 + 20 + 90 = \mathbf{190 \text{ s}}$.

**(ii) With 2 map task failures (3 marks):**
- First wave: 16 mappers in 40 s, of which 2 fail.
- Failures are detected near the end (e.g., at $t = 38$ s by heartbeat).
- Re-execution takes 50 s on free slots. These start at $t = 40$ s, finishing at $t = 90$ s.
- However, the original 4 remaining mappers (the 2nd wave) start at $t = 40$ s and finish at $t = 80$ s.
- **Re-executed mappers dominate**: they finish at $t = 90$ s.
- Add shuffle + reduce: $T = 90 + 20 + 90 = \mathbf{200 \text{ s}}$.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in MapReduce Pipeline Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `JobTracker` (Hadoop 1.x) with `ApplicationMaster` (YARN 2.x+).** Examiners explicitly test this in the new 2024 scheme. Always state "in YARN" or "in Hadoop 1.x" before naming the master.
> 2. **Forgetting that the last HDFS split is partial.** If asked "how many map tasks for a 480 MB file?", students often say $480/128 = 3.75$ → answer is **4**, not 3.
> 3. **Writing the partitioner formula as `hash(k) % R` in code-block form** — KTU wants you to use LaTeX `$r(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod R$`, and 1 mark is reserved for the *correct symbolic form*.
> 4. **Drawing a pipeline diagram but omitting the `Combine` and `Sort` sub-stages.** The examiner's rubric deducts 1 mark if these are missing.
> 5. **Stating "speculative execution handles failures."** It does *not*. Speculative execution is a **latency optimization**, not a fault-tolerance mechanism. Mark-loss of 1 mark guaranteed.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

- ✅ **Pipeline order:** Input → Split → Map → *(Combine)* → Shuffle → Sort → Reduce → Output.
- ✅ **Map input/output types:** $(k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{List}\langle (k_2, v_2) \rangle$.
- ✅ **Reduce input/output types:** $(k_2, \text{List}\langle v_2 \rangle) \rightarrow \text{List}\langle (k_3, v_3) \rangle$.
- ✅ **YARN components:** `ResourceManager` (global) + `ApplicationMaster` (per-job) + `NodeManager` (per-node).
- ✅ **Hadoop 1.x components:** `JobTracker` (global) + `TaskTracker` (per-node).
- ✅ **Default HDFS block size:** 128 MB (was 64 MB pre-Hadoop 2.x).
- ✅ **Default replication factor:** 3.
- ✅ **Partitioner formula:** $r(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod R$.
- ✅ **Combiner** = local mini-reducer, must be commutative + associative.
- ✅ **Speculative execution** = latency optimization, NOT failure handling.
- ✅ **Data locality tiers** (best → worst): node-local → rack-local → off-rack.
- ✅ **Fault tolerance:** failed task re-executed on a *different* node (no in-place retry).
- ✅ **Circular buffer** size for map output: 100 MB; spill threshold: 80%.
- ✅ **JobTracker failure = cluster down** (Hadoop 1.x); AM failure = only that job is re-launched (YARN) → YARN is more robust.
- ✅ **One-liner for examiners:** "The shuffle and sort phase is the most network- and disk-intensive stage of the pipeline."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runtime Coordination and Task Management in MapReduce
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Runtime Coordination and Task Management in MapReduce

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — RUNTIME COORDINATION AND TASK MANAGEMENT IN MAPREDUCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Runtime Coordination and Task Management in MapReduce** refers to the set of distributed mechanisms, scheduling policies, and resource negotiation protocols that govern the lifecycle, placement, execution, monitoring, and fault-tolerance of parallel *Map* and *Reduce* tasks across a commodity cluster. In the classical (Hadoop 1.x) architecture, this duty is centralised in the **JobTracker** and distributed across **TaskTrackers**. In the modern (Hadoop 2.x+) **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** architecture, it is decoupled into a **ResourceManager (RM)**, per-application **ApplicationMasters (AM)**, and per-node **NodeManagers (NM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Verbatim Emphasis):** *“Familiarize the MapReduce execution model, runtime coordination (JobTracker / TaskTracker / YARN), task scheduling, data locality, speculative execution, and failure recovery.”*

In operational terms, the system must satisfy four guarantees simultaneously:
1. **Liveness** — all submitted tasks eventually finish.
2. **Resource Fairness** — multi-tenant jobs share the cluster predictably.
3. **Fault Tolerance** — worker crashes and slow tasks do not stall the job.
4. **Data Locality** — computation is pushed toward data, not the reverse.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — *The Airport Control Tower*

Imagine a busy international airport (the **cluster**) handling hundreds of flights (the **tasks**):

| Airport Element | MapReduce Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Control Tower | JobTracker (v1) / ResourceManager (v2) |
| Runways & Apron | TaskTracker (v1) / NodeManager (v2) |
| Air Traffic Controller | Scheduler (FIFO / Fair / Capacity) |
| Aircraft on stand-by | Speculative / Backup Tasks |
| Emergency Re-routing Protocol | Failure & Recovery Manager |
| Flight Plan | Job Configuration (`JobConf`) |

The **tower** (coordinator) never flies a plane. It only allocates a *slot* (runway) and a *time window* to each pilot (task). If a plane stalls, the tower launches a **backup aircraft (speculative task)** to avoid delaying the schedule. This separation of *“who decides”* (coordinator) from *“who executes”* (worker) is the essence of runtime coordination.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> - **Job** — the full unit of work submitted by the client.
> - **Task** — an individual Map or Reduce instance running on one node.
> - **Slot** *(Hadoop 1)* — a static execution capacity on a TaskTracker.
> - **Container** *(Hadoop 2 / YARN)* — a dynamic bundle of CPU + RAM on a NodeManager.
> - **Speculative Execution** — launching a duplicate of a slow task on a faster node to bound latency.
> - **Heartbeat** — a periodic RPC (default 3 s) used for liveness and slot/container offers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Evolution: MapReduce v1 → YARN

### 2.1.1 Hadoop 1.x — The Monolithic Master

In the original Google MapReduce paper (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004) and its Hadoop open-source counterpart, a single **JobTracker** owned *all* responsibilities:

1. **Job Scheduling** — FIFO by default.
2. **Task Assignment** — Map tasks first, then Reduce tasks.
3. **Progress Monitoring** — via periodic heartbeats from TaskTrackers.
4. **Speculative Execution** — slow task detection and re-launch.
5. **Fault Recovery** — re-execution on TaskTracker failure.

Each **TaskTracker** ran a fixed number of *map slots* and *reduce slots* (`mapreduce.tasktracker.map.tasks.maximum`, default 2 each), reporting back to the JobTracker every 3 seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scalability Ceiling:** Because all decisions passed through one JVM, Hadoop 1 supported only up to **~4 000 nodes** and **~40 000 tasks**. The single JobTracker became a hot-spot and SPOF (Single Point of Failure).

### 2.1.2 Hadoop 2.x / YARN — Decoupled Resource & Application Logic

**YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** introduced a two-level hierarchy:

- **ResourceManager (RM)** — cluster-wide authority that arbitrates *containers* and runs the pluggable **Scheduler** (Capacity, Fair, FIFO).
- **NodeManager (NM)** — per-node agent that supervises containers, enforces CPU/RAM limits, and emits heartbeats.
- **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — *one per job*, negotiaties resources with the RM and coordinates all map/reduce tasks on its own.
- **Container** — flexible allocation unit = N CPU cores + M MB RAM.

This decoupling means a single cluster can host **MapReduce jobs, Spark jobs, Tez DAGs, and Storm topologies simultaneously** — a feature known as **multi-tenancy** or **workload diversity**.

### 2.2 The Task Lifecycle (Six-State Machine)

Every map/reduce task transitions through a deterministic state machine:

| # | State | Triggered By | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `UNASSIGNED` | Scheduler has not yet chosen a node | Awaits placement |
| 2 | `ASSIGNED` | Scheduler picked a node (slot/container offered) | Sent to worker |
| 3 | `RUNNING` | Worker starts JVM child | Executes user code |
| 4 | `COMMIT_PENDING` | Task finished, releasing resources | Final commit |
| 5 | `SUCCEEDED` | Two consecutive heartbeats confirm completion | Marked complete |
| 6 | `FAILED` / `KILLED` | Exception or timeout | Reported to AM / RM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Speculative tasks** are launched in a `RUNNING` state only if the original task’s progress lag exceeds the threshold $T_{spec} = 1.0$ minute after the first attempt, OR progress is more than **20 %** behind the average of its peers (configurable).

## 2.3 Scheduling Policies

| Policy | Class | Best Fit | Decision Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FIFO** | Single-queue | Small labs, dev | Arrival time |
| **Capacity Scheduler** | Hierarchical queues | Multi-tenant prod (Hadoop default) | Guaranteed share + elasticity |
| **Fair Scheduler** | Pool-based, max-min fairness | Shared research clusters | Dominant share fairness |
| **Delay Scheduling** | Plug-in to Fair | Data-locality-sensitive jobs | Wait $\tau$ seconds for a local container |

### 2.3.1 Dominant-Share Fairness (Math)

For a job consuming $\vec{r}$ resources, its **dominant share** is:

$$d_i \;=\; \max_{r}\,\left\{\frac{r_i}{R_r}\right\}$$

where $R_r$ is the total cluster resource of type $r$. The scheduler allocates to the job with the *smallest* $d_i$ — a max-min fair allocation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Sheet

| # | Metric | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of Map Tasks** | $\vert M \vert = \lceil \text{Total Input Size} \oslash \text{HDFS Block Size} \rceil$ | integer | Block-bound |
| 2 | **Number of Reduce Tasks** | $\vert R \vert = 0.95 \cdot N_{nodes} \cdot r_{slot}$ | integer | Heuristic; $r_{slot} \in [1,2]$ |
| 3 | **Speculative Lag Threshold** | $L = \dfrac{\bar{p} - p_{i}}{\bar{p}} \ge 0.20$ | ratio | Default in `mapred-site.xml` |
| 4 | **Heartbeat Interval** | $T_{hb} = 3$ s | seconds | Lower ⇒ higher control-plane load |
| 5 | **Dominant Share** | $d_i = \max_r\!\left(\dfrac{r_i}{R_r}\right)$ | dimensionless | Used by Fair Scheduler |
| 6 | **Slow-start Fraction** | $f_{ss} = \dfrac{\text{completed maps}}{\text{total maps}}$ | ratio | Default 0.05; gates reduce launch |
| 7 | **Shuffle Parallelism** | $P_{shuf} = \vert R \vert \cdot \dfrac{\text{map.output.threads}}{1}$ | threads | Typically 5–10 |
| 8 | **Theoretical Mapper Speed-up** | $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}} \le \dfrac{\vert M \vert}{1 + \alpha \cdot (\vert M \vert - 1)}$ | ratio | $\alpha$ = overhead fraction |
| 9 | **Slot Occupancy (v1)** | $U = \dfrac{\text{active tasks}}{\text{map + reduce slots}}$ | ratio | Aim $U \approx 1$ |
| 10 | **Container Limit (v2)** | $C_{max} = \lfloor \dfrac{RAM_{node} - \text{reserved}}{heap_{cont}} \rfloor$ | containers | NM-enforced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse** *slot* (Hadoop 1, static, role-locked) with *container* (Hadoop 2, dynamic, role-agnostic). Examiners love this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capacity planning:** Formula 2 gives a quick first-pass reducer count.
- **SLAs in production Hadoop:** Formula 3 caps the *tail-latency* contribution of stragglers.
- **Multi-tenant cluster governance:** Dominant-share fairness (Eq. 5) is the mathematical heart of every Fair Scheduler tuning guide in the industry (LinkedIn, Yahoo, Facebook engineering blogs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Estimating the Number of Mappers

### 3.1.1 Statement

For a MapReduce job whose input is stored as $B$-byte HDFS blocks totalling $S$ bytes, derive the *expected* number of map tasks.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Files in HDFS are pre-split into fixed-size blocks.**  
When a client writes a file, the NameNode records the block locations. The default block size $B$ in HDFS is **128 MB** (Hadoop 2+) and is configured via `dfs.blocksize`.

**Step 2 — A *new* map task is spawned for every block that must be processed.**  
This is because the `InputFormat` (typically `TextInputFormat`) treats each block as one *split*, and `InputSplit.getLength() \le B$.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of total size to block size.**  
The number of *complete* blocks in $S$ bytes is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{complete} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{S}{B} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
&= \text{number of full blocks.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for a *partial* last block.**  
If $S$ is not an exact multiple of $B$, one additional map task processes the remaining bytes:

$$
N_{partial} = 
\begin{cases}
1, & S \bmod B \neq 0 \\[2pt]
0, & S \bmod B = 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine both contributions.**  
Total number of map tasks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert M \vert &= N_{complete} + N_{partial} \\[4pt]
&= \left\lfloor \dfrac{S}{B} \right\rfloor + \left(1 \,\text{if}\, S \bmod B \neq 0 \,\text{else}\, 0\right) \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{S}{B} \right\rceil
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Worked numeric example.**  
For $S = 1\,024\ \text{MB}$ and $B = 128\ \text{MB}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{complete} &= \lfloor 1024 / 128 \rfloor = \lfloor 8 \rfloor = 8 \\[4pt]
N_{partial} &= 1024 \bmod 128 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0 \\[4pt]
\therefore\ \vert M \vert &= 8 + 0 = 8\ \text{map tasks.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation B — Capacity-Scheduler Queue Resource Guarantee

### 3.2.1 Statement

Show how the *absolute capacity* $C_q$ (in % of cluster CPU) is converted into a guaranteed *container count* for a queue.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Total cluster CPU.**  
Let the cluster have $K$ nodes, each with $\Phi$ cores, hence:

$$
\text{CPU}_{\text{total}} = K \cdot \Phi \quad \text{(cores)}
$$

**Step 2 — Cluster RAM available for YARN.**  
Let each node expose $\Gamma$ MB to YARN (`yarn.nodemanager.resource.memory-mb`):

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{total}} = K \cdot \Gamma \quad \text{(MB)}
$$

**Step 3 — Queue CPU share from capacity percentage.**  
If queue $q$ has a configured capacity fraction $\rho_q \in (0,1]$ (e.g., 0.40 for 40 %):

$$
\text{CPU}_{q} = \rho_q \cdot \text{CPU}_{\text{total}}
$$

**Step 4 — Container footprint chosen by the cluster admin.**  
A standard container is provisioned with $\phi_c$ cores and $\gamma_c$ MB RAM. We pick the *bottleneck*:

$$
N_{\text{by\,CPU}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{CPU}_q}{\phi_c} \right\rfloor, \qquad
N_{\text{by\,RAM}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{RAM}_q}{\gamma_c} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 5 — Effective container guarantee.**

$$
C_{q} = \min(N_{\text{by\,CPU}},\ N_{\text{by\,RAM}})
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical instance.**  
$K = 10$ nodes, $\Phi = 16$ cores, $\Gamma = 64$ GB, $\rho_q = 0.50$, $\phi_c = 1$ core, $\gamma_c = 4$ GB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPU}_{q} &= 0.5 \cdot 10 \cdot 16 = 80\ \text{cores} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{by\,CPU}} &= \lfloor 80 / 1 \rfloor = 80 \\[4pt]
\text{RAM}_{q} &= 0.5 \cdot 10 \cdot 64 = 320\ \text{GB} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{by\,RAM}} &= \lfloor 320 / 4 \rfloor = 80 \\[4pt]
C_q &= \min(80,\ 80) = \mathbf{80\ containers.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
