> The Capacity Scheduler allows **elasticity** — a queue can exceed its capacity if cluster resources are free, but on contention it is rescaled back to $\rho_q$. The maximum elasticity is bounded by the `maximum-capacity` parameter $\rho_q^{max}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Scheduler

| Criterion | FIFO | Capacity | Fair | Delay |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Latency for small jobs | ✗ (starved) | ✓ (separate queue) | ✓ (immediate start) | ✓ |
| Cluster utilisation | High | High | Medium | High |
| Multi-tenant fairness | ✗ | ✓ (guaranteed shares) | ✓ (max-min) | ✓ |
| Data-locality support | Weak | Weak | Optional plug-in | **Strong** |
| Production deployments | Hive ad-hoc | YARN default | CDH / Cloudera | LinkedIn, Facebook |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 YARN Runtime Coordination — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client["CLIENT"] --> submit["Submit Job to RM"]
    submit --> rm["RESOURCE MANAGER"]
    rm --> scheduler["SCHEDULER FIFO Capacity Fair"]
    rm --> appsmgr["APPLICATIONS MANAGER"]

    appsmgr --> amBoot["BOOT ApplicationMaster"]
    amBoot --> am["APPLICATION MASTER"]
    am --> rm
    am --> req1["Request Container 1"]
    am --> req2["Request Container 2"]
    rm --> alloc1["Allocate Container 1"]
    rm --> alloc2["Allocate Container 2"]

    alloc1 --> nmA["NODE MANAGER A"]
    alloc2 --> nmB["NODE MANAGER B"]

    nmA --> mapT1["MAP TASK 1"]
    nmA --> redT1["REDUCE TASK 1"]
    nmB --> mapT2["MAP TASK 2"]
    nmB --> redT2["REDUCE TASK 2"]

    mapT1 --> hb1["HEARTBEAT 3 sec"]
    redT1 --> hb1
    mapT2 --> hb2["HEARTBEAT 3 sec"]
    redT2 --> hb2
    hb1 --> am
    hb2 --> am
    hb1 --> nmA
    hb2 --> nmB

    am --> spec["SPECULATIVE LAUNCH if lag 20pct"]
    spec --> nmA
    spec --> nmB

    mapT1 --> shuffle["SHUFFLE AND SORT"]
    mapT2 --> shuffle
    shuffle --> redT1
    shuffle --> redT2

    nmA --> fail1["NODE FAILURE"]
    fail1 --> rm
    rm --> reroute["RELAUNCH on healthy NM"]
    reroute --> nmB
```

## 4.2 Task Lifecycle — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UNASSIGNED
    UNASSIGNED --> ASSIGNED : Scheduler picks node
    ASSIGNED --> RUNNING : Worker JVM child started
    RUNNING --> COMMIT_PENDING : User code returns
    COMMIT_PENDING --> SUCCEEDED : Two heartbeats confirm
    RUNNING --> FAILED : Exception or timeout
    RUNNING --> KILLED : Speculative loser or preemption
    RUNNING --> RUNNING : Speculative duplicate launched
    SUCCEEDED --> [*]
    FAILED --> UNASSIGNED : Retry budget remaining
    FAILED --> [*] : Max attempts exceeded
    KILLED --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Failure & Recovery — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["HEARTBEAT MISSED 3 cycles"] --> b["Mark Node LOST"]
    b --> c["All RUNNING tasks of that node?"]
    c -- yes --> d["Report to ApplicationMaster"]
    d --> e["Reschedule with attempt counter plus 1"]
    e --> f{"Attempts less than 4?"}
    f -- yes --> g["Place on healthy NM with data locality"]
    f -- no --> h["Job FAILED"]
    c -- no --> i["Continue normal flow"]
```

## 4.4 Speculative Execution — Duplication Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Task t1 running on Node N1"] --> prog["Progress p1 reported"]
    start --> peer["Peer average progress p_avg computed"]
    prog --> test1{"p_avg minus p1 greater than 20pct?"}
    peer --> test1
    test1 -- no --> cont["Continue original"]
    test1 -- yes --> test2{"After 1 min since start?"}
    test2 -- no --> cont
    test2 -- yes --> launch["Launch duplicate t1 dash on faster node"]
    launch --> race["First to commit wins"]
    race --> kill["Kill the slower instance via KILLED state"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*
**Differentiate between the JobTracker/TaskTracker model of Hadoop 1 and the YARN model of Hadoop 2 with respect to runtime coordination. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- In **Hadoop 1**, runtime coordination is **centralised** in a single **JobTracker** that performs scheduling, monitoring, and failure handling. Each **TaskTracker** runs a fixed number of *map* and *reduce slots*. (1 Mark)
- In **Hadoop 2 (YARN)**, coordination is **decoupled** into a global **ResourceManager (RM)** for cluster-wide resource arbitration and a per-job **ApplicationMaster (AM)** that negotiates with the RM and supervises its own tasks. Per-node **NodeManagers** manage dynamic *containers*. (1 Mark)
- This separation overcomes the **scalability bottleneck (~4 000 nodes)** and the **single point of failure** of Hadoop 1, and enables multi-tenant, multi-framework clusters (MapReduce, Spark, Tez). (1 Mark)

### Q2. *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]*
**What is speculative execution in MapReduce? Under what conditions is a speculative copy launched? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Speculative execution** is the runtime mechanism that launches a *duplicate* of a slow-running task on a different node to bound job latency caused by *stragglers* (slow workers, disk hot-spots, GC pauses). (1 Mark)
- The duplicate is launched if (a) the task has been running for more than **1 minute**, **and** (b) its progress lags the average of its peers by more than **20 %** (controlled by `mapreduce.map.speculative` and `mapreduce.reduce.speculative`). (1 Mark)
- Whichever copy finishes first **commits**; the slower instance is moved to the `KILLED` state and its output is discarded. (1 Mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A *(14 Marks)* — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]*

**(a) Describe the architecture of YARN with a neat block diagram and explain the role of each component in runtime coordination. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **ResourceManager (RM)** — The cluster-wide master that arbitrates resources. It hosts the pluggable **Scheduler** (Capacity / Fair / FIFO) and the **ApplicationsManager (AsM)** that launches AMs. (2 Marks)
2. **NodeManager (NM)** — Per-node agent. Manages *containers*, enforces CPU/RAM limits, monitors health, and emits heartbeats to the RM. (1 Mark)
3. **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — One per job. Negotiates resources with the RM, requests *containers* for map/reduce tasks, monitors their progress, and handles task-level retries. (2 Marks)
4. **Container** — A runtime allocation of CPU cores + RAM on a node; a *logical* JVM sandbox that hosts one task. (1 Mark)
5. **Scheduler** — Decides *which* job gets *how much* and *where* using queue capacities, dominant-share fairness, and delay-scheduling extensions for data locality. (1 Mark)

*(Refer to the Mermaid functional architecture in Section 4.1 for the labelled block diagram — examiners award 1 bonus mark for the diagram if it is well-labelled and correct.)*

**(b) Explain the six-state task lifecycle used in MapReduce and discuss how failures are detected and recovered. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| State | Description | Mark |
|---|---|---|
| `UNASSIGNED` | Task created, awaiting placement by the AM. | 0.5 |
| `ASSIGNED` | AM selected a node and dispatched the task. | 0.5 |
| `RUNNING` | Worker JVM child is executing user code. | 0.5 |
| `COMMIT_PENDING` | Task finished; final commit phase. | 0.5 |
| `SUCCEEDED` | Confirmed by two consecutive heartbeats. | 0.5 |
| `FAILED` / `KILLED` | Failure, timeout, speculative loss, or preemption. | 0.5 |

**Failure Detection & Recovery (4 Marks):**
1. **Node failure** — RM misses $\geq 3$ heartbeats (default 3 s × 3 = 9 s), marks the NM as `LOST`. (1 Mark)
2. The AM is notified of all `RUNNING` tasks on the lost node and re-schedules them on healthy NMs, with `attempts` counter incremented. (1 Mark)
3. If `attempts > mapreduce.map.maxattempts` (default 4), the **task** is marked permanently failed; if too many tasks of a job fail, the **job** is marked `FAILED`. (1 Mark)
4. **Speculative re-execution** bounds the *straggler* penalty by launching a duplicate; the first to finish wins, the loser is `KILLED`. (1 Mark)

---

### QUESTION B *(14 Marks)* — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a) Compare FIFO, Capacity, and Fair schedulers in YARN. Which is the default and why? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | FIFO | Capacity | Fair |
|---|---|---|---|
| Queue model | Single | Hierarchical | Pools |
| Multi-tenant isolation | None | Strong (guaranteed) | Medium (weighted) |
| Latency for small jobs | Poor | Good (dedicated queue) | Good (immediate start) |
| Cluster utilisation | High | High | Medium |
| Preemption | No | No (by default) | Yes |
| **Default in YARN** | — | **Yes** | — |

**Reason Capacity is default (2 Marks):** It offers *guaranteed* cluster shares to tenants (production workloads are protected) while still allowing *elasticity* (queues can use idle capacity) — a balance between predictability and efficiency. (2 Marks)

**Differences (4 Marks):**
- FIFO is the simplest but suffers starvation. (1 Mark)
- Capacity uses *hierarchical* queues with `capacity` and `maximum-capacity`. (1 Mark)
- Fair uses *max-min* dominant-share fairness; supports preemption. (1 Mark)
- Delay-scheduling is a plug-in to Fair that waits a few seconds for a local container, improving data locality. (1 Mark)

**(b) A 1 TB input file is stored on HDFS with a 128 MB block size. The job is configured with 1 GB containers and 8 reducers per node on a 10-node cluster. Estimate the number of map tasks and the theoretical container count. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Map tasks — using the derivation in §3.1:** (3 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 1\ \text{TB} = 1\,048\,576\ \text{MB} \\
B &= 128\ \text{MB} \\
\vert M \vert &= \left\lceil \dfrac{1\,048\,576}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 8192 \right\rceil = \mathbf{8\,192\ \text{map tasks}.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Reducer containers:** (2 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert R \vert &= 0.95 \cdot N_{nodes} \cdot r_{slot} \\
&= 0.95 \cdot 10 \cdot 8 = 76 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{76\ \text{reduce tasks.}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total map + reduce containers at peak:** (2 Marks)

$$
\text{Total} = 8\,192\ (\text{maps}) + 76\ (\text{reduces}) = \mathbf{8\,268\ \text{containers.}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (Examiner’s Warning):**
> 1. **1 TB ≠ 1 000 GB** — use $1\ \text{TB} = 1\,048\,576\ \text{MB}$ (binary), not $1\,000\,000\ \text{MB}$. (–1 Mark)
> 2. The 0.95 factor in the reducer heuristic is **often forgotten** by students. (–1 Mark)
> 3. Confusing *slots* (Hadoop 1, static) with *containers* (Hadoop 2, dynamic) in a YARN question is an instant **–2 Mark** penalty.
> 4. **Speculative execution** is *not* a debugging tool — it is a *latency-bounding* mechanism; writing otherwise costs a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ MapReduce **runtime coordination** = scheduling + placement + monitoring + recovery.
- ✅ Hadoop 1 = **JobTracker (master) + TaskTracker (slave)** — single master, slot-based.
- ✅ Hadoop 2 = **YARN**: **ResourceManager + NodeManager + ApplicationMaster** + **containers**.
- ✅ A **Container** = (CPU cores, RAM) bundle; dynamic, role-agnostic.
- ✅ **Heartbeat interval** = 3 seconds (default). Missing 3 heartbeats ⇒ node declared `LOST`.
- ✅ **Speculative execution** triggers after **1 minute** *and* **>20 %** peer-lag.
- ✅ **Max attempts** = 4 (configurable). Exceeding it fails the task.
- ✅ **Number of mappers** = $\lceil S \oslash B \rceil$ where $B$ = HDFS block size (default 128 MB).
- ✅ **Number of reducers** = $0.95 \cdot N_{nodes} \cdot r_{slot}$ (heuristic).
- ✅ **Capacity Scheduler** is the YARN default; supports hierarchical queues & elasticity.
- ✅ **Fair Scheduler** uses *dominant-share* fairness; supports preemption & delay scheduling.
- ✅ **Data locality** levels: node-local > rack-local > off-rack (penalty grows).
- ✅ **Slow-start** fraction default = 0.05 (5 % of maps done before reducers can launch).
- ✅ *Slots are static & role-locked*; *containers are dynamic & role-agnostic* — examiners test this distinction.
- ✅ Task states: `UNASSIGNED → ASSIGNED → RUNNING → COMMIT_PENDING → SUCCEEDED` (or `FAILED` / `KILLED`).
- ✅ YARN enables **multi-tenancy** — MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Flink can coexist on one cluster.
- ✅ JobTracker of Hadoop 1 was a **Single Point of Failure** — YARN removes it via RM HA (active/standby).
- ✅ The **AM** is the *only* job-specific component; everything else (RM, NM) is shared.
- ✅ Shuffle & sort phase = bridge between map and reduce; **dominant cost** in many jobs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing MapReduce Implementations: Using MapReduce as a Framework for Parallel Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Designing MapReduce Implementations: Using MapReduce as a Framework for Parallel Processing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **MapReduce** is a programming model and an associated software framework introduced by Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004) for processing and generating large-scale datasets in parallel on commodity clusters. It abstracts distributed parallel computation into two user-defined functions — **`map`** and **`reduce`** — while the framework transparently handles data partitioning, task scheduling, fault tolerance, and inter-process communication. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **MapReduce as a Framework for Parallel Processing** means: - The programmer **only** writes the `map(key, value)` and `reduce(key, list[value])` functions. - The **framework** (Hadoop/YARN) automatically **parallelises** the execution across hundreds or thousands of nodes, **partitions** the input, **groups** intermediate data, and **reassembles** the output. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The design focus is on **how to think in MapReduce** — i.e., how to express any parallelisable problem in terms of `(key, value)` pairs so that the framework can distribute the work. This is the foundational skill for solving all subsequent big data problems (sorting, joins, indexing, graph processing, ML). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Indexing Factory Imagine a librarian who is given **10 million books** and asked to count how many times each **distinct word** appears across all of them. Doing this on a single machine would take days. Instead, the librarian designs a **factory pipeline**: | Stage | Analogy | MapReduce Counterpart | |-------|---------|------------------------| | **Splitting** | Divide the 10M books into 1,000 piles of 10,000 each | **Input Splitting** by the `InputFormat` | | **Map** | Hire 1,000 clerks; each reads one pile and writes a slip `(word → 1)` for every occurrence | `map()` emits `(word, 1)` | | **Shuffle & Sort** | A conveyor belt groups all slips for the same word into a single basket | Framework's `Partitioner` + `Shuffle` phase | | **Reduce** | For each basket, one accountant sums the counts | `reduce(word, list[1,1,1,...])` sums them | | **Output** | A master index book | HDFS output files | The librarian (programmer) **never** tells the clerks how to communicate, how to recover from a sneeze (node failure), or how to balance the conveyor belt. The **factory manager** (the MapReduce framework) handles all of that. This is the essence of **MapReduce as a parallel processing framework**. > [!TIP] > The two powerful design principles that fall out of this analogy are **(a) Data Locality** — the clerks work on the books stored at their desk (computation moves to data), and **(b) Fault Tolerance** — if a clerk falls sick, the manager reassigns the pile to another clerk without restarting the whole factory. ### 1.3 Why MapReduce Qualifies as a "Framework" A **library** is code you *call*. A **framework** is code that *calls you* (Inversion of Control). In MapReduce: - You submit `map()` and `reduce()` implementations. - The framework's **JobTracker / ResourceManager** decides **when**, **where**, and **how many** tasks to launch. - The framework invokes your code at the right moment with the right data chunk. This inversion is what makes MapReduce a *true* parallel processing framework rather than just a parallel library. ### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics to Remember | Metric | Typical Value (Hadoop 3.x) | Meaning | |--------|----------------------------|---------| | **Block Size** | **128 MB** (default HDFS block) | Unit of input split | | **Mapper Count** | ≈ (Input size) / 128 MB | One per split by default | | **Reducer Count** | **0.95 to 1.75 × (no. of nodes × containers_per_node)** | Tunable parameter | | **Data Locality** | < 1 ms (same node), < 5 ms (same rack) | Latency to move compute to data | | **Speculative Execution** | Enabled by default | Runs slow tasks a second time on another node | > [!IMPORTANT] > All the **bold** values above are **KTU-favourite numbers**. Examiners love to test them in 3-mark direct questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Execution Phases of a MapReduce Job A MapReduce job is executed in **three principal phases**, each managed by the framework: ### Phase 1 — Map Phase (Parallel & Distributed) - The input file is split into **InputSplits** (typically HDFS block size = **128 MB**). - For each split, the framework launches a **Mapper task** on a node that ideally **holds the block locally** (data locality). - The user-defined `map(k1, v1)` processes one record at a time and emits a list of intermediate `(k2, v2)` pairs. - Output is buffered in memory and periodically **spilled** to local disk, partitioned by the `Partitioner`. ### Phase 2 — Shuffle & Sort Phase (Framework-Controlled) This is the **bridge** between Map and Reduce and is the most **network-intensive** part of a MapReduce job. - **Partitioning**: The `Partitioner` (default = `HashPartitioner`) assigns each `(k2, v2)` to a reducer number `r` such that: $$r = \text{hash}(k2) \bmod R$$ where $R$ is the total number of reducers. - **Shuffling**: The framework **transfers** the partitioned data from mapper nodes to reducer nodes over the network (HTTP). - **Merging & Sorting**: Each reducer **merges** the incoming sorted runs from all mappers and **groups** all values belonging to the same key together. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Shuffle phase is **not** user-defined. Optimising it (via combiners, custom partitioners, compression) is the **single biggest performance lever** in MapReduce design. ### Phase 3 — Reduce Phase (Parallel & Distributed) - For each unique intermediate key, the framework invokes `reduce(k2, list[v2])` exactly **once**. - The output is written back to HDFS, typically as **one file per reducer**. ## 2.2 Design Steps for a MapReduce Algorithm To "think in MapReduce," follow this **4-step framework**: 1. **Identify the parallelism granularity** — what is the smallest unit of work? (a line, a document, a record, a block) 2. **Define the key** — what do you want to group by? This decides your reducer logic. 3. **Write the `map` function** — emit `(key, value)` for every record of interest. 4. **Write the `reduce` function** — aggregate the values for each key. ## 2.3 Auxiliary Components in the Framework | Component | Purpose | User-Written? | |-----------|---------|---------------| | **InputFormat** | Defines how input is read and split (e.g., `TextInputFormat`, `KeyValueTextInputFormat`) | Yes (optional) | | **RecordReader** | Converts a split into `(k, v)` records | Optional | | **Mapper** | Processes each record | **Yes (required)** | | **Combiner** | Local pre-aggregation on the mapper node to reduce shuffle volume | Yes (optional, but recommended) | | **Partitioner** | Decides which reducer receives a given key | Yes (optional) | | **Reducer** | Aggregates per-key values | **Yes (required)** | | **OutputFormat** | Writes final output to HDFS (e.g., `TextOutputFormat`, `SequenceFileOutputFormat`) | Yes (optional) | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Rule | Description | Used In | |---|----------------|-------------|---------| | 1 | $R = \text{hash}(k) \bmod r$ | Default partitioning rule (HashPartitioner) | Shuffle phase | | 2 | $\text{Total Mappers} = \lceil \text{Input Size} / \text{Block Size} \rceil$ | Mappers = number of input splits | Job sizing | | 3 | $N_{\text{reducers}} \approx 0.95 \cdot N_{\text{nodes}} \cdot C_{\text{node}}$ | Hadoop-recommended reducer count | Tuning | | 4 | $\text{Speedup} = T_1 / T_p \le N_{\text{nodes}}$ | Amdahl's law bound on MapReduce | Performance | | 5 | $\text{Combiner used} \iff \text{Reduce op is commutative \& associative}$ | Validity check for combiners | Optimisation | | 6 | $\text{Intermediate data} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \text{OutputSize}(\text{mapper}_i)$ | Shuffle volume (before compression) | Cost analysis | | 7 | $\text{Shuffle Cost} \propto \sum_{j=1}^{R} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \text{Data}(m_i \to r_j)$ | Network bytes moved | Optimisation | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Rule for Combiners:** A combiner can only be used if the reduce operation satisfies **Commutativity** ($a + b = b + a$) **AND** **Associativity** ($(a + b) + c = a + (b + c)$). Sum, Max, Min, Union are valid; Average, Median are **not** directly valid. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Log Analytics at Yahoo / Facebook**: Petabytes of clickstream data processed nightly. - **Search Engine Indexing**: Inverted index construction (Google's original use case). - **ETL Pipelines**: Transforming raw transactional data into warehouse-ready tables. - **Genomics & Bioinformatics**: Aligning short-read sequences across thousands of nodes. - **Recommendation Engines**: Iterative matrix multiplications (precursor to Spark). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs This section walks through **three canonical MapReduce design problems** that appear repeatedly in KTU exams. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Word Count (The "Hello World" of MapReduce) **Problem Statement:** Given a collection of text files, count the frequency of every distinct word. **Input:** Two text files - `file1.txt` → `"dear bear car"` - `file2.txt` → `"bear car car dear"` ### Step 1 — Input Splitting (Framework) HDFS splits the input. Each line becomes a record with `(byte_offset, line_text)` as `(k1, v1)`. ### Step 2 — Map Function For every line, the mapper tokenises by whitespace and emits `(word, 1)`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{map}(k_1, v_1) &\to \text{list of }(k_2, v_2) \\ \text{map}(0, \texttt{"dear bear car"}) &\to [(\texttt{dear},1),(\texttt{bear},1),(\texttt{car},1)] \\ \text{map}(0, \texttt{"bear car car dear"}) &\to [(\texttt{bear},1),(\texttt{car},1),(\texttt{car},1),(\texttt{dear},1)] \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Combiner (Local Pre-aggregation) Since `+` is commutative and associative, we **can** use a combiner. It runs on the mapper node after the in-memory buffer spills. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{combiner for } \texttt{bear} &: [1, 1] \to (\texttt{bear}, 2) \\ \text{combiner for } \texttt{car} &: [1, 1, 1] \to (\texttt{car}, 3) \\ \text{combiner for } \texttt{dear} &: [1, 1] \to (\texttt{dear}, 2) \end{aligned} $$ This **drastically reduces** the bytes sent over the network in the shuffle phase. ### Step 4 — Shuffle & Sort (Framework) The framework groups all values for each unique key. With $R = 2$ reducers, the default `HashPartitioner` uses $r = \text{hash}(k) \bmod 2$: - Assume `hash("bear")=0`, `hash("car")=1`, `hash("dear")=1`. - **Reducer 1** receives: `[(\texttt{bear}, [2])]` - **Reducer 2** receives: `[(\texttt{car}, [3]), (\texttt{dear}, [2])]` ### Step 5 — Reduce Function $$ \begin{aligned} \text{reduce}(\texttt{bear}, [2]) &\to (\texttt{bear}, 2) \\ \text{reduce}(\texttt{car}, [3]) &\to (\texttt{car}, 3) \\ \text{reduce}(\texttt{dear}, [2]) &\to (\texttt{dear}, 2) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Output Two files written to HDFS, one per reducer: ``` part-r-00000: bear 2 part-r-00001: car 3 dear 2 ``` > [!TIP] > **Why this example matters for KTU:** The examiner often asks "Why is a combiner an *optimisation* and not a *correctness* requirement?" Answer: Without the combiner, the final counts are identical. The combiner only reduces network traffic. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Inverted Index Construction **Problem Statement:** Given $N$ documents, build an **inverted index** mapping each word → list of (document_id, frequency). **Map function:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{map}(\text{docID}, \text{content}) \to & \text{ for each unique word } w \text{ in content:} \\ & \text{emit } (w, \text{docID}:f_w) \end{aligned} $$ where $f_w$ is the frequency of $w$ in that document (counted in-memory before emitting). **Reduce function:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{reduce}(w, \text{list of } [\text{docID}_i:f_{w,i}]) \to & \text{ append all postings into a list} \\ & \text{emit } (w, [\text{docID}_1:f_1, \text{docID}_2:f_2, \ldots]) \end{aligned} $$ **Example trace:** | Input line | Mapper emits | |------------|--------------| | `d1 → "big data big"` | `(big, d1:2)`, `(data, d1:1)` | | `d2 → "data science"` | `(data, d2:1)`, `(science, d2:1)` | After shuffle & reduce: - `(big, [d1:2])` - `(data, [d1:1, d2:1])` - `(science, [d2:1])` --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency) This is a **two-MapReduce-job** design — a classic KTU 14-marker. **Definitions:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TF}(t, d) &= \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} \\ \text{IDF}(t, D) &= \log_e \frac{\vert D \vert}{\vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert + 1} \\ \text{TF-IDF}(t, d, D) &= \text{TF}(t, d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t, D) \end{aligned} $$ where $\vert D \vert$ is the total number of documents and the denominator $+1$ is **Laplace smoothing** to avoid division by zero. ### Job 1 — Compute TF for every (word, document) **Map:** For each document $d$, emit `(word, docID:count)`. **Reduce:** For each `(word, docID)`, sum counts → emit `(word#docID, TF_raw)`. ### Job 2 — Compute IDF and final TF-IDF **Map (read Job 1 output):** Parse `(word#docID, TF_raw)`; emit `(word, docID:TF_raw)` **and** `(word, *)` (an asterisk marker for document counting). **Reduce:** For each `word`, collect all postings to compute: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \text{total documents (global constant)} \\ \text{df}(w) &= \text{number of } * \text{ markers for } w \\ \text{IDF}(w) &= \log_e \frac{N}{\text{df}(w) + 1} \\ \text{For each } (docID : \text{TF}): \quad \text{emit } (word\#docID, \text{TF} \times \text{IDF}) \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to pass the **global $N$** into the reducer. In practice, $N$ is either **broadcast** via `JobConf` (Hadoop) or computed by a **preliminary counting job**. Examiners will deduct marks if this is not addressed. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm vs. MapReduce Design | Algorithm | Map Output Key | Map Output Value | Reduce Logic | Combiner Valid? | |-----------|----------------|------------------|--------------|-----------------| | **Word Count** | word | 1 | Sum | ✅ Yes | | **Inverted Index** | word | docID:freq | Append postings | ❌ No (list append not commutative) | | **Sort by Key** | full record | null | Identity (relies on shuffle sort) | ❌ No | | **Grep (Pattern Search)** | matched line | offset | Identity | ❌ No | | **TF-IDF (Job 1)** | word#docID | count | Sum | ✅ Yes | | **PageRank Iteration** | node | partial rank | Sum | ✅ Yes | | **Distributed Grep** | matched line | byte offset | None | ❌ No | | **Join (Reduce-side)** | join key | tagged tuple | Group & cross-product | ❌ No | | **Min/Max** | key | value | Min/Max | ✅ Yes | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End MapReduce Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[HDFS Input
file1 file2 file3] --> B[InputSplit 1
128 MB] A --> C[InputSplit 2
128 MB] A --> D[InputSplit 3
128 MB] B --> E[Mapper 1] C --> F[Mapper 2] D --> G[Mapper 3] E --> H[Combiner 1
Local Reduce] F --> I[Combiner 2
Local Reduce] G --> J[Combiner 3
Local Reduce] H --> K[Partitioner
hash k mod R] I --> K J --> K K --> R1[Reducer 1] K --> R2[Reducer 2] K --> R3[Reducer 3] R1 --> O1[Output File
part-r-00000] R2 --> O2[Output File
part-r-00001] R3 --> O3[Output File
part-r-00002] O1 --> Z[HDFS Output] O2 --> Z O3 --> Z classDef nodeStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef redStyle fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef greenStyle fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff class A,Z nodeStyle class B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K redStyle class R1,R2,R3,O1,O2,O3 greenStyle ``` ## 4.2 Detailed MapReduce Execution Architecture (YARN) ```mermaid flowchart TB Client[Client submits Job] --> RM[ResourceManager YARN] RM --> AM[ApplicationMaster
per job] AM --> NM1[NodeManager 1] AM --> NM2[NodeManager 2] AM --> NM3[NodeManager 3] NM1 --> CT1[Container 1
Mapper Task] NM1 --> CT2[Container 2
Reducer Task] NM2 --> CT3[Container 3
Mapper Task] NM2 --> CT4[Container 4
Reducer Task] NM3 --> CT5[Container 5
Mapper Task] NM3 --> CT6[Container 6
Reducer Task] HDFS[HDFS NameNode
metadata] -.block locations.-> NM1 HDFS -.block locations.-> NM2 HDFS -.block locations.-> NM3 CT1 -- heartbeat --> NM1 CT2 -- heartbeat --> NM1 CT3 -- heartbeat --> NM2 CT4 -- heartbeat --> NM2 CT5 -- heartbeat --> NM3 CT6 -- heartbeat --> NM3 NM1 -- status report --> RM NM2 -- status report --> RM NM3 -- status report --> RM classDef master fill:#312e81,stroke:#a78bfa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef worker fill:#134e4a,stroke:#5eead4,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef storage fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fdba74,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff class RM,AM master class NM1,NM2,NM3,CT1,CT2,CT3,CT4,CT5,CT6 worker class HDFS storage ``` ## 4.3 MapReduce Job Lifecycle State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> JobSubmitted JobSubmitted --> JobInitialized : ResourceManager accepts JobInitialized --> JobRunning : ApplicationMaster registered JobRunning --> MapPhase : First mapper scheduled MapPhase --> ShufflePhase : All mappers complete ShufflePhase --> ReducePhase : All reducers start fetching ReducePhase --> Committing : All reducers finish Committing --> JobSucceeded : Output committed to HDFS Committing --> JobFailed : Write conflict or task failure JobRunning --> JobFailed : Task attempt exceeded max retries JobSucceeded --> [*] JobFailed --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In the data flow, notice that the **Combiner** sits *before* the Partitioner — it works on local data only. In the YARN architecture, the **ApplicationMaster** is the *brain* of a single job and negotiates containers with the central **ResourceManager**. The **NodeManager** is the *worker* daemon on every node. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a Mapper and a Combiner in Hadoop MapReduce. When is a combiner *not* applicable?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Mapper | Combiner | |--------|--------|----------| | **Where runs** | Once per input split on the data node | Locally on the mapper node, after spill | | **Mandatory?** | Yes | No (optional optimisation) | | **Purpose** | Primary transformation of input records | Local pre-aggregation to reduce shuffle volume | | **Number of invocations** | Exactly $M$ (one per split) | 0 or more per mapper | A combiner is **not applicable** when the reduce function is **not commutative and associative** (e.g., computing average, median, list-append for an inverted index). Using it in such cases would yield **incorrect results**, not just an optimisation loss. *(1 mark for table, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for non-applicability condition.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is the role of the `Partitioner` in MapReduce? Write the formula used by Hadoop's default partitioner.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `Partitioner` determines **which reducer** will receive a given intermediate `(key, value)` pair emitted by any mapper. It controls the **distribution of the intermediate keyspace** across the $R$ reducers. The **default `HashPartitioner`** uses the formula: $$r = (\text{hash of key}) \bmod R$$ where $R$ is the total number of reduce tasks. *(1 mark for role, 2 marks for formula with explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Module 2 offers an internal choice. You must answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B. Each carries **14 marks**, split into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **three execution phases** of a MapReduce job with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **Combiner** and **Partitioner** in detail. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design a MapReduce algorithm to compute the **term frequency (TF)** of every word in a given document collection. Show the map and reduce functions clearly with an example input containing 3 documents. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Phase 1 – Map Phase (1.5 marks):** - Input is split into chunks of HDFS block size (default **128 MB**). - A Mapper task is launched on a node holding the block (data locality). - User-defined `map(k1, v1)` is called for every record; it emits intermediate `(k2, v2)` pairs. - Output is buffered in a **circular memory buffer** (default 100 MB). **Phase 2 – Shuffle & Sort Phase (2.5 marks):** - The **Partitioner** assigns each `(k2, v2)` to a reducer: $r = \text{hash}(k2) \bmod R$. - The **Combiner** (if defined) performs local pre-aggregation on the mapper node after each spill, reducing network traffic. - The intermediate data is **transferred** over HTTP to the assigned reducer nodes (this is the **shuffle**). - The reducer **merges** and **sorts** all incoming data, then groups values by key. **Phase 3 – Reduce Phase (1 mark):** - For each unique key, the framework calls `reduce(k2, list[v2])` exactly once. - The output is written to HDFS, typically as one file per reducer (`part-r-NNNNN`). **Role of Combiner (1 mark):** Local aggregation on mapper node; **valid only when reduce op is commutative and associative**. Reduces shuffle bytes. **Role of Partitioner (1 mark):** Controls load balance across reducers; default is `HashPartitioner`; can be customised (e.g., `TotalOrderPartitioner` for global sorting). *(Diagrammatic representation: refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the data flow.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Algorithm — Term Frequency (TF) Computation:** Given $D$ documents, for each word $w$ in document $d$: $$\text{TF}(w, d) = f_{w,d} = \text{count of } w \text{ in } d$$ **Map Function (2 marks):** ``` map(document_id d, content c): word_counts ← empty hash map for each token t in tokenize(c): word_counts[t] ← word_counts[t] + 1 for each (word, count) in word_counts: emit(word, d:count) // key = word, value = "docID:count" ``` **Reduce Function (2 marks):** ``` reduce(word w, list of [d_i:c_i]): // Aggregation per (word, document) is already done by mapper // Final output is per (word, document) for each (d_i, c_i) in input list: emit(w#d_i, c_i) // compound key = word#docID ``` **Example Trace (3 marks):** Input documents: - `d1: "hadoop mapreduce hadoop"` - `d2: "mapreduce framework"` - `d3: "hadoop framework hadoop"` | Map Output | After Combiner (if applicable) | Reduce Output | |------------|-------------------------------|---------------| | `(hadoop, d1:2)`, `(mapreduce, d1:1)` | combiner: sum → `(hadoop,d1:2)`, `(mapreduce,d1:1)` | `hadoop#d1 → 2`, `mapreduce#d1 → 1` | | `(mapreduce, d2:1)`, `(framework, d2:1)` | as-is | `mapreduce#d2 → 1`, `framework#d2 → 1` | | `(hadoop, d3:2)`, `(framework, d3:1)` | as-is | `hadoop#d3 → 2`, `framework#d3 → 1` | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often emit `(word, 1)` per occurrence instead of first counting in-memory and emitting `(word, d:count)` once per document. While the final *sum* is the same, the **shuffle volume** explodes by a factor equal to the document length. Examiners will deduct **1–2 marks** for this design inefficiency. --- ### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MapReduce framework architecture** under Hadoop YARN. List the responsibilities of the **ResourceManager**, **ApplicationMaster**, and **NodeManager**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design a MapReduce algorithm to **construct an inverted index** for a collection of documents. Show map and reduce functions and explain why a **combiner cannot be used** here. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **YARN Architecture (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):** **ResourceManager (RM) – 2 marks:** - Global daemon; **one per cluster**. - Allocates **containers** (CPU + memory) to applications. - Has two sub-components: **Scheduler** (capacity/fair) and **ApplicationsManager (AsM)**. **ApplicationMaster (AM) – 2 marks:** - One per submitted job; negotiates resources with RM. - Works with NodeManagers to **execute and monitor** mapper/reducer tasks. - Handles **task failures** by requesting re-execution. **NodeManager (NM) – 2 marks:** - Per-node daemon; supervises **containers** and reports heartbeats to RM. - Manages local resources (disk, memory, network) and container lifecycle. **Container – 1 mark:** An abstract concept representing allocated **CPU + RAM** on a node where a task runs. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Problem:** Build an inverted index: word → [docID:tf, docID:tf, ...] **Map Function (2 marks):** ``` map(docID d, content c): freq ← empty hash map for each token t in tokenize(c): freq[t] ← freq[t] + 1 for each (word, count) in freq: emit(word, d + ":" + count) ``` **Reduce Function (2 marks):** ``` reduce(word w, postings = [d_1:c_1, d_2:c_2, ...]): list ← [] for each (d_i, c_i) in postings: append (d_i, c_i) to list emit(w, list) ``` **Why Combiner Cannot Be Used (3 marks):** - A combiner must perform a **commutative, associative** operation that yields the same final result as the reducer. - Here the reducer performs **list appending**. Appending lists in different orders gives **semantically different** outputs. - Example: appending `[d1:1, d2:1]` may yield `[d1:1, d2:1]` or `[d2:1, d1:1]` depending on partition arrival order. - Therefore, applying a combiner would either be **impossible** (the logic is structural) or **change the result semantics** (if we tried to merge postings lists by set union, we would lose document ordering). - Hence, **no combiner is used** for inverted-index construction. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A common error is writing a combiner that merges postings via `union()` and arguing it's "associative." While set union *is* associative, the requirement of an **inverted index** is often a **preserved document order** for ranking algorithms (e.g., BM25). Examiners will deduct marks if you don't explicitly state this distinction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: MapReduce Design** - ✅ MapReduce has **two user-defined functions** (`map`, `reduce`) and the framework handles everything else. - ✅ Default HDFS **block size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x/3.x). - ✅ Mappers ≈ input size / block size; reducers are **user-configured** (recommend ≈ 0.95 × nodes × containers). - ✅ **Combiner** = local pre-aggregation on the mapper node. Valid **only if** the reduce op is **commutative and associative**. - ✅ **Partitioner** = decides which reducer gets a key. Default = `HashPartitioner` using $\text{hash}(k) \bmod R$. - ✅ **Shuffle** is the most **network-intensive** phase; minimise it using combiners, compression, and custom partitioners. - ✅ **Data locality** principle: computation moves to where the data resides. - ✅ Word Count, Inverted Index, TF-IDF, Sort, Grep, PageRank are the **canonical design templates**. - ✅ Multi-job MapReduce designs (e.g., TF-IDF) pass intermediate data via HDFS between jobs. - ✅ YARN separates **ResourceManager** (global) from **ApplicationMaster** (per-job) from **NodeManager** (per-node). - ✅ Hadoop provides **speculative execution** to mitigate stragglers (slow tasks) by running a duplicate on another node. - ✅ Failure model: failed task → restart on another node; failed AM → entire job restarts. - ✅ **TotalOrderPartitioner** is used when **global sorting** of the output is required (samples the input keyspace). - ✅ Examine the **map and reduce I/O contracts**: input key/value types must match what `RecordReader` produces; output key/value types must match what `OutputFormat` expects. > 🎯 **Golden Rule for KTU MapReduce Answers:** Always (1) state the intermediate `(k, v)` pair format, (2) show a small example trace, (3) comment on combiner validity, and (4) state assumptions (e.g., "assume $R = 2$ reducers"). Examiners reward this discipline with full marks.

topics.title: Face Recognition Example: Simple Data Processing with MapReduce
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Face Recognition Example: Simple Data Processing with MapReduce

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Face Recognition using MapReduce** is a canonical introductory paradigm in distributed data processing that demonstrates how a computationally intensive image-analysis task can be decomposed into parallelisable `map` and `reduce` phases executing across a Hadoop cluster. In this model, a corpus of digital images stored on the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** is processed by a `Mapper` that performs local feature extraction (e.g., face detection bounding boxes, Eigen-face projection, or pixel-intensity histograms), emitting intermediate key–value pairs. The `Reducer` then aggregates these partial results to produce a consolidated output, such as a count of detected faces, an identity label, or a similarity index.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> This topic belongs to the "Examples of MapReduce Applications" sub-unit. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to understand the *logical flow* of a non-trivial real-world problem (face recognition) expressed entirely in the MapReduce programming model — not the underlying computer-vision mathematics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **classroom of 1000 students**, and the teacher hands each of 30 student-volunteers a different photograph. The teacher asks: *"In how many photos can you find a human face?"*

- Each volunteer inspects **one photo at a time** (this is the `Mapper` — works on a single data chunk).
- Each volunteer shouts out their result: *"Photo #7 has 2 faces"*, *"Photo #22 has 0 faces"*, etc.
- A senior student at the board listens to all shouts and **sums them up** to give the final answer (this is the `Reducer`).

Now scale this to **petabytes of images** spread across thousands of commodity machines: that is precisely the Face Recognition MapReduce pipeline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "intelligence" of face recognition is *parallel-safe* — looking at one image does not depend on looking at another. This *data independence* is exactly what makes the problem embarrassingly parallel and therefore a textbook fit for MapReduce.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| HDFS Block Size (default) | $B_{HDFS}$ | **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+) | Granularity at which input is split |
| Hadoop Replication Factor | $R$ | **3** (default) | Fault-tolerance redundancy |
| Mapper Input Format (for images) | – | `SequenceFileInputFormat` / `WholeFileInputFormat` | Binary-friendly reader |
| Typical Image Dimensions | $W \times H$ | $640 \times 480$ to $4096 \times 3072$ | Affects mapper workload |
| Eigen-face Feature Length | $d$ | **100 – 4096** | Dimensionality of feature vector |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition into MapReduce Phases

The face-recognition workflow is decomposed into **four logical MapReduce stages**:

### Stage 1 — Input Splitting & Distribution
- The input dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{I_1, I_2, \dots, I_N\}$ (a set of $N$ images) is uploaded to HDFS.
- Hadoop's `InputFormat` splits the dataset into $M$ splits of approximately $B_{HDFS}$ each.
- Each split is assigned to one `Mapper` instance.

### Stage 2 — Map Phase (Local Feature Extraction)
For each image $I_k$ assigned to a mapper, the algorithm performs:

1. **Face Detection** — locates $f_k$ face bounding boxes $\{(x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i)\}_{i=1}^{f_k}$.
2. **Feature Extraction** — for each detected face region, computes a feature vector $\mathbf{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.
3. **Emission** — outputs key–value pairs $(K_i, V_i)$ where:

$$K_i = \text{image\_id}(I_k) \quad \text{and} \quad V_i = \mathbf{v}_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mapper may also emit $(K_i, \text{face\_count})$ for simple counting applications.

### Stage 3 — Shuffle & Sort (Framework Handled)
- The Hadoop runtime groups all values with the same key.
- For our example, this means all feature vectors belonging to the same image are routed to the same reducer.

### Stage 4 — Reduce Phase (Aggregation / Classification)
The reducer receives $\langle K_k, [V_1, V_2, \dots, V_{f_k}] \rangle$ and performs:

- **Counting:** $\text{total\_faces} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} f_k$
- **Classification:** Compares each $\mathbf{v}_i$ against a centroid database (e.g., using nearest-neighbour search):

$$\text{identity}(i) = \arg\min_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \Vert \mathbf{v}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_c \Vert_2$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of known identity centroids and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ is the mean feature vector of class $c$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of mappers | $M \approx \lceil \text{Total Input Size} / B_{HDFS} \rceil$ | Integers |
| 2 | Mapper output records | $R_{map} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} f_k$ | Per image, $f_k$ faces |
| 3 | Face count aggregation | $F_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} f_k$ | Global total |
| 4 | Euclidean distance for classification | $D(\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(v_j - \mu_j)^2}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | Cosine similarity (alternative) | $S(\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}}{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \boldsymbol{\mu} \Vert_2}$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 6 | Pixel intensity (grayscale) | $I(x,y) \in [0, 255]$ | 8-bit channel |
| 7 | Image as matrix | $\mathbf{I} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W}$ | $H$=height, $W$=width |
| 8 | Eigen-face reconstruction | $\hat{\mathbf{I}} = \bar{\mathbf{I}} + \sum_{j=1}^{d} w_j \mathbf{u}_j$ | Approximation |
| 9 | Confidence threshold | $\tau$ (e.g., **0.85**) | Match-decision cut-off |
| 10 | MapReduce time complexity (ideal) | $T_{MR} \approx T_{map} + T_{shuffle} + T_{reduce}$ | Sequential stages |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** In the formulas above, the boundary condition for a "match" is $S(\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) \geq \tau$. Anything below $\tau$ is classified as **unknown**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surveillance & Security:** Real-time face matching across CCTV feeds (e.g., airport gates).
- **Social Media Tagging:** Facebook's "tag suggestion" pipeline uses distributed face recognition.
- **Mobile Unlocking:** Apple's Face ID and Android's Trusted Face operate on smaller datasets, but the architectural principles (map = extract, reduce = compare) are identical.
- **Forensic Identification:** Law-enforcement agencies process terabytes of surveillance imagery using Hadoop-style MapReduce clusters.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Worked Example)

> **Given:** A directory `/user/ktu/face_dataset/` on HDFS contains **5 images**: `img01.jpg`, `img02.jpg`, …, `img05.jpg`. Each image has been pre-scanned, and the ground-truth number of faces per image is:
>
> $f_1 = 2, \quad f_2 = 0, \quad f_3 = 1, \quad f_4 = 3, \quad f_5 = 1$
>
> **Task:** Write a MapReduce program that, for each image, outputs the image filename and the detected face count. The reducer should also produce the global total.

## 3.2 Mapper Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Pseudocode derivation:**

1. Read input key–value pair: $\langle \text{image\_path}, \text{image\_bytes} \rangle$.
2. Decode bytes into a processable image object.
3. Apply face-detection algorithm (e.g., Haar-cascade, HOG + SVM, or a deep CNN). This step is encapsulated in the function $\mathcal{F}_{detect}(I_k) \to \{(x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i)\}_{i=1}^{f_k}$.
4. Count the number of bounding boxes: $f_k = \vert \mathcal{F}_{detect}(I_k) \vert$.
5. Emit intermediate pair: $\langle \text{image\_path}, f_k \rangle$.

**Worked numerical example for `img01.jpg`:**

$$\mathcal{F}_{detect}(\texttt{img01.jpg}) = \{(120, 80, 60, 60),\ (250, 100, 55, 55)\}$$

$$f_1 = \vert \mathcal{F}_{detect}(\texttt{img01.jpg}) \vert = 2$$

$$\text{emit}(\texttt{img01.jpg}, 2)$$

**Emission table from all mappers:**

| Image | Faces Detected ($f_k$) | Emitted Key | Emitted Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| img01.jpg | 2 | `img01.jpg` | 2 |
| img02.jpg | 0 | `img02.jpg` | 0 |
| img03.jpg | 1 | `img03.jpg` | 1 |
| img04.jpg | 3 | `img04.jpg` | 3 |
| img05.jpg | 1 | `img05.jpg` | 1 |

## 3.3 Shuffle & Sort Phase

The Hadoop framework groups by key. Since each image filename is **unique** in our problem, each reducer will receive exactly one entry per key. (In a more general workflow, keys could be person IDs, and a reducer would aggregate per-person results.)

## 3.4 Reducer Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Pseudocode derivation:**

1. For each input key $K$, receive an iterable of values: $[V_1, V_2, \dots]$.
2. Since keys are unique filenames, simply take $V = V_1$.
3. Emit $\langle K, V \rangle$ for the per-image result.
4. (Optional, using a `Counter` or a second job) Accumulate the global sum:

$$F_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} f_k = 2 + 0 + 1 + 3 + 1$$

## 3.5 Exhaustive Numerical Evaluation

Let us compute the global total step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= f_1 + f_2 + f_3 + f_4 + f_5 \\
&= 2 + 0 + 1 + 3 + 1 \\
&= (2 + 0) + (1 + 3) + 1 \\
&= 2 + 4 + 1 \\
&= 7
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the dataset contains **7 faces** distributed across **5 images**.

**Final reducer output (per-image view):**

| Output Key (Image) | Output Value (Face Count) |
|---|---|
| img01.jpg | 2 |
| img02.jpg | 0 |
| img03.jpg | 1 |
| img04.jpg | 3 |
| img05.jpg | 1 |
| **TOTAL** | **7** |

## 3.6 Reference Java MapReduce Skeleton (KTU 2024)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **canonical Hadoop MapReduce code skeleton** expected in KTU 2024 theory questions. KTU examiners award partial credit for correct `map()`, `reduce()` signatures, context API usage, and `Job` configuration.

```java
// MAPPER
public class FaceCountMapper 
       extends Mapper<LongWritable, BytesWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
    private final static IntWritable faceCount = new IntWritable(0);
    private Text imageName = new Text();

    @Override
    protected void map(LongWritable key, BytesWritable value, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        // 1. Convert bytes to image
        byte[] imgBytes = value.getBytes();
        BufferedImage image = ImageIO.read(new ByteArrayInputStream(imgBytes));

        // 2. Detect faces
        int count = detectFaces(image); // returns f_k

        // 3. Set & emit
        faceCount.set(count);
        imageName.set(((FileSplit) context.getInputSplit()).getPath().getName());
        context.write(imageName, faceCount);
    }
}

// REDUCER
public class FaceCountReducer 
       extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
    @Override
    protected void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        int sum = 0;
        for (IntWritable v : values) {
            sum += v.get();
        }
        context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum));
    }
}

// DRIVER
public class FaceCountDriver {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Configuration conf = new Configuration();
        Job job = Job.getInstance(conf, "Face Count MapReduce");
        job.setJarByClass(FaceCountDriver.class);
        job.setMapperClass(FaceCountMapper.class);
        job.setReducerClass(FaceCountReducer.class);
        job.setInputFormatClass(WholeFileInputFormat.class);
        job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class);
        job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class);
        FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job, new Path(args[0]));
        FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job, new Path(args[1]));
        System.exit(job.waitForCompletion(true) ? 0 : 1);
    }
}
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Face Recognition MapReduce Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HDFS Image Dataset] --> B[InputFormat Split]
    B --> C1[Mapper Node 1]
    B --> C2[Mapper Node 2]
    B --> C3[Mapper Node 3]
    C1 --> D1[Local Face Detection on Image i]
    C2 --> D2[Local Face Detection on Image j]
    C3 --> D3[Local Face Detection on Image k]
    D1 --> E1[Emit key equals imageID value equals feature vector]
    D2 --> E2[Emit key equals imageID value equals feature vector]
    D3 --> E3[Emit key equals imageID value equals feature vector]
    E1 --> F[Shuffle and Sort Group By Key]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G1[Reducer Node Aggregator]
    G1 --> H1[Per Image Face Count]
    G1 --> H2[Global Total Faces]
    G1 --> H3[Identity Match Output]
    H1 --> I[HDFS Result Directory]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

## 4.2 Detailed Data-Flow Block Diagram (Architecture View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientLayer[Client Layer]
        CLI[KTU User Submits Job]
    end

    subgraph NameNodeLayer[NameNode Layer]
        NN[HDFS NameNode Metadata Manager]
    end

    subgraph DataNodeLayer[DataNode Cluster]
        DN1[DataNode 1 Stores Image Block]
        DN2[DataNode 2 Stores Image Block]
        DN3[DataNode 3 Stores Image Block]
    end

    subgraph MapLayer[Map Phase Executors]
        M1[Mapper 1 reads from DN1]
        M2[Mapper 2 reads from DN2]
        M3[Mapper 3 reads from DN3]
    end

    subgraph EngineLayer[Hadoop MapReduce Engine]
        SHF[Shuffle and Sort Service]
        RDC[Reducer Container]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        OUT[HDFS Output Path]
    end

    CLI --> NN
    NN --> DN1
    NN --> DN2
    NN --> DN3
    DN1 --> M1
    DN2 --> M2
    DN3 --> M3
    M1 --> SHF
    M2 --> SHF
    M3 --> SHF
    SHF --> RDC
    RDC --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Mapper-to-Reducer Logical Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Partition 0: img01] --> MP1[Mapper Instance 1]
    P2[Partition 1: img02 and img03] --> MP2[Mapper Instance 2]
    P3[Partition 2: img04 and img05] --> MP3[Mapper Instance 3]
    MP1 --> R1[Reducer receives img01 with value 2]
    MP2 --> R2a[Reducer receives img02 with value 0]
    MP2 --> R2b[Reducer receives img03 with value 1]
    MP3 --> R3a[Reducer receives img04 with value 3]
    MP3 --> R3b[Reducer receives img05 with value 1]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture (Fallback for Complex Visuals)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Image Input Bytes] --> DECODE[Decode JPEG to Bitmap]
    DECODE --> GRAY[Convert to Grayscale]
    GRAY --> DETECT[Face Detection Algorithm]
    DETECT --> CROP[Crop Face Region]
    CROP --> NORM[Normalize and Resize to d by d]
    NORM --> FEAT[Feature Vector Extraction PCA or CNN]
    FEAT --> EMIT[Emit Intermediate Key Value Pair]
    EMIT --> SHUFFLE[Network Shuffle to Reducer]
    SHUFFLE --> CLASSIFY[Classifier Compares with Known Centroids]
    CLASSIFY --> DECIDE[Match if Similarity greater than threshold tau]
    DECIDE --> OUTPUT[Final Identity Label]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Why is face recognition considered a "good fit" for the MapReduce programming model? Justify with two technical reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Embarrassingly parallel nature:** Face recognition operates on independent images; processing one image does not require results from another. This data independence allows Hadoop to split the input across thousands of mappers without inter-node communication during the map phase. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **High computation-to-I/O ratio with aggregate-friendly output:** Each image is computationally heavy (millions of pixel operations) but the final outputs are compact (counts, labels, similarity scores). This amortises the HDFS I/O cost and keeps the shuffle phase small. *(1 mark)*

3. **Scalability through HDFS block distribution:** Since images are stored as 128 MB blocks, the dataset scales linearly with the cluster size, making it ideal for distributed processing. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between the `Mapper` and `Reducer` phases in the context of a face-recognition MapReduce job. State the input/output types of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mapper | Reducer |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | $\langle \text{offset}, \text{image\_bytes} \rangle$ | $\langle \text{image\_id}, [\text{feature\_vectors}] \rangle$ |
| **Processing** | Face detection & feature extraction (per-image) | Aggregation, classification, or counting |
| **Output** | $\langle \text{image\_id}, \text{feature\_vector} \rangle$ | $\langle \text{image\_id}, \text{label/count} \rangle$ |
| **Executed on** | Every HDFS block (data-local) | Grouped-by-key partitions |

*(3 marks — full distribution table + brief explanatory sentence for each row.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** *Answer either Question A or Question B in full.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Model QP]`

**(a)** Explain the **complete MapReduce data flow** for processing a face-recognition dataset stored on HDFS. Use a labelled diagram and describe the role of `InputFormat`, `RecordReader`, `Mapper`, `Combiner`, `Partitioner`, `Shuffle & Sort`, and `Reducer`. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Hadoop cluster processes **1,00,000 images** of average size **2 MB** each. The HDFS block size is **128 MB** and the replication factor is **3**.
  - (i) Compute the **number of mappers** that will be launched. *(3 marks)*
  - (ii) Compute the **total physical storage** consumed on the cluster. *(2 marks)*
  - (iii) If the face-detection algorithm takes **2.5 seconds per image** and the cluster has **10 mapper slots**, calculate the **minimum wall-clock time** for the map phase. *(2 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A

**(a) MapReduce Data Flow Explanation (7 marks):**

The MapReduce framework executes a job in the following sequential stages:

1. **InputFormat & Splitting (1 mark):** The `ImageInputFormat` (a custom or `WholeFileInputFormat`) divides the HDFS dataset into logical splits. Each split = 1 image (when using `WholeFileInputFormat`) or a chunk of $\approx$ 128 MB.

2. **RecordReader (0.5 mark):** Converts each split into key–value records. For images, the key is the byte offset and the value is the raw image bytes.

3. **Mapper (1.5 marks):** For each image, performs face detection. Emits intermediate key–value pairs: $\langle \text{image\_id}, \text{feature\_vector} \rangle$.

4. **Combiner (0.5 mark):** An optional local reducer that runs on the mapper node to pre-aggregate outputs (e.g., summing face counts per image before network transfer).

5. **Partitioner (0.5 mark):** Hashes the intermediate key to decide which reducer will receive it. Default: `HashPartitioner`.

6. **Shuffle & Sort (1 mark):** Framework-managed phase where intermediate data is transferred over the network, merged, and sorted by key.

7. **Reducer (1 mark):** Receives grouped data, performs classification/counting, and writes final output to HDFS.

8. **OutputFormat (0.5 mark):** Writes the reducer output back to HDFS in the specified format (e.g., `TextOutputFormat`).

A complete labelled flow diagram is shown in **Section 4.2** of these notes.

**(b) Numerical Solutions (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of mappers (3 marks):**

Total input size:
$$S_{input} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 2 \text{ MB} = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ MB} = 200 \text{ GB}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of blocks} &= \left\lceil \frac{S_{input}}{B_{HDFS}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{200{,}000 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 1562.5 \right\rceil \\
&= 1563 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **1563 mappers** will be launched (one per HDFS block). *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Total physical storage (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{physical} &= S_{input} \times R \\
&= 200 \text{ GB} \times 3 \\
&= 600 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks — with replication factor $R=3$.)*

**(iii) Minimum wall-clock time for map phase (2 marks):**

Total mappers needed = 1563 (from part i).
Mapper slots available = 10.
Time per mapper = 2.5 s.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{map} &= \left\lceil \frac{1563}{10} \right\rceil \times 2.5 \text{ s} \\
&= 157 \times 2.5 \text{ s} \\
&= 392.5 \text{ s} \approx 6 \text{ min } 32.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks — include the ceiling operation for non-divisible workloads.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted Model QP]`

**(a)** Describe the **Mapper** and **Reducer** logic in detail for a face-recognition MapReduce program that outputs (i) the number of faces per image, and (ii) the total number of faces across the entire dataset. Provide Java pseudocode for both functions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a face-recognition system that uses **Eigen-face decomposition**. Each face is represented as a feature vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{100}$. The known-identity centroid database contains $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2, \boldsymbol{\mu}_3$.
  - (i) Given $\mathbf{v} = (0.8, 0.1, 0.05, \dots, 0.01)$ (101 values) and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (0.7, 0.15, 0.04, \dots, 0.02)$, compute the **Euclidean distance** to determine if the face matches identity 2. Use $\tau = 5.0$ as the threshold. *(3 marks)*
  - (ii) State **two advantages** and **one limitation** of using MapReduce for face recognition. *(4 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-B

**(a) Mapper and Reducer Logic (7 marks):**

**Mapper pseudocode (3.5 marks):**

```java
class FaceMapper extends Mapper<LongWritable, BytesWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
    private Text imageKey = new Text();
    private IntWritable faceCount = new IntWritable();

    public void map(LongWritable offset, BytesWritable imageBytes, Context ctx) {
        BufferedImage img = decode(imageBytes);
        int count = runFaceDetection(img);   // returns f_k
        imageKey.set(extractImageName(ctx.getInputSplit()));
        faceCount.set(count);
        ctx.write(imageKey, faceCount);
    }
}
```

Explanation (per-line credit): (1) Decode raw bytes into an image object. (2) Invoke face detector $\mathcal{F}_{detect}$. (3) Set output key = image filename. (4) Set output value = face count. (5) Emit to context. *(0.5 mark per line)*

**Reducer pseudocode (3.5 marks):**

```java
class FaceReducer extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
    public void reduce(Text imageKey, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context ctx) {
        int sum = 0;
        for (IntWritable v : values) sum += v.get();
        ctx.write(imageKey, new IntWritable(sum));
    }
}
```

Explanation: The reducer iterates over all face counts associated with a given image, sums them, and emits the final result. For the global total, a separate `Counter` or a second MapReduce job aggregates across all images. *(3.5 marks — code + explanation)*

**(b) Eigen-face & MapReduce Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) Euclidean distance computation (3 marks):**

Given vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{100}$, we need to show the general formula and one representative component computation:

$$D(\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{100}(v_j - \mu_{2,j})^2}$$

For the first three components:

$$\begin{aligned}
(v_1 - \mu_{2,1})^2 &= (0.80 - 0.70)^2 = (0.10)^2 = 0.0100 \\
(v_2 - \mu_{2,2})^2 &= (0.10 - 0.15)^2 = (-0.05)^2 = 0.0025 \\
(v_3 - \mu_{2,3})^2 &= (0.05 - 0.04)^2 = (0.01)^2 = 0.0001
\end{aligned}$$

The total (assuming uniform $\approx 0.0001$ per remaining 97 components) would be approximately:

$$D \approx \sqrt{0.0126 + 97 \times 0.0001} = \sqrt{0.0126 + 0.0097} = \sqrt{0.0223} \approx 0.149$$

Since $D = 0.149 < \tau = 5.0$, the face **matches identity 2**. *(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for computation, 1 for decision with threshold.)*

**(ii) Two advantages and one limitation (4 marks):**

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Horizontal scalability:** Adding more DataNodes linearly increases throughput. (1 mark)
2. **Fault tolerance:** HDFS replication (default $R=3$) ensures no image is permanently lost. (1 mark)

**Limitation (2 marks):**
1. **High latency for small datasets:** MapReduce job-launch overhead (~30 s) makes it inefficient for single-image or real-time face recognition. For real-time use, alternative frameworks (e.g., Spark Streaming, Flink) are preferred. *(2 marks — must explain why, not just state.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Face Recognition MapReduce Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in mapper-count calculations. KTU examiners strictly deduct 1 mark if a student writes $1562.5$ mappers instead of $\lceil 1562.5 \rceil = 1563$. Always use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ for block counts.
> 2. **Ignoring replication factor** when computing total storage. The physical storage = logical size × $R$. Skipping $R$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Mapper input/output types.** Common mistake: writing $\langle \text{image\_id}, \text{image\_bytes} \rangle$ as mapper output. Correct: mapper OUTPUT is $\langle \text{image\_id}, \text{feature\_vector/count} \rangle$.
> 4. **Omitting the threshold check** in classification problems. Always state: *"Match if $D \leq \tau$" or "Match if $S \geq \tau$"* — partial credit is lost otherwise.
> 5. **Skipping the Shuffle & Sort explanation.** It is a framework-managed phase, but KTU expects students to describe it in 1–2 lines for full credit on 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Face Recognition with MapReduce**

- **Core Paradigm:** MapReduce = embarrassingly parallel + aggregate. *(High-yield keyword for 2-mark questions.)*
- **Mapper Role:** Local face detection + feature extraction per image. Output = $\langle \text{image\_id}, \text{feature/count} \rangle$.
- **Reducer Role:** Aggregation (counting) or classification (nearest-centroid matching).
- **Data Locality:** Mappers run on the same DataNode where the image block resides — minimises network I/O.
- **Block Size:** Default HDFS block = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+); 64 MB in older versions.
- **Replication:** Default replication factor $R = 3$; physical storage = logical size × 3.
- **Mapper Count Formula:** $M = \lceil S_{input} / B_{HDFS} \rceil$.
- **Wall-Clock Time:** $T_{map} = \lceil M / \text{slots} \rceil \times t_{per\_image}$.
- **Classification Formula:** $D = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(v_j - \mu_j)^2}$; match iff $D \leq \tau$.
- **Cosine Similarity:** $S = \dfrac{\mathbf{v} \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}}{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert \cdot \Vert \boldsymbol{\mu} \Vert}$; match iff $S \geq \tau$.
- **WholeFileInputFormat:** Required for binary image inputs (text-based `TextInputFormat` will corrupt JPEG/PNG bytes).
- **Combiner:** Optional local pre-aggregation to reduce shuffle traffic.
- **Driver Class:** Must set `Mapper`, `Reducer`, `InputFormat`, `OutputKeyClass`, `OutputValueClass`, input/output `Path`.
- **Suitability:** MapReduce is *not* suitable for real-time face recognition due to job-launch overhead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverted Indexes Example
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverted Indexes Example

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverted Index (Definition – KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> An **Inverted Index** is a data structure used in Information Retrieval (IR) and Big Data systems that maps every unique **term (word/token)** appearing in a document collection to a list of **postings**, where each posting records the documents in which the term occurs along with auxiliary metadata such as term frequency and positional information.

The index is called "inverted" because it reverses the natural relationship: instead of storing *document → list of words* (a **forward index**), it stores *word → list of documents*. It is the foundational data structure behind modern search engines such as **Google**, **Elasticsearch**, **Apache Solr**, and **Lucene**.

The two principal components of an inverted index are:

- **Dictionary (Vocabulary / Lexicon)** — the sorted set of all distinct terms in the collection.
- **Postings List** — for every term $t$, the list $P(t)$ containing all document IDs $d$ where $t$ appears, typically augmented with the term frequency $tf_{t,d}$ and positional offsets.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition: The Index Page of a Textbook**
> Imagine the index at the back of a 1,000-page engineering textbook. Instead of reading every page to find where "Transformer" is mentioned, you look up the keyword alphabetically and get a list: *page 14, page 87, page 203, page 412*. That is exactly what an **Inverted Index** does for billions of web pages — it turns a slow full-scan search into an **O(1) dictionary lookup followed by O(k) postings traversal**, where $k$ is the number of matching documents.

In a Big Data context, this is critical: scanning a **petabyte-scale** web corpus in real time is impossible, so the inverted index (often sharded and replicated across a cluster) is built *offline* and queried *online*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant / Metric (KTU Highlight)**
> Standard reporting unit for index size: the **number of postings per document** is called the **positional postings count**. For a collection of $N$ documents, the dictionary size $V$ is typically bounded by **Heaps' Law**: $V = K \cdot N^{\beta}$, where empirically $K \in [30, 100]$ and $\beta \approx 0.5$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Construction

Building an inverted index from a raw document collection proceeds in six logical stages:

1. **Document Acquisition** — gather the raw text documents $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N\}$ and assign each a unique integer **docID**.
2. **Tokenization** — split each document into a sequence of tokens (words, numbers, punctuation stripped).
3. **Linguistic Pre-processing** — apply *lowercasing*, *stopword removal* (e.g., "is", "the", "of"), and *stemming* (e.g., "running" → "run").
4. **Term–DocID Pair Generation** — emit a pair $(t, d)$ for every occurrence of term $t$ in document $d$.
5. **Sorting & Grouping** — sort all pairs lexicographically by term, then group postings under each term.
6. **Postings List Finalization** — deduplicate docIDs, accumulate $tf_{t,d}$, and store positional offsets if positional queries are supported.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Why sort by term?** It enables **binary search** on the dictionary, reducing lookup time from $O(N \cdot L)$ (linear scan) to $O(\log V)$ per query term.
- **Why store frequency in postings?** It allows the ranking algorithm to use **TF-IDF / BM25** without re-reading the documents.
- **Why store positions?** It enables **phrase queries** ("big data") and **proximity queries** ("data" within 3 words of "science").

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Term Frequency (raw) | $tf_{t,d} = \text{count of } t \text{ in } d$ | How often term $t$ appears in document $d$ |
| Document Frequency | $df_t = \vert \{d \in D : t \in d\} \vert$ | Number of documents containing term $t$ |
| Inverse Document Frequency | $idf_t = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | Discriminative power of $t$ (rarer $\Rightarrow$ higher weight) |
| TF-IDF Weight | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot idf_t$ | Composite relevance score |
| Heaps' Law (Vocabulary Size) | $V = K \cdot N^{\beta}$ | Total distinct terms $V$ for $N$ documents |
| Zipf's Law (Rank Frequency) | $cf_i \approx \dfrac{c}{i}$ | $i$-th most frequent term has frequency $\propto 1/i$ |
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(q, d) = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}$ | Relevance ranking between query $q$ and doc $d$ |
| Boolean AND | $P(a) \cap P(b)$ | Intersect postings lists |
| Boolean OR | $P(a) \cup P(b)$ | Merge postings lists |
| Boolean NOT | $U \setminus P(a)$ | Complement w.r.t. universe $U$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> Inverted indexes power **Google Search**, **Elasticsearch clusters**, **GitHub code search**, **e-commerce product search (Amazon)**, and **log analytics platforms (Splunk)**. In Big Data frameworks like **Hadoop MapReduce** and **Apache Spark**, the inverted index is the canonical "first example" for the *Map → Shuffle → Reduce* pattern because it cleanly exhibits the *group-by-key* abstraction.

### 2.4 Worked Example Document Collection (Used Throughout)

For the remainder of these notes, we use the following three documents to make every concept concrete:

- **D1:** "data science is the future of technology"
- **D2:** "big data processing is essential for data science"
- **D3:** "the future of big data is bright"

Here $N = 3$ total documents, and after standard pre-processing the vocabulary $V$ contains 12 distinct terms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Building the Inverted Index — Exhaustive Walk-Through

**Step 1 — Tokenization & Pair Emission**
Each document is split and every token is paired with its docID. Stopword removal is deferred (we keep all words here to keep the example traceable).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &\Rightarrow (\text{data}, 1),(\text{science}, 1),(\text{is}, 1),(\text{the}, 1),(\text{future}, 1),(\text{of}, 1),(\text{technology}, 1) \\
D_2 &\Rightarrow (\text{big}, 2),(\text{data}, 2),(\text{processing}, 2),(\text{is}, 2),(\text{essential}, 2),(\text{for}, 2),(\text{data}, 2),(\text{science}, 2) \\
D_3 &\Rightarrow (\text{the}, 3),(\text{future}, 3),(\text{of}, 3),(\text{big}, 3),(\text{data}, 3),(\text{is}, 3),(\text{bright}, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sort Lexicographically by Term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\text{big},2),(\text{big},3),(\text{bright},3),(\text{data},1),(\text{data},2),(\text{data},2),(\text{data},3),(\text{essential},2),\\
&(\text{future},1),(\text{future},3),(\text{for},2),(\text{is},1),(\text{is},2),(\text{is},3),(\text{of},1),(\text{of},3),(\text{processing},2),\\
&(\text{science},1),(\text{science},2),(\text{technology},1),(\text{the},1),(\text{the},3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Group & Deduplicate per Term** (collapse duplicates, count frequencies)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{big}      &\to [\,d_2 \to 1,\ d_3 \to 1\,] \quad \text{(docIDs 2, 3; tf = 1 each)} \\
\text{bright}   &\to [\,d_3 \to 1\,] \\
\text{data}     &\to [\,d_1 \to 1,\ d_2 \to 2,\ d_3 \to 1\,] \\
\text{essential}&\to [\,d_2 \to 1\,] \\
\text{future}   &\to [\,d_1 \to 1,\ d_3 \to 1\,] \\
\text{for}      &\to [\,d_2 \to 1\,] \\
\text{is}       &\to [\,d_1 \to 1,\ d_2 \to 1,\ d_3 \to 1\,] \\
\text{of}       &\to [\,d_1 \to 1,\ d_3 \to 1\,] \\
\text{processing}&\to [\,d_2 \to 1\,] \\
\text{science}  &\to [\,d_1 \to 1,\ d_2 \to 1\,] \\
\text{technology}&\to [\,d_1 \to 1\,] \\
\text{the}      &\to [\,d_1 \to 1,\ d_3 \to 1\,]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Inverted Index (Dictionary + Postings)**

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Term } t & \textbf{Postings } P(t) & df_t & \text{Postings} \\
\hline
\text{big}       & \{2, 3\}        & 2 & (2,1),(3,1) \\
\text{bright}    & \{3\}           & 1 & (3,1) \\
\text{data}      & \{1, 2, 3\}     & 3 & (1,1),(2,2),(3,1) \\
\text{essential} & \{2\}           & 1 & (2,1) \\
\text{future}    & \{1, 3\}        & 2 & (1,1),(3,1) \\
\text{for}       & \{2\}           & 1 & (2,1) \\
\text{is}        & \{1, 2, 3\}     & 3 & (1,1),(2,1),(3,1) \\
\text{of}        & \{1, 3\}        & 2 & (1,1),(3,1) \\
\text{processing}& \{2\}           & 1 & (2,1) \\
\text{science}   & \{1, 2\}        & 2 & (1,1),(2,1) \\
\text{technology}& \{1\}           & 1 & (1,1) \\
\text{the}       & \{1, 3\}        & 2 & (1,1),(3,1) \\
\hline
\end{array}
\;}
$$

### 3.2 Boolean Query Processing (Set Operations on Postings)

> [!NOTE]
> **Query 1:** `data AND science` — find documents containing **both** terms.

$$
P(\text{data}) \cap P(\text{science}) = \{1, 2, 3\} \cap \{1, 2\} = \{1, 2\}
$$

**Result:** Documents **D1** and **D2** are returned.

> **Query 2:** `data OR bright`

$$
P(\text{data}) \cup P(\text{bright}) = \{1, 2, 3\} \cup \{3\} = \{1, 2, 3\}
$$

**Result:** All three documents.

> **Query 3:** `data AND NOT science`

$$
P(\text{data}) \setminus P(\text{science}) = \{1, 2, 3\} \setminus \{1, 2\} = \{3\}
$$

**Result:** Only **D3**.

### 3.3 Phrase Query Processing with a Positional Postings List

To answer the phrase query **"big data"**, we need positional information.

| Term | Positional Postings |
|---|---|
| `big`   | $\to d_2 [1],\ d_3 [4]$ |
| `data`  | $\to d_1 [1],\ d_2 [2],\ d_3 [5]$ |

For each common document, we check whether any position of `big` is exactly **one less** than a position of `data` (i.e., `big` immediately followed by `data`):

- **D1:** `big` is absent $\Rightarrow$ rejected.
- **D2:** `big` at pos 1, `data` at pos 2. Since $1 + 1 = 2$ ✓ → **match**.
- **D3:** `big` at pos 4, `data` at pos 5. Since $4 + 1 = 5$ ✓ → **match**.

**Result:** The phrase **"big data"** is found in **D2** and **D3**.

### 3.4 TF-IDF Weight Computation

Using $N = 3$ and base-10 logarithm:

$$
idf_t = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
idf_{\text{big}}       &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.176 \\
idf_{\text{bright}}    &= \log_{10}(3/1) = 0.477 \\
idf_{\text{data}}      &= \log_{10}(3/3) = 0.000 \\
idf_{\text{essential}} &= \log_{10}(3/1) = 0.477 \\
idf_{\text{future}}    &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.176 \\
idf_{\text{is}}        &= \log_{10}(3/3) = 0.000 \\
idf_{\text{science}}   &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.176 \\
idf_{\text{technology}}&= \log_{10}(3/1) = 0.477 \\
idf_{\text{the}}       &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.176 \\
idf_{\text{of}}        &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.176 \\
