# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

All diagrams use Mermaid (KTU 2024 scheme expects clear architectural sketches). Node IDs follow the alpha-prefix rule and labels contain no markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 Diagram: Java Compilation and Execution Pipeline

This diagram shows the **WORA workflow** — the foundation of Java's role in distributed Big Data systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Java Source File Hello java] -->|javac compile| bc[Bytecode Hello class]
    bc -->|ClassLoader loads| meta[Method Area Metadata]
    bc -->|Verification| ver[Bytecode Verifier]
    ver -->|Safe to run| exec[Execution Engine]
    exec -->|Interpret line by line| interp[Interpreter]
    exec -->|Detect hot methods| jit[JIT Compiler]
    jit -->|Native code cache| cache[Code Cache]
    interp --> out[Program Output]
    cache --> out
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The source file is **never** executed directly — it must become bytecode.
- The Bytecode Verifier is a security gate that prevents malicious code.
- The JIT compiler kicks in only for *hot* (frequently-called) methods, optimizing them to native code.

---

## 4.2 Diagram: OOP Four-Pillars Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    oop[Object Oriented Programming]
    oop --> enc[Encapsulation]
    oop --> inh[Inheritance]
    oop --> poly[Polymorphism]
    oop --> abs[Abstraction]

    enc --> encDesc[Bind data and methods. Hide fields via private. Expose via getters and setters.]
    inh --> inhDesc[Acquire parent properties via extends keyword. Single inheritance for classes.]
    poly --> polyDesc[One name many forms. Method overloading compile time. Method overriding runtime.]
    abs --> absDesc[Hide implementation. Use abstract class or interface. Cannot instantiate.]

    inhDesc --> mapEx[Example extends Mapper]
    absDesc --> writableEx[Custom Type implements Writable]
    polyDesc --> dispatchEx[Animal ref equals new Dog. JVM dispatches Dogs speak method]
    encDesc --> beanEx[EmployeeBean with private fields and public getters]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram: Hadoop Mapper Class Inheritance Hierarchy

This is the **most important diagram for Big Data** — it shows exactly how Java inheritance and polymorphism power the Hadoop framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mapperBase[org apache hadoop mapreduce Mapper K1 V1 K2 V2]
    mapperBase -->|abstract method| mapMethod[map K1 key V1 value Context context]
    mapperBase -->|protected setup cleanup run methods| hooks[Lifecycle hooks]
    mapperBase -->|inherited by| myMapper[MyMapper extends Mapper LongWritable Text Text IntWritable]

    myMapper -->|overrides| mapImpl[map method implementation]
    myMapper -->|overrides| setupImpl[setup method for initialization]

    yarn[YARN ApplicationMaster] -->|invokes| myMapper
    yarn -.->|holds reference of type| mapperBase

    ctx[Context object] -->|write key value| outBuff[Map output buffer]
    outBuff -->|shuffle and sort| reducer[Reducer phase]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The base `Mapper` is **abstract** — you cannot instantiate it directly.
- Your `MyMapper` extends it and overrides `map()` (polymorphism).
- YARN's framework code holds a reference **of type `Mapper`** (the parent) and invokes the overridden `map()` on **your subclass** (the child) at runtime — this is dynamic method dispatch.

---

## 4.4 Diagram: Java Collections Framework Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    iter[java lang Iterable]
    iter --> coll[Collection]
    coll --> list[List]
    coll --> set[Set]
    coll --> queue[Queue]

    list --> al[ArrayList]
    list --> ll[LinkedList]

    set --> hs[HashSet]
    set --> ts[TreeSet]

    queue --> pq[PriorityQueue]

    map[Map separate hierarchy] --> hm[HashMap]
    map --> tm[TreeMap]
    map --> ht[Hashtable]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `List`, `Set`, `Queue` all extend `Collection`, which extends `Iterable`.
- `Map` is **not** part of the Collection hierarchy — it has its own root.

---

## 4.5 Diagram: Exception Hierarchy and Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    throwable[java lang Throwable]
    throwable --> err[java lang Error]
    throwable --> exc[java lang Exception]

    err --> soe[StackOverflowError]
    err --> oom[OutOfMemoryError]

    exc --> checked[Checked Exceptions]
    exc --> runtime[RuntimeException unchecked]

    checked --> io[IOException]
    checked --> sql[SQLException]
    checked --> cust[Custom Exception extends Exception]

    runtime --> npe[NullPointerException]
    runtime --> ari[ArithmeticException]
    runtime --> aioob[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Errors** are JVM-level (do not catch).
- **Checked exceptions** must be declared with `throws` or caught.
- **Runtime exceptions** (unchecked) can propagate without declaration.

---

## 4.6 Diagram: Access Modifier Visibility Scope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    priv[private] -->|same class only| cls1[Same Class]
    def[default no keyword] -->|same package| pkg[Same Package]
    prot[protected] -->|package plus subclasses| sub[Subclasses in different packages]
    pub[public] -->|everywhere| world[Entire World]

    cls1 --> def
    pkg --> prot
    sub --> pub
```

---

## 4.7 Diagram: Bytecode-to-Native Execution (JVM Internals)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    jvm[JVM Execution Engine]
    jvm --> interp[Interpreter]
    jvm --> jit[JIT Compiler]
    jvm --> gc[Garbage Collector]

    interp -->|slow path| native1[Native Code]
    jit -->|hot method detection| native2[Optimized Native Code in Code Cache]

    gc -->|young gen| eden[Eden Space]
    gc -->|old gen| tenured[Tenured Space]
    gc -->|marks and sweeps| collect[Reclaims unreachable objects]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The JVM has **three main subsystems** for execution.
- The Interpreter handles cold code; JIT accelerates hot code.
- The Garbage Collector reclaims heap memory automatically — one of the biggest Big Data advantages (no manual `free()` like in C).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section provides the **complete question bank** modeled on KTU 2024 scheme patterns, plus a rapid-revision summary.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define JDK, JRE, and JVM. Explain the relationship between them. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **JVM (Java Virtual Machine):** An abstract runtime engine that loads, verifies, and executes Java bytecode. It is platform-specific.
- **JRE (Java Runtime Environment):** The JVM **plus** the standard class libraries required to **run** Java programs. It is platform-specific.
- **JDK (Java Development Kit):** The JRE **plus** development tools (`javac` compiler, `jar`, `javadoc`, debugger) used to **develop** Java programs. It is platform-specific.

**Relationship (formula):**
$$
\text{JDK} = \text{JRE} \cup \{\text{javac}, \text{jar}, \text{javadoc}, \text{debugger}\}
$$
$$
\text{JRE} = \text{JVM} \cup \{\text{standard libraries}\}
$$

> **Memory trick:** **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM** (the JVM is the innermost core).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between `==` and `.equals()` in Java. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `==` | `.equals()` |
|--------|------|-------------|
| **Compares** | Reference (memory address) for objects; value for primitives | Logical value of the object |
| **Defined in** | Java language operator (not overridable) | `Object` class method (overridable) |
| **For primitives** | Compares values (e.g., `5 == 5` → true) | Not applicable (primitives have no methods) |
| **For Strings** | `s1 == s2` true only if same object | `s1.equals(s2)` true if same content |
| **KTU example** | `String a = "hi"; String b = new String("hi"); a == b` → **false** | `a.equals(b)` → **true** |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** (a student answers ONE of two options). Below, both **Option A** and **Option B** are provided.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming in Java with suitable examples.**

#### Part (a) — Encapsulation and Inheritance (7 marks)

**Encapsulation (3.5 marks):**

**Definition:** Encapsulation is the bundling of data (fields) and methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class), combined with **access restriction** to the internal state via access modifiers.

**Code example:**
```java
class BankAccount {
    private double balance;     // private = hidden

    public void deposit(double amt) {
        if (amt > 0) balance += amt;   // controlled write
    }
    public double getBalance() {
        return balance;                // controlled read
    }
}
```

**Key benefits:**
1. **Data hiding:** Internal state cannot be corrupted from outside.
2. **Validation:** The `deposit()` method enforces `amt > 0`.
3. **Maintainability:** Internal implementation can change without affecting callers.

**Inheritance (3.5 marks):**

**Definition:** A mechanism by which a child class acquires (extends) the fields and methods of a parent class, promoting code reuse and establishing an *is-a* relationship.

**Code example:**
```java
class Animal {
    String name;
    Animal(String n) { this.name = n; }
    void eat() { System.out.println(name + " eats."); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog(String n) { super(n); }          // call parent constructor
    void bark() { System.out.println("Woof!"); }
}
public class TestInh {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog("Bruno");
        d.eat();     // inherited method
        d.bark();    // own method
    }
}
```

**Key types:**
- **Single inheritance:** `class B extends A` (Java allows only one parent class).
- **Multilevel inheritance:** `C extends B extends A`.
- **Hierarchical inheritance:** Multiple children extend one parent.

**Why it matters in Big Data:** `MyMapper extends Mapper<K1,V1,K2,V2>` — your class inherits the framework's lifecycle methods (`setup`, `map`, `cleanup`, `run`) and you only override what you need.

---

#### Part (b) — Polymorphism and Abstraction (7 marks)

**Polymorphism (3.5 marks):**

**Definition:** *Poly* (many) + *morph* (forms) — the same method name behaves differently based on the object type at runtime.

**Two types:**

1. **Compile-time (Method Overloading):** Multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** in the same class.
   ```java
   int add(int a, int b)         { return a + b; }
   double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
   ```

2. **Runtime (Method Overriding):** A subclass provides a **new implementation** for an inherited method. Resolved at runtime by the JVM using the **vtable** mechanism.
   ```java
   class Animal { void speak() { System.out.println("..."); } }
   class Dog extends Animal {
       @Override
       void speak() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
   }
   public class Test {
       public static void main(String[] args) {
           Animal a = new Dog();   // parent reference, child object
           a.speak();              // → "Bark" (runtime dispatch)
       }
   }
   ```

**Abstraction (3.5 marks):**

**Definition:** Hiding implementation details and showing only the essential features to the user.

**Two vehicles:**

1. **Abstract Class** — `abstract class Shape { abstract double area(); }`. Can have both abstract and concrete methods. Cannot be instantiated.

2. **Interface** — `interface Writable { void write(DataOutput out) throws IOException; }`. (Pre-Java 8: 100% abstract. Java 8+: can have `default` and `static` methods.)

**Big Data example:**
```java
public interface Writable {
    void write(DataOutput out) throws IOException;
    void readFields(DataInput in) throws IOException;
}
// Hadoop's IntWritable implements Writable
```

**Key difference between abstract class and interface:**

| Aspect | Abstract Class | Interface |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| Keyword | `abstract class` | `interface` |
| Methods | Abstract + concrete | Abstract (Java 7); abstract + default + static (Java 8+) |
| Variables | Any | `public static final` only |
| Inheritance | `extends` (single) | `implements` (multiple allowed) |
| Constructor | Can have | Cannot have |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain Java's Exception Handling mechanism with a custom exception. Also explain the Java Collections Framework hierarchy.**

#### Part (a) — Exception Handling with Custom Exception (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Exception handling is a mechanism to handle runtime errors gracefully, separating error-handling code from normal business logic.

**Hierarchy (2 marks):**
```
Throwable
 ├── Error         (JVM-level, unrecoverable — StackOverflowError, OutOfMemoryError)
 └── Exception     (recoverable)
      ├── IOException (checked)
      ├── SQLException (checked)
      └── RuntimeException (unchecked — NullPointerException, ArithmeticException)
```

**Five keywords (1 mark):** `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, `throws`.

**Complete code example (3 marks):**
```java
// Custom checked exception
class InsufficientFundsException extends Exception {
    public InsufficientFundsException(String msg) { super(msg); }
}
class Account {
    private double balance = 1000.0;
    public void withdraw(double amt) throws InsufficientFundsException {
        if (amt > balance)
            throw new InsufficientFundsException("Insufficient balance. Requested: " + amt);
        balance -= amt;
        System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amt + ", New Balance: " + balance);
    }
}
public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account acc = new Account();
        try {
            acc.withdraw(500);
            acc.withdraw(700);   // throws
        } catch (InsufficientFundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Transaction complete.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Withdrawn: 500.0, New Balance: 500.0
Caught: Insufficient balance. Requested: 700.0
Transaction complete.
```

**Explanation of flow:**
1. First `withdraw(500)` succeeds → balance becomes 500.
2. Second `withdraw(700)` triggers `throw` → method aborts.
3. Exception propagates to `main()` → `catch` block matches and handles it.
4. `finally` block **always** executes (cleanup logic).

---

#### Part (b) — Java Collections Framework (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The Java Collections Framework (JCF) is a unified architecture for representing and manipulating groups of objects. It provides interfaces, implementations, and algorithms.

**Core interface hierarchy (3 marks):**
```
Iterable
 └── Collection
      ├── List       (ordered, duplicates allowed)
      │     ├── ArrayList
      │     └── LinkedList
      ├── Set        (no duplicates)
      │     ├── HashSet
      │     └── TreeSet
      └── Queue      (FIFO)
            └── PriorityQueue
Map (separate hierarchy)
 ├── HashMap
 ├── TreeMap
 └── Hashtable
```

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Feature | `ArrayList` | `LinkedList` | `HashSet` | `HashMap` |
|---------|-------------|--------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Backing** | Resizable array | Doubly-linked list | Hash table | Hash table of entries |
| **Duplicates** | Allowed | Allowed | Not allowed | Unique keys |
| **Order** | Insertion | Insertion | None | None |
| **get(i)** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ (by key) |
| **add** | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(1)$ at ends | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Use case** | Random access | Frequent inserts/deletes | Unique elements | Key-value lookup |

**Big Data Use:** Hadoop's `Context.write(key, value)` uses an internal `Map` (or buffer) to collect map outputs. Spark's `JavaPairRDD.reduceByKey()` uses a `HashMap`-like combiner.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**

1. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()`** (1–2 marks lost): Always state that `==` compares references for objects, while `.equals()` compares content.
2. **Forgetting `throws` declaration in `map()`** (compile-time error in Hadoop code): Always include `throws IOException, InterruptedException` in your Mapper method.
3. **Writing `interface` method with a body (pre-Java 8):** All interface methods are `public abstract` by default — you cannot provide a body.
4. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation:** Without it, the method becomes *overloaded* instead of *overridden* — the parent class method is silently invoked.
5. **Saying "HashMap is always $O(1)$":** Always specify *"average case $O(1)$, worst case $O(n)$ due to hash collisions."*
6. **Confusing abstract class with interface:** They look similar but differ in: (a) abstract class can have concrete methods, (b) interface supports multiple inheritance, (c) interface variables are `public static final`.
7. **Catching `Throwable` or `Error`:** This is bad practice. Catch only specific `Exception` subtypes.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Java:**

### 🟢 Fundamentals
- Java is **WORA** (Write Once Run Anywhere) thanks to bytecode + JVM.
- **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM** — develop with JDK, run with JRE, JVM is the runtime engine.
- Java has **8 primitive types**; `int` and `double` are defaults for numeric literals.
- `String` is **immutable**; always compare with `.equals()`, never `==`.

### 🟢 OOP Four Pillars
- **Encapsulation:** `private` fields + `public` getters/setters.
- **Inheritance:** `class Child extends Parent` — single inheritance only.
- **Polymorphism:** Overloading (compile-time) + Overriding (runtime via dynamic dispatch).
- **Abstraction:** `abstract class` (0–100% abstract) or `interface` (was 100% abstract pre-Java 8).

### 🟢 Access Modifiers (most → least restrictive)
- `private` → `default` → `protected` → `public`.

### 🟢 Memory Model
- **Stack** = primitives, references, method frames (thread-local, fast).
- **Heap** = objects (shared, GC-managed, can throw `OutOfMemoryError`).

### 🟢 Exception Handling
- **5 keywords:** `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, `throws`.
- **Checked** exceptions must be declared or caught; **unchecked** (Runtime) need not.
- `finally` block **always** executes — use for resource cleanup.
- `Error` (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) is JVM-level — never catch.

### 🟢 Collections Framework
- `List` (ordered + duplicates) → `ArrayList` ($O(1)$ access), `LinkedList` ($O(1)$ insert at ends).
- `Set` (no duplicates) → `HashSet` ($O(1)$), `TreeSet` (sorted, $O(\log n)$).
- `Map` is **not** a Collection — its own hierarchy. `HashMap` has $O(1)$ average lookup.
- **Generics** provide compile-time type safety; type erasure occurs at runtime.

### 🟢 Big Data Connection (always cite in answers)
- Hadoop `Mapper`/`Reducer` are **abstract base classes** — you extend and override `map()`/`reduce()`.
- Hadoop `Writable` is an **interface** — every Hadoop data type implements it for serialization.
- **Polymorphism** allows YARN to invoke your custom mapper via a parent reference.
- **Generics** parameterize Mapper/Reducer: `Mapper<K1,V1,K2,V2>`.
- **Exceptions** thrown in `map()` must be `IOException, InterruptedException` (or wrapped in `RuntimeException`).
- **JVM** portability lets the same Hadoop JAR run on heterogeneous cluster nodes — this is **WORA in action**.

### 🟢 Common KTU Exam Triggers
- *"Justify Java for Big Data"* → WORA + multithreading + GC + ecosystem.
- *"Differentiate overloading vs overriding"* → compile-time vs runtime.
- *"Why is `String` immutable?"* → security, caching, thread-safety, hash key stability.
- *"Explain dynamic method dispatch"* → vtable lookup, runtime resolution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HBase and Stream Data Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HBase and Stream Data Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: HBase and Stream Data Model

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 HBase — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HBase** is an open-source, distributed, non-relational (NoSQL), versioned, column-oriented database modeled after Google's **Bigtable**. It runs on top of the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** and provides fault-tolerant, random, real-time read/write access to large-scale sparse datasets.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, HBase is classified as a **wide-column store** (also called an *extensible record store*) — a category that is neither a pure row-store (RDBMS) nor a pure column-store (analytical warehouses like Vertica), but a hybrid that groups columns into **column families** stored together on disk.

### 1.2 Stream Data Model — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stream Data Model** is a representation of data as continuous, unbounded, ordered (or partially ordered) sequences of records (events) that arrive at high velocity in real-time, where querying is performed on data-in-motion rather than data-at-rest.

The **Stream Data Model** is the conceptual foundation for systems like **Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, Apache Storm, and Spark Streaming**, which handle event streams such as clickstreams, sensor telemetry, financial ticks, and server logs.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **HBase Analogy — "A Sorted Filing Cabinet Distributed Across Branches"**
>
> Imagine a massive library where:
> - **Books (rows)** are arranged in strictly alphabetical order by a unique *call number* (the **row key**).
> - Each book contains several **labeled sections (column families)** — e.g., "Personal Info", "Address", "Transaction History".
> - A new book version simply **adds a new page (timestamped cell)** — the old page is never deleted instantly; it's kept for versioning.
> - The library has many **branches (RegionServers)**, each storing a slice of the collection. A **head librarian (HMaster)** tracks which branch holds which slice.
> - A **central coordinator (ZooKeeper)** keeps all branches in sync, like a real-time directory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stream Data Model Analogy — "A Fire Hydrant vs. A Bucket"**
>
> - A **bucket** is a traditional database: you wait for it to fill (batch), then you analyze.
> - A **fire hydrant** is a data stream: water (data) is **continuously gushing out**, and you must process it **as it flows past** — you cannot store it all in the bucket. You need **real-time taps** (stream processors) to drink/analyze the water as it passes.

---

### 1.4 Core Terminology Block

| Term | Definition (KTU-aligned) |
|---|---|
| **Row Key** | A unique byte-string identifier for a row; HBase is sorted lexicographically by this key. |
| **Column Family (CF)** | A logical grouping of columns stored together on disk; defined at table creation. |
| **Column Qualifier (CQ)** | A sub-column under a CF; can be dynamically created at write time. |
| **Cell** | The intersection of row, CF, CQ, and timestamp; holds the actual value. |
| **Timestamp** | A 64-bit integer used for versioning each cell. |
| **Namespace** | A logical grouping of tables (analogous to a database in RDBMS). |
| **Region** | A contiguous range of rows (sorted by row key) that are served by a single RegionServer. |
| **MemStore** | An in-memory write buffer where data is staged before flush to HFile. |
| **HFile** | The on-disk, immutable, sorted file format used by HBase. |
| **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** | An append-only log ensuring durability before MemStore write. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HBase Data Model — Deep Dive

The HBase data model is best described as a **sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map**. Formally:

$$
\text{Map} : (\text{RowKey}, \text{ColumnFamily}, \text{ColumnQualifier}, \text{Timestamp}) \rightarrow \text{Value}
$$

### Key Logical Properties:

- **Sorted by Row Key**: Rows are stored in **lexicographic (byte-wise) order** of the row key. This is the single most important design decision — all access patterns revolve around it.
- **Column-Oriented Storage**: A row's data is stored column-family-by-column-family, not row-by-row. This means columns within the same CF are physically adjacent on disk.
- **Sparse**: Empty cells are **not stored** — a cell exists only if a value is written. This is critical for wide tables with many columns.
- **Versioned**: Every cell can have multiple versions, identified by timestamp. Default max versions = **3**.
- **Flexible Schema**: Column Qualifiers are not pre-declared — they can be created on-the-fly during writes.

### Logical View vs. Physical View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **logical view**, a table looks like a 2-D grid (rows × columns). However, in **physical storage**, only non-empty cells with the same row key + column family are stored as a contiguous HFile block on disk. This logical-to-physical transformation is what makes HBase "sparse and wide".

---

## 2.2 HBase Architecture — Component-by-Component

### 2.2.1 ZooKeeper

- Maintains **server state** and **coordination** of distributed services.
- Tracks which servers are **alive** and available.
- Provides a **failover mechanism** for HMaster.
- Stores **META table location** (which RegionServer holds the `-ROOT-` or `hbase:meta`).

### 2.2.2 HMaster

- Manages and monitors **RegionServer** instances.
- Performs **region assignment** and **load balancing**.
- Handles **DDL operations** (create, delete, alter tables).
- Responsible for **schema changes** and metadata operations.

### 2.2.3 RegionServer

- Serves data for **read/write requests** to a set of regions.
- Holds:
  - **MemStore** (in-memory, sorted buffer).
  - **BlockCache** (in-memory read cache for HFiles).
  - **WAL** (write-ahead log on local disk or HDFS).
- Performs **flushes** (MemStore → HFile) and **compactions** (merge small HFiles into larger ones).

### 2.2.4 HDFS

- Provides the **underlying durable storage** for HBase data.
- Stores **HFiles, WAL, and metadata**.
- Replication factor typically **3** for fault tolerance.

---

## 2.3 Write Path — Step-by-Step

1. Client writes to **WAL** (for durability).
2. Data is placed into the appropriate **MemStore** (in-memory, sorted by row key + column).
3. Client receives acknowledgement.
4. When MemStore reaches threshold (default **128 MB**), it is **flushed** to an HFile on HDFS.

## 2.4 Read Path — Step-by-Step

1. Client queries **ZooKeeper** to locate the `hbase:meta` table.
2. `hbase:meta` provides the **RegionServer** for the requested row key range.
3. Client queries that RegionServer.
4. RegionServer consults **BlockCache** (recently read data) → **MemStore** (recently written data) → **HFiles** (old data on disk).
5. Result merged and returned, with version selection based on timestamp.

---

## 2.5 Stream Data Model — Deep Dive

### 2.5.1 Formal Definition

A data stream is a sequence of data items:

$$
S = \langle s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots, s_n, \ldots \rangle
$$

Each element $s_i$ is a **tuple** $(key, value, timestamp)$ — often referred to as a **record** or **event**.

### 2.5.2 Core Characteristics of Streams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Unbounded** — Streams have no defined start or end.
> 2. **Ordered (partially)** — Often timestamped; order is usually by time of arrival or event time.
> 3. **High-Velocity** — Rate can be millions of events/second.
> 4. **Immutable** — Once an event is emitted, it cannot be altered.
> 5. **Real-time** — Processing must occur with low latency (milliseconds to seconds).

### 2.5.3 Stream Processing Operations

| Operation Type | Description | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Filtering** | Select events matching a predicate | `temperature > 100°C` |
| **Mapping** | Transform one stream into another | Convert JSON to Avro |
| **Windowing** | Aggregate over time/count/session boundaries | Tumbling 5-min window average |
| **Joining** | Combine two streams (stream-stream, stream-table) | Match clickstream with user table |
| **Aggregation** | Compute count, sum, avg, min, max | Total transactions per minute |

### 2.5.4 Windowing Types (Critical for Examinations)

- **Tumbling Window**: Fixed-size, non-overlapping. $[0, 5), [5, 10), [10, 15), \ldots$
- **Sliding Window**: Fixed-size, overlapping. $[0, 5), [2, 7), [4, 9), \ldots$
- **Session Window**: Dynamically sized based on inactivity gap.
- **Count Window**: Triggered by a fixed number of events (e.g., every 100 events).

### 2.5.5 Time Notions in Stream Processing

> [!NOTE]
> - **Event Time**: When the event actually occurred at the source.
> - **Processing Time**: When the event is processed by the system.
> - **Ingestion Time**: When the event enters the stream processing pipeline.
> - **Watermarks**: Logical timestamps that signal the progress of event time in the system.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| Concept | Key Value / Formula | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Cell lookup | $(\text{RowKey}, \text{CF}, \text{CQ}, \text{Timestamp}) \rightarrow \text{Value}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Core data model |
| MemStore flush threshold | **128 MB** (default) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| HFile block size | **64 KB** (default) | ⭐⭐ |
| WAL replication | Replicated via HDFS (default RF=**3**) | ⭐⭐ |
| Max cell versions (default) | **3** | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| HMaster failover | Managed by **ZooKeeper** | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `hbase:meta` location | Stored in **ZooKeeper** | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Stream time domains | Event / Processing / Ingestion | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Window size formula | For sliding: $\text{slide} \le \text{size}$ | ⭐⭐ |
| HBase consistency model | **Strong consistency** for single row | ⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logical-to-Physical Storage in HBase

The logical view of an HBase table can be represented as a 4-D tensor indexed by (row, column family, column qualifier, timestamp). The physical storage collapses this along the time axis (retaining only non-empty cells) and along the column-qualifier axis (grouping by column family).

### Step 1 — Logical Cell Definition

$$
C = (r, f, q, t, v)
$$

where $r$ = row key, $f$ = column family, $q$ = column qualifier, $t$ = timestamp, $v$ = value.

### Step 2 — Sparse Projection

For a given row $r$, define the set of existing cells as:

$$
\mathcal{C}(r) = \{(f, q, t, v) : v \neq \emptyset\}
$$

The physical storage only persists $\mathcal{C}(r)$, not the Cartesian product of all possible $(f, q)$ pairs.

### Step 3 — Column Family Grouping

Cells belonging to the same row and same column family are stored together:

$$
\mathcal{C}_f(r) = \{(q, t, v) \in \mathcal{C}(r) : f = \text{this family}\}
$$

### Step 4 — HFile Layout

The HFile is the on-disk representation. Its block layout follows:

$$
\text{HFile} = \langle \text{Data Blocks}, \text{Index Blocks}, \text{Bloom Filter}, \text{Metadata}, \text{Trailer} \rangle
$$

- **Data Blocks**: Contain the KV pairs (KeyValue), sorted by (row key, column family, column qualifier, timestamp descending).
- **Index Blocks**: Multi-level block index for fast seek.
- **Bloom Filter**: Probabilistic structure — answers *"does this HFile contain key K?"* with O(1) lookup.
- **Trailer**: Pointer to all other sections.

### Step 5 — Bloom Filter Math (Probabilistic False Positive)

A Bloom filter with $m$ bits and $k$ hash functions for $n$ inserted elements has a **false positive probability** of:

$$
p_{fp} = \left(1 - e^{-\frac{kn}{m}}\right)^k
$$

> **Conversion Logic**: This explains why HBase can answer *"Is this row in this HFile?"* in constant time *without* reading the file, drastically reducing read I/O.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: HBase vs RDBMS vs Stream Processing

| Dimension | HBase | RDBMS (e.g., MySQL) | Stream Processing (Flink/Storm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Wide-column, sparse | Row-oriented, dense | Unbounded event sequences |
| Schema | Flexible (CQ dynamic) | Rigid (predefined) | Schema-on-read |
| Latency | Milliseconds | Milliseconds–seconds (write), varies (read) | Milliseconds (sub-second) |
| Throughput | Very high (millions ops/s) | Limited (thousands ops/s) | Very high (millions events/s) |
| Consistency | **Strong** (per-row) | ACID | Eventual (exactly-once/at-least-once) |
| Best For | Random read/write on Big Data | OLTP, complex joins | Real-time analytics, CEP |
| Query Language | API (Get, Scan), Phoenix SQL | SQL | DataStream API, SQL |
| Storage | HDFS (distributed) | Local/Network block storage | In-memory + external sink |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: How a Write Flows Through HBase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a high-frequency KTU question. Walk through each step.

**Given**: A client wants to write a cell $(row1, cf1:cq1, value="Kerala", timestamp=T)$.

**Step 1 — Region Lookup**
The client asks ZooKeeper for the `hbase:meta` table location. `hbase:meta` returns the RegionServer hosting the region containing `row1`.

**Step 2 — WAL Append**
The client appends the record to the WAL of that RegionServer. This is the durability guarantee — even if the RegionServer crashes before flushing, the WAL is replayed.

**Step 3 — MemStore Insert**
The KV pair is added to the **MemStore** of the appropriate column family. The MemStore is a sorted in-memory structure (ConcurrentSkipListMap).

**Step 4 — Acknowledgement**
The client receives a successful write response **only after the WAL sync is complete**.

**Step 5 — Asynchronous Flush**
When the MemStore reaches the **flush size (128 MB)** threshold, a flush is triggered:
- A new HFile is created on HDFS.
- The MemStore is cleared.
- The WAL is archived.

**Step 6 — Compaction (Background)**
Periodically, the RegionServer runs **minor compaction** (merges small HFiles) and **major compaction** (merges all HFiles into one, deleting obsolete versions).

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Stream Window Aggregation Example

**Given**: A stream of stock trade events $E = \{(AAPL, 150, 09:30:01), (GOOG, 2800, 09:30:02), (AAPL, 152, 09:30:15), \ldots\}$.

**Task**: Compute the average price of each stock in a **tumbling 1-minute window**.

**Step 1 — Window Assignment**
For event $e$ with event time $t_e$, the window is:
$$
W(t_e) = \left(\left\lfloor \frac{t_e - T_0}{60s} \right\rfloor \cdot 60s + T_0, \ \left\lfloor \frac{t_e - T_0}{60s} \right\rfloor \cdot 60s + T_0 + 60s \right)
$$

where $T_0$ = window start reference (epoch 0).

**Step 2 — Aggregation per Window**
For each window $W_k$ and each stock $s$, maintain:
$$
\text{sum}_s(k) = \sum_{e \in W_k, e.\text{stock}=s} e.\text{price}, \quad
\text{count}_s(k) = \sum_{e \in W_k, e.\text{stock}=s} 1
$$

**Step 3 — Result Emission**
$$
\overline{\text{price}}_s(k) = \frac{\text{sum}_s(k)}{\text{count}_s(k)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic**: This is the **incremental aggregation pattern** used in Flink/Spark Streaming, where state is updated per event and result is emitted at watermark completion.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Why Row Key Design Is Critical

Given a row key $r$ of length $L$ bytes, the storage cost per cell is:

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{cell}} = L + \text{len}(f) + \text{len}(q) + 8 + \text{len}(v)
$$

where the **8 bytes** are the timestamp. Every HFile index entry must include the full row key.

**Implication — Hotspotting**:
If a poorly designed row key (e.g., timestamp prefix) causes new writes to concentrate in a single region, **one RegionServer** handles all writes → **write hotspot**. The fix is **salting** (prefix a hash) or **field-promotion** (use a high-cardinality prefix).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HBase Master–RegionServer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Application] -->|Get / Put / Scan| zk[ZooKeeper Ensemble]
    zk -->|Location of hbase:meta| client
    client -->|Direct read/write| rs1[RegionServer 1]
    client -->|Direct read/write| rs2[RegionServer 2]
    client -->|Direct read/write| rs3[RegionServer 3]
    hmaster[HMaster] -->|Assigns regions| rs1
    hmaster -->|Assigns regions| rs2
    hmaster -->|Assigns regions| rs3
    hmaster -.->|Heartbeat| zk
    rs1 -->|Read/Write| hdfs1[HDFS DataNode 1]
    rs2 -->|Read/Write| hdfs2[HDFS DataNode 2]
    rs3 -->|Read/Write| hdfs3[HDFS DataNode 3]
    rs1 -->|WAL + HFiles| hdfs1
    rs2 -->|WAL + HFiles| hdfs2
    rs3 -->|WAL + HFiles| hdfs3
```

**Explanation**:
- The **Client** never talks to HMaster for data operations — only ZooKeeper is consulted for metadata location.
- **HMaster** is a *lightweight coordinator* responsible for region assignment, load balancing, and DDL.
- All actual data I/O happens directly between Client ↔ RegionServer ↔ HDFS.
- **ZooKeeper** is the *single source of truth* for cluster state and is also used for HMaster election (HA).

---

## 4.2 HBase Internal Storage — RegionServer Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph rs[RegionServer]
        wal[WAL - Write Ahead Log]
        bc[BlockCache - Read Cache]
        ms1[MemStore CF1]
        ms2[MemStore CF2]
        reg1[Region for rows A to M]
        reg2[Region for rows N to Z]
    end
    wal -->|Flush trigger| ms1
    wal -->|Flush trigger| ms2
    ms1 -->|Flush at 128MB| hf1[HFile 1]
    ms1 -->|Flush at 128MB| hf2[HFile 2]
    ms2 -->|Flush at 128MB| hf3[HFile 3]
    bc -.->|Cached reads| hf1
    bc -.->|Cached reads| hf2
    bc -.->|Cached reads| hf3
    reg1 --- wal
    reg2 --- wal
```

**Explanation**:
- Each column family has its **own MemStore** — flushing one does not block the other.
- **BlockCache** serves hot reads; **MemStore** buffers hot writes; **HFiles** are the cold immutable storage tier.
- This three-tier hierarchy (BlockCache → MemStore → HFiles) is the heart of HBase's read performance.

---

## 4.3 HBase Logical Data Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tbl[HBase Table: Employee]
        direction TB
        subgraph r1[Row Key = emp001]
            c1[personal:name = "Ravi"]
            c2[personal:age = "28"]
            c3[address:city = "Kochi"]
            c4[address:zip = "682001"]
            c5[address:city = "Kochi v2 - older"]
        end
        subgraph r2[Row Key = emp002]
            d1[personal:name = "Anu"]
            d2[salary:base = "50000"]
        end
    end
    c1 -.->|timestamp T3| r1
    c2 -.->|timestamp T2| r1
    c3 -.->|timestamp T5 latest| r1
    c4 -.->|timestamp T5| r1
    c5 -.->|timestamp T1 oldest| r1
```

**Explanation**:
- Row keys `emp001` and `emp002` are sorted lexicographically.
- Column families `personal`, `address`, and `salary` group related columns.
- The cell `address:city` for `emp001` has **two versions**: T1 (older "Kochi") and T5 (latest "Kochi v2"). The `T1` version is retained until compaction deletes it.

---

## 4.4 Stream Data Model Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[IoT Sensors] -->|Event Stream| kafka[Apache Kafka Broker]
    src2[Web Clickstream] -->|Event Stream| kafka
    src3[App Logs] -->|Event Stream| kafka
    kafka -->|Topic: raw_events| flink[Apache Flink Job]
    flink -->|Filter / Map| stage1[Stage 1 - Cleansing]
    stage1 -->|Window 1 min| stage2[Stage 2 - Windowed Aggregation]
    stage2 -->|Watermark trigger| stage3[Stage 3 - Sink]
    stage3 --> sink1[(HBase - Real-time OLTP)]
    stage3 --> sink2[(Dashboard / Alerting)]
    stage3 --> sink3[(Object Store - Cold Archive)]
```

**Explanation**:
- **Sources** emit events continuously.
- **Kafka** decouples producers from consumers and provides replayable, ordered, partitioned logs.
- **Flink** performs stream processing with stateful operators, windowing, and event-time semantics.
- The final **sink** may be HBase (for low-latency random lookup), dashboards, or cold storage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the HBase data model with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

> The HBase data model is a sparse, multi-dimensional, sorted map indexed by **RowKey, ColumnFamily, ColumnQualifier, and Timestamp**. A table consists of rows identified by a unique row key, sorted lexicographically. Each row contains multiple **column families** (defined at table creation), and each column family contains dynamic **column qualifiers**. Cells are versioned by timestamps, and empty cells are not stored.

**Example**: Consider a `Student` table with row keys `S001`, `S002`, …, and column families `profile` and `academics`.

| RowKey | profile:name | profile:age | academics:cgpa | academics:sem |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S001 | Ravi | 21 | 8.9 | 6 |
| S002 | Anu | 22 | 9.1 | 7 |

Here, `profile` and `academics` are the two column families. The model is sparse — if a student has no `cgpa` yet, the cell is simply absent, not stored as NULL.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between batch processing and stream processing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Batch Processing | Stream Processing |
|---|---|---|
| Data nature | Bounded, finite datasets | Unbounded, infinite sequences |
| Latency | High (minutes to hours) | Low (milliseconds to seconds) |
| Data size | Very large, known in advance | Continuous, unknown in advance |
| Processing time | Job time | Event time / processing time |
| Examples | Hadoop MapReduce, Spark Batch | Apache Flink, Spark Streaming, Storm |
| Use case | Historical analytics, reporting | Real-time fraud detection, monitoring |
| Storage | Process-then-store (HDFS) | Process-as-it-arrives (in-memory) |
| Recomputation | Easy (re-run job) | Hard (must replay stream or use checkpoints) |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the HBase architecture in detail. Explain the roles of HMaster, RegionServer, ZooKeeper, and HDFS. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the HBase data model with an example. What are column families, column qualifiers, cells, and timestamps? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**HBase Architecture — Detailed Explanation**

HBase follows a **master-slave architecture** built on top of HDFS, with ZooKeeper providing coordination.

**1. ZooKeeper**
- Acts as the **centralized coordination service**.
- Maintains a list of available RegionServers and HMaster status.
- Stores the location of the `hbase:meta` table (which holds region-to-RegionServer mapping).
- Handles HMaster **leader election** in HA setups.

**2. HMaster**
- The master node responsible for **administrative operations**.
- Performs **region assignment** — distributes regions across RegionServers.
- Handles **load balancing** — moves regions from overloaded to underloaded servers.
- Manages **DDL operations** — table creation, deletion, schema changes.
- Monitors RegionServer health via ZooKeeper heartbeats.

**3. RegionServer**
- The workhorse — handles all **read/write requests** for the regions it hosts.
- Components inside a RegionServer:
  - **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** — append-only log for durability.
  - **MemStore** — in-memory sorted write buffer per column family.
  - **BlockCache** — LRU in-memory read cache.
  - **HFiles** — on-disk sorted files (actual data).
- Performs **flushes** (MemStore → HFile) and **compactions** (HFile merges).

**4. HDFS**
- Provides **durable, replicated storage** for HBase data.
- Stores HFiles, WAL, and `hbase:meta` table.
- Default replication factor of **3** ensures fault tolerance.

**Workflow**:
- Client first contacts ZooKeeper for `hbase:meta` location.
- Then contacts the relevant RegionServer directly for read/write.
- HMaster is involved only in administrative tasks, not in the data path.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**HBase Data Model — Detailed**

The HBase data model is conceptually a **sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map** with the following dimensions:

$$
\text{(RowKey, ColumnFamily:ColumnQualifier, Timestamp)} \rightarrow \text{Value}
$$

**Core Elements**:

1. **Row Key**: A unique byte array identifying a row. Rows are stored in **lexicographic order** by row key.
2. **Column Family**: A logical grouping of columns, defined at table creation. Stored together on disk. Example: `personal`, `address`.
3. **Column Qualifier**: A sub-column within a family, created on-the-fly at write time. Example: `personal:name`, `personal:age`.
4. **Cell**: The (row, family, qualifier, timestamp) tuple holding a value.
5. **Timestamp**: A 64-bit integer identifying a version. Default = system time at write.

**Example — Employee Table**:

| Row Key | personal:name | personal:age | address:city | salary:base |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E001 | Ravi | 28 | Kochi | 50000 |
| E002 | Anu | 32 | Trivandrum | 60000 |
| E003 | Manu | 25 | Calicut | 45000 |

**Key Properties**:
- **Sparse**: If E003 has no `salary:bonus` cell, it is simply not stored.
- **Versioned**: Updating `address:city` for E001 to "Bangalore" creates a new version with a new timestamp; the old "Kochi" version is retained until compaction.
- **Wide and Tall**: There can be billions of rows and millions of columns per row.
- **Schema-flexible**: Different rows can have different column qualifiers within the same family.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Stream Data Model in detail. Discuss its characteristics and challenges. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Describe the various windowing techniques used in stream processing with examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Stream Data Model — Detailed Explanation**

A **data stream** is an unbounded, continuous, ordered sequence of records (events) arriving at high velocity in real time.

**Formal Representation**:
$$
S = \langle s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots \rangle, \quad s_i = (key_i, value_i, timestamp_i)
$$

**Key Characteristics**:

1. **Unbounded**: No defined start or end — the stream is theoretically infinite.
2. **High Velocity**: Millions of events per second (e.g., Twitter, IoT sensors).
3. **Ordered (partially)**: Events have timestamps; total order is often infeasible due to network delays.
4. **Immutable**: Once emitted, an event cannot be modified (only new events can correct it).
5. **Heterogeneous**: Different events may have different schemas.
6. **Real-time**: Processing must keep up with the arrival rate to avoid backlog.

**Challenges**:

1. **Latency vs. Throughput Trade-off**: Achieving sub-second latency at millions of events/s is hard.
2. **Out-of-Order Events**: Events may arrive late, requiring **watermarks** and **event-time processing**.
3. **State Management**: Stateful operations (aggregations, joins) require **distributed, fault-tolerant state**.
4. **Exactly-Once Semantics**: Hard to achieve in distributed stream processing; systems use **checkpointing** and **idempotent sinks**.
5. **Time Skew**: Different notions of time (event vs. processing) cause non-determinism.
6. **Backpressure**: When the consumer is slower than the producer, the system must apply backpressure or buffer.

**Real-world Examples**: Stock tick streams, clickstream analytics, fraud detection, IoT sensor monitoring, social media feeds.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Windowing Techniques in Stream Processing**

Windowing is the mechanism to bound an unbounded stream into finite subsets for aggregation.

**1. Tumbling Window**
- Fixed-size, **non-overlapping** intervals.
- Each event belongs to **exactly one** window.
- Example: Count page views per 5-minute interval.
- $[0,5), [5,10), [10,15), \ldots$

**2. Sliding Window**
- Fixed-size, **overlapping** intervals.
- Defined by a *size* and a *slide* (step) parameter.
- Example: 10-minute window sliding every 2 minutes.
- $[0,10), [2,12), [4,14), \ldots$
- Constraint: $\text{slide} \le \text{size}$.

**3. Session Window**
- **Dynamically sized** based on user activity gaps.
- A new window starts when an inactivity gap exceeds a threshold.
- Example: User browsing session; window closes after 30 minutes of inactivity.

**4. Count Window (Global)**
- Triggered by event count rather than time.
- Example: Aggregate every 1000 events.

**5. Time-based Variants**:
- **Event-time windows**: Driven by the event's own timestamp.
- **Processing-time windows**: Driven by the system clock at arrival.

**Example — Tumbling Window of 1 Minute on Stock Prices**:
- Window $[09:30, 09:31)$: avg(AAPL) = 150.5
- Window $[09:31, 09:32)$: avg(AAPL) = 152.1

**Why Windowing Matters**:
- Streams are infinite — you cannot "group all events" the way you can in a batch job.
- Windowing makes aggregation, ranking, and pattern detection tractable.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic (costly marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing HBase with HDFS** — HBase is a *database* on top of HDFS; HDFS is just a *file system*. Do not interchange them.
> 2. **Drawing the HMaster in the data path** — HMaster does NOT handle client reads/writes. Clients go through ZooKeeper → RegionServer directly.
> 3. **Writing row keys as timestamps** — This causes *write hotspotting*. Always justify row key design.
> 4. **Mixing up the four stream time domains** — Event time ≠ Processing time ≠ Ingestion time. State which one your window uses.
> 5. **Forgetting that empty cells are not stored** — A common answer mistake is to claim HBase "stores NULLs".
> 6. **Confusing `HBase:meta` with ZooKeeper** — ZooKeeper stores the *location* of `hbase:meta`; `hbase:meta` itself is a real HBase table.
> 7. **Forgetting that column qualifiers are dynamic** — Many students wrongly say "schema is fixed like RDBMS".

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - [ ] HBase is a **wide-column, distributed, NoSQL** store built on HDFS, modeled after Google Bigtable.
> - [ ] Data model = **sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map** indexed by (Row, CF, CQ, Timestamp).
> - [ ] **Row keys** are sorted lexicographically — design them to avoid hotspotting.
> - [ ] **Column families** are fixed at table creation; **column qualifiers** are dynamic.
> - [ ] Empty cells are **never stored** — that's what makes HBase sparse.
> - [ ] **HMaster** = coordinator, manages regions, DDL, load balancing.
> - [ ] **RegionServer** = data plane, holds MemStore, BlockCache, HFiles, WAL.
> - [ ] **ZooKeeper** = coordination, stores `hbase:meta` location, HMaster election.
> - [ ] **HDFS** = durable storage for HFiles and WAL (replication factor 3).
> - [ ] **Write path**: WAL → MemStore → HFile (on flush at 128 MB).
> - [ ] **Read path**: BlockCache → MemStore → HFiles (with Bloom filter optimization).
> - [ ] **Strong consistency** per row; eventual compaction removes old versions.
> - [ ] **Stream Data Model** = unbounded, ordered, high-velocity, immutable event sequences.
> - [ ] Three time notions: **Event time**, **Processing time**, **Ingestion time**.
> - [ ] Four key window types: **Tumbling, Sliding, Session, Count**.
> - [ ] **Watermarks** track event-time progress in out-of-order streams.
> - [ ] Real-time stream pipelines: Source → Kafka → Flink → Sink (HBase, dashboard, archive).
> - [ ] Stream challenges: out-of-order events, state management, exactly-once semantics, backpressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**HBase** is an open-source, distributed, versioned, non-relational (NoSQL) database modeled after Google's **Bigtable**. It runs on top of the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) and provides **real-time, random read/write access** to large-scale datasets (typically billions of rows × millions of columns).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exam-Ready):**
> *HBase is a sparse, distributed, persistent, multidimensional sorted map. The map is indexed by a row key, column key, and a timestamp. It is built on top of HDFS and provides high-throughput, fault-tolerant storage and retrieval of structured as well as semi-structured data.*

Mathematically, the HBase data model is represented as a mapping:

$$
\text{HBase} : (\text{RowKey},\ \text{ColumnFamily},\ \text{ColumnQualifier},\ \text{Timestamp}) \rightarrow \text{Value}
$$

Where each unique combination of these four coordinates yields an atomic cell storing an uninterpreted array of bytes (the value).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Excel Workbook on Steroids"

Imagine a **giant Google Spreadsheet**:

- **Rows** are the records (e.g., one row per customer ID).
- **Columns** are grouped into **Column Families** (e.g., `personal_info`, `purchase_history`, `login_data`). Each family stores related columns together.
- **Cells** are the actual values (e.g., `purchase_history:electronics = "₹45,000"`).
- **Timestamps** allow you to see the history of each cell — every change is preserved, not overwritten.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Sparse"?** Most cells are empty. HBase only stores populated cells, which makes it extremely space-efficient for wide, sparse tables (e.g., 1 million columns per row but only 10 actually used).

Unlike a normal spreadsheet, HBase can stretch this workbook across **thousands of commodity servers**, automatically split, replicate, and recover from failures — all while serving millions of reads/writes per second.

## 1.3 Where HBase Fits in the Hadoop Ecosystem

| Layer | Component | Role |
|-------|-----------|------|
| **Storage** | **HDFS** | Underlying distributed file system (raw blocks) |
| **Coordination** | **ZooKeeper** | Maintains cluster state, leader election |
| **Compute** | **MapReduce / Spark** | Batch processing over HBase tables |
| **Query** | **HBase Shell / REST / Thrift / Phoenix** | Read/Write APIs |
| **Database** | **HBase** | Real-time, low-latency NoSQL access |

> [!TIP]
> HBase is the **"online" counterpart** of HDFS. While HDFS excels at batch sequential reads (MapReduce), HBase provides **random, low-latency access** to the same data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier HBase Architecture

HBase follows a classic **Master–Worker (Master–Slave)** topology with ZooKeeper acting as the coordinator. The system has four major daemons/processes:

1. **HMaster**
2. **RegionServer (Worker nodes)**
3. **ZooKeeper Quorum**
4. **HDFS DataNodes** (where actual HFiles live)

### 2.1.1 HMaster — The "Cluster Brain"

The HMaster is the master node responsible for **administrative and coordination tasks**, not direct data serving.

**Responsibilities:**

- **Region Assignment:** Decides which RegionServer hosts a given Region.
- **Load Balancing:** Re-distributes Regions across servers when one is overloaded.
- **DDL Operations:** Handles Create/Delete/Update of tables and Column Families.
- **Coordination with ZooKeeper:** Detects RegionServer failures and reassigns their Regions.
- **Garbage Collection:** Triggers compaction of HFiles.

> [!NOTE]
> HMaster is **not a Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** because all metadata lives in ZooKeeper. If the HMaster dies, the cluster continues to serve reads/writes — only administrative tasks are paused.

### 2.1.2 RegionServer — The "Data Workhorse"

A RegionServer runs on every worker node and serves data for a subset of Regions.

**Internal Components of a RegionServer:**

- **Regions:** Horizontal partitions of a table (one Region = contiguous row-key range).
- **MemStore:** In-memory write buffer (per Column Family). Writes go here first.
- **StoreFile (HFile):** Immutable, sorted file on HDFS where data is eventually flushed.
- **WAL (Write-Ahead Log / HLog):** Crash-recovery log; every write is first written here before MemStore.
- **BlockCache:** Read cache (LRU) for frequently read blocks.
- **RegionCache (Meta Cache):** Cached `META` table entries for fast region lookup.

### 2.1.3 ZooKeeper — The "Coordinator"

ZooKeeper maintains the live state of the cluster. It stores:

- Location of the `-ROOT-` and `.META.` catalog tables (in newer versions, only `.META.`).
- Address of the current active HMaster.
- Heartbeat status of every RegionServer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HBase **cannot function** without a running ZooKeeper quorum. KTU exams frequently test this dependency.

### 2.1.4 Catalog Tables — `ROOT` and `META`

HBase uses a two-level (formerly three-level) lookup hierarchy for region locations:

1. **ZooKeeper** stores the address of the `.META.` table.
2. **`.META.` table** maps every region in every user table → its hosting RegionServer.
3. The client connects to the indicated RegionServer and performs the read/write.

## 2.2 The HBase Data Model (Deep Dive)

A table in HBase is structured as follows:

$$
\text{Row} \rightarrow \left\{ \text{ColumnFamily}_1,\ \text{ColumnFamily}_2,\ \ldots \right\}
$$

$$
\text{ColumnFamily} \rightarrow \left\{ \text{ColumnQualifier}_1 : \text{Value}_{t_1},\ \ldots \right\}
$$

### 2.2.1 Core Components

- **Row Key (Primary Index):** A unique byte-string identifying a row. Stored in **lexicographic sorted order** — this is the *only* index in HBase.
- **Column Family:** A grouping of related columns, defined at table creation. Stored together on disk. Recommended ≤ 3 families per table.
- **Column Qualifier:** A dynamic column inside a family (no need to pre-define).
- **Cell:** The atomic unit, uniquely identified by (RowKey, ColumnFamily, ColumnQualifier, Timestamp).
- **Version / Timestamp:** A 64-bit integer (default = system time at write). HBase retains the last **N** versions (configurable per family).

### 2.2.2 Physical Storage Layout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HBase stores data **column-wise within a column family**, not row-wise. This is the key to its high compression and fast aggregation on specific columns.

Within a Column Family, all cells are stored sorted by (RowKey, Timestamp). Each family has its own set of HFiles on disk.

## 2.3 Read & Write Path — High-Yield Concept

### 2.3.1 Write Path (Step-by-Step)

1. Client writes first to the **WAL** (for crash safety).
2. Then data is placed in the corresponding **MemStore** (one per Column Family per Region).
3. Once the MemStore reaches a threshold (default **128 MB**), it is flushed to disk as a new immutable **HFile**.
4. HFiles accumulate until a **compaction** merges them.

### 2.3.2 Read Path (Step-by-Step)

1. Client consults `.META.` (or uses its cache) → finds the RegionServer holding the region.
2. RegionServer first checks **BlockCache** (in-memory) → if miss, scans **MemStore** → if miss, reads the relevant **HFiles** from HDFS.
3. Results from all three sources are merged and returned (tombstones, versioning rules applied).

## 2.4 Region Lifecycle — Splitting & Compaction

- **Region Splitting:** When a Region exceeds `hbase.hregion.max.filesize` (default **10 GB**), it splits into two at the middle row key.
- **Minor Compaction:** Merges a small set of HFiles into a larger one.
- **Major Compaction:** Merges **all** HFiles in a Store into one and discards expired/deleted cells.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Configuration Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol / Default | Formula or Rule | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Cell Coordinates | $(r, c_f, c_q, t)$ | $V = f(r, c_f, c_q, t)$ | Uniquely identifies a value |
| Region Size Threshold | $S_{R} = 10\ \text{GB}$ (default) | If Region size $> S_R$, split | Controls horizontal partitioning |
| MemStore Flush Size | $M_{F} = 128\ \text{MB}$ | Flush if $\text{MemStore} \geq M_F$ | Controls frequency of disk I/O |
| HFile Block Size | $B_{H} = 64\ \text{KB}$ (default) | Configurable: 8 KB – 1 MB | Trade-off: random vs sequential reads |
| Replication Factor | $R$ | $R = 3$ (typical for HBase) | Fault-tolerance via HDFS replicas |
| Number of Column Families | $\vert CF \vert$ | Recommended $\vert CF \vert \leq 3$ | Each family = separate HFile set |
| Bloom Filter FPR | $p$ | $p = 0.1\%$ (configurable) | Reduces HFile lookups on misses |
| BlockCache Size | $C_{B}$ | $C_B = 0.4 \times \text{Heap}$ (default) | Read-side memory allocation |
| MemStore Size | $C_{M}$ | $C_M = 0.4 \times \text{Heap}$ (default) | Write-side memory allocation |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** A common 3-mark question asks *“What happens when MemStore is full?”* — Answer: It is flushed to a new HFile on HDFS. If you forget to mention HDFS, you lose the mark.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

HBase powers production systems requiring **low-latency access to massive datasets**, including:

- **Facebook Messenger** (message storage, billions of messages/day)
- **Pinterest** (graph data)
- **Yahoo** (user-ad interaction data)
- **Twitter** (tweets, timelines)
- **Financial fraud detection** (real-time transaction lookup)
- **IoT sensor telemetry** (time-series storage)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & HFile Internals

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Cell Address & Storage Equation

Every cell in HBase is addressed by a 4-tuple. Let us formalize the storage and retrieval model step by step.

**Step 1 — Define the address tuple:**

$$
A_{cell} = (K_r,\ K_{cf},\ K_{cq},\ t)
$$

Where:
- $K_r$ = Row Key (byte-string of length $\vert K_r \vert$)
- $K_{cf}$ = Column Family name
- $K_{cq}$ = Column Qualifier
- $t$ = Timestamp (64-bit long)

**Step 2 — Define the storage mapping function:**

$$
V = \mathcal{S}(K_r,\ K_{cf},\ K_{cq},\ t)
$$

If the cell has never been written, $\mathcal{S}$ returns `null` (sparse storage — no allocation).

**Step 3 — Derive the on-disk size of a cell entry:**

For a single cell, the on-disk size (in bytes) of the key-value pair written to HFile is:

$$
S_{cell} = \vert K_r \vert + \vert K_{cf} \vert + \vert K_{cq} \vert + 8 + \vert V \vert + \text{overhead}
$$

Where the constant 8 bytes accounts for the 64-bit timestamp $t$, and overhead includes key length, value length, and tag fields (typically **8–16 bytes per cell**).

**Step 4 — Derive the MemStore flush trigger condition:**

Let $M_i$ be the current size of the MemStore for Column Family $i$. The flush trigger fires when:

$$
M_i \geq M_F = 128\ \text{MB} = 128 \times 2^{20}\ \text{bytes} = 134{,}217{,}728\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Region split trigger condition:**

Let $S_{R}$ be the current size of a Region. The HMaster triggers a split when:

$$
S_{R} \geq S_{R,\max} = 10\ \text{GB} = 10 \times 2^{30}\ \text{bytes} = 10{,}737{,}418{,}240\ \text{bytes}
$$

The split occurs at the middle row key, denoted $K_{r,\text{mid}}$, such that:

$$
K_{r,\text{mid}} = \underset{K_r}{\operatorname{argmin}} \left\vert \sum_{k < K_r} S_{cell}(k) - \frac{1}{2} S_{R,\max} \right\vert
$$

That is, the row key that divides the total region size as close to 50:50 as possible.

**Step 6 — Derive total MemStore memory allocation:**

If a RegionServer has heap size $H$ (in bytes), the maximum MemStore allocation across all families is:

$$
C_{M,\text{total}} = \alpha_M \times H
$$

Where $\alpha_M = 0.4$ by default. If there are $N$ Column Families, per-family share is:

$$
C_{M,\text{per-family}} = \frac{\alpha_M \times H}{N}
$$

**Step 7 — HFile block-cache lookup probability:**

With Bloom Filter of false-positive rate $p = 0.001$ (0.1%), the probability of an unnecessary HFile scan is:

$$
P_{FPR} = 1 - (1 - p)^B \approx B \cdot p
$$

Where $B$ is the number of HFiles per Store. For a freshly compacted Store with $B = 1$:

$$
P_{FPR} = 0.001 = 0.1\%
$$

This is why Bloom Filters dramatically reduce I/O on point lookups.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: HBase vs RDBMS (High-Yield for KTU)

| Feature | HBase (NoSQL) | RDBMS (e.g., MySQL) |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| Data Model | Wide-column, sparse | Fixed-schema, dense |
| Schema | Flexible (qualifiers dynamic) | Rigid (predefined) |
| Scaling | Horizontal (add nodes) | Vertical (add RAM/CPU) |
| Transactions | Single-row only | Full ACID |
| Query Language | Get/Scan/Filter API, Phoenix SQL | Full SQL |
| Joins | Not supported natively | First-class support |
| Indexing | Only on RowKey | Multiple secondary indexes |
| Consistency | Strong per row | Strong per transaction |
| Storage Format | HFile (columnar within family) | Row-oriented (InnoDB) |
| Best For | Billions of rows, sparse data | OLTP with complex queries |

## 3.3 HFile Internal Structure (Memory Exam Tip)

An HFile is a sorted, indexed, block-based file. Its layout is:

$$
\text{HFile} = \left\{ \text{Magic Header}\ \vert\ \text{Data Blocks}\ \vert\ \text{Index Blocks}\ \vert\ \text{Bloom Filter}\ \vert\ \text{Trailer} \right\}
$$

| Block | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| **Magic Header** | "HFILE" magic bytes for validation |
| **Data Blocks** | Store actual KeyValue pairs (sorted) |
| **Leaf Index Block** | Mid-level index for block lookup |
| **Root Index Block** | Top-level index (kept in BlockCache) |
| **Bloom Filter Block** | Fast negative lookups |
| **File Info Block** | Meta: last key, average key size, etc. |
| **Trailer** | Pointers to all index blocks (fixed size) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level HBase Architecture (Master–Worker + ZooKeeper)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[Client Application]
    clientB[HBase Shell]
    clientC[Phoenix SQL]

    zkQ[ZooKeeper Quorum]
    master[HMaster]

    rs1[RegionServer 1]
    rs2[RegionServer 2]
    rs3[RegionServer 3]

    regionA1[Region: A-M]
    regionA2[Region: N-Z]
    regionB1[Region: 0-9]
    regionB2[Region: A-F]

    hdfs[HDFS NameNode]
    dn1[DataNode 1]
    dn2[DataNode 2]
    dn3[DataNode 3]

    metaT[.META. Catalog Table]

    clientA --> zkQ
    clientB --> zkQ
    clientC --> zkQ
    zkQ --> metaT
    metaT --> rs1
    metaT --> rs2
    metaT --> rs3

    master -. Monitors .-> rs1
    master -. Monitors .-> rs2
    master -. Monitors .-> rs3

    rs1 --> regionA1
    rs1 --> regionB1
    rs2 --> regionA2
    rs3 --> regionB2

    regionA1 --> dn1
    regionA1 --> dn2
    regionB1 --> dn2
    regionB2 --> dn3
    hdfs -. Manages .-> dn1
    hdfs -. Manages .-> dn2
    hdfs -. Manages .-> dn3
```

## 4.2 RegionServer Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rs[RegionServer Process]
    wal[WAL HLog on HDFS]
    region1[Region: Users A-M]
    region2[Region: Users N-Z]

    subgraph CF1[Column Family: profile]
        mem1[MemStore profile]
        sf1a[StoreFile 1 HFile]
        sf1b[StoreFile 2 HFile]
        sf1c[StoreFile 3 HFile]
    end

    subgraph CF2[Column Family: activity]
        mem2[MemStore activity]
        sf2a[StoreFile 1 HFile]
        sf2b[StoreFile 2 HFile]
    end

    bc[BlockCache LRU]
    rcache[Meta Cache]

    rs --> wal
    rs --> region1
    rs --> region2
    rs --> bc
    rs --> rcache

    region1 --> CF1
    region1 --> CF2
    wal -. Recovery .-> mem1
    wal -. Recovery .-> mem2
    mem1 -. Flush at 128 MB .-> sf1c
    mem2 -. Flush at 128 MB .-> sf2b
    sf1a -. Minor Compaction .-> sf1b
    sf1b -. Major Compaction .-> sf1c
    bc -. Read Cache .-> sf1a
    bc -. Read Cache .-> sf1b
```

## 4.3 HBase Write Flow (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client writes Put request] --> B{ZooKeeper lookup}
    B --> C[Find RegionServer from META]
    C --> D[Append to WAL on HDFS]
    D --> E[Write to MemStore CF1]
    E --> F[Write to MemStore CF2]
    F --> G{MemStore full?}
    G -- No --> H[Acknowledge to Client]
    G -- Yes --> I[Flush MemStore to HFile]
    I --> J[New HFile appears in Store]
    J --> K[Periodic Compaction]
```

## 4.4 HBase Data Model Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Row1[Row Key: user_101]
        R1CF1[profile: name Alice]
        R1CF2[profile: age 28]
        R1CF3[orders: 1001]
        R1CF4[orders: 1002]
    end

    subgraph Row2[Row Key: user_102]
        R2CF1[profile: name Bob]
        R2CF2[orders: 2001]
    end

    subgraph Row3[Row Key: user_103]
        R3CF1[profile: name Carol]
        R3CF2[orders: 3001]
    end

    R1CF1 -. Timestamp 1699000000 .-> V1[Value: Alice]
    R1CF2 -. Timestamp 1699000000 .-> V2[Value: 28]
    R3CF1 -. Sparse empty cells .-> S1[profile: age is null]
```

## 4.5 Compaction Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HFile 1] --> D[Merged HFile]
    B[HFile 2] --> D
    C[HFile 3] --> D
    D --> E[Discard Tombstones]
    E --> F[Discard Expired Versions]
    F --> G[Final Sorted HFile]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define HBase. List any four features of HBase. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

HBase is an open-source, distributed, column-oriented NoSQL database built on top of HDFS. It provides real-time, random read/write access to large datasets using a sparse, sorted map data model.

**Four features of HBase:**

1. **Distributed & Scalable:** Data is partitioned across nodes; new nodes can be added seamlessly.
2. **Strongly Consistent:** Single-row read/write operations are strongly consistent (per row, not table-wide).
3. **Sparse & Multi-dimensional:** Stores only non-empty cells; supports millions of columns per row.
4. **Versioned:** Every cell retains multiple versions (timestamped), useful for audit and time-series data.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of ZooKeeper in HBase? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

ZooKeeper acts as the **centralized coordination service** in an HBase cluster. Its roles include:

1. **Cluster State Management:** Maintains live/halted state of all RegionServers via heartbeats.
2. **Leader Election:** Selects the active HMaster (in case of master failover).
3. **Metadata Storage:** Stores the address of the `.META.` catalog table used for region lookups.
4. **Configuration Hub:** Stores cluster-wide configuration that all nodes can access.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "ZooKeeper stores data of HBase tables." ❌ This is **wrong**. HBase tables are stored in HDFS, not ZooKeeper. ZooKeeper only stores **metadata and coordination state**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the HBase architecture in detail with a neat diagram. List the responsibilities of the HMaster. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1.)*

The HBase architecture is a **distributed master–worker model** with three main components:

**1. HMaster:**
- Performs DDL operations (Create/Delete/Alter tables).
- Assigns regions to RegionServers.
- Monitors health of RegionServers (via ZooKeeper heartbeats).
- Triggers region balancing and compaction.
- Handles failover and recovery of RegionServers.

**2. RegionServers:**
- Serve reads/writes for their assigned regions.
- Maintain MemStores, StoreFiles (HFiles), WAL, and BlockCache.
- Report load back to HMaster.

**3. ZooKeeper:**
- Coordinates cluster state and stores `.META.` table location.
- Performs leader election for HMaster.

**4. HDFS:**
- Provides durable, replicated storage for HFiles and WAL.

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the HBase data model with an example. Explain the role of Row Key, Column Family, and Timestamp. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The HBase data model is a **sparse, distributed, persistent, multi-dimensional sorted map** indexed by (RowKey, ColumnFamily, ColumnQualifier, Timestamp).

**Example Table: `UserActivity`**

| Row Key | Column Family: `profile` | Column Family: `activity` |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| user_101 | `name=Alice, age=28` | `login=2024-01-01, clicks=152` |
| user_102 | `name=Bob` | `login=2024-01-02` |

**Roles:**

- **Row Key:** Uniquely identifies a row. Stored in **sorted (lexicographic) order**. Critical for performance — poor design causes *Region Server hotspots*.
- **Column Family:** A logical grouping of columns. Defined at table creation. Each family is stored in **separate HFiles** for efficient I/O. Recommended ≤ 3 families per table.
- **Column Qualifier:** Dynamic column name inside a family — no need to pre-declare.
- **Timestamp:** Allows multiple versions of the same cell. Default stores last 3 versions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing that HBase is "schema-less." This is partially true but **misleading**. Column Families must be pre-declared; only qualifiers are dynamic. Examiners deduct marks for this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the read and write path in HBase with a flowchart. What is the role of WAL in crash recovery? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Write Path:**

1. Client sends `Put` request to the RegionServer (found via ZooKeeper and `.META.`).
2. RegionServer first **appends to WAL** (Write-Ahead Log) on HDFS — for durability.
3. Data is then written to **MemStore** (in-memory, per Column Family).
4. Client receives `ack` immediately.
5. When MemStore reaches 128 MB, it is **flushed to a new HFile** on HDFS.

**Read Path:**

1. Client queries ZooKeeper → gets `.META.` table location → finds correct RegionServer.
2. RegionServer checks **BlockCache** → if miss, scans **MemStore** → if miss, reads relevant **HFiles** from HDFS.
3. Results are merged across all three sources (with version filtering and tombstone resolution).

**Role of WAL in Crash Recovery:**

- The WAL is written **before** the MemStore, ensuring every change is durably recorded.
- If a RegionServer crashes, the new RegionServer **replays the WAL** to rebuild the MemStore state.
- WAL is deleted only after MemStore is successfully flushed to HFile.

---

**Q. (b)** What is HFile? Describe its internal structure. Explain minor and major compaction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**HFile (StoreFile):**

An HFile is the **on-disk, sorted, immutable file format** used by HBase to store data. It is built on Hadoop's `SequenceFile`/`MapFile` concept and is block-based for fast random access.

**Internal Structure (top to bottom):**

1. **Magic Header** – "HFILE" identifier for file validation.
2. **Data Blocks** – Sorted KeyValue pairs (the actual cell data).
3. **Leaf Index Blocks** – Mid-level index for locating data blocks.
4. **Bloom Filter Block** – Probabilistic structure to skip HFiles that don't contain the row.
5. **File Info Block** – Metadata (last key written, average key size, etc.).
6. **Root Index Block** – Top-level index.
7. **Trailer** – Fixed-size footer pointing to all indexes (loadable into memory at file open).

**Minor Compaction:**

- Merges a **small subset** of HFiles into a larger one.
- Reduces the number of HFiles per Store.
- Runs frequently and quickly.

**Major Compaction:**

- Merges **all** HFiles in a Store into a single HFile.
- **Deletes tombstones** (markers for deleted cells).
- **Drops expired versions** (older than `TTL`).
- Runs periodically (configurable) — expensive I/O operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse HFile with HDFS block. Remember: **HDFS block** is a physical storage unit (default 128 MB). **HFile block** is a logical read unit (default 64 KB) for fast random lookup.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — HBase Architecture**

- ✅ HBase = **NoSQL, distributed, column-oriented, versioned** database on HDFS.
- ✅ Data model: $(RowKey,\ ColumnFamily,\ ColumnQualifier,\ Timestamp) \rightarrow Value$.
- ✅ **Row Key** is the only index — design it carefully to avoid hotspots.
- ✅ **Column Families** must be pre-defined; **Qualifiers** are dynamic.
- ✅ **HMaster** = Admin (DDL, region assignment, balancing). **Not SPOF** thanks to ZooKeeper.
- ✅ **RegionServer** = Worker (serves reads/writes for Regions).
- ✅ **ZooKeeper** = Coordinator (cluster state, leader election, `.META.` location).
- ✅ **WAL (HLog)** ensures durability and crash recovery.
- ✅ **MemStore** (in-memory, 128 MB) → flushed to immutable **HFile** on HDFS.
- ✅ **BlockCache** is the LRU read cache (40% of heap by default).
- ✅ **Region** splits at 10 GB by default; regions are row-key contiguous ranges.
- ✅ **`.META.`** table is the two-level catalog for region lookup (ZooKeeper → `.META.` → RegionServer).
- ✅ **HFile** = sorted, block-based, indexed, contains Bloom Filter for fast negative lookups.
- ✅ **Minor Compaction** = merge few HFiles; **Major Compaction** = merge all + delete tombstones.
- ✅ HBase gives **strong consistency per row**, not table-wide ACID.
- ✅ HBase does **not** support SQL natively (use Apache Phoenix or Hive for that).
- ✅ Real-world users: Facebook, Pinterest, Yahoo, Twitter, Adobe.

**One-line mnemonic for the architecture:**
> **"Zoo-Master-Region-HDFS"** = **Z**ooKeeper + **M**aster + **R**egionServer + **H**DFS — these are the four pillars.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HBase Schema Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** HBase Schema Design # 📘 HBase Schema Design — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **HBase Schema Design** is the structured process of modeling application data into HBase tables by carefully defining **row keys**, **column families**, **column qualifiers**, and **cell timestamps** such that the resulting layout is optimized for the dominant read and write access patterns of a distributed, horizontally-scalable, sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map built on top of the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). In HBase, the schema is **NOT** a rigid relational model. Instead, it is a **flexible, sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map** that maps row keys to a set of column families, where each column family contains an unbounded set of column qualifiers, and every cell is versioned by a timestamp. > [!NOTE] > **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > HBase Schema Design is a *high-weightage* topic in Module 3 because it determines the entire performance, scalability, and consistency profile of a NoSQL application. Marks are frequently asked on row key design strategies, column family design trade-offs, and the comparison between tall-narrow vs. wide-flat tables. ## 1.2 The Five Logical Building Blocks of an HBase Schema | # | Building Block | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Table** | A logical grouping of rows, analogous to a table in RDBMS, but with no fixed schema. | | 2 | **Row Key** | A unique byte-array identifier for each row. Rows are **stored in lexicographically sorted order** by row key. | | 3 | **Column Family** | A physical grouping of columns that are stored together on disk. Defined **at schema creation time**. | | 4 | **Column Qualifier** | A dynamic sub-column inside a family. Can be added on-the-fly without altering the schema. | | 5 | **Cell / Version** | The intersection of `{row key, column family, column qualifier, timestamp}` — stores the actual value. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card Catalog 🗂️ Imagine a **massive library** that has millions of books scattered across thousands of shelves.
