Instead of using a complex search engine, the librarian organizes the books in **one strict, sorted order** — by an ID printed on the spine. - The **Row Key** is the book's ID on the spine — books are always shelved in sorted order, so a librarian (HBase RegionServer) can find any book in **O(log N)** time using binary search. - The **Column Family** is a *section of the shelf* reserved for a specific type of information (e.g., "Title Info", "Author Info", "Borrow History"). All books in that section share the same physical shelving rules. - The **Column Qualifier** is a *specific sub-tag* inside that section — for example, under "Author Info" you may have tags like `first_name`, `last_name`, `biography`. New tags can be added at any time without reorganizing the library. - The **Timestamp** is the *date the book was added or updated* — if a book's title changes, the old title isn't deleted; instead, a new entry is stamped with a fresh date. You can roll back to older versions. - The **Cell** is the *actual content* (e.g., the string `"The Hobbit"`). > [!TIP] > **Why is this analogy powerful?** > Because just as a librarian's primary job is to *minimize walking distance* between lookups, an HBase schema designer's primary job is to *minimize region-server scans* by ensuring related data lands on the *same row* or in the *same sorted neighborhood*. This intuition is what makes HBase blazingly fast. ## 1.4 Why HBase Schema Design is Fundamentally Different from RDBMS | Feature | RDBMS Schema | HBase Schema | |---|---|---| | Schema rigidity | **Fixed** at creation | **Flexible**, columns added dynamically | | Storage model | Row-oriented | Column-family-oriented | | Indexing | Many secondary indexes | **Only one** primary index (row key) | | Joins | First-class feature | **Not supported** (must be done in app) | | Normalization | **Highly normalized** | **Denormalized** by design | | Access pattern | Schema-first | **Query-first** (design table per access pattern) | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often answer HBase schema questions by describing a *normalized* relational design. This is **wrong** — HBase rewards denormalization and embedding data into a single row because it eliminates expensive joins and multi-region scans. # 📘 HBase Schema Design — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The HBase Logical Data Model — Mathematical View The HBase data model can be expressed as a sparsely populated, multi-dimensional map: $$ \text{HBase Table} : (\text{RowKey} \times \text{ColumnFamily} \times \text{ColumnQualifier} \times \text{Timestamp}) \rightarrow \text{Value} $$ Where: - $\text{RowKey} = $ a byte array of length $L_r$ (typically $8$ to $64$ bytes). - $\text{ColumnFamily} = $ a string of length $L_{cf}$ (defined at table creation). - $\text{ColumnQualifier} = $ a dynamic byte array. - $\text{Timestamp} = $ a 64-bit integer, conventionally Unix epoch time in milliseconds. - $\text{Value} = $ an uninterpreted byte array. The full coordinates of any cell are therefore the 4-tuple: $$ C_{\text{coord}} = \{ r, cf : cq, t \} $$ And the value retrieved is: $$ V = M\bigl( r, cf : cq, t \bigr) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this formula matters for exams:** This 4-tuple is the *single most-asked* conceptual point in KTU Module 3. Examiners expect you to write this address structure and explain that the value is *always* versioned. ## 2.2 Core Schema Design Principles — The Seven Golden Rules > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 syllabus tests these principles directly. Memorize them as exam-ready bullets. ### **Rule 1 — One Table Per Access Pattern** HBase is **not** a general-purpose query engine. Each distinct query pattern of an application should ideally be served by its own dedicated HBase table. This eliminates the need for secondary indexes and joins. ### **Rule 2 — Choose the Right Row Key (The Single Most Important Decision)** The row key determines: - The **physical sort order** of all rows. - The **distribution of data** across RegionServers (avoid hotspots). - The **read performance** (single-row lookup vs. scan range). - The **locality** of related data (keys in the same range land in the same region). > [!TIP] > If you design a bad row key, **no amount of column-family tuning will save you**. This is why KTU examiners repeatedly ask "Justify your row key design with an example." ### **Rule 3 — Tall-Narrow vs. Wide-Flat Table Design** | Strategy | Structure | When to Use | |---|---|---| | **Tall-Narrow** | Many rows, few columns | High write throughput, time-series data, event logs | | **Wide-Flat** | Few rows, many columns / qualifiers | Aggregated entity views, "document per row" pattern | The KTU favourite question is: *"Design a schema for sensor data with 1 million readings per device per day"* — answer: **Tall-Narrow** with row key = ``. ### **Rule 4 — Column Family Cardinality Discipline** - A practical HBase table should have **at most 3 to 5 column families** (the official limit is much higher, but performance degrades). - Each column family is stored as a separate HFile in HDFS. - All columns in a family are read together during a scan → put columns that are *frequently accessed together* in the same family. ### **Rule 5 — Timestamps and Cell Versioning** - Each cell can store up to $N$ versions, where $N$ is the family-level parameter `VERSIONS`. - Older versions beyond $N$ are compacted away. - For time-series data, use the **timestamp as part of the row key** for fast range scans. ### **Rule 6 — Avoid Hotspotting** A *hotspot* is a single RegionServer receiving disproportionately high read/write traffic. Causes: - Monotonically increasing row keys (e.g., timestamps). - Low-cardinality leading prefixes (e.g., all rows starting with `"IN"`). Solutions: **Salting**, **Hashing**, **Key Reversal** (explained in detail in Section 2.3). ### **Rule 7 — Denormalize Aggressively** Duplicate data across rows or even across tables. HBase storage is cheap; **joins are expensive**. ## 2.3 Row Key Design Anti-Patterns and Remedies — The KTU Hot-Question Set | # | Anti-Pattern | Problem Caused | Remedy | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Monotonic timestamp as row key | All writes hit the **last region** → hotspot | Add a **prefix salt** or **reverse the timestamp** | | 2 | User phone number as row key | Uneven distribution, privacy risk | **Hash** the number (MD5/SHA-1) to spread load | | 3 | Composite key with high-cardinality prefix first | Range scan becomes a **full-table scan** | Place the **most-selective, low-cardinality equality predicate** first | | 4 | Random UUIDs as row keys | Loses natural sort order, hard to do range scans | Use **structured prefixes** (e.g., `region_id + user_id + timestamp`) | | 5 | Very long row keys | Wastes memory (row keys are cached in BlockCache) | Keep row keys **≤ 64 bytes** | ## 2.4 The Three Classic Hotspotting Techniques — Detailed > [!NOTE] > These three techniques are the **most-repeated 14-mark questions** in KTU Module 3. Master them. ### **A. Salting** Prepend a *random byte* (0 to N-1) to the actual row key. The salt evenly distributes writes across N regions. $$ \text{SaltedKey} = \text{salt} \oplus \text{OriginalKey} $$ where $\text{salt} \in \{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\}$ is chosen uniformly at random. **Drawback:** Range scans become harder because logically related keys are no longer adjacent. The application must issue N parallel scans (one per salt) and merge. ### **B. Hashing** Apply a one-way hash function to part (or all) of the row key. $$ \text{HashedKey} = \text{Hash}(\text{OriginalKey} \vert \text{ suffix}) $$ - Distributes keys **uniformly** across regions. - Loses natural ordering → cannot do lexicographic range scans. - Used when the workload is dominated by **point lookups** rather than range scans. ### **C. Key Reversal** Reverse the byte order of a portion of the row key. $$ \text{ReversedKey} = \text{reverse}(\text{TimestampBytes}) \oplus \text{ConstantPrefix} $$ - Solves the "latest timestamp = rightmost region" hotspot. - Preserves *some* range query capability (e.g., scan a day's data). - Doesn't randomize — same input always gives same output → good for deterministic reads. ## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table | # | Concept | Symbol / Formula | Practical Constraint / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cell address | $\{r,\ cf: cq,\ t\}$ | 4-tuple, byte arrays | | 2 | Number of cell versions kept | $N_{ver}$ | Set by `VERSIONS` per family, typically $N_{ver} \le 3$ | | 3 | Recommended max column families per table | $F_{max}$ | $F_{max} \approx 3$ to $5$ for production | | 4 | Recommended row key length | $L_r$ | $L_r \le 64$ bytes | | 5 | Cell timestamp resolution | $t$ | 64-bit long, ms-precision Unix epoch | | 6 | Salting bucket count | $N_{salt}$ | $N_{salt} \approx 10$ to $20$ typical | | 7 | Region split trigger | $\text{RegionSize} > R_{max}$ | Default $R_{max} = 10\ \text{GB}$ | | 8 | Data model cardinality | $(r,\ cf,\ cq,\ t) \rightarrow v$ | Sparse: most cells are empty | > [!TIP] > **Engineering utility:** HBase Schema Design is used in production at Facebook (Messages & Search), Pinterest (graph storage), Adobe (analytics), and Salesforce (real-time CRM). The same techniques appear in Google Bigtable, Apache Cassandra partition-key design, and ScyllaDB clustering-key design. # 📘 HBase Schema Design — Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing a Twitter-Like "Tweets by User" Table ### **Step 1: Identify the Access Pattern** The dominant access pattern is: *"Fetch the latest 20 tweets of user $U$ in reverse-chronological order."* ### **Step 2: Identify the Entities and Cardinalities** - Users: $\approx 10^8$. - Tweets per user: $\approx 10^4$ to $10^6$. - Tweet attributes: `tweet_id`, `text`, `timestamp`, `retweet_count`, `likes`. ### **Step 3: Decide Tall-Narrow vs. Wide-Flat** We expect a high volume of *append* operations (new tweets) and *time-bounded* range reads. A **Tall-Narrow** design is optimal. ### **Step 4: Construct the Row Key** We need: - All tweets of a user to be **co-located** → user_id must be a prefix. - Latest tweet to be retrieved first → we want descending order, but HBase sorts ascending → use **Long.MAX_VALUE − timestamp** as a suffix. $$ \text{RowKey} = \text{user\_id}\ \vert\ \text{Long.MAX\_VALUE} - \text{tweet\_timestamp} $$ ### **Step 5: Define Column Families** We separate read-hot columns from analytical columns: | Column Family | Contents | Rationale | |---|---|---| | `meta` | `tweet_id`, `user_id` | Small, always read together | | `content` | `text`, `media_url` | Read with `meta` 90% of the time | | `stats` | `retweet_count`, `like_count`, `reply_count` | Updated independently, read less often | ### **Step 6: Set Family Parameters** | Parameter | Value | Reason | |---|---|---| | `meta : VERSIONS` | $1$ | Metadata rarely changes | | `content : VERSIONS` | $1$ | Tweets are immutable | | `stats : VERSIONS` | $2$ | Allow brief overlap during counter updates | | `stats : TTL` | $\infty$ | Counters must persist | | Block size | $64\ \text{KB}$ | Small cells, frequent seeks | ### **Step 7: Validate the Design** A scan from row key `user_42\x00\x7F\xFF\xFF\xFF\xFF\xFF\xFF` for the next 20 rows gives the 20 most recent tweets of `user_42` — **a single-region scan, no joins, sub-second response**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — IoT Sensor Time-Series Schema (The KTU Classic) ### **Step 1: Access Pattern** *"Given a sensor device $D$ and a time range $[t_1, t_2]$, return all readings."* ### **Step 2: Volume Estimation** - Devices: $10^6$. - Readings per device per day: $10^6$. - One year of data: $\approx 3.6 \times 10^{11}$ rows. ### **Step 3: Row Key Construction** We need both **co-location by device** and **time-ordering**. We pick: $$ \text{RowKey} = \text{device\_id}\ \vert\ \text{reverse(timestamp)} $$ where $\text{reverse(timestamp)}$ flips the byte order so that the **newest** timestamp is the **smallest** numeric value, ensuring it sorts first. **Rationale of reversal:** If $\text{ts}_A < \text{ts}_B$, then under natural order $\text{ts}_A$ sorts first, so the scan returns *oldest first*. After reversal, $\text{reverse}(\text{ts}_A) > \text{reverse}(\text{ts}_B)$, so $\text{ts}_B$ (newer) sorts first → we get the **latest data on top**. ### **Step 4: Column Family Design** A single family `s` is sufficient because every column (`temperature`, `humidity`, `pressure`, `battery_voltage`) is read together. | Column Family | Columns | `VERSIONS` | `TTL` | |---|---|---|---| | `s` | `temp`, `hum`, `pres`, `volt` | $1$ | 90 days (auto-purge old data) | ### **Step 5: Handle Hotspotting** If we expect a few "noisy" devices that emit millions of readings per second, apply **salting** with $N_{salt} = 10$: $$ \text{FinalRowKey} = \text{byte(salt)} \oplus \text{device\_id} \oplus \text{reverse(timestamp)} $$ The application must then run $10$ parallel scans, one per salt value, and merge results. ### **Step 6: Quantify Performance** For $D = \text{"sensor\_0001"}$ and time window $[t_1, t_2]$, the scan range is: $$ [\ \text{"sensor\_0001"} \oplus \text{reverse}(t_2),\ \ \text{"sensor\_0001"} \oplus \text{reverse}(t_1)\ ] $$ This is a **single contiguous range scan** within one region — no full-table scan, no index lookup. Expected latency for $10^4$ rows: $\approx 50$–$200\ \text{ms}$. --- ## 3.3 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Row-Key Strategy | Access Pattern Profile | Recommended Row-Key Strategy | Why | |---|---|---| | Pure point lookup by ID | **Hash of ID** | Uniform distribution, no scan needed | | Time-range scan per entity | **Entity-ID prefix + reverse timestamp** | Co-location + correct sort order | | Append-mostly, no reads by time | **Salted key** | Maximum write throughput | | Latest-first feed | **Entity-ID + Long.MAX_VALUE − timestamp** | Descending order in one scan | | Multi-tenant SaaS | **Tenant-ID prefix + entity ID** | Tenant isolation per region | | Search by secondary attribute | **Index table** (second HBase table) | HBase has no secondary indexes | ## 3.4 Cost-Benefit Analysis of Column-Family Splits > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** Adding a new column family in HBase is **not free**. Each family produces independent MemStores, HFiles, flushes, compactions, and block cache usage. The rule of thumb is to split only when read/write access patterns differ by $\geq 10\times$. $$ \text{Cost}_\text{family} = \text{MemStore}_\text{overhead} + \text{HFile}_\text{count} \times \text{Compaction}_\text{IO} $$ $$ \text{Benefit}_\text{split} = \Delta_{\text{scan performance}} + \Delta_{\text{compaction efficiency}} $$ The split is justified only if $\text{Benefit}_\text{split} > \text{Cost}_\text{family}$. # 📘 HBase Schema Design — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The HBase Logical Address Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Row Key
byte array, primary index"] --> B["Column Family
defined at create time"] B --> C["Column Qualifier
dynamic, no schema change"] C --> D["Timestamp
64-bit long, version stamp"] D --> E["Cell Value
uninterpreted bytes"] A --> F["HBase Sorted Map
lexicographic order"] F --> G["Region 1
key range A-M"] F --> H["Region 2
key range N-Z"] G --> I["RegionServer 1
HDFS blocks"] H --> J["RegionServer 2
HDFS blocks"] ``` ## 4.2 HBase Schema Design Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Start: Identify Access Pattern"] --> Q1{"Read by
single point lookup?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Hash["Use Hashed Row Key
uniform distribution"] Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Read by
time range per entity?"} Q2 -- Yes --> Reversed["Use Entity-ID prefix
plus reversed timestamp"] Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Append-mostly
no time reads?"} Q3 -- Yes --> Salted["Use Salted Row Key
max write throughput"] Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Multi-attribute
lookup needed?"} Q4 -- Yes --> Index["Build Secondary Index Table
HBase has no native index"] Q4 -- No --> Natural["Use Natural Composite Key
Entity1 plus Entity2 prefix"] Hash --> CF["Define Column Families
3 to 5 max"] Reversed --> CF Salted --> CF Index --> CF Natural --> CF CF --> Validate["Validate via Region Distribution
and Scan Cost Analysis"] ``` ## 4.3 Tall-Narrow vs. Wide-Flat Table — Architectural View ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TallNarrow["Tall-Narrow Design - Time Series"] T1["RowKey: device_A_ts1
meta: temperature=22C"] T2["RowKey: device_A_ts2
meta: temperature=23C"] T3["RowKey: device_A_ts3
meta: temperature=24C"] T4["RowKey: device_A_ts4
meta: temperature=25C"] T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 end subgraph WideFlat["Wide-Flat Design - Entity Document"] W1["RowKey: user_42
profile:name=John
profile:email=john@x.com
profile:age=30
activity:last_login=2024
activity:posts=145
activity:likes=2300"] end ``` ## 4.4 Hotspotting Mitigation Techniques — Comparative Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Bad["Anti-Pattern: Monotonic Timestamp Key"] B1["ts_001 -> Region A"] B2["ts_002 -> Region A"] B3["ts_003 -> Region A"] B4["ts_004 -> Region A"] B5["All writes hit same Region A - HOTSPOT"] end subgraph Good["Solution: Salting plus Reversal"] G1["salt_3_ts_001 -> Region C"] G2["salt_7_ts_002 -> Region G"] G3["salt_1_ts_003 -> Region A"] G4["salt_5_ts_004 -> Region E"] G5["Writes spread evenly across regions"] end ``` ## 4.5 End-to-End HBase Write/Read Path (Schema Impact) ```mermaid flowchart LR Client["Client Application"] --> ZK["ZooKeeper
locate meta region"] ZK --> Meta["META Table
region to RegionServer map"] Meta --> RS["RegionServer"] RS --> Mem["MemStore
in-memory sorted skip list"] Mem --> HFile["HFile on HDFS
immutable sorted file"] HFile --> Compaction["Minor plus Major Compaction
merges HFiles per family"] Client --> ReadPath["Read Path: BlockCache
plus MemStore plus HFile scan"] ReadPath --> RS ``` # 📘 HBase Schema Design — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the HBase data model. How is a cell identified within an HBase table?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The HBase data model is a *sparse, distributed, persistent, multi-dimensional sorted map*. The map is indexed by a four-part key — the **row key**, the **column family**, the **column qualifier**, and the **timestamp** — and the value at that key is an uninterpreted byte array called the **cell value**. Mathematically, a cell is uniquely identified by the 4-tuple: $$ \{ \text{row\_key},\ \text{family} : \text{qualifier},\ \text{timestamp} \} $$ HBase keeps multiple versions of the same cell (default 1, configurable per family), allowing time-travel queries. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for naming the model, 1 mark for the 4-tuple, 1 mark for versioning mention.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is hotspotting in HBase? List any two techniques to avoid it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Hotspotting** occurs when a large fraction of client traffic is directed at a *single* RegionServer, usually because consecutive row keys land in the same region — for example, when row keys are monotonically increasing timestamps. This negates HBase's horizontal scalability. Two techniques to avoid hotspotting: 1. **Salting** — prepend a random byte (0 to N-1) to the row key so writes spread across N regions. 2. **Key Reversal** — reverse the byte order of the timestamp portion so that newer data writes do not all land in the same region. 3. *(Optional third technique)* **Hashing** the row key to uniformly randomize its distribution. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for naming and briefly explaining two techniques.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **building blocks** of the HBase data model. Compare **tall-narrow** and **wide-flat** table design strategies with a real-world example for each. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Design an HBase schema for storing **e-commerce order data** with the following access patterns: - Fetch all orders of a customer in the last 30 days (latest first). - Update the shipping status of a specific order. - Count the number of orders per product in a given month. Justify your **row key**, **column families**, and any **index tables** you create. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — HBase Building Blocks & Tall-Narrow vs. Wide-Flat (7 marks)** **Building blocks (3 marks):** 1. **Row Key** — byte array that uniquely identifies a row. Rows are stored in *lexicographically sorted order*. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Column Family** — physical grouping of columns defined at table creation. All qualifiers of a family are stored in the same HFile. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Column Qualifier** — sub-column inside a family. Can be added on-the-fly without schema change. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Cell** — the intersection of row key, family, qualifier, and timestamp, holding the actual value. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Timestamp** — version identifier (64-bit long), enabling multi-version storage per cell. *(0.5 mark)* 6. **Table** — logical grouping of rows; supports atomic row-level operations. *(0.5 mark)* **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Aspect | Tall-Narrow | Wide-Flat | |---|---|---| | Shape | Millions of rows, $\le 5$ columns | Few rows, hundreds of columns | | Best For | Time-series, event logs, sensor data | Entity-document view, sparse user profiles | | Write pattern | High-throughput appends | Few writes, many column updates | | Read pattern | Range scan by time | Single-row GET with selective columns | | Example: Twitter | Row key = `user_id + reverse_ts`, 1 column | *(Not used)* | | Example: User profile | *(Not used)* | Row key = `user_id`, families: `profile`, `prefs`, `activity` | | Example: Stock ticks | Per-second ticks as separate rows | *(Not used)* | | Example: Patient record | *(Not used)* | One row per patient, families: `vitals`, `history`, `meds` | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently *describe* tall and wide but fail to *justify* with a real example. **You lose 2 marks** if you skip the example. Always give one concrete real-world instance for each. --- #### **Part (b) — E-commerce Schema Design (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Decompose access patterns (2 marks):** | # | Access Pattern | Implication | |---|---|---| | 1 | Customer + last 30 days, latest first | Row key must begin with `customer_id` and have a sortable time suffix in *descending* order | | 2 | Update shipping status of one order | Requires fast point-lookup by `order_id` → **secondary index table** | | 3 | Count orders per product per month | Requires lookup by `product_id + month` → **secondary index table** | **Step 2 — Primary Table: `orders_by_customer` (3 marks):** $$ \text{RowKey} = \text{customer\_id} \oplus (\text{Long.MAX\_VALUE} - \text{order\_timestamp}) $$ | Column Family | Qualifiers | `VERSIONS` | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | `info` | `order_id`, `product_id`, `amount`, `currency` | $1$ | Static order metadata | | `shipping` | `status`, `tracking_id`, `carrier` | $5$ | Status changes over time, keep history | | `payment` | `method`, `txn_id`, `paid_at` | $1$ | Immutable once paid | A range scan with prefix `customer_42` and time bound $\text{now} - 30\ \text{days}$ returns the customer's recent orders in latest-first order, in a single contiguous scan. **Step 3 — Index Table 1: `order_lookup` (1 mark):** $$ \text{RowKey} = \text{order\_id}\ (\text{e.g., UUID stored as bytes}) $$ | Column Family | Qualifiers | |---|---| | `ref` | `customer_id`, `primary_row_key` | Stores a pointer to the full order's primary row, enabling O(1) point-lookup by `order_id`. **Step 4 — Index Table 2: `product_monthly_counter` (1 mark):** $$ \text{RowKey} = \text{product\_id} \oplus \text{YYYY-MM} $$ | Column Family | Qualifiers | |---|---| | `count` | `total_orders`, `total_revenue` (using HBase's native `Increment` API) | This is updated transactionally via `checkAndPut` or `incrementColumnValue` for atomic counters. **Step 5 — Justification Summary:** - Primary table satisfies pattern 1 with one scan. - Index table 1 satisfies pattern 2 with a single GET. - Index table 2 satisfies pattern 3 with a single GET and atomic increment. - No joins needed, no full-table scans, all data co-located by primary access dimension. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* With a suitable diagram, describe the **HBase logical architecture** (META table, ZooKeeper, RegionServer, HFile, MemStore). Explain the role of each in serving a read and a write. **(b)** *(7 marks)* For an **IoT sensor network** generating 1 million readings per device per day across 1 million devices: 1. Design the row key. 2. Justify the column family structure. 3. Show how you would mitigate hotspotting caused by a few "noisy" sensors. 4. Estimate the **storage cost** for one year, given an average cell value of 50 bytes and 4 sensors per device family. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B (Concise Outline)** #### **Part (a) — HBase Architecture (7 marks)** Refer to **Section 4.5 diagram** for the read/write path. Roles: - **ZooKeeper (1 mark):** Maintains cluster state, coordinates master election, and stores the location of the META table. - **META Table (1 mark):** A special HBase table mapping each region to the RegionServer hosting it. - **RegionServer (1.5 marks):** Serves reads/writes for a set of regions; manages MemStore and BlockCache. - **MemStore (1.5 marks):** In-memory sorted buffer where writes are first appended before flush to HFile. - **HFile (1.5 marks):** Immutable, sorted file on HDFS holding the actual cell data; read via BlockCache. - **WAL (0.5 mark):** Write-Ahead Log ensures durability before data lands in MemStore. **Read path:** Client → ZooKeeper → META → RegionServer → BlockCache (hit) → return; else scan MemStore + HFile. **Write path:** Client → RegionServer → WAL append → MemStore insert → ack to client; later, MemStore flush creates an HFile. --- #### **Part (b) — IoT Schema Design (7 marks)** **1. Row key design (2 marks):** $$ \text{RowKey} = \text{device\_id} \oplus \text{reverse(timestamp\_bytes)} $$ Reason: co-locates all readings of a device; reversal makes the latest reading sort first. **2. Column family design (1 mark):** One family `s` with qualifiers `temp`, `hum`, `pres`, `volt`; `VERSIONS = 1`, `TTL = 90 days` for auto-expiry of cold data. **3. Hotspotting mitigation (2 marks):** Apply **salting** with $N_{salt} = 10$ for known noisy sensors: $$ \text{FinalRowKey} = \text{byte(salt)} \oplus \text{device\_id} \oplus \text{reverse(timestamp)} $$ Application runs 10 parallel scans, one per salt, and merges results client-side. Additionally, the application can **detect** noisy sensors (via a counter column) and **route their writes** to a separate "hot sensor" table with even more aggressive salting ($N_{salt} = 50$). **4. Storage cost estimation (2 marks):** Total readings per year: $$ N_{\text{rows}} = 10^6\ \text{devices} \times 10^6\ \text{readings/day} \times 365\ \text{days} = 3.65 \times 10^{14}\ \text{rows} $$ Per-row storage (approximate): $$ S_{\text{row}} = L_r + L_{cf} + L_{cq} + L_t + L_v \approx 16 + 8 + 8 + 8 + 50 \approx 90\ \text{bytes} $$ Total raw data: $$ S_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{rows}} \times S_{\text{row}} = 3.65 \times 10^{14} \times 90 \approx 3.3 \times 10^{16}\ \text{bytes} \approx 33\ \text{PB} $$ After 3-way HDFS replication: $$ S_{\text{replicated}} = 3 \times 33\ \text{PB} \approx 99\ \text{PB} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget **HDFS replication (default 3×)** and the **WAL overhead (~10%)**. Marks are docked for missing these. Always include replication in storage calculations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] HBase data model = sparse, sorted, multi-dimensional map; cell address is the 4-tuple `{row, family:qualifier, timestamp}`. - [x] **Row key** is the *only* primary index — design it first, before anything else. - [x] Keep **column families ≤ 3–5**; each family is an independent HFile stream. - [x] **Tall-Narrow** for time-series / append-heavy workloads; **Wide-Flat** for entity-document views. - [x] Avoid **monotonic row keys** — use salting, hashing, or reversal to prevent hotspotting. - [x] For "latest first" reads, use **`Long.MAX_VALUE − timestamp`** as a time suffix. - [x] For time-range reads per entity, use **`entity_id + reverse(timestamp)`**. - [x] **HBase has no joins and no native secondary indexes** → use **index tables** for alternate access paths. - [x] Denormalize aggressively; storage is cheap, joins are expensive. - [x] Use **`VERSIONS`** and **`TTL`** at the family level for retention control. - [x] **Salting** trades range-scan efficiency for write throughput; **hashing** trades all range scans for uniform distribution; **reversal** preserves some range capability. - [x] HBase default replication = **3×** — include it in storage estimates. - [x] Design **one table per access pattern** — this is the cardinal rule of NoSQL schema design. - [x] Final exam tip: Always justify your row key with a *concrete example query* to score full marks.

topics.title: Introduction to Stream Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Stream Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Big Data Processing, a **Data Stream** is formally defined as a **continuous, unbounded, real-time (or near real-time) sequence of data elements (called tuples, events, or records)** that are generated at a high velocity from one or more sources and must be processed within a constrained time window — typically with limited memory and the inability to revisit past elements arbitrarily.

Mathematically, an unbounded stream $S$ arriving over time $t$ can be represented as:

$$S = \{(e_1, t_1), (e_2, t_2), (e_3, t_3), \ldots\}$$

where each element $e_i$ is a tuple (key-value set) and $t_i$ is the timestamp associated with that element.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Format):**
> "A data stream is a continuous, ordered, time-varying sequence of data items that arrive at a system for processing, where the underlying distribution generating the data is typically non-stationary and the volume of data is too large to store in memory in its entirety."

### 1.2 The Four V's of Stream Data

| Characteristic | Symbol | Description |
|----------------|:------:|-------------|
| **Velocity** | $V_v$ | Speed at which data arrives (events per second) |
| **Volume** | $V_o$ | Total size of data (often unbounded) |
| **Variety** | $V_a$ | Heterogeneity in data types and sources |
| **Veracity** | $V_c$ | Uncertainty, noise, and quality of the stream |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The River of Data"

Imagine a **continuously flowing river** carrying countless leaves, each leaf representing a data point. As a stream processing system, you stand on the bank with a **small bucket** (limited memory) and a **fishing net with fixed mesh size** (filtering rules). You cannot store the entire river, and the leaves that pass you by are gone forever. Your only job is to make **instant decisions** — count the leaves, identify the red ones, or estimate how many leaves flow per minute.

This analogy captures the three pillars of stream processing:
1. **Unbounded data** — the river never ends.
2. **Limited memory** — your bucket fills quickly.
3. **Real-time processing** — you must act *before* leaves drift away.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis**: Examiners repeatedly test the conceptual difference between **Batch Processing** (finite dataset, processed later) and **Stream Processing** (infinite dataset, processed now). Master this distinction before attempting any application question.

### 1.4 Terminology Snapshot

- **Tuple / Event**: The atomic unit of a data stream (a record).
- **Stream Source**: The generator of data (e.g., IoT sensor, web click, server log).
- **Stream Sink**: The destination where processed results are sent (e.g., database, dashboard).
- **Stream Processor**: The computing engine that performs transformations and analytics.
- **Window**: A finite, time-bounded slice of an otherwise infinite stream.
- **Sliding Window**: A window that moves forward in time continuously.
- **Tumbling Window**: A non-overlapping, fixed-size window.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Stream Processing Pipeline

A stream processing system typically follows this structured logic:

1. **Data Ingestion** — High-speed ingestors (e.g., Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis) collect raw events from heterogeneous sources.
2. **Pre-processing / Filtering** — Irrelevant or duplicate events are dropped based on predicates $P(e_i)$.
3. **Transformation** — Each tuple is enriched, joined, or aggregated using a user-defined function (UDF).
4. **Windowed Aggregation** — Continuous queries are evaluated over finite slices of the stream.
5. **Output / Sink** — Results are pushed to databases, dashboards, or downstream applications.

### 2.2 Stream vs. Batch Processing — The KTU Distinction

| Parameter | Batch Processing | Stream Processing |
|-----------|:----------------:|:----------------:|
| Data Bound | Finite (bounded) | Infinite (unbounded) |
| Latency | Minutes to hours | Milliseconds to seconds |
| Storage | Full dataset stored | Limited memory / sliding windows |
| Computation | Retrospective | Real-time / prospective |
| Re-processing | Easy (replay dataset) | Hard (data is gone) |
| Examples | Hadoop MapReduce, Spark Batch | Apache Flink, Storm, Kafka Streams |
| Time Model | Processing time only | Event time + processing time |

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Description |
|---------|:--------------------:|:-----:|-------------|
| **Stream Data Rate** | $R = \frac{N}{T}$ | events/sec | Average arrival rate of tuples |
| **Peak-to-Average Ratio** | $PAR = \frac{R_{peak}}{R_{avg}}$ | dimensionless | Burstiness indicator |
| **Sliding Window Memory** | $M = R \times W \times S_{tuple}$ | bytes | Memory needed for window of size $W$ |
| **Tumbling Window Count** | $N_w = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{total}}{W} \right\rfloor$ | windows | Number of windows in time $T_{total}$ |
| **Reservoir Sampling Probability** | $p_i = \frac{k}{i}$ | probability | Inclusion probability for $i$-th element |
| **Bloom Filter FP Rate** | $f \approx \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$ | dimensionless | False positive rate with $k$ hashes, $m$ bits, $n$ elements |
| **Sampling Standard Error** | $SE = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2}{k}}$ | varies | Error of sample mean of size $k$ |
| **Exponential Histogram Error** | $\epsilon \cdot N$ | count | Approximation error of stream count |
| **Stream Throughput** | $\lambda = \frac{1}{R}$ | sec/event | Inter-arrival time inverse |
| **Watermark Lag** | $\Delta t = t_{proc} - t_{event}$ | sec | Time difference between processing and event time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip — Table Construction Rule**: In KTU answer sheets, the **Sliding Window Memory** formula and **Bloom Filter FP Rate** formula are the two highest-weightage equations examiners expect in 14-mark derivations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stream processing is the backbone of:
- **Financial Trading**: Detecting fraud in milliseconds using credit card swipe streams.
- **IoT and Smart Cities**: Aggregating temperature / traffic sensor streams in real time.
- **Social Media Analytics**: Tracking trending hashtags (e.g., Twitter firehose at **~500 million tweets per day**).
- **Telecommunications**: Monitoring call detail records (CDRs) for anomaly detection.
- **E-commerce**: Real-time recommendation engines based on clickstream behavior.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Stream Processing

- **Decisions must be fast** — stock trades lose value in microseconds.
- **Data is too big to store** — a single modern airplane engine generates **~10 TB of sensor data every 30 minutes**.
- **Patterns drift over time** — non-stationary distributions require online learning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sliding Window Memory Requirement

We want to determine the memory required to maintain a sliding window of size $W$ (in seconds) over a stream with average rate $R$ (events per second), where each tuple occupies $S_{tuple}$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Number of tuples entering the window per second**

$$N = R \times W$$

This is the count of tuples that arrive within the window horizon $W$. Explanation: if $R$ events come per second and the window spans $W$ seconds, then by the product rule, $R \cdot W$ tuples are within the window.

**Step 2 — Total memory in bytes**

$$M_{bytes} = N \times S_{tuple}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2**

$$M_{bytes} = (R \times W) \times S_{tuple}$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form**

$$M = R \cdot W \cdot S_{tuple}$$

> *Engineering Insight*: For a stream with $R = 10,000$ events/sec, $W = 60$ sec, and $S_{tuple} = 256$ bytes, the memory bound is $M = 10{,}000 \times 60 \times 256 = 153{,}600{,}000$ bytes $\approx 146.5$ MB. This is the minimum RAM a stream processor must reserve.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bloom Filter False Positive Rate

A Bloom filter uses $m$ bits, $k$ hash functions, and stores $n$ elements.

**Step 1 — Probability a single bit remains 0 after one insertion**

$$p_{0} = 1 - \frac{1}{m}$$

**Step 2 — Probability a bit remains 0 after $n$ insertions (with $k$ hashes per element)**

$$P_{0} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn}$$

**Step 3 — Approximate using the exponential identity $\lim_{x \to \infty}(1 - 1/x)^x = 1/e$**

$$P_{0} \approx e^{-kn/m}$$

**Step 4 — Probability of a false positive (all $k$ bits are 1)**

$$f = (1 - P_{0})^k \approx \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$$

**Step 5 — Optimal number of hash functions for minimum FP**

Differentiating $f$ with respect to $k$ and setting $\partial f / \partial k = 0$, the optimal $k$ is:

$$k_{opt} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Reservoir Sampling Probability

In **Reservoir Sampling Algorithm R** (Vitter, 1985), we want each of the $N$ stream elements to have an equal probability $k/N$ of being in the final sample of size $k$.

**Step 1 — The first $k$ elements enter the reservoir with probability 1**

$$P(e_i \in \text{Reservoir}) = 1 \quad \text{for } i \le k$$

**Step 2 — For the $i$-th element ($i > k$), the probability of entering is**

$$p_i = \frac{k}{i}$$

**Step 3 — For element $e_i$ to remain in the reservoir after $i + 1$ subsequent elements have been considered, the probability is**

$$P(\text{stay}) = \prod_{j=i+1}^{N}\left(1 - \frac{k}{j} \cdot \frac{1}{k}\right) = \frac{k}{N}$$

This shows uniform sampling — every element has equal probability $k/N$ regardless of arrival order.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Memory | Accuracy | Use Case |
|-----------|:------:|:--------:|----------|
| **Exact Sliding Window** | $O(N)$ | 100% | Small windows, financial ticks |
| **Exponential Histogram** | $O\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon} \log N\right)$ | $\pm \epsilon N$ | Approximate counts |
| **DGIM Algorithm** | $O(\log^2 N)$ | Within 50% error | 1-bit stream counting |
| **Bloom Filter** | $O(m)$ bits | Probabilistic | Membership testing |
| **CM-Sketch** | $O(\frac{1}{\epsilon^2} \log \delta)$ | $(\epsilon, \delta)$-approximation | Frequency estimation |
| **Reservoir Sampling** | $O(k)$ | Exact on sample | Random sample selection |
| **HyperLogLog** | $O(\log \log N)$ | ~0.81% error | Cardinality estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight**: The **DGIM algorithm** and **Bloom Filter** appear in nearly every KTU 2024 scheme question paper for Module 3. Memorize their trade-offs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stream Processing Architecture — High-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[IoT Sensors] --> ing
    src2[Web Click Logs] --> ing
    src3[Social Media APIs] --> ing
    src4[Financial Feeds] --> ing
    ing[Stream Ingestor Kafka] --> pre
    pre[Pre Processing Filter] --> trans
    trans[Transformation UDF Engine] --> win
    win[Window Aggregator] --> out1[Dashboards]
    win --> out2[Real Time Database]
    win --> out3[Alert System]
    win --> out4[Downstream ML Pipeline]
    mon[Monitoring Metrics Collector] -.-> ing
    mon -.-> pre
    mon -.-> trans
    mon -.-> win
```

**Caption**: The above diagram shows the canonical stream processing architecture. The four heterogeneous sources on the left feed into a centralized ingestor (Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis). The ingestor hands tuples to a pre-processing filter, then to a transformation engine, then to a windowed aggregator, and finally outputs to multiple sinks. A side-channel monitoring subsystem observes latency, throughput, and error rates across all stages.

### 4.2 Stream Data Lifecycle — Event Time vs. Processing Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Event Generated at Source]) --> evt[Event Time t1]
    evt --> queue[Network Buffer and Queue]
    queue --> proc[Processing Time t2]
    proc --> result([Result Emitted to Sink])
    note1[Watermark tracks event time progress] -.-> queue
    note2[Out of order arrivals handled by buffering] -.-> proc
```

**Caption**: The lifecycle shows that event time $t_1$ (when the event actually happened at the source) differs from processing time $t_2$ (when the system handles it) due to network delay. **Watermarks** are heuristic markers that allow the processor to decide when all events for a given event-time window have arrived.

### 4.3 Comparison: Batch vs. Stream Data Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BATCH[Batch Processing World]
        b1[Collect All Data] --> b2[Store in Data Lake]
        b2 --> b3[Periodic Job Runs]
        b3 --> b4[Generate Report]
    end
    subgraph STREAM[Stream Processing World]
        s1[Continuous Data] --> s2[In Memory Window]
        s2 --> s3[Continuous Query Engine]
        s3 --> s4[Real Time Output]
    end
```

**Caption**: Batch processing collects, stores, then processes periodically. Stream processing ingests continuously, holds a finite in-memory window, and emits results in real time.

### 4.4 Sliding Window State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s0[Stream t0] --> s1[Window Active t1]
    s1 --> s2[Slide Forward delta t]
    s2 --> s3[Old tuples evicted]
    s3 --> s4[New tuples added]
    s4 --> s5[Aggregation recomputed]
    s5 --> s1
```

**Caption**: A sliding window is in a perpetual state of evaluation — at each $\Delta t$ step, the oldest tuples are evicted, the newest tuples are inserted, and the aggregation is recomputed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`
> **Define a data stream. List any four characteristics of stream data.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **data stream** is a continuous, ordered, unbounded sequence of data items (tuples) that arrive at high velocity and must be processed in real time, often without the ability to store the entire dataset.

Four characteristics of stream data:
1. **Velocity** — Data arrives at a high, often unpredictable rate.
2. **Unbounded Volume** — The dataset has no defined end.
3. **Temporal Ordering** — Tuples are timestamped and time-sensitive.
4. **One-Pass Constraint** — Each element is typically processed once and then discarded.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`
> **Differentiate between event time and processing time in stream processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Event Time | Processing Time |
|--------|:----------:|:---------------:|
| Definition | Time when event occurred at the source | Time when event is handled by the system |
| Affected by | Source clock | Network delay, queue depth, system load |
| Use | Windowing, watermarks | Performance monitoring, scheduling |
| Consistency | Stable across retries | Variable |

Stream processors must reconcile both clocks to produce accurate, consistent results.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the architecture of a stream processing system with a neat block diagram. List any **two real-world applications**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **Bloom Filter false positive rate** and compute the optimal number of hash functions for $m = 8000$ bits, $n = 1000$ elements. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A stream processing system architecture consists of the following stages:

1. **Data Sources (1 mark)** — IoT sensors, web logs, financial feeds, social APIs. These emit raw events continuously.

2. **Stream Ingestor (1 mark)** — A high-throughput message broker (e.g., Apache Kafka) that decouples producers from consumers and handles backpressure.

3. **Pre-Processing and Filtering Layer (1 mark)** — Removes duplicates, drops malformed records, applies schema validation.

4. **Transformation and Enrichment (1 mark)** — User-defined functions (UDFs) join the stream with reference data, compute derived fields.

5. **Windowed Aggregation Engine (1 mark)** — Evaluates continuous queries over finite windows (sliding, tumbling, session).

6. **Output Sinks (1 mark)** — Dashboards, real-time databases (Redis), alerting systems, ML pipelines.

7. **Real-world applications (1 mark):**
   - **Credit card fraud detection** — flagging suspicious transactions within 200 ms.
   - **Smart traffic management** — adaptive signal control based on real-time camera feeds.

[Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the block diagram.]

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The Bloom filter uses an $m$-bit array, $k$ hash functions, and stores $n$ elements.

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Probability a bit is 0 after one hash: $p_0 = 1 - 1/m$

**Step 2 (1 mark)** — After inserting $n$ items with $k$ hashes each:

$$P_0 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^{kn}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)** — Using $\lim_{x \to \infty}(1 - 1/x)^x = 1/e$:

$$P_0 \approx e^{-kn/m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — False positive probability (all $k$ queried bits are 1):

$$f = (1 - P_0)^k = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^k$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)** — Optimal hash functions:

$$k_{opt} = \frac{m}{n} \ln 2$$

**Step 6 (2 marks)** — Numerical evaluation with $m = 8000$, $n = 1000$:

$$k_{opt} = \frac{8000}{1000} \times \ln 2 = 8 \times 0.6931 = 5.545$$

Rounding down to an integer for practical implementation, $k = 5$ hash functions. The resulting false positive rate is:

$$f = (1 - e^{-5 \times 1000 / 8000})^5 = (1 - e^{-0.625})^5 = (1 - 0.5353)^5 = (0.4647)^5 \approx 0.0217$$

So the false positive rate is approximately **2.17%**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]`
>
> **(a)** Compare and contrast **batch processing** and **stream processing** in detail. Mention any **three differences** with examples. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe the **DGIM algorithm** for counting 1s in a sliding window over a binary stream. Show how the memory requirement is reduced to $O(\log^2 N)$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Parameter | Batch Processing | Stream Processing |
|-----------|:----------------:|:----------------:|
| Latency | Hours to days | Milliseconds |
| Storage | Full dataset | Limited memory |
| Re-processing | Trivial (re-run job) | Hard (data already gone) |
| Example | Daily sales report | Real-time stock price alert |
| Computation model | Retrospective | Prospective / online |

(2 marks for the comparison table; 1 mark each for three crisp differences with examples)

**Three crisp differences with examples (3 marks):**

1. **Latency**: Hadoop MapReduce batches nightly logs and reports fraud the next morning (batch); Apache Flink detects credit card fraud in 100 ms (stream).
2. **Storage**: Batch uses HDFS to store terabytes; Stream uses in-memory sliding windows or specialized sketches (e.g., HyperLogLog).
3. **Use case**: Batch is best for monthly billing; Stream is best for live sports score updates.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The **Datar-Gionis-Indyk-Motwani (DGIM) algorithm** approximates the number of 1s in a sliding window of size $N$ using $O(\log^2 N)$ memory.

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Time is divided into buckets. Each bucket has a timestamp and a count of 1s (always a power of 2).

**Step 2 (1 mark)** — Bucket sizes double: 1, 2, 4, 8, ..., up to at most $N/2$. Only two buckets of any size are allowed.

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — When a new bit arrives:
   - If it is 0, ignore it.
   - If it is 1, create a new bucket of size 1.
   - If three buckets of the same size now exist, merge the two oldest into one bucket of double size.

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — When querying, drop buckets whose end timestamp is older than $t_{now} - N$.

**Step 5 (2 marks)** — The estimate is half of the size of the last bucket plus the full sizes of all other buckets still in the window. This guarantees an error of at most **50%** (or can be refined to arbitrary $\epsilon$).

**Memory analysis:** The number of buckets of each size is at most 2, and bucket sizes grow geometrically. The largest bucket has size $N/2$, so the total number of distinct bucket sizes is $O(\log N)$. With at most $2 \log N$ buckets and each bucket storing a timestamp + count, the total memory is $O(\log^2 N)$.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Stream Concepts Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing event time and processing time** — A 1-mark loss if you swap these in a windowing question.
> 2. **Forgetting to express units** — "R = 100" is incomplete; KTU expects "100 events/sec".
> 3. **Skipping the Bloom Filter derivation** — Examiners allocate 1 mark for *each* of the four intermediate steps. Do not jump to the final formula.
> 4. **Writing `|x|` in a markdown table** — Always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to avoid table syntax breakage.
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams** — A stream architecture diagram without labels for "ingestor", "windowing", and "sink" will lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Using wrong notation for $k_{opt}$** — The correct formula is $k_{opt} = (m/n) \ln 2$, not $\ln(m/n)$.
> 7. **Ignoring the bounded memory constraint** — Stream algorithms are evaluated on *space efficiency*. Failing to mention memory bounds is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Introduction to Stream Concepts**
>
> - A **data stream** is **unbounded, continuous, time-ordered**, and must be processed in (near) real time.
> - The **Four V's** of stream data are **Velocity, Volume, Variety, Veracity**.
> - **Batch processing** = finite + delayed + full storage; **Stream processing** = infinite + real-time + bounded memory.
> - **Event time** = when the data was produced; **Processing time** = when the system sees it. **Watermarks** bridge the gap.
> - A **window** is the only way to make unbounded stream data computable. Variants: **tumbling, sliding, session**.
> - The **sliding window memory** bound is given by $M = R \cdot W \cdot S_{tuple}$.
> - The **Bloom filter false positive rate** is $f \approx (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$, with optimal $k_{opt} = (m/n) \ln 2$.
> - The **DGIM algorithm** approximates 1-counts in a stream using $O(\log^2 N)$ memory with at most 50% error.
> - **Reservoir Sampling R** provides uniform random sampling of size $k$ from an arbitrary stream.
> - **HyperLogLog** estimates cardinality (count of unique elements) using $O(\log \log N)$ memory.
> - **Stream sources** include IoT, web logs, social media, financial ticks, and clickstreams.
> - **Stream sinks** include dashboards, real-time databases, alerting systems, and downstream ML.
> - Always mention the **one-pass constraint** and **bounded memory** when defining stream processing.
> - In KTU answer sheets, **draw labeled block diagrams** for any architecture-related question worth ≥ 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Data Model and Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Stream Data Model and Architecture # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Stream Data** refers to continuous, unbounded, real-time sequences of data records that are generated at high velocity from various sources such as sensors, social media feeds, financial markets, network logs, IoT devices, and web clickstreams. Unlike traditional static datasets that are stored persistently and queried later, stream data is processed on-the-fly as it arrives, often with strict latency constraints measured in **milliseconds** or **seconds**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Big Data Processing*, the **Stream Data Model** is a formal abstraction that represents data as an *ordered, time-stamped, potentially infinite sequence* of tuples, where each tuple belongs to a predefined schema and arrives asynchronously. The model supports operations such as filtering, aggregation, joining, windowing, and pattern detection over sliding temporal intervals. **Stream Data Architecture** refers to the end-to-end system design — comprising ingestion, processing, storage, and analysis layers — that handles unbounded data streams with guarantees on throughput, latency, fault-tolerance, and exactly-once semantics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme places special emphasis on understanding the *differences between traditional DBMS data models and stream data models*, the *concept of a Data Stream Management System (DSMS)*, and the *architectural blueprints* of modern stream processors (e.g., Apache Kafka, Flink, Spark Streaming). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fire Hydrant vs. Water Bucket" Model Imagine you are standing beside a **fire hydrant** that is releasing water continuously at 1,000 litres per minute. You cannot store all the water in a small bucket, and you certainly cannot wait until tomorrow to drink it. You must process (drink, filter, or divert) the water *as it flows*. - The **fire hydrant** = your data source (Twitter API, sensor network, stock ticker) - The **continuous water flow** = the unbounded data stream - The **bucket** = limited in-memory buffer (bounded memory) - The **act of drinking/diverting in real time** = stream processing - The **time** = latency constraint (you must act within milliseconds) > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** If you try to "store first, process later" with stream data, you will run out of disk space within hours. This is the **fundamental paradigm shift** from the *store-and-query* model of RDBMS to the *process-and-store* model of stream systems. ## 1.3 Defining Metrics (KTU Standard Terminology) | Metric | Definition | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | **Throughput** | Records processed per second | 10⁴ – 10⁷ events/sec | | **Latency** | Time from event arrival to result emission | 1 ms – 10 s | | **Cardinality** | Number of distinct values in a stream column | 10⁶ – 10⁹ | | **Window Size (W)** | Temporal interval for aggregation | 1 s – 24 hr | | **Slide Interval (β)** | Frequency of window advancement | 100 ms – 1 hr | | **Backpressure** | Mechanism to throttle producers when consumers are slow | Enabled | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Characteristics of Stream Data (The "7 V's of Velocity") 1. **Continuous** — Data arrives in a non-stop fashion, 24/7. 2. **Unbounded** — There is no predefined end; the stream is conceptually infinite. 3. **Ordered (by time)** — Each record carries a timestamp; order is the primary axis. 4. **Heterogeneous** — Schema can evolve; data sources vary in format. 5. **Transient** — Once processed (and not stored), the data is gone forever. 6. **High-velocity** — Bursts can reach millions of events per second. 7. **Imperfect** — Late arrivals, duplicates, out-of-order events are the norm. ## 2.2 Stream Data Model — Formal Specification A stream **S** is defined as a possibly infinite sequence of tuples: $$ S = \{(t_1, \mathbf{x}_1), (t_2, \mathbf{x}_2), (t_3, \mathbf{x}_3), \ldots\} $$ where: - $t_i \in \mathbb{R}^+$ is the **timestamp** (or sequence number) of the $i$-th tuple - $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_{i,1}, x_{i,2}, \ldots, x_{i,n})$ is the **attribute vector** drawn from schema $\mathcal{S}$ ### 2.2.1 Window Models (Critical for KTU) **1. Tumbling Window** — Non-overlapping, fixed-size windows: $$ W_k = \{ (t_i, \mathbf{x}_i) \mid k \cdot \beta \leq t_i < (k+1) \cdot \beta \} $$ **2. Sliding Window** — Overlapping windows advancing by a smaller slide: $$ W_k = \{ (t_i, \mathbf{x}_i) \mid k \cdot \beta \leq t_i < k \cdot \beta + W \} $$ **3. Session Window** — Dynamically sized, based on activity gaps: $$ W_{\text{session}} = \max(\Delta t_{\text{activity}}) \leq T_{\text{gap}} $$ ## 2.3 Data Stream Management System (DSMS) — Core Components A DSMS is the *streaming equivalent* of a traditional DBMS. The reference architecture has **6 layers**: 1. **Input Layer** — Source connectors (Kafka, Kinesis, MQTT, Sockets) 2. **Ingestion Layer** — Bounded in-memory queues + disk spillover 3. **Processing Layer** — Stream Processing Engine (SPE) running user-defined operators 4. **Storage Layer** — State backends (RocksDB), time-series DBs (InfluxDB) 5. **Output Layer** — Sinks (Databases, dashboards, alerting systems) 6. **Coordination & Monitoring Layer** — Cluster managers, metrics (Prometheus) ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **For absolute value in formulas, use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.** | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Tumbling Window Count | $N = \lfloor T_{\text{total}} / W \rfloor$ | Number of windows in total time | | 2 | Sliding Window Overlap | $N_{\text{windows}} = \lfloor (T - W) / \beta \rfloor + 1$ | Total windows emitted | | 3 | Throughput | $\lambda = N_{\text{events}} / \Delta t$ | Events per second | | 4 | Latency (E2E) | $L = t_{\text{ingest}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{egress}}$ | Sum of stage latencies | | 5 | Bloom Filter FP Rate | $P_{fp} = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ | Where $m$ = bits, $n$ = items, $k$ = hash funcs | | 6 | Reservoir Sampling | $P(\text{keep}) = k / i$ for $i > k$ | Prob. of keeping $i$-th item in size-$k$ sample | | 7 | Exponential Moving Avg | $EMA_t = \alpha \cdot x_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot EMA_{t-1}$ | Smoothing factor $0 < \alpha \le 1$ | | 8 | Count-Min Sketch Est. | $\hat{f}(a) = \min_j T[j, h_j(a)]$ | Approximate frequency of item $a$ | | 9 | Data Drift (KL Div) | $D_{KL}(P \vert\vert Q) = \sum P(x) \log(P(x)/Q(x))$ | Divergence between distributions | | 10 | Backpressure Rate | $r_{bp} = \min(\lambda_{\text{prod}}, \mu_{\text{cons}})$ | Throttled ingestion rate | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Stream Application | Latency Target | |---|---|---| | **Finance** | Fraud detection on credit-card swipes | < 50 ms | | **Telecom** | Network intrusion detection (DDoS) | < 100 ms | | **E-Commerce** | Real-time recommendation engine | < 200 ms | | **IoT / Smart City** | Traffic-light adaptive control | < 1 s | | **Healthcare** | ICU patient vital-sign monitoring | < 5 s | | **Social Media** | Trending-topic detection | < 1 min | | **Logistics** | Fleet GPS tracking & route optimization | < 10 s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Modern stream architectures are *lambda* (batch + speed layers) or *kappa* (unified log + stream processor) architectures. The kappa architecture has become the de-facto standard in cloud-native systems because it eliminates the dual-code-path problem. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Sliding Window Cardinality **Problem:** A stock ticker stream emits 1 event every 10 ms. A sliding window has size $W = 5$ s and slide $\beta = 1$ s. How many windows are emitted in 1 hour? **Step 1:** Identify the parameters. $$ W = 5 \text{ s}, \quad \beta = 1 \text{ s}, \quad T = 3600 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the sliding window formula. $$ N_{\text{windows}} = \left\lfloor \frac{T - W}{\beta} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ **Step 3:** Substitute values. $$ N_{\text{windows}} = \left\lfloor \frac{3600 - 5}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 $$ **Step 4:** Compute. $$ N_{\text{windows}} = \lfloor 3595 \rfloor + 1 = 3596 \text{ windows} $$ **Step 5:** Verify intuition. Since the slide is 1 s and total time is 1 hour, we expect approximately 3600 windows. The 5-second initial delay (because the window must first fill) accounts for the difference — **3596 is consistent**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Bloom Filter False-Positive Rate (Used for Stream Membership Queries) **Problem:** A stream processor uses a Bloom filter to detect whether an event ID has been seen in the last hour. The filter has $m = 10^6$ bits, $n = 10^4$ expected elements, and uses $k = 5$ hash functions. Find the false-positive probability. **Step 1:** Recall the canonical formula. $$ P_{fp} = \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^{k} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the exponent. $$ -\frac{kn}{m} = -\frac{5 \times 10^4}{10^6} = -0.05 $$ **Step 3:** Compute the exponential. $$ e^{-0.05} = 0.951229 $$ **Step 4:** Compute the inner difference. $$ 1 - 0.951229 = 0.048771 $$ **Step 5:** Raise to power $k$. $$ P_{fp} = (0.048771)^{5} \approx 2.7 \times 10^{-7} $$ **Step 6:** Interpret the result. A false-positive probability of $\approx 0.000027\%$ is excellent for production stream systems — for every 10 million lookups, fewer than 3 will be false alarms. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stream vs. Traditional DBMS | Property | Traditional RDBMS | Stream Data Model | |---|---|---| | **Data Nature** | Bounded, persistent | Unbounded, transient | | **Query Model** | One-time, ad-hoc | Continuous, standing | | **Execution** | Pull-based (query pulls data) | Push-based (data pushes query) | | **Memory Assumption** | Disk-resident data fits in storage | Stream is too large; main memory only | | **Result Semantics** | Exact | Often approximate | | **Latency Goal** | Seconds to minutes | Milliseconds to seconds | | **Time Semantics** | Valid time / transaction time | Event time, ingestion time, processing time | | **Concurrency Control** | ACID transactions | At-most-once / at-least-once / exactly-once | | **Updates** | In-place UPDATE/DELETE | Immutable append-only | | **Schema** | Rigid, schema-on-write | Flexible, schema-on-read (often Avro/Protobuf) | ## 3.4 Three Semantics of Stream Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU frequently tests this tri-class taxonomy.** 1. **At-most-once** — No retries. Lowest latency, weakest guarantee. Risk: data loss. 2. **At-least-once** — Retries on failure. May produce duplicates. Risk: double counting. 3. **Exactly-once** — Idempotent processing with checkpointing. Strongest guarantee. Requires transactional sinks. Mathematically, the delivery guarantees relate to the system reliability $R$ and retry count $r$: $$ P(\text{exactly-once}) = R \cdot (1 - (1 - R)^r) \quad \text{approaching 1 as } r \to \infty $$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Stream Data Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA[IoT Sensors] -->|MQTT| ingIn srcB[Web Clickstream] -->|HTTPS| ingIn srcC[Database CDC] -->|Binlog| ingIn srcD[Kafka Topics] -->|Kafka Protocol| ingIn ingIn[Ingestion Layer
Kafka / Pulsar] --> procIn procIn[Stream Processing Engine
Flink / Spark Streaming] --> stateDB procIn --> winOp{Window
Operator} winOp --> aggOp[Aggregator
Sum / Avg / Count] winOp --> mlOp[ML Inference
via Model Server] stateDB[(State Backend
RocksDB / S3)] --> procIn aggOp --> sinkA[Sink Dashboards
Grafana] mlOp --> sinkB[Sink Alerts
PagerDuty] procIn --> sinkC[Sink Data Lake
Iceberg / Delta] mon[Monitoring
Prometheus / Grafana] -.->|Metrics| procIn orch[Orchestrator
Kubernetes] -.->|Schedules| procIn ``` ## 4.2 Lambda vs. Kappa Architecture Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Lambda_Arch [LAMBDA ARCHITECTURE] L1[Data Source] --> L2[Message Queue] L2 --> L3[Speed Layer
Storm / Samza] L2 --> L4[Batch Layer
Hadoop / Spark] L3 --> L5[Serving Layer
NoSQL Store] L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6[Query / Dashboard] end subgraph Kappa_Arch [KAPPA ARCHITECTURE] K1[Data Source] --> K2[Unified Log
Kafka] K2 --> K3[Stream Processor
Flink] K3 --> K4[Serving Layer
Materialized View] K4 --> K5[Query / Dashboard] end ``` ## 4.3 Sliding vs. Tumbling Window Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["t=0s"]:::marker A1["t=1s"]:::marker A2["t=2s"]:::marker A3["t=3s"]:::marker A4["t=4s"]:::marker A5["t=5s"]:::marker subgraph Tumbling[ "TUMBLING WINDOW (W=2s, β=2s)"] TW1["Window 1: 0s–2s"] TW2["Window 2: 2s–4s"] TW3["Window 3: 4s–5s+"] end subgraph Sliding[ "SLIDING WINDOW (W=3s, β=1s)"] SW1["W1: 0s–3s"] SW2["W2: 1s–4s"] SW3["W3: 2s–5s"] SW4["W4: 3s–6s+"] end classDef marker fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Tumbling windows partition the timeline into non-overlapping chunks. Sliding windows emit results at every slide interval, with consecutive windows overlapping by $W - \beta$ seconds. ## 4.4 Time Semantics in Streams ```mermaid flowchart LR ev1[Event A
Happens at 10:00:00]:::event ev2[Event B
Happens at 10:00:05]:::event ing1[Ingested at 10:00:03]:::ingest ing2[Ingested at 10:00:08]:::ingest proc1[Processed at 10:00:04]:::proc proc2[Processed at 10:00:12]:::proc ev1 -.->|Event Time: 10:00:00| proc1 ev1 -.->|Ingestion Time: 10:00:03| ing1 proc1 -.->|Processing Time: 10:00:04| proc1 classDef event fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#fff classDef ingest fill:#FF9800,stroke:#E65100,color:#fff classDef proc fill:#2196F3,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#fff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Differentiate between the data model of a traditional RDBMS and a Data Stream Management System (DSMS). Mention any three points.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | RDBMS | DSMS | |---|---|---| | 1. **Data Persistence** | Data is persistent and stored on disk | Data is transient; only summaries are stored (1 mark) | | 2. **Query Execution** | One-shot queries, results are exact | Continuous/standing queries, results are often approximate (1 mark) | | 3. **Data Arrival** | Static; loaded in batches | Continuous, unbounded, time-ordered (1 mark) | --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define the term *sliding window* in the context of stream data processing. Give its formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **sliding window** is a time-based or count-based mechanism that continuously evaluates a query over the *most recent W* units of time, with the window advancing forward by a fixed **slide interval $\beta$** (1 mark). **Formula (1 mark):** $$ W_k = \{ (t_i, \mathbf{x}_i) \mid k \cdot \beta \leq t_i < k \cdot \beta + W \} $$ **Example (1 mark):** With $W = 10$ min and $\beta = 2$ min, the system emits a fresh aggregate every 2 minutes over the trailing 10 minutes of data. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, candidates answer **ONE of TWO** alternatives. Each question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* **(a)** Explain the **Lambda Architecture** for stream data processing with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **advantages** and **disadvantages**. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. Definition (2 marks):** Lambda Architecture, proposed by Nathan Marz, processes big data using two parallel paths: - **Batch Layer** — processes the *entire* historical dataset in batches to produce accurate, comprehensive views. - **Speed Layer** — processes *only the recent* (real-time) data to provide low-latency approximations. - **Serving Layer** — merges results from both layers to answer user queries. **2. Block Diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Data Source] --> mq[Message Queue] mq --> batch[Batch Layer
Hadoop MapReduce] mq --> speed[Speed Layer
Storm / Flink] batch --> sview[(Batch Views
HBase)] speed --> rview[(Real-time Views)] sview --> merge[Merged Query Results] rview --> merge merge --> user[User Query] ``` **3. Advantages (1.5 marks):** - Fault-tolerant: batch layer can recompute from raw data. - Robust to hardware failures. - Handles both historical and real-time analytics. **4. Disadvantages (1.5 marks):** - **Dual code path** — same logic must be written twice (once in batch, once in stream). - High maintenance overhead. - Resource-intensive (two complete pipelines). --- **(b)** Compare **Lambda and Kappa Architectures** in detail. Why is the Kappa architecture gaining popularity in modern systems? *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Lambda | Kappa (1.5 marks) | |---|---|---| | Layers | 3 (Batch, Speed, Serving) | 2 (Stream + Serving) | | Code | Duplicated | Single codebase | | Reprocessing | Batch replay | Replay from log (Kafka) | | Latency | Mixed | Uniform low | | Storage | Immutable master + speed views | Single immutable log | | Complexity | High | Moderate | **Why Kappa is winning (5.5 marks — write any 4):** 1. **Single source of truth** — Kafka (or Pulsar) acts as the *unified log* holding all events. 2. **Reprocessing via log replay** — no separate batch job; just reset offsets and re-run the Flink job. 3. **No code duplication** — only stream-processing code exists, reducing bugs. 4. **Easier operational model** — fewer moving parts, less to monitor. 5. **Cloud-native fit** — works seamlessly with Kubernetes, serverless functions. 6. **Real-time + historical via same engine** — Flink can do both bounded (batch) and unbounded (stream) processing. 7. Industry adoption: Uber, LinkedIn, Netflix, Alibaba have all moved to Kappa-style architectures. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **(a)** What is a **Data Stream Management System (DSMS)**? Describe the **reference architecture** of a DSMS with the function of each layer. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** A DSMS is a software system designed to manage and query continuous, unbounded, time-ordered data streams using continuous query semantics — the streaming counterpart of a DBMS. **Reference Architecture (6 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TB A[1. Source Layer
Sensors, Logs, APIs] --> B[2. Ingestion Layer
Buffers, Message Queues] B --> C[3. Continuous Query Layer
Operators, Windows] C --> D[4. Storage Layer
State + History] D --> E[5. Output Layer
Sinks, Alerts, Views] E --> F[6. Monitoring Layer
Metrics, Alerts] F -.-> C ``` | Layer | Function | |---|---| | 1. **Source** | Origin of streaming data (1 mark) | | 2. **Ingestion** | Decouples producers from consumers; ensures ordering & backpressure (1 mark) | | 3. **Continuous Query** | Executes user-defined operators (filter, join, aggregate, ML) (1.5 marks) | | 4. **Storage** | Holds in-memory state and time-indexed summaries (1 mark) | | 5. **Output** | Pushes results to dashboards, databases, or actions (0.5 mark) | | 6. **Monitoring** | Tracks latency, throughput, fault recovery (1 mark) | --- **(b)** Explain the **three delivery semantics** in stream processing: *at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once*. Give one real-world scenario for each where it is most appropriate. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **1. At-Most-Once (1.5 marks)** - The system acknowledges an event the moment it is received, *without retrying* on failure. - Trade-off: lowest latency, but events can be lost. - **Scenario:** Real-time stock-tick display where missing one tick is acceptable but staleness is not. **2. At-Least-Once (1.5 marks)** - The system retries failed events until acknowledged, *possibly producing duplicates*. - Trade-off: no data loss, but may over-count. - **Scenario:** Log-aggregation systems (e.g., Filebeat → Logstash) where duplicate log lines are tolerable and can be deduplicated downstream. **3. Exactly-Once (2 marks)** - Combines idempotent processing + transactional sinks to guarantee no loss and no duplication. - Trade-off: highest latency and resource cost. - **Scenario:** Financial transaction processing (NEFT/IMPS) where double-charging a customer is unacceptable. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Property | At-Most-Once | At-Least-Once | Exactly-Once | |---|---|---|---| | Data Loss | Possible | No | No | | Duplicates | No | Possible | No | | Latency | Lowest | Medium | Highest | | Implementation | Trivial | Easy | Hard (needs 2PC) | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Zones — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam!** > > 1. **Confusing Event Time with Processing Time** — A 1-mark deduction per occurrence. Event time = when the data *happened*; processing time = when the system *sees* it. In exam answers, always label which one your windowing uses. > > 2. **Mixing Lambda and Kappa in the same diagram** — Examiners will not give partial credit. Pick one architecture per question and stay consistent. > > 3. **Forgetting the formula for sliding-window count** — Many students write only the concept but skip the formula. You will lose 2 marks. > > 4. **Not stating *why* a particular delivery semantic is chosen** — Simply listing them fetches only 50% marks. You MUST justify the choice with a real-world scenario. > > 5. **Spelling DSMS as "DSMDS" or "DSSM"** — These are auto-flagged as careless errors by evaluators. > > 6. **Skipping the diagram** — In a 7-mark sub-question on architecture, the diagram carries **2 marks** by itself. Always draw one, even if rough. > > 7. **Confusing "throughput" with "latency"** — They are inverse concepts. Throughput = events/sec; Latency = time-per-event. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision? Read only this section.** - [x] **Stream Data** = continuous, unbounded, time-ordered, transient. - [x] **Stream Data Model** $S = \{(t_1, \mathbf{x}_1), (t_2, \mathbf{x}_2), \ldots\}$ - [x] **Three window types** = Tumbling, Sliding, Session. - [x] **Sliding window formula** — $\lfloor (T - W) / \beta \rfloor + 1$ windows. - [x] **Tumbling window formula** — $\lfloor T / W \rfloor$ windows. - [x] **DSMS = Streaming DBMS**, uses *continuous queries*, not one-shot. - [x] **Lambda Architecture** = Batch + Speed + Serving layers. - [x] **Kappa Architecture** = Single unified log + Stream processor. - [x] **Three delivery semantics** = At-most-once, At-least-once, Exactly-once. - [x] **Three time semantics** = Event time, Ingestion time, Processing time. - [x] **Bloom filter FP rate** — $P_{fp} = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ - [x] **Backpressure** throttles producers to protect consumers. - [x] **State backends** (RocksDB) keep windowed aggregates in-memory. - [x] **KTU-favourite keywords** to use in answers: *unbounded, continuous query, push-based, sliding window, event time, exactly-once, backpressure, checkpointing, watermark*. - [x] **Real-world examples to memorize:** Kafka (log), Flink (processor), Spark Streaming (micro-batch), Storm (legacy SPE), Pulsar (unified log). - [x] **Industry adopters of Kappa:** Uber, Netflix, LinkedIn, Alibaba. - [x] **Draw the diagram** for every architecture question — earns 2 marks instantly. --- > **END OF NOTES — Stream Data Model and Architecture | KTU 2024 Scheme | Module 3**

topics.title: Sampling Data in a Stream
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sampling Data in a Stream

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stream Sampling** is the process of selecting a representative subset (sample) of data items from a continuous, unbounded, and rapidly arriving data stream such that the sample can be used to make reliable inferences about aggregate properties of the entire stream, while operating under strict constraints of **single-pass access**, **limited memory**, and **real-time latency**.

In the context of **Big Data Processing**, a *data stream* is a sequence of data elements $S = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, \dots, e_n, \dots\}$ that arrives incrementally, often at very high velocity (millions of records per second), and whose total cardinality $N$ may be either **unknown** or **effectively infinite**. Because storing the entire stream is infeasible, we use **sampling** to maintain a small, dynamically updated subset that approximates the characteristics of the full stream.

**Key Technical Vocabulary (KTU 2024 Standardized):**
- **Universe $U$**: The set of all possible elements in the stream.
- **Sample $S$**: The selected subset of elements from $U$.
- **Sampling Rate $f$**: The ratio of sample size to total observed elements, $f = \vert S \vert / n$.
- **Inclusion Probability $p_i$**: The probability that element $e_i$ is selected into the sample.
- **Sampling Frame**: The logical window of the stream from which elements can be drawn.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sampling is positioned as a *pre-processing* step that enables later computation of stream aggregates (counts, quantiles, moments, heavy hitters) using bounded memory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **public health researcher** studying the eating habits of 1.3 billion Indians. You cannot interview every single person — it is too expensive, too slow, and physically impossible. Instead, you randomly select **5,000 people** across different states, ages, and incomes, and ask them about their diet. From this small sample, you estimate the national average sugar consumption.

Now, replace the people with **data records**, and you have **stream sampling**:
- The "interview" is the **computation** you wish to perform.
- The "5,000 people" is your **reservoir** (a fixed-size sample).
- The "nationally unknown total population" is the **unbounded stream length**.

Another powerful analogy: think of a **river of water** (the stream) flowing past a small **collection cup** (your sample buffer). The cup can only hold so much water, so as new water flows in, you occasionally replace some of the existing water with the incoming water, ensuring the cup contains a fresh, fair representation of the entire river.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is sampling hard in streams?** Unlike traditional statistics where you have the entire dataset upfront, streams are *adversarial* in nature — you do not know $N$ in advance, and elements arrive in a single pass. Once an element is skipped, it is **gone forever**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the standard parameters universally adopted in stream sampling literature (and expected to be known in KTU 2024 examinations):

- **Sample Size $k$**: The desired number of elements to retain. Typical values range from $10^3$ to $10^6$.
