# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Attribute | Structured | Semi-Structured | Unstructured |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Fixed, predefined | Flexible, self-describing | None |
| **Storage** | RDBMS tables | JSON/XML/NoSQL | Data lake (HDFS, S3) |
| **Example** | MySQL `student(id, name, cgpa)` | JSON file `{"id":1,"name":"Anu"}` | A lecture video recording |
| **Query** | SQL | XQuery, JSONPath, Spark SQL | NLP, ML pipelines |
| **Volume share** | 5–10% | 5–10% | 80–90% |

**One-line definitions (for KTU board's 3-mark answer):**
- **Structured:** Data that conforms to a predefined schema, organized in rows and columns (e.g., RDBMS table).
- **Semi-Structured:** Data that has organizational tags/metadata but no fixed schema (e.g., JSON, XML).
- **Unstructured:** Data with no inherent schema or format (e.g., text, images, video).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of "Schema-on-Read" vs "Schema-on-Write" in the context of Big Data file formats.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Schema-on-Write:** The schema is enforced **before** the data is stored. The data is validated, typed, and rejected if it does not conform. Used in **traditional RDBMS and data warehouses** (e.g., Hive external tables with strict DDL). High write-time cost, high read-time efficiency.

- **Schema-on-Read:** The schema is applied **at query time**. Raw data is stored as-is in a data lake (HDFS, S3) and the schema is inferred/parsed when read. Used in **Parquet/Avro with Spark, NoSQL, and data lake architectures**. Low write-time cost, flexible, supports heterogeneous data.

**KTU-Board-Expected One-Liner:** *"Schema-on-Write = strict RDBMS, validate before storing; Schema-on-Read = flexible data lake, interpret when reading."*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the three main categories of data in Big Data with suitable examples. Compare their storage mechanisms and query approaches. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three categories of data in Big Data are:

**1. Structured Data**
- **Definition:** Data that conforms to a fixed, predefined schema and is organized into rows and columns of a relational table.
- **Examples:** MySQL transaction tables, Oracle ERP records, Hive managed tables.
- **Storage:** RDBMS, Data Warehouses (Teradata, Snowflake).
- **Query:** SQL (SELECT, JOIN, GROUP BY).
- **Properties:** ACID-compliant, rigid, efficient for OLTP.
- **Volume share:** ~5–10% of enterprise data.

**2. Semi-Structured Data**
- **Definition:** Data that does not follow a rigid tabular schema but carries organizational metadata (tags, key-value pairs) enabling partial structure.
- **Examples:** JSON files, XML documents, MongoDB BSON, email `.eml` files.
- **Storage:** NoSQL databases, file systems, document stores.
- **Query:** XQuery, JSONPath, Spark SQL, MongoDB Query Language.
- **Properties:** BASE consistency, flexible, web-native.
- **Volume share:** ~5–10% of enterprise data.

**3. Unstructured Data**
- **Definition:** Data with no predefined schema or format; cannot be directly parsed by relational engines.
- **Examples:** Images, videos, audio, text logs, PDFs, CCTV footage.
- **Storage:** Data Lakes (HDFS, Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake).
- **Query:** NLP, ML/CV models, regex extraction, Spark UDFs.
- **Properties:** Schema-on-Read, requires preprocessing.
- **Volume share:** ~80–90% of enterprise data.

**Comparison Table (KTU Board Expectation):**

| Aspect | Structured | Semi-Structured | Unstructured |
|---|---|---|---|
| Schema | Fixed | Flexible | None |
| Storage | RDBMS | NoSQL/Files | Data Lake |
| Query | SQL | JSONPath/XQuery | NLP/ML |
| Consistency | ACID | BASE | Eventual |
| Share | 5–10% | 5–10% | 80–90% |

---

#### (b) Differentiate between row-oriented and column-oriented file storage. Explain Parquet and ORC file formats with their internal architecture. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Row-Oriented Storage (Avro, SequenceFile, CSV):**
- All columns of a single row are stored contiguously on disk.
- **Read pattern:** Must read the entire row to access even a single column.
- **Best for:** Write-heavy OLTP, streaming, full-row retrieval.
- **Drawback:** Wasteful for analytical queries that touch only a few columns.

**Column-Oriented Storage (Parquet, ORC):**
- All values of a single column are stored contiguously.
- **Read pattern:** Reads only the required columns → **column pruning**.
- **Best for:** Read-heavy OLAP, Spark SQL, data warehousing.
- **Advantage:** Higher compression (similar values stack together → RLE + dictionary encoding).

**Parquet Internal Architecture:**
- File is divided into **Row Groups** (horizontal partitions, default ~128 MB).
- Each Row Group contains **Column Chunks** (one per column).
- Each Column Chunk is divided into **Pages** (default 1 MB).
- **File Footer** stores:
  - Schema descriptor
  - Row group statistics (min/max per column)
  - Column chunk offsets
- **Predicate Pushdown:** Query engines skip entire row groups whose min/max statistics exclude the query predicate.
- **Compression:** Snappy, Gzip, LZO applied per page.

**ORC Internal Architecture:**
- File is divided into **Stripes** (default 250 MB) — analogous to Parquet row groups.
- Each stripe contains **Index Data**, **Row Data**, and **Stripe Footer**.
- **File-Level Footer** stores overall statistics.
- **Lightweight indexes** (min/max, bloom filters) per column.
- **ACID support** in Hive via Hive 3.x transaction manager.
- **Compression:** Zlib, Snappy; uses RLE + bit-packing + dictionary.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Parquet | ORC |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Twitter + Cloudera | Hortonworks |
| Partition | Row Group | Stripe |
| Indexes | Min/Max statistics | Min/Max + Bloom |
| ACID | Limited (via Delta Lake) | Native (Hive 3.x) |
| Best Engine | Spark | Hive |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) What is a file format in Big Data? Compare CSV, Avro, and Parquet with respect to storage layout, compression, splittability, and use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **file format** in Big Data is a **storage encoding convention** that determines how data is physically written to and read from distributed storage systems. The choice of format directly impacts I/O efficiency, storage cost, and schema evolution capability.

**Detailed Comparison:**

| Attribute | CSV | Avro | Parquet |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Text row-based | Binary row-based | Binary column-based |
| **Human-Readable** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Schema Support** | ❌ None | ✅ Embedded JSON | ✅ Embedded schema |
| **Storage Layout** | Row-by-row | Row-by-row (binary) | Column-by-column |
| **Compression** | None natively | Snappy, Deflate (3–6×) | Snappy, Gzip (8–15×) |
| **Splittability** | ✅ Yes (line-based) | ✅ Yes (sync markers) | ✅ Yes (row groups) |
| **Schema Evolution** | ❌ Poor | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good |
| **Read Pattern** | Full row | Full row | Columnar / Row |
| **Best For** | Small data exchange | Streaming / Kafka | Spark SQL / Analytics |
| **Engine Fit** | Any | Kafka, Spark, Flink | Spark, Hive, Impala |

**Use-Case Recommendations:**
- **CSV:** Small datasets, ETL interchange, human inspection.
- **Avro:** Kafka streaming pipelines, write-heavy workloads, long-term archival with schema evolution.
- **Parquet:** Spark/Hive analytics, OLAP queries, data lakehouse storage layer.

---

#### (b) Explain the Avro file format in detail. Describe how it supports schema evolution with a suitable example. Also explain the role of Schema Registry in Kafka. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Avro File Format:**
- Apache Avro is a **language-neutral, binary, row-oriented serialization format** developed within the Hadoop ecosystem.
- Each Avro file consists of:
  1. **Header:** Magic bytes `Obj\x01` + embedded **JSON schema** (`.avsc`).
  2. **Data Block(s):** Binary-serialized records compressed with codec (Snappy, Deflate, Bzip2).
  3. **Sync Markers:** 16-byte markers inserted between blocks for **splittability** — Hadoop can split a file at sync markers without breaking records.

**Schema Evolution in Avro:**
Avro supports four evolution operations:
1. **Add a field** with a default value.
2. **Remove a field** (default = removed).
3. **Reorder fields** (transparent to Avro).
4. **Promote a type** (e.g., `int` → `long`, `float` → `double`).

**Example — Adding a New Field:**

*Original schema (v1):*
```json
{"type":"record","name":"User",
 "fields":[
   {"name":"id","type":"int"},
   {"name":"name","type":"string"}]}
```

*Updated schema (v2):*
```json
{"type":"record","name":"User",
 "fields":[
   {"name":"id","type":"int"},
   {"name":"name","type":"string"},
   {"name":"email","type":["null","string"],"default":null}]}
```

- A **new reader** reading **v1 data**: applies the default `null` for `email` → **backward compatible**.
- An **old reader** reading **v2 data**: ignores the unknown `email` field → **forward compatible**.

**Schema Registry in Kafka:**
- The **Confluent Schema Registry** is a centralized service that stores and versions Avro (and Protobuf/JSON) schemas.
- Producers register schema versions before publishing; consumers fetch schema by ID from the registry.
- Prevents schema mismatch, enforces backward/forward compatibility, and avoids sending full schema with every message (only a 4-byte schema ID is transmitted, reducing payload size).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing row vs column storage:** Students often claim "Parquet stores rows." It does NOT — it stores **columns contiguously** in column chunks. This is the #1 mark-loss area.
> 2. **Forgetting splittability:** Many students recommend Gzip compression for distributed workloads, ignoring that Gzip is **NOT splittable** → 1 mapper bottleneck.
> 3. **Misidentifying JSON as columnar:** JSON is hierarchical and row-oriented in nature, not columnar.
> 4. **Schema-on-Read vs Schema-on-Write confusion:** The examiner specifically tests if you can state which is used for *data lakes* vs *data warehouses*.
> 5. **Forgetting Avro's sync markers:** Students forget to mention sync markers when explaining Avro splittability, losing 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Compression vs Format confusion:** Gzip/Snappy are *codecs*, not file formats. Parquet/Avro/ORC are *formats*. Students often blur this line.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- **Data Types (3 types):** Structured (RDBMS, SQL, ACID, 5–10%), Semi-Structured (JSON/XML, NoSQL, BASE, 5–10%), Unstructured (Data Lake, HDFS, 80–90%).
- **Schema-on-Write** = RDBMS, data warehouses; **Schema-on-Read** = data lakes, Hadoop, Spark.
- **Volume Distribution:** Unstructured dominates (~80–90%) — primary driver of Big Data tools.
- **File Format Categories:** Text-based (CSV, JSON, XML), Binary Row (Avro, SequenceFile), Binary Column (Parquet, ORC).
- **Row-Oriented** = best for **write-heavy, OLTP, streaming, full-row reads** (Avro, SequenceFile, CSV).
- **Column-Oriented** = best for **read-heavy, OLAP, column-projection queries** (Parquet, ORC).
- **Columnar I/O Advantage:** Reading $k$ out of $n$ columns reads only $k/n$ fraction of data.
- **Parquet Anatomy:** Row Groups → Column Chunks → Pages + File Footer (schema + min/max stats).
- **ORC Anatomy:** Stripes → Index Data + Row Data + Stripe Footer + File Footer; supports ACID.
- **Avro Anatomy:** Header (magic + JSON schema) + Data Blocks + Sync Markers (for splittability).
- **Splittability Rule:** Splittable formats = CSV (line-based), Avro (sync markers), Parquet (row groups), ORC (stripes), Bzip2 (block markers). **NOT Splittable** = Gzip, Pretty JSON, multi-line XML.
- **Schema Evolution Champion:** **Avro** — supports both backward and forward compatibility via union types with defaults.
- **Compression Codecs:** Gzip (high ratio, NOT splittable), Snappy (fast, splittable), Bzip2 (high ratio, splittable), LZO (fast, splittable).
- **Best Format by Use-Case:** Kafka streaming → Avro; Spark SQL analytics → Parquet; Hive warehouse → ORC; API ingestion → JSON; MapReduce intermediate → SequenceFile; Data exchange → CSV.
- **KTU Mantra:** *"Choose row format for writes, column format for reads. Compression codec choice affects parallelism. Schema evolution is non-negotiable for production."*
- **Common Confusions to Avoid:** Compression ≠ Format; Splittability ≠ Compression; Columnar ≠ Compressed; JSON is NOT columnar.
- **Industry Context (Kerala):** KTU graduates working in Technopark/Infopark data engineering roles will encounter Parquet + Avro in ~70% of pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primitive and Collection Data Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Primitive and Collection Data Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Big Data Processing frameworks** (Apache Spark, Hive, Pig, etc.), a **Data Type** defines the classification of a value and the operations that can be performed on it. KTU 2024 scheme categorizes them into two principal families:

- **Primitive Data Types (Scalar / Atomic Types):** Simple, immutable, single-valued types that cannot be decomposed further. Examples: `Integer`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `String`, `Boolean`, `Date`, `Timestamp`, `Binary`.
- **Collection Data Types (Complex / Composite Types):** Container types that aggregate multiple values, possibly of heterogeneous kinds. Examples: `Array`, `Map`, `Struct` (and nested combinations of these).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Every column in a DataFrame or field in a SerDe (Serializer/Deserializer) record in Hive **must** be assigned exactly one data type. The choice directly impacts storage footprint, serialization cost, and query optimization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are packing for a trip:

- A **Primitive data type** is like a **single sealed box** — it holds exactly one item (one number, one letter, one yes/no answer). You cannot break the box into smaller logical parts without losing meaning. A `Long` is a sealed box that can hold an integer up to **9.2 quintillion**.
- A **Collection data type** is like a **suitcase or organizer tray** — it holds *many* items organized in a specific way:
  - **Array** → a row of identical items in a shelf (e.g., a list of phone numbers).
  - **Map** → a key-value label system (e.g., Country → Capital).
  - **Struct** → a multi-compartment toolbox where each compartment has its own label and its own type (e.g., a person record: `name: String, age: Integer`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Big Data:** When you process **terabytes** of data, even a 2-byte saving per field (e.g., choosing `Short` over `Int`) translates to **gigabytes** of I/O and network savings. The KTU paper frequently tests this cost-aware design thinking.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Property | Standard Value (Big Data Context) |
|---|---|
| Default numeric precision | 64-bit Double (IEEE 754) |
| Default integer type | 32-bit Integer |
| Default string encoding | UTF-8 |
| Boolean representation | 1 byte (true/false) |
| Timestamp epoch | Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primitive Data Types — The Atomic Building Blocks

Primitive types are **immutable, atomic, and non-divisible**. They form the leaves of any data type tree.

### 2.1.1 Logical Hierarchy of Primitive Types

The classification breaks down into **Numeric** and **Non-Numeric** families:

- **Numeric Family**
  - **Integral sub-family:** `ByteType`, `ShortType`, `IntegerType`, `LongType`
  - **Floating-point sub-family:** `FloatType`, `DoubleType`, `DecimalType`
- **Non-Numeric Family:** `StringType`, `BinaryType`, `BooleanType`, `DateType`, `TimestampType`, `NullType`

### 2.1.2 Why Each Primitive Exists (The "Why")

- **Why `Byte`?** Sensor data (IoT, network packets) and categorical encoding (0–255) consume massive volumes → 1-byte storage is crucial.
- **Why `Short`?** Legacy systems, audio samples (16-bit PCM), and small counters where `Int` wastes memory.
- **Why `Long`?** Big Data IDs (transaction IDs, timestamps in milliseconds) overflow 32-bit Integer.
- **Why `Decimal`?** Financial computations where binary floating-point (`Float`/`Double`) introduces rounding errors (e.g., $0.1 + 0.2 \neq 0.3$ in IEEE 754).
- **Why `String`?** Free-form text — logs, names, descriptions.
- **Why `Binary`?** Storing images, serialized objects, encrypted blobs.
- **Why `Date`/`Timestamp`?** Temporal analytics (event-time processing, windowing).

## 2.2 Collection Data Types — The Composite Containers

Collection types can recursively contain **other collection types**, enabling arbitrarily complex schemas (e.g., an `Array` of `Map` of `Struct`).

### 2.2.1 Array Type

- **Definition:** An ordered sequence of elements, all of the **same type**, indexed positionally (0-based).
- **Example:** `[1, 2, 3, 4]` — an `Array[Int]`.
- **Why used:** Time-series vectors, batched inputs to ML models, comma-separated tags.
- **Key property:** Homogeneous (single element type), ordered, allows duplicates.

### 2.2.2 Map Type

- **Definition:** An unordered collection of **key-value pairs** where every key has a single associated value.
- **Example:** `{"name": "Alice", "age": 30}` — a `Map[String, Any]`.
- **Why used:** JSON-like attributes, lookup tables, configuration parameters.
- **Key property:** Heterogeneous values allowed, unique keys, retrieval by key (not index).

### 2.2.3 Struct Type

- **Definition:** A **record** with a fixed set of named fields, each with its **own type** (the equivalent of a row/tuple).
- **Example:** `Struct{name: String, age: Integer, address: Struct{...}}` — nested struct.
- **Why used:** Tabular rows, JSON objects, Avro/Parquet schema definition.
- **Key property:** Field-name access (`row.name`), heterogeneous fields, fixed schema.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. KTU 14-mark questions frequently require computing **storage size** of a row or **type-casting** logic.

| Data Type | Storage (Bytes) | Range / Format | Boundary Condition (Min) | Boundary Condition (Max) | Default Literal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ByteType` | 1 | $-2^7$ to $2^7 - 1$ | **-128** | **127** | `127.cast(Byte)` |
| `ShortType` | 2 | $-2^{15}$ to $2^{15}-1$ | **-32,768** | **32,767** | `0.cast(Short)` |
| `IntegerType` | 4 | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | **-2,147,483,648** | **2,147,483,647** | `0` |
| `LongType` | 8 | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ | **$-9.22 \times 10^{18}$** | **$9.22 \times 10^{18}$** | `0L` |
| `FloatType` | 4 | IEEE 754 single | $\approx -3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | `0.0f` |
| `DoubleType` | 8 | IEEE 754 double | $\approx -1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | $\approx 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | `0.0` |
| `DecimalType(p,s)` | Variable | Exact precision | $-(10^{p-s}-1)/10^{s}$ | $(10^{p-s}-1)/10^{s}$ | `0.00` |
| `StringType` | Variable | UTF-8 | Empty `""` | $2^{31}-1$ chars (typical) | `"x"` |
| `BinaryType` | Variable | Byte array | Empty `[]` | $2^{31}-1$ bytes | `byte[]` |
| `BooleanType` | 1 | True/False | `false` | `true` | `false` |
| `DateType` | 4 | Days since epoch | **0001-01-01** | **9999-12-31** | `date "2024-01-01"` |
| `TimestampType` | 8 | Microseconds since epoch | **0001-01-01 00:00:00Z** | **9999-12-31 23:59:59Z** | `timestamp "..."` |
| `ArrayType` | $n \times \text{size}(\text{elemType})$ | Indexed list | `[]` | Size limited by memory | `[v1, v2, ...]` |
| `MapType` | $n \times (\text{keySize} + \text{valSize})$ | K→V mapping | `{}` | Size limited by memory | `{}` |
| `StructType` | $\sum \text{size}(\text{field}_i)$ | Named record | N/A | N/A | `struct(a,b,c)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In Parquet/ORC columnar storage, choosing `Long` over `Integer` doubles the on-disk size of that column. For a **1 PB dataset**, that is **500 TB of avoidable I/O** — a direct cost penalty in cloud billing (AWS S3, Azure Data Lake).

## 2.4 Type Coercion and Casting Rules

- **Widening Conversion (Implicit, Safe):** `Byte` → `Short` → `Integer` → `Long` → `Float` → `Double`.
- **Narrowing Conversion (Explicit, Lossy):** `Double` → `Integer` (truncates decimal, may overflow).
- **Type Promotion in Expressions:** `Integer + Long = Long`; `Integer + Double = Double` (smallest type that preserves both values).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Row-Size Calculation (KTU High-Yield Skill)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Given a schema, compute the storage size of a single record."* This is the most awarded sub-part in 14-mark questions.

### Worked Example

**Problem:** A Hive table has the schema:
`(id BIGINT, name STRING(20), tags ARRAY<STRING>, metadata MAP<STRING,STRING>, is_active BOOLEAN)`
Compute the **approximate row size in bytes** for a row containing 5 tags and 3 map entries.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}_{\text{row}} &= \text{Size}(id) + \text{Size}(name) + \text{Size}(tags) + \text{Size}(metadata) + \text{Size}(is\_active)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Size of `id` (LongType):**

$$
\text{Size}(id) = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Size of `name` (String of 20 chars):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}(name) &= (\text{length} \times 1) + \text{overhead} \\
&= (20 \times 1) + 4 \\
&= 24 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each UTF-8 character = 1 byte (ASCII assumption), plus a 4-byte length prefix.

**Step 3 — Size of `tags` (Array of 5 strings, assume avg 10 chars each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}(tags) &= \text{Size}(\text{array}) + 5 \times \text{Size}(\text{string}) \\
&= 4 + 5 \times (10 + 4) \\
&= 4 + 70 \\
&= 74 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Array overhead stores the count (4 bytes), then each element is stored sequentially.

**Step 4 — Size of `metadata` (Map with 3 entries, key=10 chars, value=20 chars):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}(metadata) &= 4 + 3 \times \bigl(\text{Size}(key) + \text{Size}(value)\bigr) \\
&= 4 + 3 \times \bigl((10 + 4) + (20 + 4)\bigr) \\
&= 4 + 3 \times 38 \\
&= 4 + 114 \\
&= 118 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Map overhead (4 bytes for count) + each entry = key bytes + value bytes.

**Step 5 — Size of `is_active` (BooleanType):**

$$
\text{Size}(is\_active) = 1 \text{ byte}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Row Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}_{\text{row}} &= 8 + 24 + 74 + 118 + 1 \\
&= 225 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Scale to Big Data Volume (1 Billion rows):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Size} &= 225 \text{ bytes} \times 10^9 \\
&= 2.25 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 225 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how a few `String` and `Map` fields dominate the row size. This is why KTU problems emphasize choosing tighter types (e.g., `SMALLINT` instead of `STRING` for IDs).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Operation Compatibility

| Operation | `Int` + `Int` | `Int` + `Long` | `Int` + `Double` | `Int` + `String` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Result Type | `Int` | `Long` | `Double` | **Error** |
| Precision Loss? | No | No | **Yes** (if value > $2^{53}$) | N/A |
| Implicit/Explicit | Implicit | Implicit | Implicit | Explicit `cast()` only |
| Example | `3 + 4 = 7` | `3 + 4L = 7L` | `3 + 4.5 = 7.5` | `cast(3 as string) + "4"` |

## 3.3 Derivation: Type Narrowing Overflow Check

**Problem:** Cast `Long` value $9,000,000,000$ to `Int`. Will it overflow?

**Step 1 — Identify Int range:**

$$
\text{Range}_{Int} = [-2^{31},\ 2^{31} - 1] = [-2,147,483,648,\ 2,147,483,647]
$$

**Step 2 — Compare:**

$$
9,000,000,000 > 2,147,483,647
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

$$
\text{Overflow occurs. Result = } 9,000,000,000 - 2^{32} = 9,000,000,000 - 4,294,967,296 = 4,705,032,704
$$

> **Logic:** Narrowing wraps around modulo $2^{32}$ in two's complement. This is a **classic KTU trap**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid: Data Type Hierarchy (Big Data / Spark Perspective)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DataType Root] --> B[Primitive / Scalar]
    A --> C[Collection / Complex]

    B --> D[Numeric]
    B --> E[NonNumeric]

    D --> F[Integral]
    D --> G[FloatingPoint]
    F --> F1[ByteType]
    F --> F2[ShortType]
    F --> F3[IntegerType]
    F --> F4[LongType]
    G --> G1[FloatType]
    G --> G2[DoubleType]
    G --> G3[DecimalType]

    E --> E1[StringType]
    E --> E2[BinaryType]
    E --> E3[BooleanType]
    E --> E4[DateType]
    E --> E5[TimestampType]
    E --> E6[NullType]

    C --> H[ArrayType]
    C --> I[MapType]
    C --> J[StructType]

    H --> H1[Contains one element type]
    H1 --> H2[Ordered and indexable]

    I --> I1[Key Value pairs]
    I1 --> I2[Key must be primitive]
    I2 --> I3[Value can be any type]

    J --> J1[Fixed named fields]
    J1 --> J2[Each field has its own type]
    J2 --> J3[Nesting allowed]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Type Resolution in Spark

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Schema Definition] --> B[Parser Tokenization]
    B --> C[Logical Type Resolver]
    C --> D{Type Category Check}

    D -->|Primitive| E[Map to Spark Atomic Type]
    D -->|Collection| F[Recursive Field Validation]
    D -->|Unknown| G[Throw TypeError]

    E --> H[Generate Catalyst DataType]
    F --> H
    G --> Z[Error Log and Reject]

    H --> I[Tungsten Encoder]
    I --> J[Internal Row Representation]
    J --> K[JVM OffHeap Binary Format]
    K --> L[Optimized Execution]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style H fill:#fff4e1
    style L fill:#e8f5e9
    style Z fill:#ffebee
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: Casting and Coercion Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Type ST] --> B{Target Type TT}
    B -->|Same type| C[NoOp Cast]
    B -->|Widening| D[Implicit Safe Cast]
    B -->|Narrowing| E{Check Range}
    B -->|Incompatible| F[Explicit Cast Function]

    E -->|In range| G[Truncate to TT]
    E -->|Out of range| H[Null or Overflow]
    F --> I[Format Conversion]
    D --> J[Promote to TT]
    C --> J
    G --> J
    H --> K[Log Warning]
    I --> J

    style D fill:#c8e6c9
    style E fill:#fff9c4
    style F fill:#ffccbc
    style H fill:#ffcdd2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Differentiate between primitive and collection (complex) data types in Apache Spark. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Primitive data types are atomic, immutable, single-valued types that cannot be decomposed further. They form the leaves of any schema. Examples: `IntegerType`, `StringType`, `BooleanType`.

Collection (complex) data types are composite container types that aggregate multiple values, possibly of heterogeneous kinds. They can be nested recursively. Examples: `ArrayType`, `MapType`, `StructType`.

**Key Distinctions:**
- **Decomposability:** Primitive = indivisible; Collection = decomposable into sub-fields.
- **Homogeneity:** Primitive holds one value of fixed type; `ArrayType` holds multiple values of *one* type; `StructType` holds *heterogeneous* typed fields.
- **Access pattern:** Primitive accessed directly; `Array` by index, `Map` by key, `Struct` by field name.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Why is `DecimalType` preferred over `DoubleType` for financial computations in Big Data systems?**

**Model Answer:**

`DoubleType` follows the IEEE 754 floating-point standard, which represents numbers in binary. Values like $0.1$ and $0.2$ cannot be represented exactly in binary, leading to rounding errors such as $0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004$. In financial systems, even a fraction-of-a-penny error compounds across millions of transactions.

`DecimalType(p, s)` stores values as exact base-10 integers with a fixed precision `p` (total digits) and scale `s` (digits after the decimal point). This guarantees **exact arithmetic** with no representation error, making it the **KTU-recommended** choice for monetary fields like `account_balance`, `tax_amount`, or `transaction_value`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule (Mandatory):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the following data types with one use-case each: (i) `BinaryType` (ii) `DateType` (iii) `TimestampType` (iv) `ArrayType` (v) `MapType` (vi) `StructType` (vii) `NullType`.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) `BinaryType`**
- **Definition:** Stores a sequence of raw bytes.
- **Storage:** Variable (similar to string, but for bytes, not characters).
- **Use case:** Storing images, encrypted passwords, serialized Avro/Protobuf objects, or compressed data blobs in a data lake.

**(ii) `DateType`**
- **Definition:** Represents a calendar date without time-of-day.
- **Storage:** 4 bytes (integer days since Unix epoch 1970-01-01).
- **Use case:** Birth dates, event dates, business-day calculations, partition columns in Hive (`dt='2024-05-15'`).

**(iii) `TimestampType`**
- **Definition:** Represents an instant in time with microsecond precision.
- **Storage:** 8 bytes (microseconds since epoch).
- **Use case:** Event-time processing in streaming pipelines, log ingestion timestamps, SCD (Slowly Changing Dimensions) tracking.

**(iv) `ArrayType`**
- **Definition:** Ordered collection of elements, all of the same type.
- **Use case:** Vector of features for ML models (`[0.1, 0.5, 0.9]`), tags list (`["hadoop", "spark", "hive"]`).

**(v) `MapType`**
- **Definition:** Unordered collection of key-value pairs.
- **Use case:** Storing JSON attributes (`{"country": "IN", "city": "Kochi"}`), application configuration parameters, lookup tables.

**(vi) `StructType`**
- **Definition:** A record with fixed, named, typed fields.
- **Use case:** A single row of a relational table — a customer record with `(id: Long, name: String, email: String)`.

**(vii) `NullType`**
- **Definition:** Represents the absence of a value or unknown data.
- **Use case:** Optional fields, missing data indicators, results of failed computations, sentinel values in schema evolution.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Consider the following schema for a Big Data warehouse:
`(order_id BIGINT, customer STRUCT<id: BIGINT, name: STRING, email: STRING>, items ARRAY<STRUCT<sku: STRING, qty: INT, price: DECIMAL(10,2)>>, order_date DATE, status STRING, metadata MAP<STRING, STRING>)`
If a typical order has **3 items** in the array and **2 metadata entries** (with keys averaging 15 characters and values averaging 50 characters), compute the **total storage size of one row in bytes**. State your assumptions clearly.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
- String overhead = 4 bytes (length prefix).
- Array/Map overhead = 4 bytes (element count).
- All strings are pure ASCII (1 byte per char).
- DecimalType(10,2) = 16 bytes (Spark internal representation).
- BooleanType = 1 byte (not used here).

**Step 1 — `order_id` (BIGINT):**
$$
\text{Size} = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — `customer` (STRUCT, assume name=20 chars, email=30 chars):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size} &= 8 + (20+4) + (30+4) \\
&= 8 + 24 + 34 \\
&= 66 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — `items` (ARRAY of 3 STRUCT, sku=12 chars avg):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size} &= 4_{\text{array}} + 3 \times \bigl((12+4) + 4 + 16\bigr) \\
&= 4 + 3 \times 36 \\
&= 4 + 108 \\
&= 112 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — `order_date` (DATE):**
$$
\text{Size} = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — `status` (STRING, 10 chars):**
$$
\text{Size} = 10 + 4 = 14 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6 — `metadata` (MAP of 2 entries, key=15 chars, value=50 chars):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size} &= 4_{\text{map}} + 2 \times \bigl((15+4) + (50+4)\bigr) \\
&= 4 + 2 \times 73 \\
&= 4 + 146 \\
&= 150 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Total Row Size:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 8 + 66 + 112 + 4 + 14 + 150 \\
&= 354 \text{ bytes per row}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Scaling to 1 Billion orders:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Storage} &= 354 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 354 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The schema is dominated by `metadata` and `items`. KTU examiners award marks for identifying the **dominant cost field** and suggesting optimization (e.g., flattening the array into a separate fact table).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the type coercion and casting rules in Spark SQL with examples. What happens when you add an `IntegerType` to a `LongType`? What about `IntegerType + StringType`?

**Model Solution:**

**Type Coercion** is the implicit conversion of a value from one data type to another by the engine. **Casting** is the explicit conversion invoked by the user (e.g., `cast(x as type)`).

**Rules of Coercion (Spark SQL):**

1. **Widening (Safe, Implicit):** When two types of different precision are used in the same expression, Spark promotes to the *least restrictive* type that holds both values.
   - `Integer + Long` → result is `Long` (Long can hold every Integer).
   - `Integer + Double` → result is `Double`.
   - `Float + Double` → result is `Double`.

2. **Narrowing (Unsafe, Explicit):** Converting a wider type to a narrower one (e.g., `Long → Int`) requires an explicit `cast()` and may cause **truncation or overflow**.

3. **Incompatible Types:** Types from different families (numeric vs string) **cannot** be coerced implicitly.

**Answering the Specific Cases:**

**Case 1: `IntegerType + LongType`**

$$
\text{Int}(3) + \text{Long}(5L) \rightarrow \text{Long}(8L)
$$

The Integer is promoted to Long (widening). No data loss. This is **implicit and safe**.

**Case 2: `IntegerType + StringType`**

$$
\text{Int}(3) + \text{String}("5") \rightarrow \text{AnalysisException}
$$

Numeric and string types are **fundamentally incompatible**. The user must explicitly cast, e.g., `cast(3 as string) + "5"` → result is `String("35")` (concatenation, not addition).

**Cast Examples:**
- `cast(3.14 as int)` → `3` (truncation).
- `cast("2024-01-01" as date)` → `Date(2024,1,1)`.
- `cast(null as int)` → `null` (NullType is compatible with all types).

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare `StructType`, `ArrayType`, and `MapType` in detail. Construct a real-world JSON example that exercises all three (with nesting) and explain how Spark would parse it.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | `StructType` | `ArrayType` | `MapType` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Fixed named fields | Ordered list | Key-value pairs |
| **Homogeneity** | Heterogeneous fields | **Homogeneous** elements | Heterogeneous values, homogeneous keys |
| **Access** | By field name | By index (0-based) | By key |
| **Nesting** | Fields can be Array/Map/Struct | Elements can be Struct/Map | Values can be Struct/Array/Map |
| **Use case** | Tabular rows | Lists, vectors | JSON attributes, configs |

**Real-World JSON Example (an IoT sensor reading):**

```json
{
  "device_id": "sensor_42",
  "location": {
    "lat": 9.9312,
    "lon": 76.2673
  },
  "readings": [22.5, 23.1, 22.8, 23.4],
  "metadata": {
    "firmware": "v1.2.3",
    "battery": "87%"
  }
}
```

**Corresponding Spark Schema (StructType definition):**

- **Root:** `StructType` containing 4 fields.
- `device_id`: `StringType` (Primitive).
- `location`: `StructType` with two `DoubleType` fields (Nested Struct).
- `readings`: `ArrayType[DoubleType]` (Array of primitive).
- `metadata`: `MapType[StringType, StringType]` (Map of primitives).

**Parsing Flow:**

1. Spark's JSON reader infers the schema automatically (sampling the data) OR the user provides a predefined `StructType` schema for **schema-on-read** control.
2. The root object becomes a `Row` (instance of `StructType`).
3. The `location` object becomes a nested `Row` accessed via `row.location.lat`.
4. The `readings` array becomes a Scala `Seq[Double]`, accessed via `row.readings(0)`.
5. The `metadata` map becomes a `Map[String, String]`, accessed via `row.metadata("firmware")`.

**Why This Matters:** This single JSON object exercises **all three collection types plus nesting**, which is the exact pattern tested in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Array and Map:** Students frequently write that `Array` stores key-value pairs. **No** — `Array` is positional. Use `Map` for key-value access.
> 2. **Forgetting String Overhead:** When computing storage, students often write `Size(name) = length`, forgetting the **4-byte length prefix**. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Misidentifying Date Range:** Writing `Date` range as epoch-based (Unix) instead of `0001-01-01 to 9999-12-31` loses marks. The range is **logical**, not epoch-based.
> 4. **DecimalType vs DoubleType:** Saying "both are floats" is a fatal error. `Decimal` is **exact base-10**, `Double` is **approximate binary**. KTU questions on financial data test precisely this distinction.
> 5. **NullType Confusion:** Students confuse `NullType` with the SQL `NULL` value. `NullType` is a **data type** (the absence of a type), while `null` is a **value instance**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Primitive types** are atomic: `Byte`, `Short`, `Int`, `Long`, `Float`, `Double`, `Decimal`, `String`, `Binary`, `Boolean`, `Date`, `Timestamp`, `Null`.
- [x] **Collection types** are composite: `Array` (homogeneous, ordered), `Map` (K→V, unique keys), `Struct` (heterogeneous named fields).
- [x] **Storage sizes** in bytes: Byte=1, Short=2, Int=4, Long=8, Float=4, Double=8, Date=4, Timestamp=8, Boolean=1.
- [x] **String overhead** = 4 bytes (length prefix) + N bytes (UTF-8 content).
- [x] **Widening conversion** is implicit and safe (Int → Long → Double).
- [x] **Narrowing conversion** requires explicit `cast()` and may cause overflow/truncation.
- [x] **`DecimalType(p,s)`** is **exact** base-10 → use for money.
- [x] **`DoubleType`** is **approximate** IEEE 754 binary → avoid for money.
- [x] **`Integer + Long = Long`**, `Integer + Double = Double`, `Integer + String = Error`.
- [x] **Date range** is `0001-01-01` to `9999-12-31` (logical), stored as days since epoch.
- [x] **Struct vs Row:** `StructType` is the *schema definition*; `Row` is the *data instance*.
- [x] **Nested collections** are allowed: `Array<Struct<...>>`, `Map<String, Array<Int>>`, etc.
- [x] **Int max = $2^{31} - 1$** = 2,147,483,647. Casting a larger `Long` to `Int` causes silent overflow.
- [x] **KTU 14-mark questions** almost always ask for: (1) explanation of 3–4 types, (2) schema storage calculation, OR (3) parsing a nested JSON example.
- [x] **Default types in Spark/Hive:** `Integer` for ints, `Double` for floats, `String` for text, `Boolean` for flags.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HiveQL - Creating Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HiveQL - Creating Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HiveQL (Hive Query Language)** is a SQL-like declarative query language used in Apache Hive to perform data definition, data manipulation, and query operations on large datasets residing in distributed storage (HDFS) or compatible systems like S3, HBase, or Azure Blob Storage. It abstracts the complexity of writing low-level MapReduce, Tez, or Spark jobs by translating high-level SQL statements into distributed execution plans.

**Creating Tables** in Hive is the foundational Data Definition Language (DDL) operation that defines the **schema, storage format, location, and physical structure (partitioning/bucketing)** of a dataset before any data can be loaded, queried, or analyzed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under *Module 4: HiveQL* and is directly assessed through the Course Outcome **CO4** – *"Apply HiveQL operations for structured and semi-structured data analysis on Hadoop ecosystems."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of **creating a table in Hive** like **designing a warehouse rack system before stocking it**.

- The **column names** are like the labels on each shelf.
- The **data types** are like the size and shape of boxes you will place (e.g., small, medium, large — INT, STRING, BIGINT).
- The **storage format** (ORC, Parquet, TextFile) is the *material* of the rack — wood (cheap, slow) vs. steel (durable, fast).
- **Partitioning** is like dividing the warehouse into zones (by date, region) so you don't scan the entire building to find a parcel.
- **Bucketing** is like subdividing each zone into numbered bins for fast lookup.

> Just as a warehouse without properly designed racks is chaotic, data in HDFS without a well-defined Hive schema becomes a swamp of unstructured files.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Defaults

| Hive Property | Default Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `hive.metastore.warehouse.dir` | `/user/hive/warehouse` | Default HDFS location for managed tables |
| Storage Format | **TextFile** | Plain text, line-delimited; slowest |
| Row Format | **Delimited** | Default field separator is Ctrl-A (`\001`) |
| Field Delimiter | `\001` (Ctrl-A) | Rare character, unlikely to appear in data |
| Collection Delimiters | `\002`, `\003` | Between map keys/values, array/struct items |
| Null Definition | `\N` | Default null marker in text files |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Ctrl-A (`\001`)?** Hive's default delimiters use control characters (non-printable ASCII) precisely because they almost never occur naturally in real datasets, preventing accidental column-splitting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why HiveQL Exists — The "Why"

Before Hive, every data operation on Hadoop required writing verbose **MapReduce programs** in Java. For a SQL developer, this was a productivity nightmare. HiveQL solves this by:

1. **Translating** SQL-like statements into MapReduce/Tez/Spark jobs.
2. **Abstracting** the complexity of distributed computing.
3. **Leveraging** existing SQL knowledge — ~90% of HiveQL syntax mirrors ANSI SQL.

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Hive CREATE TABLE Statement

A complete `CREATE TABLE` statement is a multi-clause contract between the user and the Hive Metastore. Each clause has a specific engineering purpose.

```
CREATE [EXTERNAL] TABLE [IF NOT EXISTS] [db_name.]table_name
(
    column1 column1_datatype [COMMENT '...'],
    column2 column2_datatype,
    ...
    PARTITIONED BY (partition_col datatype)
    CLUSTERED BY (bucket_col) [SORTED BY (sort_col)] INTO N BUCKETS
)
[ROW FORMAT row_format]
[STORED AS file_format]
[LOCATION 'hdfs_path']
[TBLPROPERTIES (...)]
```

### 2.3 Clause-by-Clause Deep Dive

**① EXTERNAL TABLE vs MANAGED TABLE**

| Feature | **MANAGED** (Internal) | **EXTERNAL** |
|---|---|---|
| Default | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (must specify `EXTERNAL`) |
| Data location | `/user/hive/warehouse/db/table` | User-specified `LOCATION` |
| `DROP TABLE` behavior | **Deletes data + metadata** | **Deletes metadata ONLY** |
| Use case | Temporary/transient ETL outputs | Production data shared with other tools (Spark, Pig) |
| Ownership | Hive owns the data lifecycle | User owns the data; Hive just reads it |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** If `DROP TABLE` is executed on a *managed* table, **HDFS files are physically deleted**. On an *external* table, only the metastore entry is removed — the underlying data remains intact. This is a frequent 3-mark exam question.

**② ROW FORMAT — The "How to Read" Specification**

- **DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','** — Defines column separator.
- **LINES TERMINATED BY '\n'** — Defines row separator.
- **COLLECTION ITEMS TERMINATED BY ','** — For arrays/structs.
- **MAP KEYS TERMINATED BY ':'** — For map key-value pairs.
- **SERDE 'org.apache.hadoop.hive.serde2.RegexSerDe'** — For regex-based parsing (e.g., logs).
- **SERDE 'org.openx.data.jsonserde.JsonSerDe'** — For JSON files.

**③ STORED AS — The "How to Store" Specification**

| Format | Type | Compression | Query Speed | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TextFile** | Row-based | Low | Slowest | Raw ingestion, debugging |
| **SequenceFile** | Row-based (binary) | Moderate | Slow | Hadoop-native binary |
| **RCFile** | Columnar (Record Columnar) | Good | Fast | Legacy Hive |
| **ORC** | Columnar + Indexes | **Excellent** | **Fastest** | **Production Hive tables** |
| **Parquet** | Columnar | Excellent | Very Fast | Cross-platform (Spark, Impala) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Most past-year exam answers on storage formats expect students to know that **ORC (Optimized Row Columnar)** is Hive's *native* columnar format and provides built-in indexes, predicate pushdown, and run-length encoding.

**④ Partitioning vs Bucketing**

- **Partitioning** divides a table into **separate physical directories** based on partition column values (e.g., `year=2024/month=01/`). It uses directory pruning to skip irrelevant data.
- **Bucketing** divides data within a partition into a **fixed number of equal-sized files** (buckets) using a hash function on the bucket column. It enables efficient **SAMPLING** and **map-side JOINs**.

**⑤ TBLPROPERTIES — The "Metadata Tags"**

Used to store arbitrary key-value metadata, including the table creator, retention info, statistics, and SerDe-specific configuration. Common properties:
- `'creator' = 'admin'`
- `'last_modified_time' = '...'`
- `'orc.compress' = 'SNAPPY'`
- `'transactional' = 'true'` (for ACID tables)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Pattern | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| Managed Table | `CREATE TABLE t (c INT);` | High (3 marks) |
| External Table | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE t (...) LOCATION '/path';` | **High (7 marks)** |
| Partitioned Table | `PARTITIONED BY (year INT)` in `CREATE` | Very High (7 marks) |
| Bucketed Table | `CLUSTERED BY (id) INTO 4 BUCKETS` | High (7 marks) |
| ORC Storage | `STORED AS ORC` | Medium (3 marks) |
| SerDe | `ROW FORMAT SERDE '...' STORED AS TEXTFILE` | Medium (3 marks) |
| Default Location | `/user/hive/warehouse/<db>.db/<table>/` | Low (1 mark) |
| Null Marker | `\N` (TextFile) | Low (1 mark) |
| CTAS | `CREATE TABLE t AS SELECT ...` | High (7 marks) |
| SHOW Command | `SHOW TABLES; SHOW PARTITIONS t;` | Low (1 mark) |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In production Hadoop clusters, well-partitioned and ORC-stored Hive tables routinely achieve **10x–100x faster query performance** over plain TextFile tables, while reducing HDFS storage by **~75%** through Snappy or Zlib compression embedded in the columnar format.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Syntax Construction — Managed (Internal) Table

**Step 1:** Start with the base keyword.

$$
\text{Step 1: } \texttt{CREATE TABLE}
$$

This initiates the DDL operation and tells the Hive Driver to register a new table in the Metastore.

**Step 2:** Add an optional database qualifier and naming clause.

$$
\text{Step 2: } \texttt{CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sales\_db.orders}
$$

- `IF NOT EXISTS` is an **idempotency guard** — if the table already exists, Hive skips creation and returns success (no error). This is critical in ETL pipelines that re-run.
- `sales_db.orders` uses the fully qualified `<database>.<table>` form. If omitted, Hive uses the **current working database** (set by `USE sales_db;`).

**Step 3:** Define the column schema inside parentheses.

$$
\text{Step 3: } \texttt{(order\_id INT, customer\_name STRING, amount DOUBLE, order\_date TIMESTAMP)}
$$

Each entry follows the strict pattern: `column_name data_type [COMMENT '...']`. The `COMMENT` is stored in the metastore for documentation.

**Step 4:** (Optional) Add row format specification.

$$
\text{Step 4: } \texttt{ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','}
$$

This instructs the **SerDe (Serializer/Deserializer)** to read each line of the raw file and split it on the comma character. Default is `\001` (Ctrl-A).

**Step 5:** (Optional) Add storage format.

$$
\text{Step 5: } \texttt{STORED AS TEXTFILE}
$$

This is the **physical file format** on HDFS. TextFile means plain text with `\n` line separators.

**Step 6:** (Optional) Specify location.

$$
\text{Step 6: } \texttt{LOCATION '/data/raw/orders/'}
$$

If omitted for a managed table, Hive uses `/user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db/orders/`. For an external table, `LOCATION` is **mandatory**.

**Final Compiled Statement:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sales_db.orders (
    order_id     INT        COMMENT 'Unique order identifier',
    customer_name STRING    COMMENT 'Customer full name',
    amount       DOUBLE     COMMENT 'Transaction amount in INR',
    order_date   TIMESTAMP  COMMENT 'UTC timestamp of order'
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;
```

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — All Table Creation Variants

| Variant | Statement Pattern | Distinguishing Feature | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Managed (Basic)** | `CREATE TABLE t (c INT) STORED AS TEXTFILE;` | No EXTERNAL keyword, no LOCATION | Temp/scratch tables |
| **External** | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE t (c INT) ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ',' LOCATION '/p';` | Has `EXTERNAL` and `LOCATION` | Shared data, persistent files |
| **Partitioned** | `CREATE TABLE t (id INT) PARTITIONED BY (dt STRING);` | `PARTITIONED BY` clause | Time-series, date-based queries |
| **Bucketed** | `CREATE TABLE t (id INT) CLUSTERED BY (id) INTO 4 BUCKETS;` | `CLUSTERED BY ... INTO N BUCKETS` | Sampling, large joins |
| **Partitioned + Bucketed** | `... PARTITIONED BY (yr INT) CLUSTERED BY (id) INTO 8 BUCKETS STORED AS ORC;` | Both clauses combined | Production analytics tables |
| **ORC Columnar** | `CREATE TABLE t (id INT) STORED AS ORC TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress'='SNAPPY');` | `STORED AS ORC` + properties | Optimized analytics |
| **CTAS (Create Table As Select)** | `CREATE TABLE t STORED AS ORC AS SELECT col1, COUNT(*) FROM src GROUP BY col1;` | `AS SELECT` at the end | Materialized results |
| **CREATE TABLE LIKE** | `CREATE TABLE t2 LIKE t1;` | Copies schema only, no data | Cloning schema, re-population |
| **JSON SerDe** | `CREATE TABLE t (...) ROW FORMAT SERDE 'org.openx.data.jsonserde.JsonSerDe' STORED AS TEXTFILE;` | Uses JSON SerDe library | Semi-structured data |
| **Avro SerDe** | `CREATE TABLE t ROW FORMAT SERDE 'org.apache.hadoop.hive.serde2.avro.AvroSerDe' STORED AS INPUTFORMAT ... OUTPUTFORMAT ...;` | Avro container file | Schema evolution workflows |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Production-Grade Partitioned + Bucketed ORC Table

Let us derive the complete statement for a real-world clickstream log analysis scenario.

**Business Requirement:**
- Store clickstream events with schema `(user_id, url, session_id)`.
- Partition by `event_date` (daily partitions).
- Bucket by `user_id` into 16 buckets.
- Store in ORC with Snappy compression for fast analytics.

**Step 1:** Base keyword and table name.

$$
\texttt{CREATE TABLE clickstream\_logs}
$$

**Step 2:** Add column definitions.

$$
\texttt{( user\_id STRING, url STRING, session\_id STRING, event\_hour INT )}
$$

Note: `event_date` is **NOT** a column — it is the partition key defined separately.

**Step 3:** Add partition clause.

$$
\texttt{PARTITIONED BY (event\_date DATE)}
$$

**Step 4:** Add bucketing clause.

$$
\texttt{CLUSTERED BY (user\_id) SORTED BY (event\_hour) INTO 16 BUCKETS}
$$

The optional `SORTED BY` within buckets physically sorts records inside each bucket file — this further speeds up range queries.

**Step 5:** Add storage format and properties.

$$
\texttt{STORED AS ORC TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress' = 'SNAPPY')}
$$

**Complete Compiled Statement:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE clickstream_logs (
    user_id     STRING,
    url         STRING,
    session_id  STRING,
    event_hour  INT
)
PARTITIONED BY (event_date DATE)
CLUSTERED BY (user_id) SORTED BY (event_hour) INTO 16 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress' = 'SNAPPY');
```

**Step 6:** After creation, the user must manually add a partition:

```sql
ALTER TABLE clickstream_logs ADD PARTITION (event_date='2024-01-15');
```

**Logical Workflow (Textual Reasoning):**

1. The Metastore Service (HMS) receives the `CREATE TABLE` request.
2. HMS writes the schema, partition keys, SerDe, and storage info into the relational Metastore DB (MySQL/PostgreSQL).
3. The Table object is registered, but **no data files exist yet** — it is an empty shell.
4. HDFS directory `/user/hive/warehouse/clickstream_logs/` is created.
5. After `ADD PARTITION`, subdirectory `/event_date=2024-01-15/` is created.
6. When data is loaded via `LOAD DATA` or `INSERT ... SELECT`, Hive writes 16 ORC files into the partition directory (one per bucket).

### 3.4 Create Table As Select (CTAS) — Detailed Derivation

The CTAS statement is unique because it **combines schema inference from a query with table creation**.

$$
\texttt{CREATE TABLE target\_table STORED AS ORC AS SELECT col\_a, col\_b, SUM(col\_c) FROM source\_table GROUP BY col\_a, col\_b;}
$$

**Rules of CTAS:**
1. The new table's columns are **automatically derived** from the SELECT list — no manual column list allowed.
2. Column names are taken from the SELECT's output aliases or column references.
3. Data types are inferred by Hive's type-checking.
4. If `STORED AS` is omitted, defaults to `TEXTFILE` (often undesirable in production).
5. The command **does not allow partitions** to be defined in the `CREATE` portion of CTAS — the source table's partition scheme (if any) is used; otherwise, the target is unpartitioned.

### 3.5 Mathematical Insight: Bucketing Hash Function

Buckets are computed using a deterministic hash function on the bucket column.

$$
\text{For each row: } \quad \text{hash}(bucket\_col) \bmod N \;=\; bucket\_id
$$

where $N$ is the `INTO N BUCKETS` value and $\text{hash}(\cdot)$ is Hive's internal hash (typically a murmur-like hash for strings, value-preserving for integers).

This means rows with the same `bucket_col` value always land in the same bucket — guaranteeing **co-location** for efficient map-side joins.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hive CREATE TABLE — End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["User SQL Script"] --> B["Hive CLI / Beeline / Thrift Server"]
    B --> C["Hive Driver Compiler"]
    C --> D["Semantic Analyzer"]
    D --> E["Query Planner and Optimizer"]
    E --> F["Physical Plan DAG Tez or Spark or MR"]
    F --> G["HDFS HDFS Write Phase"]
    D --> H["Hive Metastore HMS MySQL or Derby"]
    H --> I["Schema, Partition Keys, SerDe Info, Storage Format"]
    G --> J["user/hive/warehouse/sales_db db/orders/"]
    I --> K["Metastore DB tables TBLS, COLUMNS_V2, PARTITIONS"]
    H --> L["Block Metadata for Buckets"]
    J --> M["Partition Directory year=2024 month=01"]
    M --> N["Bucket File 00000_0 ORC SNAPPY"]
    M --> O["Bucket File 00001_0 ORC SNAPPY"]
    M --> P["Bucket File 00015_0 ORC SNAPPY"]
```

### 4.2 Logical Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Table Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need to create a Hive table?"] --> Q1{"Is data shared with other tools?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes (Spark, Pig, raw HDFS files)" --> EX["CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE"]
    Q1 -- "No, Hive owns the data" --> Q2{"Is data queried by date, region, or category?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> PART["PARTITIONED BY (key TYPE)"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Need sampling or fast map-side joins?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> BUCK["CLUSTERED BY (col) INTO N BUCKETS"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> SIMPLE["Simple Managed Table"]
    EX --> Q4{"Need fast columnar reads?"}
    PART --> Q4
    BUCK --> Q4
    SIMPLE --> Q4
    Q4 -- "Yes (analytics, BI)" --> ORC["STORED AS ORC + Snappy/Zlib"]
    Q4 -- "No (raw ingestion, debugging)" --> TXT["STORED AS TEXTFILE"]
```

### 4.3 Internal Metastore Object Structure (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DB["Database: sales_db"] --> TBL["Table: orders"]
    TBL --> COLS["Columns: order_id INT, amount DOUBLE"]
    TBL --> PART["Partition Keys: year INT, month INT"]
    TBL --> BUCK["Bucket Spec: CLUSTERED BY (user_id) INTO 8 BUCKETS"]
    TBL --> SERDE["Row Format SerDe: OpenCSVSerde"]
    TBL --> SF["Stored As: ORC"]
    TBL --> LOC["Location: /warehouse/sales_db/orders/"]
    TBL --> PROP["TblProperties: orc.compress=SNAPPY"]
    PART --> PDIR["HDFS: /orders/year=2024/month=01/"]
    PDIR --> B0["Bucket File 00000_0"]
    PDIR --> B1["Bucket File 00001_0"]
    PDIR --> B7["Bucket File 00007_0"]
```

### 4.4 Lifecycle: DROP TABLE Behavior Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DropCmd["DROP TABLE orders"] --> Q{"Is table EXTERNAL?"}
    Q -- "No (MANAGED)" --> D1["1. Delete metadata from HMS"]
    D1 --> D2["2. Recursively DELETE HDFS data files"]
    D2 --> D3["Result: Data is GONE"]
    Q -- "Yes (EXTERNAL)" --> E1["1. Delete metadata from HMS"]
    E1 --> E2["2. HDFS files are PRESERVED"]
    E2 --> E3["Result: Data is SAFE"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Differentiate between a managed table and an external table in Hive.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **managed (internal) table** is the default Hive table type where Hive controls both the **metadata and the data lifecycle**. When a managed table is dropped, both the metastore entry and the underlying HDFS data files are **physically deleted**. The data resides at `/user/hive/warehouse/<db>.db/<table>/` by default.

An **external table** is created using the `EXTERNAL` keyword and points to data stored at a user-specified `LOCATION` outside the warehouse directory. When dropped, only the **metadata** is removed; the HDFS files are **preserved** untouched. This makes external tables ideal for production datasets that must be shared with Spark, Pig, or other Hadoop ecosystem tools without Hive owning the data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write that external tables *cannot* be dropped. This is **wrong** — they can be dropped, but the data survives. Marks are awarded specifically for stating that metadata is removed but HDFS data is preserved.

---

**Q2. What is the default storage format and field delimiter in Hive?** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **default storage format** in Hive is `TEXTFILE`, which stores data as plain, line-delimited text files on HDFS. The **default field delimiter** is **Ctrl-A** (ASCII `\001`), a non-printable control character used to separate columns within each row. Additional default delimiters include Ctrl-B (`\002`) for collection items and Ctrl-C (`\003`) for map key-value pairs. The default null marker is `\N`. These control characters are chosen because they almost never appear in real datasets, preventing accidental column mis-interpretation.

---

### Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Partitioned & Bucketed Tables with ORC**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **partitioning and bucketing** in Hive with a suitable example. State the differences between them. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Partitioning** is a Hive optimization technique that divides a large table into **separate physical subdirectories** on HDFS, where each subdirectory corresponds to a unique value (or combination of values) of the partition column(s). When a query is executed with a `WHERE` clause on the partition column, Hive performs **partition pruning** — it scans only the relevant subdirectories, drastically reducing I/O.

**Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE sales (
    sale_id    INT,
    product    STRING,
    amount     DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (sale_year INT, sale_month INT)
STORED AS ORC;
```

HDFS layout after loading data:

```
/user/hive/warehouse/sales/sale_year=2024/sale_month=01/file.orc
/user/hive/warehouse/sales/sale_year=2024/sale_month=02/file.orc
```

**Bucketing** is a finer-grained technique that divides data within a partition (or table) into a **fixed number of equal-sized files (buckets)** based on a hash function applied to the bucket column. Each bucket is stored as a separate file.

**Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE user_logs (
    user_id   STRING,
    action    STRING
)
CLUSTERED BY (user_id) INTO 8 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC;
```

For each row, Hive computes: $\text{hash}(\texttt{user\_id}) \bmod 8$ to determine the bucket number (00000 to 00007).

**Differences:**

| Feature | Partitioning | Bucketing |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Directory-level | File-level (within partition) |
| Cardinality | Should be **low** (e.g., date, country) | Can be **high** (e.g., user_id) |
| Mechanism | Value-based directory split | Hash-based file split |
| Pruning | Skips entire directories | Used for sampling & map-joins |
| Defined by | `PARTITIONED BY` | `CLUSTERED BY ... INTO N BUCKETS` |
| Data distribution | Uneven (skewed if low cardinality) | Even (hash-balanced) |

---

**(b)** With a neat example, illustrate how to create a **partitioned, bucketed table stored in ORC format with Snappy compression** in Hive. Also explain the role of the Hive Metastore in this process. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**HiveQL Statement:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE employee_salary (
    emp_id        STRING,
    emp_name      STRING,
    salary        DOUBLE,
    bonus         DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (dept STRING)
CLUSTERED BY (emp_id) SORTED BY (salary DESC) INTO 4 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress' = 'SNAPPY');
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

- The schema lists `emp_id`, `emp_name`, `salary`, `bonus` as actual data columns.
- `dept` is the **partition column** — it is NOT listed in the column schema.
- `CLUSTERED BY (emp_id)` creates 4 hash-based buckets within each partition.
- `SORTED BY (salary DESC)` sorts records within each bucket by descending salary, enabling efficient top-N queries.
- `STORED AS ORC` uses the Optimized Row Columnar format.
- `TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress' = 'SNAPPY')` enables Snappy compression inside ORC files.

**Role of the Hive Metastore (HMS):**

The HMS is a **central relational database (MySQL/PostgreSQL/Derby)** that stores the *metadata* — i.e., the schema, column types, partition keys, SerDe class, storage format, HDFS location, and table properties — of every Hive table. When this `CREATE TABLE` is executed:

1. The Hive Driver sends the DDL to HMS.
2. HMS inserts a row into the `TBLS` table, columns into `COLUMNS_V2`, partition keys into `PARTITION_KEYS`, and the SerDe/format details into `SDS` (Storage Descriptor).
3. No data is written to HDFS at this stage — the table is an empty logical container.
4. Later, when `ALTER TABLE ... ADD PARTITION (dept='IT')` is run, a subdirectory `/dept=IT/` is created on HDFS, and 4 bucket files are written upon data load.

The HMS acts as the **single source of truth** that allows multiple clients (Hive, Spark SQL, Impala, Presto) to share a consistent view of the data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss:** Forgetting to mention that the partition column (`dept`) is **NOT** in the regular column list is a frequent error. Examiners expect students to explicitly state this "schema separation" rule. Also, students often confuse the role of the Metastore with that of the NameNode — HMS stores *table schema*, NameNode stores *file block locations*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — External Tables, CTAS, and SerDe**

**(a)** What is an **External Table** in Hive? How is it different from a managed table? Write the HiveQL syntax to create an external table for a CSV file stored at `/data/employee.csv` with columns `emp_id INT, emp_name STRING, salary DOUBLE`. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

An **external table** in Hive is a table definition where Hive references data that is **stored externally** (typically on HDFS at a user-specified location), and Hive does **not own the data**. The `EXTERNAL` keyword in the `CREATE TABLE` statement explicitly tells Hive to treat the data as *outside* its managed warehouse.

**Key Differences from a Managed Table:**

| Aspect | Managed Table | External Table |
|---|---|---|
| Data ownership | Hive owns it | User owns it |
| `LOCATION` clause | Optional (uses default warehouse) | **Mandatory** |
| `DROP TABLE` | Deletes both metadata and **HDFS data** | Deletes **metadata only**; HDFS data remains |
| Data sharing | Not safe to share with other tools | Safe — multiple tools can read same HDFS files |
| Use case | Staging, scratch, intermediate results | Production data, raw ingestion, shared datasets |

**HiveQL Statement:**

```sql
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS employee_ext (
    emp_id     INT,
    emp_name   STRING,
    salary     DOUBLE
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/data/employee.csv'
TBLPROPERTIES ('skip.header.line.count' = '1');
```

- `EXTERNAL` keyword designates it as an external table.
- `ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','` specifies comma as the field separator (CSV format).
- `STORED AS TEXTFILE` indicates plain text files.
- `LOCATION '/data/employee.csv'` is the **mandatory** HDFS path.
- `TBLPROPERTIES ('skip.header.line.count' = '1')` tells Hive to skip the first line (CSV header row) while reading.

---

**(b)** Explain the **`CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS)`** statement in Hive with a suitable example. What are the limitations of CTAS? [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The **CTAS** statement combines schema inference and data population in a single DDL operation. It creates a new table whose **schema is automatically derived** from the `SELECT` query's output columns and **populates it with the result rows** of the query — all in one atomic operation.

**Example:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE sales_summary
STORED AS ORC
AS
SELECT product_category, COUNT(*) AS total_orders, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue
FROM sales
WHERE sale_year = 2024
GROUP BY product_category;
```

**What happens internally:**

1. Hive runs the `SELECT` query as a normal MapReduce/Tez job.
2. The output schema (`product_category STRING`, `total_orders BIGINT`, `total_revenue DOUBLE`) is auto-inferred.
3. The Metastore is updated with this new schema.
4. The result rows are written to `/user/hive/warehouse/sales_summary/` as ORC files.
5. The command is **atomic** — if the query fails, no table is created.

**Limitations of CTAS:**

1. **No manual column list allowed** — the column list is inferred from the SELECT. You cannot write `CREATE TABLE t (col1 INT) AS SELECT ...`.
2. **Partition columns cannot be specified in the CREATE portion** of CTAS. If the source is partitioned, the target inherits the partition scheme; otherwise, the target is unpartitioned.
3. **Storage format defaults to TEXTFILE** if not explicitly stated — leading to slow performance unless `STORED AS ORC` is added.
4. **No support for some table features** like `CLUSTERED BY`, `SKEWED BY`, or complex constraints during creation.
5. **Cannot create external tables via CTAS** directly — the `EXTERNAL` keyword is not supported in CTAS syntax.
6. The new table does **not inherit** the source table's `TBLPROPERTIES` or `COMMENT` annotations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE t AS SELECT ...` and lose 2 marks. Always remember: **CTAS only creates MANAGED tables**. For external tables, you must first `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE` (empty), then `INSERT INTO ... SELECT`.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist for last-minute exam revision. Every point is a potential 1–2 mark question.

- ✅ **HiveQL** is a SQL-like query language for Hadoop/HDFS; it translates to MapReduce/Tez/Spark jobs.
- ✅ **Managed tables** (default) — Hive owns data; `DROP TABLE` deletes both metadata and HDFS files.
- ✅ **External tables** — Hive references external data; `DROP TABLE` deletes metadata only; `LOCATION` is **mandatory**.
- ✅ **Default storage format** is `TEXTFILE`; default field delimiter is **Ctrl-A (`\001`)**.
- ✅ **ORC** is the recommended columnar format — supports predicate pushdown, indexes, and built-in compression (Snappy/Zlib).
- ✅ **Partitioning** = directory-level data split (`PARTITIONED BY`) — best for **low-cardinality** columns like date, region.
- ✅ **Bucketing** = file-level data split (`CLUSTERED BY ... INTO N BUCKETS`) — best for **high-cardinality** columns and sampling.
- ✅ **Partition column is NOT a regular column** — it appears only in the `PARTITIONED BY` clause.
- ✅ **Row Format** uses SerDe (Serializer/Deserializer) to read/write structured data; default is delimited, but **JSON SerDe**, **Avro SerDe**, and **RegexSerDe** are common for semi-structured data.
- ✅ **CTAS** (`CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT`) creates a managed table with auto-inferred schema — does not support `EXTERNAL`, `PARTITIONED BY`, or manual column lists.
- ✅ **`CREATE TABLE ... LIKE`** copies the schema (and storage info) of an existing table — does NOT copy data.
- ✅ **`TBLPROPERTIES`** stores arbitrary key-value metadata; ORC-specific properties like `'orc.compress' = 'SNAPPY'` are common.
- ✅ **Hive Metastore (HMS)** is the central metadata repository (usually MySQL/PostgreSQL) — it does NOT store data, only schema, partition keys, and storage descriptors.
- ✅ **`IF NOT EXISTS`** in `CREATE TABLE` is an idempotency guard — prevents errors on re-execution in ETL pipelines.
- ✅ **`ALTER TABLE ... ADD PARTITION (col='value')`** is required to register a partition directory before data is loaded.
- ✅ **Bucketing hash formula:** $\text{hash}(col) \bmod N = \text{bucket\_id}$, where $N$ = number of buckets.
- ✅ **Dropping an EXTERNAL table** keeps HDFS data safe — a frequent 3-mark examiner favorite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dropping Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dropping Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Big Data Processing (particularly within **Apache Hive** and **HBase**, which are core to KTU Module 4), **Dropping a Table** is a Data Definition Language (DDL) operation that permanently removes a table's definition (schema) from the metastore/catalog and, depending on the table type, deletes the underlying data files from the distributed file system (HDFS).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Dropping a table is a DDL command that deletes the table structure and (optionally) the data. In HiveQL, the command is `DROP TABLE [IF EXISTS] table_name [PURGE]`; in HBase, it is `disable '<table_name>'` followed by `drop '<table_name>'`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your computer's file system. A **table** in Hive is like a **shortcut/pointer** on your desktop that points to a folder of data files in HDFS.

- For an **Internal (Managed) Table**: The shortcut and the folder it points to are both owned by Hive. When you drop the table, it's like deleting the shortcut AND the folder — everything is gone.
- For an **External Table**: The shortcut is on Hive's desktop, but the actual folder belongs to someone else (another team or tool). When you drop the table, it's like deleting ONLY the shortcut — the folder and its files remain safe on HDFS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Always remember the difference between **DROP** (removes schema + data for internal tables) and **TRUNCATE** (removes only data, keeps schema). Examiners frequently test this contrast.

## 1.3 Key Terminology with Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning | Default Behavior |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **DROP TABLE** | Removes table definition + data | Internal tables: data deleted; External: data kept |
| **TRUNCATE TABLE** | Removes only data rows | Schema preserved |
| **IF EXISTS** | Prevents error if table missing | Optional clause |
| **PURGE** | Skips Trash, permanent deletion | Used for internal tables |
| **CASCADE** | Drops dependent views/constraints | Hive 2.x+ support |
| **RESTRICT** | Refuses if dependents exist | Default in many systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants / Defaults in Hive:**
> - Default table type: **Managed (Internal)**
> - Default warehouse path: `/user/hive/warehouse/`
> - Trash mechanism: Files moved to `/user/hive/warehouse/.Trash/...`
> - **PURGE** bypasses the Trash mechanism for **immediate permanent deletion**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: The DROP TABLE Logic

The execution of a `DROP TABLE` statement follows a structured sequence of events:

### Step-by-Step Logic Chain

1. **Parser & Semantic Analysis**
   - HiveQL parser tokenizes the statement
   - Verifies the existence of the table (or skips check if `IF EXISTS` is specified)
   - Checks for the presence of dependent objects (views, constraints)

2. **Metastore Lock Acquisition**
   - Acquires exclusive lock on the table entry in the **Hive Metastore** (typically backed by MySQL/PostgreSQL)
   - Prevents concurrent DDL operations on the same table

3. **Dependency Check**
   - Identifies all views that reference this table
   - If `CASCADE` is specified → drops dependent views first
   - If `RESTRICT` is specified (default) → throws error if dependencies exist

4. **Data File Handling**
   - For **Internal Tables**: Deletes data files from HDFS (or moves to Trash)
   - For **External Tables**: **Data files are preserved** (only metastore entry is removed)

5. **Metastore Entry Removal**
   - The table's row in the metastore database (`TBLS`, `SDS`, `COLUMNS_V2`, `PARTITIONS` tables) is deleted
   - Column statistics and partition metadata are purged

6. **Event Notification (Hive 3.x+)**
   - ACID-aware metastore fires drop events
   - Compaction queue is notified for transactional tables

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Command Pattern | Affected Object | Data Files? | Schema? | Reversible? |
|----------------|----------------|-------------|---------|-------------|
| `DROP TABLE internal_table` | Internal Table | ✅ Deleted | ✅ Removed | ❌ No (Trash recoverable) |
| `DROP TABLE internal_table PURGE` | Internal Table | ✅ Permanently Deleted | ✅ Removed | ❌ No |
| `DROP TABLE external_table` | External Table | ❌ Preserved | ✅ Removed | ⚠️ Partial (data intact) |
| `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS table_name` | Any Table | (Conditional) | (Conditional) | (Conditional) |
| `DROP TABLE table_name CASCADE` | Internal + Dependents | ✅ Deleted | ✅ Removed (with views) | ❌ No |
| `TRUNCATE TABLE table_name` | Internal Only | ✅ Data Cleared | ❌ Schema Kept | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** You **cannot** use `TRUNCATE` on External Tables in Hive. This is a guaranteed exam question in some form.

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Data Lakehouse Management:** Production engineers regularly drop staging tables to free up HDFS cluster storage.
- **GDPR & Compliance:** The `PURGE` option is critical for **right-to-be-forgotten** requests, ensuring data doesn't sit in the Trash directory.
- **ETL Pipeline Hygiene:** Old partitioned tables from previous fiscal years are dropped to maintain query performance.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Understanding that external table drops preserve data allows teams to "undo" schema changes by recreating the DDL.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why `IF EXISTS`?** → In production, automated jobs run nightly. A missing table shouldn't crash the entire pipeline.
- **Why `PURGE`?** → The Trash directory itself takes up HDFS NameNode memory. For large datasets, you want immediate, permanent deletion.
- **Why CASCADE default OFF?** → Safety. Prevents accidental cascade deletion of business-critical views.
- **Why disable before drop in HBase?** → HBase requires tables to be offline before deletion to prevent active RegionServer operations from corrupting data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Execution Flow: `DROP TABLE employee_internal;`

Let us walk through the exhaustive evaluation of this command in Apache Hive, assuming a standard internal table `employee_internal` exists in the warehouse.

### Step A: Command Tokenization

The HiveQL parser receives the following tokens:

$$
\text{Statement} \rightarrow \text{DROP} \mid \text{TABLE} \mid \text{identifier}(employee\_internal) \mid \text{;}
$$

The parser verifies that the statement matches the production rule:

$$
\text{Grammar} \rightarrow \text{DROP TABLE} \ \text{[IF EXISTS]} \ \text{table\_name} \ \text{[PURGE|RESTRICT|CASCADE]}
$$

### Step B: Semantic Validation

The Semantic Analyzer queries the **Hive Metastore** (MySQL) using the SQL:

$$
\text{SELECT} \ * \ \text{FROM TBLS} \ \text{WHERE TBL\_NAME} = \text{'employee\_internal'}
$$

If no record is returned and `IF EXISTS` is **NOT** specified, the analyzer throws:

$$
\text{Error: AnalysisException: Table 'employee\_internal' does not exist}
$$

### Step C: Table Type Resolution

The metastore returns the table record including the column `TBL_TYPE`:

$$
\text{TBL\_TYPE} =
\begin{cases}
\text{MANAGED\_TABLE} & \Rightarrow \text{Proceed with data deletion} \\
\text{EXTERNAL\_TABLE} & \Rightarrow \text{Skip data deletion, remove schema only}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step D: Dependency Graph Traversal

For our case, assume `MANAGED_TABLE` and a dependent view `v_emp_salary` exists.

The dependency graph is:

$$
G = (V, E) \quad \text{where} \quad V = \{employee\_internal, v\_emp\_salary\}, \quad E = \{(v\_emp\_salary, employee\_internal)\}
$$

**Without CASCADE:** Operation halts with:
$$
\text{Error: Cannot drop table when there are dependent views. Use CASCADE.}
$$

**With CASCADE:** The topological sort determines deletion order:

$$
\text{Order} = \text{DFS}(v\_emp\_salary) \rightarrow \text{DFS}(employee\_internal)
$$

### Step E: HDFS Operation

The DataNode operations are:

$$
\text{HDFS Path} = \text{location}(\text{employee\_internal}) = /user/hive/warehouse/employee\_internal
$$

- **Without PURGE:** Files moved to
$$
\text{Trash Path} = /user/hive/warehouse/.Trash/\text{timestamp}/employee\_internal
$$
- **With PURGE:** Direct invocation of
$$
\text{HDFS API: FileSystem.delete(path, recursive=true)} \quad \text{with} \ \text{skipTrash=true}
$$

### Step F: Metastore Cleanup

The metastore executes the following cascade of SQL DELETE statements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
