| 6 | `ORDER` | Transformation (Relation) | `dept_summary` | `ordered` (sorted relation) |
| 7 | `STORE` | Output (Relation) | `ordered` | HDFS file `/output/dept_summary` |

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Logical Plan (DAG)

The seven statements above form a **Directed Acyclic Graph** as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{records} = \text{LOAD}(\text{path}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: } & \text{high\_earners} = \text{FILTER}(\text{records},\; \text{condition}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: } & \text{projected} = \text{FOREACH}(\text{high\_earners},\; \text{projection}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: } & \text{grouped} = \text{GROUP}(\text{projected},\; \text{key} = \text{dept}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: } & \text{dept\_summary} = \text{FOREACH}(\text{grouped},\; \text{aggregate}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6: } & \text{ordered} = \text{ORDER}(\text{dept\_summary},\; \text{key},\; \text{order}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 7: } & \text{STORE}(\text{ordered},\; \text{path})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each variable on the **left-hand side** is a **relation** (a bag of tuples). Each statement is **atomic** — it processes one bag and produces exactly one new bag. This **one-in-one-out invariant** is the structural bedrock of Pig Latin.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Structural Analysis of the `LOAD` Statement

The `LOAD` statement is the most syntactically rich statement in Pig Latin. Its full structural form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LOAD `data\_path` [USING `function` [AS `schema`]];}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example expansion:**

```
records = LOAD '/data/employees.csv' 
          USING PigStorage(',') 
          AS (emp_id:int, name:chararray, dept:chararray, salary:double);
```

| Component | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `LOAD` | Keyword | Begins the input operation |
| `'/data/employees.csv'` | String literal | HDFS path to the source file |
| `USING PigStorage(',')` | Optional clause | Specifies the load function (default is `PigStorage` with tab delimiter) |
| `AS (...)` | Optional clause | Declares the schema (field names and types) |
| `(emp_id:int, ...)` | Schema definition | Typed fields; types can be omitted for **schema-less** mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If `AS` is **omitted**, the relation has **no schema** — fields are referenced positionally as `$0, $1, $2, ...` This is a frequently tested nuance in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Structural Analysis of `FOREACH ... GENERATE`

The `FOREACH ... GENERATE` statement is the most **expressive** transformation in Pig Latin. It applies one or more expressions to every tuple in the input bag.

### 3.4.1 Three Structural Forms

**Form 1: Simple Projection**
```
result = FOREACH input GENERATE field1, field2, field3;
```

**Form 2: Expression Application**
```
result = FOREACH input GENERATE UPPER(name), salary * 1.10 AS raised_salary;
```

**Form 3: Nested `FOREACH` (with `GROUP` for aggregation)**
```
grouped = GROUP input BY category;
summary = FOREACH grouped {
            count  = COUNT(input);
            avgval = AVG(input.value);
            GENERATE group AS category, count, avgval;
          };
```

> [!NOTE]
> The nested form requires **curly braces `{ }`** and an inner variable assignment, which is unique to aggregation blocks.

---

## 3.5 Filtering and Joining — Structural Subtleties

### 3.5.1 `FILTER` Statement
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{result} = \text{FILTER}(\text{input},\; \text{boolean\_expression});
\end{aligned}
$$

**Supported comparison operators:** `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`, `matches` (regex), `is null`, `is not null`.

**Boolean connectives:** `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`.

### 3.5.2 `JOIN` Statement
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{result} = \text{JOIN}(\text{left},\; \text{BY}(\text{key1}),\; \text{right},\; \text{BY}(\text{key2}));
\end{aligned}
$$

- Default: **inner equi-join**.
- Variants: `LEFT OUTER`, `RIGHT OUTER`, `FULL OUTER`, `JOIN` (self-join by repeating the same alias).
- The `BY` clause is **mandatory**; without it, you get a `CROSS` (Cartesian product).

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Filter & Count)

**Problem:** Given 8 student records, filter students who scored above 60 and count them.

| Student | Score |
|---|---|
| A | 75 |
| B | 45 |
| C | 88 |
| D | 52 |
| E | 91 |
| F | 60 |
| G | 73 |
| H | 49 |

**Pig Latin Script:**
```
students = LOAD '/data/students.txt' AS (name:chararray, score:int);
passed = FILTER students BY score > 60;
count_passed = FOREACH (GROUP passed ALL) GENERATE COUNT(passed);
DUMP count_passed;
```

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{students} = \{ (A,75), (B,45), (C,88), (D,52), (E,91), (F,60), (G,73), (H,49) \} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2 (FILTER score > 60): } & \text{passed} = \{ (A,75), (C,88), (E,91), (G,73) \} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3 (GROUP ALL): } & \text{grouped\_all} = \{ (ALL, \{(A,75),(C,88),(E,91),(G,73)\}) \} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4 (COUNT): } & \text{count\_passed} = \{ (4) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output:** `4`

> [!TIP]
> The `GROUP ... ALL` idiom is the standard Pig Latin way to compute **global aggregates** (since there is no `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ...` like SQL).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Pig Latin Execution Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HDFS Source File] --> B[LOAD Statement]
    B --> C[Logical Plan DAG]
    C --> D[Pig Optimizer]
    D --> E[Physical Plan]
    E --> F[MapReduce / Tez / Spark]
    F --> G[HDFS Output / Console Dump]
    H[User Pig Latin Script] --> C
    I[Grunt Shell or Script File] --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Pig Engine** is the translator and optimizer between the high-level Pig Latin script and the low-level execution engine (MapReduce, Tez, or Spark).

---

## 4.2 Structural Anatomy of a Pig Latin Statement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["LHS: relation_name"] --> B[Assignment Operator =]
    B --> C["Operator Keyword e.g. FILTER"]
    C --> D["L Parenthesis ("] 
    D --> E["Input Relation"]
    E --> F["Comma Separator ,"]
    F --> G["Expression / Parameters"]
    G --> H["R Parenthesis )"]
    H --> I["Semicolon ;"]
```

---

## 4.3 Operator Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[Pig Latin Statements]
    Root --> RelOps[Relation Operators]
    Root --> BagOps[Bag Operators]
    RelOps --> LoadOp[LOAD]
    RelOps --> StoreOp[STORE]
    RelOps --> FilterOp[FILTER]
    RelOps --> ForeachOp[FOREACH GENERATE]
    RelOps --> DistinctOp[DISTINCT]
    RelOps --> OrderOp[ORDER]
    BagOps --> JoinOp[JOIN]
    BagOps --> GroupOp[GROUP]
    BagOps --> CogroupOp[COGROUP]
    BagOps --> CrossOp[CROSS]
    BagOps --> UnionOp[UNION]
    BagOps --> SplitOp[SPLIT]
```

---

## 4.4 Complete Data Flow: From Script to Cluster Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: User writes Pig Latin Script]
    S2[Step 2: Grunt Parser validates syntax]
    S3[Step 3: Pig Engine builds Logical Plan]
    S4[Step 4: Optimizer applies rules]
    S5[Step 5: Translates to Physical Plan]
    S6[Step 6: Submits to Execution Engine]
    S7[Step 7: Cluster runs Map / Reduce / Tez tasks]
    S8[Step 8: Result written to HDFS]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define Pig Latin. What is the general structure of a Pig Latin statement?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Pig Latin** is a data flow scripting language used in Apache Pig to process and analyze large datasets in parallel on a Hadoop cluster. It allows developers to write data transformation programs without dealing with the complexities of low-level MapReduce code.

The **general structure** of a Pig Latin statement is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{relation\_name} = \text{operator} (\text{expression})\;
\end{aligned}
$$

Where `relation_name` is the output relation (a bag of tuples), `operator` is the transformation verb (like `LOAD`, `FILTER`, `GROUP`), and `expression` contains the input relation(s) and parameters. Every statement **must end with a semicolon (;)**.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + General form (1) + Semicolon rule (0.5)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

### Q2. Differentiate between `DUMP` and `STORE` statements in Pig Latin.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `DUMP` | `STORE` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Displays output on the console (stdout) | Persists output to a file system (HDFS or local) |
| **Persistence** | Temporary; not saved to disk | Permanent; saved to specified directory |
| **Usage context** | Debugging and quick verification | Production output generation |
| **Syntax** | `DUMP relation_name;` | `STORE relation_name INTO 'path' USING function;` |
| **Side effect** | Triggers full execution of pipeline | Triggers full execution of pipeline |

> **Mark Distribution:** Each correct row (0.5) + Examples (1) = 3

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **major categories of Pig Latin statements** with at least **two examples** from each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **structure of a `LOAD` statement**, including the role of `USING` and `AS` clauses. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

Pig Latin statements are classified into **two major categories** based on the type of data they operate on:

### Category 1: Statements that Work With Relations (Data Operators)

These operate on the **whole relation** (the entire bag of tuples).

**Examples:**
- **`LOAD`**: Reads data from HDFS or local file system.
  ```
  data = LOAD '/input/file.txt' USING PigStorage('\t') AS (f1:int, f2:chararray);
  ```
- **`STORE`**: Writes a relation to HDFS.
  ```
  STORE result INTO '/output/result';
  ```
- **`FILTER`**: Removes tuples that do not satisfy a condition.
  ```
  clean = FILTER data BY f1 > 100;
  ```
- **`DISTINCT`**: Removes duplicate tuples.
  ```
  unique = DISTINCT data;
  ```
- **`FOREACH ... GENERATE`**: Projects/transforms fields.
  ```
  proj = FOREACH data GENERATE f2, f1 * 2 AS doubled;
  ```

### Category 2: Statements that Work With Bags (Relational Operators)

These act on **individual bags** within a relation (e.g., the result of a `GROUP` or `JOIN`).

**Examples:**
- **`JOIN`**: Equi-joins two relations.
  ```
  joined = JOIN A BY id, B BY id;
  ```
- **`GROUP`**: Groups tuples of a relation by a key.
  ```
  grouped = GROUP data BY f2;
  ```
- **`COGROUP`**: Groups two or more relations simultaneously.
  ```
  cg = COGROUP A BY a1, B BY b1;
  ```
- **`CROSS`**: Cartesian product.
  ```
  cross = CROSS A, B;
  ```
- **`UNION`**: Combines two relations with identical schema.
  ```
  combined = UNION A, B;
  ```

> **Key Distinction:** Relation operators treat the input as a *table*; bag operators treat the input as a *grouped collection* of inner bags. **(1 mark for this distinction)**

**Mark Distribution:** Category 1 explanation (2) + 3 examples (1.5) + Category 2 explanation (2) + 2 examples (1) + Distinction (0.5) = 7

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

The **`LOAD`** statement is the entry point of any Pig Latin script. Its full structural form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{relation} = \text{LOAD} \; \text{`path'} \; [\text{USING} \; \text{`function'}] \; [\text{AS} \; \text{`schema'}];
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example Script:

```
employees = LOAD '/data/employees.csv' 
            USING PigStorage(',') 
            AS (emp_id:int, name:chararray, dept:chararray, salary:double);
```

### Structural Components:

**1. The Path Component (1 mark)**
- `'/data/employees.csv'` is the HDFS path.
- Can be a single file, a glob pattern (`/data/*.csv`), or a directory.

**2. The `USING` Clause (2 marks)**
- **Optional.** Specifies the **load function** that interprets the file format.
- Default is `PigStorage` with tab delimiter.
- `PigStorage(',')` here uses comma as the delimiter.
- Other load functions: `TextLoader`, `JsonLoader`, `AvroStorage`, `OrcStorage`, `ParquetLoader`.

**3. The `AS` Clause (2 marks)**
- **Optional.** Declares the **schema** (field names + data types).
- Without `AS`, fields are accessed positionally as `$0, $1, $2...`
- Types supported: `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `chararray`, `bytearray`, `boolean`, `datetime`, etc.

**4. Why Schema Matters (2 marks)**
- With schema: `employees.name` (named access).
- Without schema: `$1` (positional access).
- Schemas enable **type checking** at parse time and prevent runtime errors.

**Mark Distribution:** General form (1) + Path (1) + USING (2) + AS (2) + Schema benefit (1) = 7

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Describe the **Pig Latin data model** with a neat diagram. Explain the **complex types** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Pig Latin script to **load student data, filter students who passed (marks ≥ 50), group them by department, and compute the average marks per department**. Show the output. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

### Pig Latin Data Model (4 Levels)

The Pig Latin data model is **four-level hierarchical**:

| Level | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Lowest) | **Atom** | A single scalar value | `'Alice'`, `30`, `3.14` |
| 2 | **Tuple** | Ordered set of fields (a record) | `('Alice', 30, 3.14)` |
| 3 | **Bag** | Unordered collection of tuples (a table) | `{ ('Alice',30), ('Bob',25) }` |
| 4 (Highest) | **Map** | Set of key-value pairs | `[ 'name'#'Alice' , 'age'#'30' ]` |

**Hierarchical Structure Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Atom: A single scalar value like 'Alice' or 30"] --> B["Tuple: An ordered set of fields (a record/row)"]
    B --> C["Bag: An unordered collection of tuples (a table)"]
    C --> D["Map: A set of key-value pairs with arbitrary keys"]
```

### Complex Types — Detailed Examples (3 marks)

**1. Tuple:**
- Denoted by parentheses `( )`.
- Ordered — the position of fields matters.
- Example: `( 'E101' , 'Alice' , 85.5 )`

**2. Bag:**
- Denoted by braces `{ }`.
- **Unordered** — no guarantee of tuple order.
- A bag can contain tuples of **different schemas** (no schema enforcement at bag level).
- Example:
  ```
  { ('E101', 'Alice', 85.5), ('E102', 'Bob', 90.0), ('E103', 'Carol', 78.2) }
  ```
- A bag can be **inner** (nested inside a tuple) — this is the basis of `GROUP` results.

**3. Map:**
- Denoted by square brackets `[ ]`.
- Keys are `chararray` literals; values can be **any** type.
- Example: `[ 'name'#'Alice', 'age'#'30', 'dept'#'CS' ]`

> **Why these are important:** Pig Latin's complex types enable **semi-structured data processing** (e.g., JSON, nested logs) without flattening — a feature SQL lacks natively.

**Mark Distribution:** Hierarchy diagram (2) + 3 complex types × 1.5 marks each + Importance (0.5) = 7

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

### Problem Requirements:
1. Load student data (id, name, dept, marks).
2. Filter passed students (marks ≥ 50).
3. Group by department.
4. Compute average marks per department.
5. Display output.

### Complete Pig Latin Script (4 marks):

```
-- Step 1: Load student data with schema
students = LOAD '/data/students.txt' 
           USING PigStorage(',') 
           AS (student_id:int, name:chararray, dept:chararray, marks:double);

-- Step 2: Filter only passed students
passed = FILTER students BY marks >= 50.0;

-- Step 3: Group by department
grouped_by_dept = GROUP passed BY dept;

-- Step 4: Compute average marks per department
dept_avg = FOREACH grouped_by_dept GENERATE 
                        group AS department, 
                        AVG(passed.marks) AS average_marks;

-- Step 5: Display result
DUMP dept_avg;
```

### Input Data (assumed): `/data/students.txt`

```
1,Alice,CS,85.0
2,Bob,CS,45.0
3,Carol,EC,72.0
4,David,EC,90.0
5,Eve,CS,55.0
6,Frank,ME,30.0
7,Grace,ME,60.0
```

### Step-by-Step Trace (3 marks):

**Step 1 (LOAD):** `students` = bag with 7 tuples, schema `(id, name, dept, marks)`.

**Step 2 (FILTER marks >= 50):** `passed` = bag with tuples where marks ≥ 50:
- (1, Alice, CS, 85.0)
- (3, Carol, EC, 72.0)
- (4, David, EC, 90.0)
- (5, Eve, CS, 55.0)
- (7, Grace, ME, 60.0)

**Step 3 (GROUP BY dept):** `grouped_by_dept` = bag with 3 tuples:
- (CS, { (1,Alice,CS,85.0), (5,Eve,CS,55.0) })
- (EC, { (3,Carol,EC,72.0), (4,David,EC,90.0) })
- (ME, { (7,Grace,ME,60.0) })

**Step 4 (AVG):** `dept_avg` = bag with 3 tuples:

| department | average_marks |
|---|---|
| CS | 70.0 |
| EC | 81.0 |
| ME | 60.0 |

### Final Output (via DUMP):
```
(CS,70.0)
(EC,81.0)
(ME,60.0)
```

**Mark Distribution:** Script (4) + Step-by-step trace (2) + Final output (1) = 7

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the semicolon `;`** at statement ends — **0.5 to 1 mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing `DUMP` and `STORE`** — `DUMP` is for console; `STORE` is for HDFS. Mixing them up costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong operator category** — Students often classify `JOIN` as a relation operator; it operates on *bags*. **Cost: 1-2 marks.**
> 4. **Omitting the `BY` clause in `JOIN`** — Without `BY`, it's a `CROSS` (Cartesian product), not a join. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Using positional fields with a declared schema** — If `AS` is used, you must use field **names**, not `$0, $1...`
> 6. **Treating `GROUP` as a `JOIN`** — `GROUP` does *not* join data; it simply partitions it. **Cost: 1-2 marks.**
> 7. **Not specifying `GROUP ... ALL` for global aggregation** — Pig Latin has no `SELECT COUNT(*)`; you must use `GROUP ... ALL` then `COUNT`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist 📋

> 🎯 **Before You Leave — Memorize These!**

- ✅ **Pig Latin = data flow language** (not a query language like SQL).
- ✅ **Statement form:** `result = operator (input [, params]);` — **always end with `;`**.
- ✅ **Two operator categories:**
  - **Relation operators** → `LOAD`, `STORE`, `FILTER`, `FOREACH GENERATE`, `DISTINCT`, `ORDER`, `LIMIT`, `SAMPLE`, `STREAM`, `MAPREDUCE`.
  - **Bag operators** → `JOIN`, `COGROUP`, `GROUP`, `CROSS`, `UNION`, `SPLIT`, `CUBE`, `ROLLUP`.
- ✅ **Data model hierarchy:** `Atom → Tuple → Bag → Map`.
- ✅ **Complex types:** Tuple `( )`, Bag `{ }`, Map `[ ]`.
- ✅ **LOAD default storage:** `PigStorage` with **tab delimiter**.
- ✅ **`DUMP` → console; `STORE` → HDFS**.
- ✅ **Schema =** `AS (field:type, field:type, ...)`; without it, fields are `$0, $1, $2...`.
- ✅ **`GROUP ... ALL`** is the idiom for **global aggregation** (no `COUNT(*)` shortcut).
- ✅ **Statements form a DAG** (Directed Acyclic Graph) optimized by the Pig Engine.
- ✅ **Execution engines:** MapReduce (legacy), **Tez** (default modern), **Spark** (optional).
- ✅ **Diagnostic statements:** `DESCRIBE` (schema), `EXPLAIN` (plan), `ILLUSTRATE` (sample data).
- ✅ **`UNION` requires identical schema** of both relations.
- ✅ **Pig Latin is loosely typed** (types are optional) but strongly typed when declared.

> **🎓 Final Tip:** In KTU exams, always include a **neat diagram of the operator classification** and a **step-by-step trace** of any script — these alone can earn 2-3 extra marks even if your explanation is partial.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Big Data Processing** frameworks such as **Apache Hive** and **Apache Spark**, a **Function** is a named, reusable, and parameterised computational routine that accepts zero or more **input arguments**, performs a deterministic or aggregate transformation on the data, and returns either a **scalar value**, a **set of values**, or **multiple rows** as output.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 4: Hive / Spark Query Languages and Functions), functions are formally classified into three major families:

1. **Built-in Functions** — Pre-shipped by the framework (e.g., `round()`, `concat()`, `year()`).
2. **User-Defined Functions (UDFs)** — Custom Java/Python/Scala routines written by developers and registered into the query engine.
3. **Aggregate Functions (UDAFs)** and **Table-Generating Functions (UDTFs)** — Specialised variants that operate on row groups or explode rows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 specifically emphasises **Hive Functions** (Built-in, UDF, UDAF, UDTF) and **Spark SQL Functions** as examinable high-weightage topics. Examiners frequently test the **difference between UDF, UDAF, and UDTF** as a 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **function in Big Data** like a **"food processor in a massive industrial kitchen"**.

- You feed it **raw ingredients** (input rows/columns).
- The processor performs a **specific operation** (chop, grind, juice).
- It outputs a **finished product** (transformed value/row set).

A **Built-in Function** is a *factory-supplied attachment* (like a default slicer). A **UDF** is a *custom blade* you manufacture yourself for a special task. A **UDAF** is a *blender* that processes a *batch* of fruits together. A **UDTF** is a *juicer* that takes one fruit and **emits many juice shots** (rows) from it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:**
> - **Scalar Function (UDF)** → 1 input row → **1 output value**
> - **Aggregate Function (UDAF)** → N input rows → **1 output value**
> - **Table-Generating Function (UDTF)** → 1 input row → **N output rows**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Default Hive function namespace: `default`
- Spark SQL built-in functions: `org.apache.spark.sql.functions._`
- UDF execution latency overhead on large clusters: typically **10–50 ms per invocation**
- Default type for numeric UDFs: `Double` / `Integer` depending on JVM boxing

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Theory of Functions in Distributed Query Engines

A function in Hive/Spark operates across **three execution layers**:

1. **Parser Layer (Logical Plan)** — The function name and signature are parsed. Type-checking validates input/output schema compatibility.
2. **Optimizer Layer (Catalyst / Hive Planner)** — Constant folding and predicate pushdown may inline deterministic functions.
3. **Execution Layer (Tez / Spark DAG)** — The function is **serialised, shipped to worker nodes, deserialised, and applied per row or per partition**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Function Category

**A. Built-in Functions**
- *Why:* Performance — implemented in native JVM, highly optimised.
- *How:* Invoked directly using SQL syntax: `SELECT round(salary, 2) FROM emp;`

**B. User-Defined Functions (UDFs)**
- *Why:* Built-ins cannot cover every business logic.
- *How:* Extend `UDF` class (Hive) or register via `udf.register()` (Spark).

**C. User-Defined Aggregate Functions (UDAFs)**
- *Why:* Custom aggregations (e.g., geometric mean, weighted average).
- *How:* Implement `GenericUDAFResolver2` (Hive) or use `Aggregator` trait (Spark).

**D. User-Defined Table-Generating Functions (UDTFs)**
- *Why:* Explode arrays/maps or generate synthetic rows.
- *How:* Extend `GenericUDTF` (Hive) or use `explode()` in Spark.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Function Type** | **Input Cardinality** | **Output Cardinality** | **Hive Class** | **Spark Equivalent** | **Example Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scalar UDF** | 1 row | 1 value | `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF` | `spark.udf.register()` | `upper(name)` |
| **Aggregate UDAF** | N rows | 1 value | `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.udf.generic.GenericUDAFResolver2` | `UserDefinedAggregateFunction` | `avg(salary)` |
| **Table-Generating UDTF** | 1 row | N rows | `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.udf.generic.GenericUDTF` | `flatMap` / `explode` | `explode(addresses)` |
| **Built-in Math** | 1 row | 1 value | Pre-shipped | `functions` object | `round(x, 2)` |
| **Built-in Date** | 1 row | 1 value | Pre-shipped | `functions` object | `year(dob)` |
| **Built-in String** | 1 row | 1 value | Pre-shipped | `functions` object | `concat(a, b)` |

### 2.4 Mathematical Representation of Cardinality

Let $R$ be a relation with $|R| = n$ rows. The function $f$ applied to $R$ has the following cardinality mapping:

$$f_{UDF} : \mathbb{R}^{1 \times k} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 1}$$
$$f_{UDAF} : \mathbb{R}^{n \times k} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 1}$$
$$f_{UDTF} : \mathbb{R}^{1 \times k} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}, \quad m \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Functions enable **ETL pipelines**, **feature engineering for ML**, **data cleansing**, and **complex event processing** on petabyte-scale data warehouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: UDF Execution in Hive

**Problem Statement:** Create a Hive UDF that converts a temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit.

**Step 1 — Mathematical Formulation:**
The conversion formula is:

$$T_F = \left(T_C \times \frac{9}{5}\right) + 32$$

**Step 2 — Hive UDF Implementation (Java):**

```java
package com.ktu.hive.udf;
import org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF;
public class CelsiusToFahrenheit extends UDF {
    public Double evaluate(Double celsius) {
        if (celsius == null) return null;
        return (celsius * 9.0 / 5.0) + 32.0;
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Compilation and Packaging:**

$$
\text{Compile} \rightarrow \text{jar} \rightarrow \text{UDF JAR} = \text{ktu\_hive\_udfs.jar}
$$

**Step 4 — Registration in Hive CLI:**

```sql
ADD JAR /path/to/ktu_hive_udfs.jar;
CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION c2f AS 'com.ktu.hive.udf.CelsiusToFahrenheit';
```

**Step 5 — Invocation in HQL:**

```sql
SELECT city, temp_c, c2f(temp_c) AS temp_f
FROM weather_data;
```

**Step 6 — Logical Evaluation per Row:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{For row } r_i = (\text{city}_i, T_{C,i}): \quad
f_{UDF}(T_{C,i}) &= \left(T_{C,i} \cdot \frac{9}{5}\right) + 32 \\
\text{If } T_{C,i} &= 100°C: \quad f(100) = \left(100 \cdot \frac{9}{5}\right) + 32 = 180 + 32 = 212°F \\
\text{If } T_{C,i} &= 0°C: \quad f(0) = 0 + 32 = 32°F \\
\text{If } T_{C,i} &= -40°C: \quad f(-40) = -72 + 32 = -40°F \quad \text{(equivalence point)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: UDTF Explosion

**Problem:** A row contains an array `tags = ['hadoop', 'spark', 'hive']`. Explode it into multiple rows.

**Spark SQL Implementation:**

```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import explode
df.select(explode("tags").alias("tag")).show()
```

**Cardinality Transformation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Input: } R &= \{r_1\}, \text{ where } r_1.\text{tags} = [t_1, t_2, t_3] \\
\text{Output: } R' &= \{r_1', r_2', r_3'\} \\
\text{where } r_i'.\text{tag} &= t_i, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, 3\} \\
|R'| &= 3 = |r_1.\text{tags}|
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Function Selection Decision Tree

| **If the task is...** | **Use this function type** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Convert one column's value | UDF | Row-level transformation |
| Compute total/sum of column | UDAF | Aggregate across rows |
| Flatten an array column | UDTF | One-to-many row emission |
| Strip special characters | UDF (String) | String manipulation |
| Compute moving average | UDAF (custom) | Windowed aggregation |
| Generate date range | UDTF (custom) | Synthetic row generation |
| Conditional null replacement | UDF (built-in `coalesce`) | Scalar handling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Function Execution Flow in Hive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HiveQL Query Submitted] --> B[Parser - Tokenizes SQL]
    B --> C[Semantic Analyzer - Resolves Function Name]
    C --> D{Function Type?}
    D -- Built-in --> E[Native JVM Execution]
    D -- UDF --> F[Load JAR from HDFS]
    D -- UDAF --> G[Map-Shuffle-Reduce Phases]
    D -- UDTF --> H[Row Explosion in Reduce Phase]
    F --> I[Serialize UDF to Worker Nodes]
    I --> J[Apply Function Row-by-Row]
    E --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Collect Results to Driver]
    K --> L[Return Final Result Set]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: UDF Development Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Write Java Class extending UDF] --> B[Implement evaluate method]
    B --> C[Compile to JAR]
    C --> D[Upload JAR to HDFS]
    D --> E[ADD JAR in Hive]
    E --> F[CREATE FUNCTION statement]
    F --> G[Use in SELECT queries]
    G --> H[Function executes on Worker Nodes]
    H --> I[Results aggregated to Driver]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Function Cardinality Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Row] -->|UDF| B[Output Value]
    C[Input Rows N] -->|UDAF| D[Output Value]
    E[Input Row] -->|UDTF| F[Output Rows M]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Spark UDF Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ClientNode["Driver Node"]
        A[DataFrame df] --> B[Register UDF: spark.udf.register]
        B --> C[SQL Query with UDF]
    end
    subgraph ClusterNode["Executor Nodes"]
        D[Task 1 - Partition 1] --> E[Apply UDF to each row]
        F[Task 2 - Partition 2] --> G[Apply UDF to each row]
        H[Task N - Partition N] --> I[Apply UDF to each row]
    end
    C --> D
    C --> F
    C --> H
    E --> J[Shuffle and Aggregate]
    G --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final Result DataFrame]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Insight:** Notice how a UDF is **shipped** from Driver to all Executor nodes. This is why UDFs must be **serialisable** and **stateless** for distributed execution.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a User-Defined Function (UDF) in Hive. How does it differ from a built-in function?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **User-Defined Function (UDF)** in Hive is a custom Java function written by the developer to extend Hive's built-in capabilities, by extending the `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF` class and overriding its `evaluate()` method.

**Differences:**

| **Aspect** | **Built-in Function** | **UDF** |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Pre-shipped with Hive | Custom code by user |
| Performance | Optimised (native) | Slight overhead due to serialisation |
| Registration | Implicit (always available) | Explicit via `ADD JAR` and `CREATE FUNCTION` |
| Reusability | Limited to vendor features | Domain-specific logic |

**(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for differences table.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*With a suitable example, explain the concept of a UDTF in Hive.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **User-Defined Table-Generating Function (UDTF)** takes a **single input row** and produces **multiple output rows** (a table). It is implemented by extending the `GenericUDTF` abstract class.

**Example:**

```sql
SELECT explode(split(tags, ',')) AS tag FROM articles;
```

Here, a row containing `"hadoop,spark,hive"` (1 input row) is transformed into **3 separate rows**: `hadoop`, `spark`, `hive`.

**(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for class + 1 mark for example.)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three categories of User-Defined Functions in Hive: **UDF, UDAF, and UDTF**. Compare them with respect to input/output cardinality and provide one real-world example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Write a complete Hive UDF in Java to compute the **Body Mass Index (BMI)** of a person from their weight (kg) and height (m). Show the steps to compile, package, register, and invoke the UDF on a sample table.

---

#### **Question A — Model Solution**

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

The three categories of Hive UDFs are distinguished by their **input-output cardinality behaviour**.

**1. UDF (User-Defined Function) — Scalar Function**
- **Input Cardinality:** 1 row
- **Output Cardinality:** 1 value
- **Base Class:** `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF`
- **Real-world Example:** Converting temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit for a weather dataset.

**2. UDAF (User-Defined Aggregate Function)**
- **Input Cardinality:** N rows (a group/partition)
- **Output Cardinality:** 1 value per group
- **Base Class:** `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.udf.generic.GenericUDAFResolver2`
- **Real-world Example:** Computing the **geometric mean** of stock prices (not natively available in Hive).

**3. UDTF (User-Defined Table-Generating Function)**
- **Input Cardinality:** 1 row
- **Output Cardinality:** N rows
- **Base Class:** `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.udf.generic.GenericUDTF`
- **Real-world Example:** Parsing a comma-separated `phone_numbers` field into multiple rows.

**Mathematical Cardinality Summary:**

$$|f_{UDF}(r)| = 1, \quad |f_{UDAF}(R)| = 1, \quad |f_{UDTF}(r)| = m \geq 0$$

**(3 marks for explanation of each type + 1 mark for base classes + 2 marks for cardinality table + 1 mark for examples.)**

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mathematical Formula:**

$$BMI = \frac{w}{h^2}$$

where $w$ is weight in kg and $h$ is height in metres.

**Step 2 — Java Implementation:**

```java
package com.ktu.bmi;
import org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF;
public class BMICalculator extends UDF {
    public Double evaluate(Double weight, Double height) {
        if (weight == null || height == null || height == 0) return null;
        return weight / (height * height);
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Compilation and JAR Creation:**

$$
\text{javac BMICalculator.java} \rightarrow \text{jar cf bmi\_udf.jar com/ktu/bmi/BMICalculator.class}
$$

**Step 4 — HQL Registration:**

```sql
ADD JAR /user/hive/jars/bmi_udf.jar;
CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION calc_bmi AS 'com.ktu.bmi.BMICalculator';
```

**Step 5 — Sample Table and Invocation:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE patients (name STRING, weight DOUBLE, height DOUBLE);
SELECT name, weight, height, calc_bmi(weight, height) AS bmi FROM patients;
```

**Step 6 — Sample Numerical Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Patient A: } w=70, h=1.75 \Rightarrow BMI &= \frac{70}{1.75^2} = \frac{70}{3.0625} = 22.86 \\
\text{Patient B: } w=85, h=1.80 \Rightarrow BMI &= \frac{85}{3.24} = 26.23 \\
\text{Patient C: } w=50, h=1.60 \Rightarrow BMI &= \frac{50}{2.56} = 19.53
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks for formula + 2 marks for Java code + 1 mark for registration + 1 mark for invocation + 1 mark for numerical verification.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the architecture of function execution in a distributed query engine. How does a UDF get executed across worker nodes in Hive/Spark? Explain with a neat diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Spark SQL Built-in Functions** and **Spark UDFs**. Write a PySpark program to register a UDF that classifies a student as `"Pass"` or `"Fail"` based on marks ≥ 50.

---

#### **Question B — Model Solution**

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

The execution of a function in a distributed query engine follows a **multi-stage pipeline**:

**Stage 1 — Driver-Side Parsing & Planning:**
The query (containing the function) is parsed by the **Hive Compiler** or **Spark Catalyst Optimizer**. The function name is resolved against the function registry. If it's a UDF, the corresponding JAR must be present on the classpath.

**Stage 2 — Serialisation & Shipping:**
The UDF class is **serialised into bytecode** and shipped across the cluster to all **Executor/Worker nodes** along with the task code.

**Stage 3 — Distributed Execution:**
Each executor applies the UDF to its assigned data partition **row by row** (in case of UDF) or **group by group** (in case of UDAF).

**Stage 4 — Result Aggregation:**
The transformed rows are shuffled (if needed) and aggregated back to the driver.

**Architecture Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Driver (parse, plan)
   |
   |-- Ship UDF JAR to all executors
   |
Executor-1 (Partition-1) --> apply UDF row-by-row
Executor-2 (Partition-2) --> apply UDF row-by-row
Executor-N (Partition-N) --> apply UDF row-by-row
   |
   | (shuffle if group-by)
   v
Driver (collect & return result)
```

**(3 marks for stages + 2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for distributed logic.)**

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Built-in Function** | **Spark UDF** |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Native JVM, optimised | Custom Python/Scala/Java code |
| Performance | High (Catalyst-optimised) | Lower (serialisation overhead) |
| Registration | Implicit | `spark.udf.register("name", func)` |
| Vectorisation | Supported | Not always supported |
| Example | `upper()`, `year()` | Custom classification logic |

**PySpark Program:**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import udf
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("StudentUDF").getOrCreate()

# Sample Data
data = [("Arjun", 85), ("Meera", 42), ("Rahul", 67), ("Anu", 30)]
columns = ["name", "marks"]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, columns)

# Define Python function
def classify(marks):
    if marks is None:
        return "Unknown"
    return "Pass" if marks >= 50 else "Fail"

# Register as UDF
classify_udf = udf(classify, StringType())

# Apply UDF
df.withColumn("result", classify_udf(df["marks"])).show()
```

**Output:**

```
+-----+-----+------+
| name|marks|result|
+-----+-----+------+
|Arjun|   85|  Pass|
|Meera|   42|  Fail|
|Rahul|   67|  Pass|
|  Anu|   30|  Fail|
+-----+-----+------+
```

**(2 marks for comparison + 3 marks for PySpark code + 2 marks for output.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing UDF with UDAF** — A UDF is **row-level (1→1)**, while a UDAF is **group-level (N→1)**. Examiners specifically deduct marks if students interchange these.
> 2. **Forgetting `ADD JAR`** — Many students write the Java code but skip the JAR registration step, losing **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Null Handling** — Failing to handle `null` inputs in UDFs is a common deduction point. Always include `if (input == null) return null;`.
> 4. **Spark UDF Return Type** — Forgetting to specify `StringType()` / `IntegerType()` in PySpark UDFs leads to runtime errors and zero credit.
> 5. **Mixing up `explode()`** — `explode()` is a UDTF (1→N), not a UDF. Examiners mark this strictly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **UDF** performs **row-level (1→1)** transformations and extends the `UDF` class in Hive.
- ✅ A **UDAF** performs **aggregate (N→1)** operations and uses `GenericUDAFResolver2` in Hive.
- ✅ A **UDTF** performs **table-generating (1→N)** operations and uses `GenericUDTF` in Hive.
- ✅ **Built-in functions** are pre-optimised and preferred over UDFs for performance-critical workloads.
- ✅ In **PySpark**, UDFs are registered using `spark.udf.register()` or `@udf` decorator, with explicit return type declaration.
- ✅ UDFs must be **stateless and serialisable** for distributed execution across executors.
- ✅ The **Catalyst Optimizer** (Spark) and **Hive Planner** may perform **constant folding** and **predicate pushdown** on deterministic functions.
- ✅ Always handle `NULL` inputs in custom UDFs to avoid `NullPointerException` on edge cases.
- ✅ The **Hive GenericUDF** is preferred over the simple `UDF` class for **complex data types** (arrays, maps, structs).
- ✅ For KTU exams, remember the **cardinality notation**: $|f_{UDF}| = 1$, $|f_{UDAF}| = 1$, $|f_{UDTF}| = m$.
- ✅ The keyword `TEMPORARY` in `CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION` ensures the function is **session-scoped** and not persisted in the metastore.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spark: Storage Layers for Spark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spark: Storage Layers for Spark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage Layers in Apache Spark** refer to the multi-tiered, in-memory and on-disk persistence architecture that governs the physical placement, serialization, replication, and lifecycle management of partitioned data blocks across the cluster's compute nodes during iterative and interactive distributed computations.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Spark's storage layer is a **two-layer abstraction**:
- **Logical Layer**: The RDD/DataFrame/Dataset API (the developer's view).
- **Physical Layer**: The `BlockManager` and `MemoryStore`/`DiskStore` infrastructure (the engine's view).

The storage layer exposes tunable **Storage Levels** that control whether a partition lives in **RAM**, on **local disk**, in **serialized (byte) form**, in **off-heap memory** (Tachyon/Alluxio), or in a **replicated (factor = 2)** form for fault tolerance.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Multi-Tier Library" Model

Imagine you are a librarian managing a vast archive of books that thousands of students want to read repeatedly:

| Tier | Real-World Analogue | Spark Equivalent |
|------|--------------------|------------------|
| **T1 — The Desk** | Books kept on the librarian's personal desk. Instant access, but limited space. | `MEMORY_ONLY` (de-serialized Java objects in JVM heap) |
| **T2 — Desk + Nearby Shelf** | Desk overflow is pushed to a shelf behind the librarian. Slightly slower. | `MEMORY_AND_DISK` |
| **T3 — The Basement** | Books go to a deep archive. Slow to retrieve, but unlimited capacity. | `DISK_ONLY` |
| **T4 — Compressed Books** | Books are stored as microfilm (compact, but you need a projector to read). | `MEMORY_ONLY_SER` / `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER` |
| **T5 — Off-Site Warehouse** | A separate, JVM-independent building (Tachyon/Alluxio). | `OFF_HEAP` |
| **T6 — Photocopied Backups** | Two copies of every book exist, in case one gets lost. | `MEMORY_ONLY_2` / `MEMORY_AND_DISK_2` |

When the **desk is full**, the librarian must decide: *throw some books out* (eviction) or *push them to the shelf* (spill-to-disk). Spark does exactly this with the **Least Recently Used (LRU)** eviction policy.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Spark's Storage

1. **RDD / DataFrame Persistence API**: `persist()` and `cache()` operations.
2. **Storage Levels**: 12 pre-defined constants in `org.apache.spark.storage.StorageLevel`.
3. **BlockManager Runtime**: The cluster-wide service that physically reads/writes blocks to/from `MemoryStore` and `DiskStore`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024)**: The module explicitly tests knowledge of *storage levels*, *cache vs persist*, *Block Manager architecture*, and *unified memory management*. Master these four concepts and you cover ~80% of the expected marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `StorageLevel` Object — A Taxonomic Breakdown

The `StorageLevel` class is a **flag-based enumeration** combining four boolean attributes:

$$
\text{StorageLevel} = (\text{useDisk},\ \text{useMemory},\ \text{useOffHeap},\ \text{deserialized},\ \text{replication} = 1 \text{ or } 2)
$$

### 2.2 The Twelve Official Storage Levels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU 2024 Module-4 exam. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Storage Level | Memory | Disk | Serialized | Replicas | Best Use Case |
|---------------|:------:|:----:|:----------:|:--------:|---------------|
| `MEMORY_ONLY` | Yes | No | No, Java objects | 1 | Default; small datasets that fit in RAM; fastest access |
| `MEMORY_AND_DISK` | Yes | Yes | No, Java objects | 1 | Large iterative jobs (ML); RAM may overflow |
| `MEMORY_ONLY_SER` | Yes | No | Yes, compact bytes | 1 | Memory-constrained; ~2–5× smaller; CPU-bound |
| `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER` | Yes | Yes | Yes, compact bytes | 1 | Like `MEMORY_AND_DISK` but memory-efficient |
| `DISK_ONLY` | No | Yes | Yes | 1 | Very large datasets; recompute is cheap |
| `MEMORY_ONLY_2` | Yes | No | No | 2 | Critical data; fast recompute via lineage |
| `MEMORY_AND_DISK_2` | Yes | Yes | No | 2 | Critical data; tolerant of RAM pressure |
| `MEMORY_ONLY_SER_2` | Yes | No | Yes | 2 | Critical + memory-efficient |
| `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER_2` | Yes | Yes | Yes | 2 | Most resilient configuration |
| `DISK_ONLY_2` | No | Yes | Yes | 2 | Critical + very large |
| `OFF_HEAP` | Yes (off) | No | Yes | 1 | Decouples from GC; uses Tachyon/Alluxio |
| `OFF_HEAP_2` | Yes (off) | No | Yes | 2 | Off-heap + replicated |

### 2.3 `cache()` vs `persist()` — The Distinction

| Aspect | `cache()` | `persist()` |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Signature | `def cache(): this.type` | `def persist(): this.type` / `persist(level: StorageLevel)` |
| Default Level | `MEMORY_ONLY` | `MEMORY_ONLY` (if no argument given) |
| Flexibility | Hard-coded | Accepts any `StorageLevel` |
| Laziness | Lazy (triggered by an action) | Lazy (triggered by an action) |
| Source Code | `cache = persist()` | Parent call |

> [!NOTE]
> **At the bytecode level**, `cache()` is literally a one-liner: `def cache(): this.type = persist()`. There is **zero** runtime difference between `cache()` and `persist(MEMORY_ONLY)`.

### 2.4 The Block Manager — The Heart of Storage

The **`BlockManager`** is a per-executor (and per-driver) service that performs the actual I/O. Its responsibilities are:

1. **Block registration** with the central `BlockManagerMaster` (on the driver).
2. **Memory management** via an in-memory `LinkedHashMap[String, MemoryEntry]`.
3. **Disk I/O** via `DiskStore` (one file per block, named by SHA-1 hash).
4. **Replication** to a peer executor's `BlockManager`.
5. **Shuffle integration** — shuffle data is stored as a special block type.
6. **Broadcast integration** — broadcast variables are also blocks.
7. **Tachyon/Alluxio integration** for `OFF_HEAP` levels.

### 2.5 Unified Memory Management (Spark 1.6+)

Before Spark 1.6, the heap was rigidly split into **Static Memory Management** (caches and shuffles had fixed boundaries). Since Spark 1.6, **Unified Memory Management** allows dynamic borrowing:

$$
\text{Total JVM Heap} = \text{Reserved Memory (300 MB)} + \text{Usable Memory } U
$$

$$
U = U_{\text{storage}} + U_{\text{execution}}
$$

Where the **soft boundary** is governed by `spark.memory.fraction` (default **0.6**) and the **storage fraction** `spark.memory.storageFraction` (default **0.5**).

$$
U_{\text{storage, max}} = U \times \text{storageFraction} = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.3 \text{ of usable memory}
$$

$$
U_{\text{execution, max}} = U \times (1 - \text{storageFraction}) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.3
$$

The remaining **0.4** of usable memory is **unaccounted** and acts as a buffer for both regions.

### 2.6 Eviction Policy — LRU

When a region's soft limit is exceeded, blocks are evicted in **LRU order** until the region falls below the initial threshold (`spark.memory.storageFraction`). If a block is still referenced by an iterator, it is **moved to disk** (for `MEMORY_AND_DISK` levels) or **deleted** (for `MEMORY_ONLY`).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value |
|--------|---------|-----------------|
| $M_{\text{reserved}}$ | Hard-reserved memory for Spark internals | **300 MB** (constant) |
| $f_{\text{use}}$ | Fraction of heap that is *usable* | `spark.memory.fraction` (default **0.6**) |
| $f_{\text{store}}$ | Initial storage fraction of usable memory | `spark.memory.storageFraction` (default **0.5**) |
| $M_{\text{usable}}$ | Usable memory for storage + execution | $(H - M_{\text{reserved}}) \times f_{\text{use}}$ |
| $M_{\text{storage,max}}$ | Maximum storage memory | $M_{\text{usable}} \times f_{\text{store}}$ |
| $M_{\text{exec,max}}$ | Maximum execution memory | $M_{\text{usable}} \times (1 - f_{\text{store}})$ |
| $R_{\text{partitions}}$ | Number of RDD partitions | $\lceil N / \text{spark.sql.shuffle.partitions} \rceil$ |
| $S_{\text{ser}}$ | Size after serialization | $S_{\text{raw}} \times k_{\text{ser}}$, $k_{\text{ser}} \in [0.2,\ 0.5]$ |
| $\rho_{\text{cache}}$ | Cache hit rate | $\text{Hits} / (\text{Hits} + \text{Misses})$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: In production Spark clusters, choosing the wrong storage level is the #1 cause of Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors. Data engineers at companies like Netflix and Uber routinely use `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER` with Kryo serialization to process petabyte-scale logs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Memory Budget for a Cached DataFrame

**Problem Statement**: A cluster has 10 worker nodes, each with **16 GB JVM heap**. The team wants to cache a DataFrame of **80 GB raw size** using `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER`. Will it fit, and what will be the per-node memory footprint?

**Step 1 — Compute reserved memory per executor**

$$
M_{\text{reserved}} = 300 \text{ MB (Spark constant)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute usable memory per executor**

$$
M_{\text{usable}} = (H - M_{\text{reserved}}) \times f_{\text{use}}
$$

$$
M_{\text{usable}} = (16384 \text{ MB} - 300 \text{ MB}) \times 0.6
$$

$$
M_{\text{usable}} = 16084 \times 0.6 = 9650.4 \text{ MB} \approx 9.42 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum storage memory per executor**

$$
M_{\text{storage,max}} = M_{\text{usable}} \times f_{\text{store}} = 9650.4 \times 0.5 = 4825.2 \text{ MB} \approx 4.71 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for serialization compression**

Assume Kryo gives a compression ratio of $k_{\text{ser}} = 0.3$ (i.e., serialized data is 30% of raw size):

$$
S_{\text{ser}} = S_{\text{raw}} \times k_{\text{ser}} = 80 \text{ GB} \times 0.3 = 24 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 5 — Per-node memory load (assuming uniform distribution)**

$$
M_{\text{per-node}} = \frac{S_{\text{ser}}}{N} = \frac{24 \text{ GB}}{10} = 2.4 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare against the per-node storage budget**

$$
M_{\text{per-node}} = 2.4 \text{ GB} \quad \text{vs.} \quad M_{\text{storage,max}} = 4.71 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
2.4 \text{ GB} < 4.71 \text{ GB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{The DataFrame FITS in memory.}
$$

**Step 7 — Free headroom (safety margin)**

$$
\Delta = M_{\text{storage,max}} - M_{\text{per-node}} = 4.71 - 2.4 = 2.31 \text{ GB}
$$

**Conclusion**: The configuration is **safe**, with a ~49% safety margin. If the team had chosen `MEMORY_ONLY` (no serialization), the per-node load would be $80/10 = 8 \text{ GB}$, which **exceeds** the 4.71 GB budget, triggering spill-to-disk or eviction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Storage-Level Decision Tree (Boolean Logic)

Let $S$ = dataset size, $M$ = available memory per executor, $I$ = number of iterations, $C$ = recompute cost.

**Rule 1 — MEMORY_ONLY**

$$
S \leq 0.6 \times M \quad \text{AND} \quad I \geq 2
$$

**Rule 2 — MEMORY_AND_DISK**

$$
0.6 \times M < S \leq 2 \times M \quad \text{AND} \quad I \geq 2
$$

**Rule 3 — MEMORY_ONLY_SER**

$$
S \leq 0.6 \times M \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{objects are large/structured}
$$

**Rule 4 — DISK_ONLY**

$$
S > 2 \times M \quad \text{AND} \quad C \text{ is low}
$$

**Rule 5 — OFF_HEAP**

$$
\text{GC pressure} > \text{threshold} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Tachyon cluster is available}
$$

**Rule 6 — `_2` variants**

$$
\text{Recompute cost } C \text{ is HIGH} \quad \text{(e.g.,} \ \text{lineage length} > 10)
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Trade-off Quadrant

| | **Low Recompute Cost (Cheap Lineage)** | **High Recompute Cost (Expensive Lineage)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Small Data (fits in RAM)** | `MEMORY_ONLY` (fast + cheap) | `MEMORY_ONLY_2` (replicated, fault-tolerant) |
| **Large Data (overflows RAM)** | `DISK_ONLY` (don't waste RAM) | `MEMORY_AND_DISK_2` (best of both worlds) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **quadrant rule of thumb**: *replication protects expensive lineage; disk protects memory from large data*. The two axes are nearly orthogonal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Spark Storage Layer — Top-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user["User Application"] --> api["RDD DataFrame Dataset API"]
    api --> persist["persist or cache call"]
    persist --> rdd["RDD.iterator"]
    rdd --> bm["BlockManager.get"]
    bm --> master["BlockManagerMaster on Driver"]
    bm --> memstore["MemoryStore JVM Heap"]
    bm --> diskstore["DiskStore Local FS"]
    bm --> offheap["OffHeapStore Tachyon Alluxio"]
    memstore --> evict["LRU Eviction"]
    diskstore --> evict
    evict --> fallback["Spill to Disk or Drop"]
    offheap --> net["RDMA Network"]
```

### 4.2 BlockManager Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph exec["Executor JVM"]
        bm["BlockManager"]
        bm --> mem["MemoryStore LinkedHashMap"]
        bm --> disk["DiskStore File per Block"]
        bm --> shuf["ShuffleClient"]
        bm --> bcast["BroadcastManager"]
        bm --> sermgr["SerializerManager Kryo Java"]
    end
    bm -.reports to.-> master["BlockManagerMaster Driver"]
    master -.heartbeat.-> bm
    peer["Peer Executor BlockManager"] -.replication.-> bm
    bm -.replication.-> peer
```

### 4.3 Unified Memory Management Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    heap["Total JVM Heap H"] --> res["Reserved Memory 300 MB Fixed"]
    heap --> usable["Usable Memory U = H minus 300 MB times 0.6"]
    usable --> store["Storage Memory 50 percent soft bound"]
    usable --> exec["Execution Memory 50 percent soft bound"]
    store -.borrow.-> exec
    exec -.evict.-> store
    store --> cache["Cached RDD Blocks"]
    store --> broad["Broadcast Variables"]
    exec --> shuf["Shuffle Write Buffers"]
    exec --> join["Join Aggregation Buffers"]
```

### 4.4 Storage Level Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Choose Storage Level"] --> B{"Data fits in RAM"}
    B -- Yes --> C{"Replicated fault tolerance needed"}
    B -- No --> D{"Recompute is expensive"}
    C -- Yes --> E["MEMORY_ONLY_2"]
    C -- No --> F{"Objects are large"}
    F -- Yes --> G["MEMORY_ONLY_SER"]
    F -- No --> H["MEMORY_ONLY default"]
    D -- Yes --> I{"Serialized"}
    D -- No --> J["DISK_ONLY"]
    I -- Yes --> K["MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER_2"]
    I -- No --> L["MEMORY_AND_DISK_2"]
```

### 4.5 Cache vs Persist — Lifecycle Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Code
    participant R as RDD
    participant S as Scheduler
    participant B as BlockManager
    participant M as MemoryStore
    participant D as DiskStore
    U->>R: rdd.persist MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER
    Note over R: Marked as persistent in lineage
    U->>R: rdd.count
    R->>S: Submit job
    S->>B: runTask stage 0
    B->>M: putBytes serialized block
    M-->>B: success
    U->>R: rdd.count again
    R->>B: get existing block
    B-->>R: cached block served
    Note over U: Second action avoids recomputation
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing `cache()` with `MEMORY_ONLY` in code samples — they are *equivalent*, not "cache is faster"; (2) writing `MEMORY_AND_DISK` when the question explicitly asked for the *serialized* variant; (3) forgetting the `_2` suffix when asked about *fault tolerance*; and (4) describing Static Memory Management (pre-1.6) when the syllabus says **Unified Memory Management**. Always state the Spark version (≥ 1.6) when answering memory questions.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define the term "Storage Level" in Apache Spark. List any four Storage Levels with their memory and disk usage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Storage Level* in Spark is a flag-based identifier that tells the `BlockManager` *where* (RAM, disk, off-heap) and *how* (serialized, de-serialized, replicated) to physically store RDD/DataFrame partitions.

| # | Storage Level | Memory | Disk |
|---|---------------|:------:|:----:|
| 1 | `MEMORY_ONLY` | Yes | No |
| 2 | `MEMORY_AND_DISK` | Yes | Yes |
| 3 | `MEMORY_ONLY_SER` | Yes (serialized) | No |
| 4 | `DISK_ONLY` | No | Yes |

**(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the table.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between `cache()` and `persist()` in Spark.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | `cache()` | `persist()` |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Default storage level | `MEMORY_ONLY` | `MEMORY_ONLY` (or user-specified) |
| Customization | Not customizable | Accepts any `StorageLevel` argument |
| Implementation | Internally calls `persist()` | The actual persistence primitive |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Lazy? | Yes | Yes |

**(3 marks for the table.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a)** *Explain the various storage levels available in Apache Spark with their trade-offs. Discuss how the choice of storage level impacts performance. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Spark offers 12 `StorageLevel` constants, controlling the physical placement (memory, disk, off-heap), format (serialized, de-serialized), and replication factor (1 or 2) of partitions.

**2. Classification of Storage Levels (4 marks):**

- **Memory-Only Family**:
  - `MEMORY_ONLY`: De-serialized Java objects in JVM heap. **Fastest** access (no deserialization), but **largest** memory footprint. Suitable when the dataset comfortably fits in RAM (e.g., 60% of `spark.memory.fraction`).
  - `MEMORY_ONLY_SER`: Serialized (Kryo/Java) bytes in RAM. ~2–5× smaller, but **CPU overhead** for deserialization on every read.

- **Memory + Disk Family**:
  - `MEMORY_AND_DISK`: Stores partitions in RAM; spills overflow to local disk. Avoids recomputation but incurs **disk I/O** for spilled blocks.
  - `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER`: Same, but with serialization, reducing RAM pressure at the cost of CPU.

- **Disk-Only Family**:
  - `DISK_ONLY`: All blocks on local disk. Used for **very large** datasets where recompute is cheap (shallow lineage).

- **Off-Heap Family**:
  - `OFF_HEAP`: Stores blocks in Tachyon/Alluxio, **outside** the JVM heap. Avoids **GC pauses**, ideal for low-latency serving.

- **Replicated Family (`_2` variants)**:
  - All of the above with **replication factor 2**. Tolerates executor loss without recomputation.

**3. Performance Impact (2 marks):**

| Choice | Speed | Memory Cost | Fault Tolerance |
|--------|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| `MEMORY_ONLY` | ★★★★★ | High | Low |
| `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER` | ★★★★ | Medium | Medium |
| `DISK_ONLY` | ★★ | None | Low |
| `MEMORY_ONLY_2` | ★★★★★ | High | High |

---

**Q.A (b)** *With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of the `BlockManager` in Spark. Explain its role in shuffle and broadcast operations. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. BlockManager Overview (2 marks):**
The `BlockManager` is a per-JVM service running on **every executor and the driver**. It translates logical RDD partitions (identified by `(rddId, partitionId)`) into physical storage blocks. It coordinates with a single driver-side `BlockManagerMaster` that maintains a global view of block locations for **task scheduling** and **data locality**.

**2. Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph node["Executor Node"]
        bm["BlockManager"]
        bm --> mem["MemoryStore"]
        bm --> disk["DiskStore"]
        bm --> shuf["ShuffleClient"]
        bm --> bcast["BroadcastManager"]
        bm --> ser["SerializerManager"]
    end
    master["BlockManagerMaster on Driver"] -.block location registry.-> bm
    bm -.heartbeat.-> master
    peer["Peer Executor BlockManager"] -.block replication.-> bm
```

**3. Role in Shuffle (1 mark):**
During a `reduceByKey` or `groupByKey`, the **map side** writes shuffle blocks to `DiskStore` (or `MemoryStore` if small). The **reduce side** fetches them via the **ShuffleClient** (Netty-based), which queries the `BlockManagerMaster` for block locations.

**4. Role in Broadcast (1 mark):**
Broadcast variables are stored as **torrent blocks** distributed via a BitTorrent-like protocol. The `BlockManager` divides the broadcast payload into chunks, and executors pull chunks from multiple peers, reducing single-node load.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B (a)** *Explain Spark's Unified Memory Management in detail with a neat diagram. How does it differ from the legacy Static Memory Management? (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Motivation (1 mark):**
Pre-1.6, Spark used **Static Memory Management**, which rigidly reserved fixed fractions for storage and execution. This caused OOM errors when, e.g., a shuffle unexpectedly grew larger than its 20% allotment.

**2. Unified Memory Manager Architecture (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    heap["Total JVM Heap H"] --> res["Reserved Memory 300 MB"]
    heap --> usable["Usable Memory U = H minus 300 times 0.6"]
    usable --> store["Storage Memory Soft Bound 0.5"]
    usable --> exec["Execution Memory Soft Bound 0.5"]
    store -.borrow via eviction.-> exec
    exec -.evict blocks to disk.-> store
    store --> cache["Cached RDD Blocks"]
    store --> broad["Broadcast Variables"]
    exec --> shuf["Shuffle Join Aggregation Buffers"]
```

- **Reserved Memory (300 MB)**: Hard-coded for Spark's internal objects and `Storage`/`Execution` accounting structures.
- **Usable Memory (U)**: 60% of `(H − 300 MB)` by default.
- **Storage Region**: Holds cached RDD partitions and broadcast variables.
- **Execution Region**: Holds shuffle, join, sort, and aggregation buffers.
- **Soft Boundary**: At `spark.memory.storageFraction = 0.5`, both regions can grow into each other up to 100% of `U`.

**3. Borrowing Rules (1 mark):**
- Execution may *evict* cached blocks (to disk if `MEMORY_AND_DISK`, else drop) until storage falls below the initial threshold.
- Storage may *ask* execution to spill buffers, but execution has higher priority during active tasks.

**4. Comparison with Static Memory Management (1 mark):**

| Feature | Static (pre-1.6) | Unified (1.6+) |
|---------|------------------|----------------|
| Boundaries | Hard | Soft |
| Borrowing | None | Bidirectional (with eviction) |
| OOM Risk | High | Low |
| Configuration | Many fractions | Two: `memory.fraction`, `storageFraction` |

---

**Q.B (b)** *Discuss the persistence options in Spark. When would you choose `MEMORY_ONLY` over `MEMORY_AND_DISK`, and vice versa? Provide a real-world example for each. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Two Persistence Options Compared (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `MEMORY_ONLY` | `MEMORY_AND_DISK` |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Where stored | JVM heap only | JVM heap, spill to local disk |
| Speed | ★★★★★ (de-serialized) | ★★★★ (in-RAM) / ★★ (on disk) |
| Recomputation risk | High (eviction → recompute) | Low (disk fallback) |
| Suitable for | Small, hot datasets | Large, iterative datasets |

**2. When to Choose `MEMORY_ONLY` (2 marks):**
- **Real-World Example**: An ML pipeline that loads a **5 GB feature matrix** used 50 times during hyper-parameter tuning. The matrix fits comfortably in 16 GB executor RAM. Using `MEMORY_ONLY` yields 50× speedup vs. recomputing from CSV each iteration.
- **Rule**: `dataset_size ≤ 0.6 × usable_memory`.

**3. When to Choose `MEMORY_AND_DISK` (2 marks):**
- **Real-World Example**: A **graph-processing job** (`GraphX`) that needs to cache a 100 GB adjacency list. RAM is insufficient, but re-deriving the graph from HDFS would cost 20 minutes. `MEMORY_AND_DISK` keeps the active hot edges in RAM and the cold edges on local SSD — net job time: 5 minutes instead of 20.
- **Rule**: `dataset_size > 0.6 × usable_memory`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Storage Level = (useDisk, useMemory, useOffHeap, deserialized, replication)**.
- **12 official levels**: `MEMORY_ONLY`, `MEMORY_AND_DISK`, `MEMORY_ONLY_SER`, `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER`, `DISK_ONLY`, `OFF_HEAP` + their `_2` variants.
- **`cache()` ≡ `persist(MEMORY_ONLY)`** — no runtime difference.
- **`persist()`** accepts a custom `StorageLevel` argument.
- **BlockManager** runs on every executor + driver; coordinates with **BlockManagerMaster** for block location registry.
- **MemoryStore** uses a `LinkedHashMap` → enables LRU eviction.
- **DiskStore** stores each block as a separate file named by SHA-1 hash.
- **Unified Memory** (≥ 1.6): Reserved (300 MB) + Usable (60% of rest); Storage and Execution share a **soft 50/50** boundary.
- **Eviction policy**: LRU; blocks may spill to disk (`MEMORY_AND_DISK`) or be dropped (`MEMORY_ONLY`).
- **Default level** if you call `persist()` with no argument: `MEMORY_ONLY`.
- **Serialization** with Kryo gives 2–5× memory savings at the cost of CPU.
- **Replication (`_2`)** protects against executor loss without re-running the lineage.
- **Off-heap (Tachyon/Alluxio)** decouples storage from GC, enabling sub-millisecond access.
- **Memory budget formula**:
$M_{\text{storage,max}} = (H - 300) \times 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.3(H - 300)$.
- **Rule of thumb**: if data fits in RAM, use `MEMORY_ONLY`; if it overflows, use `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER`; if recompute is cheap and data is huge, use `DISK_ONLY`.
- **Pitfall to avoid**: Don't confuse *cache hit* with *cache miss* — unpersisted RDDs are silently recomputed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Core Spark Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Core Spark Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Spark** is a unified, open-source, distributed **in-memory cluster-computing framework** designed for fast, large-scale data processing. It extends the **MapReduce** model with an additional **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** execution engine that supports **in-memory data sharing** across iterative and interactive computations.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBCDT504 – Big Data Processing)**, Core Spark Concepts cover the fundamental building blocks of the Spark execution model:

| Core Concept | Formal Definition |
|--------------|-------------------|
| **SparkContext** | The entry point of any Spark application; connects to the cluster manager and coordinates job execution. |
| **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** | A fault-tolerant, immutable, distributed collection of objects that can be processed in parallel. |
| **DataFrame** | A distributed collection of data organized into named columns (similar to a relational table). |
| **Dataset API** | A type-safe, object-oriented programming interface that combines RDD features with Spark SQL optimizations. |
| **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** | The execution plan representing the sequence of transformations applied to RDDs. |
| **Lazy Evaluation** | A strategy where transformations are not executed immediately; instead, a lineage graph is built and only executed when an *action* is triggered. |
| **Cluster Manager** | An external service for acquiring resources (Standalone, YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes). |
| **Driver & Executors** | The Driver coordinates the application, while Executors run tasks on worker nodes. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Spark as a "Master Chef's Industrial Kitchen."**

Imagine you run a massive restaurant chain that must prepare a million meals per day:

- The **Driver** is the **Head Chef** who reads the recipe book (your code) and plans the cooking order.
- The **Cluster Manager** is the **Kitchen Manager** who assigns prep stations and ovens to each chef.
- The **Executors** are the **Line Cooks** stationed across multiple kitchens. Each cook can chop, stir, and bake in parallel.
- The **RDD** is the **Tray of Ingredients** — a sealed, labeled box that can be split into mini-trays and sent to any cook. The tray cannot be modified; if a cook needs a variation, they must create a *new* tray.
- The **DAG** is the **Recipe Flowchart** drawn on the wall — showing the exact sequence: *chop → marinate → grill → plate*. Spark only starts cooking (executing) when the Head Chef shouts "**SERVE!**" (i.e., calls an **Action**).
- **Lazy Evaluation** is exactly this: every prep step is *planned* but not *done* until the final order is placed.

## 1.3 Why Spark Over Hadoop MapReduce?

| Parameter | Hadoop MapReduce | Apache Spark |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| Execution | Disk-based (HDFS read/write) | **In-memory** computation |
| Speed | Slower (up to 100× slower) | **Up to 100× faster** for in-memory, 10× on disk |
| Iterative Jobs | Poor performance | Excellent (**MLlib** support) |
| Ease of Use | Java only (low-level) | **Scala, Python, R, Java** |
| Cost | Cheaper for batch | Higher RAM cost but flexible |
| Real-time | Batch only | **Batch + Near Real-time (Streaming)** |

> [!TIP]
> KTU students must remember: **Spark is not a replacement for Hadoop** — it is an *enhancement* that runs *on top* of Hadoop (using HDFS for storage and YARN for resource management).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spark Architecture — Operational Breakdown

The Spark architecture operates in a **Master–Slave paradigm** with the following layered components:

### 2.1.1 The Driver Program (Master)
- Runs the `main()` function of the Spark application.
- Creates the **SparkContext** object.
- Converts user code into a **DAG** of stages and tasks.
- Schedules tasks onto executors via the **Cluster Manager**.
- Maintains metadata about all **RDDs** and their **lineage**.

### 2.1.2 The Cluster Manager
- Allocates physical resources (CPU cores, memory) across applications.
- **Types supported by Spark:**
  * **Standalone Cluster Manager** (Spark's own simple scheduler)
  * **Apache YARN** (Hadoop's resource negotiator)
  * **Apache Mesos**
  * **Kubernetes** (container orchestration)

### 2.1.3 Executors (Slaves / Workers)
- Run on **Worker Nodes**.
- Execute individual **tasks** on data partitions.
- Store computed data in **memory or disk** (caching).
- Return results to the Driver.

### 2.1.4 Job, Stage, Task Hierarchy

| Unit | Definition | Created By |
|------|------------|------------|
| **Job** | A unit of work triggered by a single **Action** | Action call |
| **Stage** | A set of tasks that can be executed in parallel without shuffling | DAGScheduler |
| **Task** | The smallest unit of work — runs on one executor for one partition | TaskScheduler |

## 2.2 RDDs (Resilient Distributed Datasets) — Core Abstraction

### 2.2.1 The Five Properties of RDD (Original Paper Definition)

1. **Partitions** — The dataset is divided into logical chunks called *partitions*.
2. **Dependencies** — Each RDD tracks its lineage (dependencies on parent RDDs).
3. **Compute Function** — A function to compute each partition.
4. **Partitioner (Optional)** — Defines how data is partitioned across nodes (e.g., HashPartitioner).
5. **Preferred Locations (Optional)** — Hints about where each partition's data resides (data locality).

### 2.2.2 RDD Operations — Two Categories

| Type | Examples | Execution |
|------|----------|-----------|
| **Transformations** (Lazy) | `map()`, `filter()`, `flatMap()`, `union()`, `groupByKey()`, `reduceByKey()`, `join()` | Builds DAG, no immediate execution |
| **Actions** (Eager) | `collect()`, `count()`, `first()`, `take(n)`, `reduce()`, `saveAsTextFile()` | Triggers job execution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Narrow vs Wide Dependencies** are the key to Spark's performance:
> - **Narrow Dependency:** Each partition of parent RDD is used by **at most one** partition of child RDD (e.g., `map`, `filter`). **No shuffle** required.
> - **Wide Dependency:** Each partition of parent RDD may be used by **multiple** partitions of child RDD (e.g., `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`). **Shuffle** required across the network.

## 2.3 Lazy Evaluation & DAG Construction — The "Why" and "How"

### Why Lazy Evaluation?
- **Optimization opportunity:** Spark's catalyst optimizer can **fuse multiple transformations** into a single stage.
- **Efficiency:** Avoids materializing intermediate results to disk.
- **Fault tolerance:** Lineage is preserved, so lost partitions can be recomputed from parent RDDs.

### How Does DAG Construction Work?
1. User submits a chain of **transformations** → Spark builds a **logical DAG**.
2. **DAGScheduler** splits the DAG into **stages** at shuffle boundaries.
3. **TaskScheduler** launches **tasks** per stage on executors.
4. When an **action** is called, the DAG is **materialized**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| $N_p$ (Number of Partitions) | $N_p = \max(N_{\text{cores}} \cdot 2, N_{\text{blocks}})$ | Determined at RDD creation |
| Stage Count | $N_{\text{stages}} = N_{\text{shuffle\_boundaries}} + 1$ | Logical execution units |
| Task Count per Stage | $N_{\text{tasks}} = N_p$ | One task per partition |
| In-Memory Speedup | $S_{\text{spark}} = \frac{T_{\text{MR}}}{T_{\text{spark}}} \leq 100\times$ | Compared to Hadoop MR |
| Cost of Recomputation | $C_{\text{recomp}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i$ | Sum of time to recompute lineage |
| Hash Partitioning | $p(x) = x \mod P$ | $P$ = number of partitions |
| Data Locality Level | $L \in \{PROCESS\_LOCAL, NODE\_LOCAL, RACK\_LOCAL, ANY\}$ | ANY is the slowest |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Transformations** (lazy) with **Actions** (eager). Only an **action** triggers a Spark **Job**!

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Spark Application |
|--------|------------------|
| **Finance** | Real-time fraud detection, risk modeling |
| **Healthcare** | Genomics processing (DNA sequencing) |
| **Retail (e.g., Walmart, Amazon)** | Recommendation engines, clickstream analysis |
| **Telecommunications** | Network log analysis, churn prediction |
| **IoT & Smart Cities** | Sensor data aggregation and anomaly detection |
| **Scientific Research** | Climate modeling, particle physics (CERN uses Spark) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: How a Spark Job Gets Scheduled

We trace every step from **user code submission** to **task execution on a worker node**.

### Step 1: User Submits a Spark Application

The user writes a program (e.g., in PySpark or Scala) and submits it via `spark-submit`. This launches the **Driver Program** in a JVM.

$$
\text{Driver} \rightarrow \text{Creates SparkContext} \rightarrow \text{Connects to Cluster Manager}
$$

### Step 2: SparkContext Allocates Executors

The Driver asks the Cluster Manager for resources. Suppose the cluster has $N_{\text{workers}}$ worker nodes, each with $c$ CPU cores and $m$ GB of RAM.

$$
\text{Total Executors Allocated} = N_{\text{workers}} \quad \text{(one executor per worker by default)}
$$

$$
\text{Total Cores} = N_{\text{workers}} \cdot c
$$

### Step 3: User Defines RDDs via Transformations

Suppose the user writes:

```python
rdd1 = sc.textFile("hdfs://data/log.txt")  # creates RDD with 4 partitions
rdd2 = rdd1.filter(lambda x: "error" in x)  # narrow transformation
rdd3 = rdd2.map(lambda x: (x.split(",")[0], 1))  # narrow transformation
rdd4 = rdd3.reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)  # WIDE transformation (shuffle!)
```

**Lineage Graph (DAG) construction:**

$$
RDD_1 \xrightarrow{\text{filter}} RDD_2 \xrightarrow{\text{map}} RDD_3 \xrightarrow{\text{reduceByKey}} RDD_4
$$

### Step 4: DAGScheduler Splits the DAG into Stages

Spark identifies the **shuffle boundary** caused by `reduceByKey`. The DAG is split into:

$$
\text{Stage 0} = \{RDD_1 \rightarrow RDD_2 \rightarrow RDD_3\} \quad \text{(no shuffle, fused pipeline)}
$$

$$
\text{Stage 1} = \{RDD_3 \rightarrow RDD_4\} \quad \text{(post-shuffle, requires data exchange)}
$$

$$
\therefore N_{\text{stages}} = 2
$$

### Step 5: TaskScheduler Launches Tasks

If each stage has $N_p = 4$ partitions:

$$
N_{\text{tasks per stage}} = N_p = 4
$$

$$
\text{Total tasks in job} = N_{\text{stages}} \cdot N_p = 2 \cdot 4 = 8
$$

### Step 6: Action Triggers Execution

The user finally calls:

```python
result = rdd4.collect()  # ACTION!
```

This triggers Spark to execute the **entire DAG**:

$$
\text{Action Trigger} \rightarrow \text{Job submitted} \rightarrow \text{Stages executed serially} \rightarrow \text{Tasks run in parallel}
$$

### Step 7: Fault Tolerance via Lineage

Suppose **Task 3 of Stage 1 fails** (executor crash). Spark uses the **lineage** to recompute:

$$
RDD_4[\text{partition 3}] \Leftarrow \text{Recompute from } RDD_3[\text{partition 3}] \Leftarrow \text{Recompute from } RDD_1[\text{partition 3}]
$$

**Recomputation Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{recomp}} = t_{\text{filter}} + t_{\text{map}} + t_{\text{reduceByKey}}
$$

where each $t$ is the time to recompute one partition.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of RDD Operations

| Operation | Type | Dependency | Shuffle? | Lazy? | Result RDD Size |
|-----------|------|------------|----------|-------|-----------------|
| `map(f)` | Transformation | Narrow | ❌ | ✅ | Same as parent |
| `filter(f)` | Transformation | Narrow | ❌ | ✅ | $\leq$ parent size |
| `flatMap(f)` | Transformation | Narrow | ❌ | ✅ | Variable |
| `union(rdd)` | Transformation | Narrow | ❌ | ✅ | Sum of parents |
| `groupByKey()` | Transformation | **Wide** | ✅ | ✅ | Same as parent |
| `reduceByKey(f)` | Transformation | **Wide** | ✅ | ✅ | $\leq$ parent (aggregated) |
| `join(rdd)` | Transformation | **Wide** | ✅ | ✅ | Cartesian-like |
| `collect()` | Action | — | — | — | Returns to driver |
| `count()` | Action | — | — | — | Returns single number |
| `saveAsTextFile(path)` | Action | — | — | — | Writes to disk |

## 3.3 Mathematical Optimization: Choosing the Right Operation

For large-scale key-value aggregation, the choice between `groupByKey` and `reduceByKey` has **massive performance implications**:

### `groupByKey` (Inefficient)
1. Shuffles **all key-value pairs** across the network.
2. Performs aggregation **only at the reducer side**.
3. Data transfer cost: $O(N)$ where $N$ = total records.

### `reduceByKey` (Efficient — Recommended)
1. Performs **local aggregation (combiner)** at each mapper first.
2. Then shuffles **already-reduced** data.
3. Data transfer cost: $O(K)$ where $K$ = unique keys (usually $K \ll N$).

$$
\therefore \text{Data Shuffled}_{\text{reduceByKey}} \ll \text{Data Shuffled}_{\text{groupByKey}}
$$

**KTU Tip:** Always prefer `reduceByKey`, `aggregateByKey`, or `combineByKey` over `groupByKey` to avoid memory overflow.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spark Cluster Architecture (Master–Worker)

