topics.title: Projects using R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Projects using R # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **R for Big Data Processing** refers to the use of the R programming language — an open-source, interpreted, functional, and object-oriented statistical computing environment — to execute end-to-end Big Data projects encompassing data ingestion, transformation, modeling, visualization, and reporting on datasets that exceed the memory capacity of a single machine or demand parallel/distributed computation. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCDT504)**, "Projects using R" under Module 4 emphasizes building **domain-specific, reproducible analytical pipelines** using R as the orchestration and analytics layer, frequently integrated with distributed backends such as **Hadoop**, **Spark**, or **NoSQL** stores. The R ecosystem relevant to Big Data projects includes: | Layer | Representative Packages | |---|---| | Data Ingestion | `readr`, `data.table::fread`, `DBI`, `odbc`, `jsonlite`, `xml2` | | Wrangling & Transformation | `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `data.table`, `stringr`, `lubridate` | | Distributed Computing | `sparklyr`, `SparkR`, `RHadoop` (`rmr2`, `rhdfs`), `parallel`, `future` | | Machine Learning | `caret`, `mlr3`, `xgboost`, `randomForest`, `glmnet` | | Visualization | `ggplot2`, `plotly`, `shiny`, `DT` | | Reporting | `rmarkdown`, `knitr` | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Master Chef in a Mega-Kitchen** > Imagine a single master chef (R) is asked to cook for **10,000 guests** simultaneously. A traditional chef works with one stove and one pan. R, by itself, is that single chef. But when connected to a **mega-kitchen** (Hadoop, Spark) with hundreds of assistants (compute nodes), the chef (R) writes the **recipe book** (R script) and **delegates** the chopping, boiling, and frying to the kitchen staff — then assembles and plates the final dish (visualization/report). The R script is the *logic*, and the Big Data framework is the *muscle*. In simple terms: - **R** = the analyst's brain that decides *what* to compute and *how* to interpret results. - **Hadoop/Spark/NoSQL** = the storage + parallel engine that handles *how much* and *how fast*. - **R Project** = the reproducible workflow that ties ingestion → cleaning → modeling → visualization → reporting. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric — In-Memory Threshold**: A single R process typically addresses **2 GB – 256 GB** of RAM depending on the OS (32-bit vs 64-bit). Beyond this, the project *must* integrate with a distributed backend. ## 1.3 Why "Projects using R" is a KTU High-Yield Topic Under the KTU 2024 outcome-based framework, this topic maps to: - **CO4**: Design and implement Big Data analytics solutions using appropriate tools. - **CO5**: Evaluate the performance and applicability of Big Data tools in real-world domains. > [!TIP] > Examiners love R-project questions because they test *applied knowledge* — the student must demonstrate end-to-end thinking, not just memorization of syntax. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of an R-based Big Data Project A canonical KTU-evaluated R-Big-Data project follows a **5-stage pipeline**: ### Stage 1 — Problem Definition & Data Sourcing - Identify the **business/research question** (e.g., "Predict customer churn from 50 GB of clickstream logs"). - Source the data from: HDFS (`rhdfs`), S3 (`aws.s3`), databases (`RMySQL`, `MongoDB` via `mongolite`), APIs (`httr`, `rtweet`). ### Stage 2 — Data Ingestion - **Chunked reading** to bypass RAM limits: - `data.table::fread("huge.csv", chunksize = 1e6)` — reads 1 million rows at a time. - `readr::read_csv_chunked()` — callback-based streaming reader. - **Direct HDFS reads** via `rhdfs::hdfs.read()` or `SparkR::read.df()`. ### Stage 3 — Distributed Processing - **Spark integration** through `sparklyr`: - `spark_connect(master = "yarn", spark_home = "/opt/spark")` - `sdf_copy_to(sc, df, "tbl")` — pushes R `data.frame` to Spark as a distributed DataFrame. - Operations like `filter`, `group_by`, `mutate`, `summarize` are translated by `dplyr` into **Spark SQL**. ### Stage 4 — Modeling - ML algorithms run on: - Local R: for samples (e.g., 1 % representative sample). - Spark: `ml_random_forest()`, `ml_kmeans()`, `ml_logistic_regression()` via `sparklyr::ml_*`. - H2O: `h2o.glm()`, `h2o.deeplearning()` — distributed in-memory ML on JVM. ### Stage 5 — Reporting & Visualization - `ggplot2` for static plots. - `shiny` for interactive dashboards. - `rmarkdown::render("report.Rmd")` for reproducible PDF/HTML reports. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes every formula, metric, and threshold a student must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic. | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Application | |---|---|---| | Sample Memory Check | $\text{bytes} \approx 8 \cdot n_{\text{cols}} \cdot n_{\text{rows}}$ | Estimate RAM before loading | | Time Complexity of `dplyr` join | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ (sort-merge) or $\mathcal{O}(n+m)$ (hash) | Predict join cost on $n$, $m$ rows | | MapReduce Combiner Reduction | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{combine}} + T_{\text{reduce}}$ | Estimate Hadoop-R pipeline time | | Spark Lazy Evaluation | DAG committed only on `collect()` / `write_parquet()` | `sdf_collect()` triggers execution | | Hash Partitioning (Spark) | $p(x) = x.\text{hash} \bmod N_{\text{partitions}}$ | Data distribution in SparkR | | Sampling Ratio for Pilot Models | $r_{\text{sample}} = \frac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}$ | Minimum sample size for valid R model | | Pearson Correlation | $\rho_{X,Y} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Feature-target relationship | | Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Model evaluation metric | | R-squared | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Variance explained by model | | Big-O for `data.table` group | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ (radix sort + GForce) | Speed of `dt[, .(mean(x)), by = g]` | | Big-O for `dplyr` group | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | Default `group_by() + summarize()` | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Naming Rule for LaTeX in Markdown**: The Pearson formula above uses `\dfrac` and `\sqrt` (not the vertical pipe `|x|`) to remain compatible with both LaTeX and markdown rendering. Students writing answers in exam scripts should write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of R in Big Data Engineering - **Healthcare**: Genomic data (TB-scale VCF files) processed via `Bioconductor` packages connected to Spark. - **Finance**: Real-time fraud detection using `sparklyr` + Kafka + Shiny dashboards. - **Social Media Analytics**: Twitter sentiment analysis pipelines using `rtweet` + `tidytext` + Hive/Spark. - **Telecommunications**: CDR (Call Detail Record) analysis — often 10+ GB daily — processed via `data.table` + Parquet. - **E-commerce**: Recommendation engines using `recommenderlab` integrated with Spark MLlib. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Footprint of a Data Frame in R This derivation is commonly asked in **Part A (3-mark)** questions. The examiner expects the student to demonstrate that an R `data.frame` consumes roughly **8 bytes per numeric cell** plus pointer overhead, which is the theoretical foundation for deciding *when* to switch from base R to `data.table` or Spark. > [!NOTE] > **Assumption**: We work with a numeric data frame of $n$ rows and $k$ columns, stored in a 64-bit R session. ### Step 1 — Per-Cell Storage A single numeric value in R is stored as a **C double** (IEEE 754, 64-bit floating point). $$ \text{size per value} = 8 \ \text{bytes} $$ ### Step 2 — Total Cell Count The data frame has $n \times k$ numeric cells: $$ \text{cells} = n \times k $$ ### Step 3 — Raw Numeric Data $$ \text{size}_{\text{raw}} = n \cdot k \cdot 8 $$ ### Step 4 — Column Pointer Overhead Each column in an R `data.frame` is a **list element** — a SEXP pointer of **8 bytes** (64-bit pointer). Thus: $$ \text{size}_{\text{pointers}} = k \cdot 8 $$ ### Step 5 — Row Names Overhead (Default) By default, `data.frame` assigns integer row names. With $n$ rows, the row-name integer vector consumes: $$ \text{size}_{\text{rownames}} = n \cdot 4 $$ ### Step 6 — Aggregation The total approximate memory consumption is: $$ \text{size}_{\text{total}} = 8nk + 8k + 4n $$ Factoring out for large $n$ and $k$: $$ \text{size}_{\text{total}} \approx 8nk \quad \text{(dominant term)} $$ ### Step 7 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > A 10-million-row, 20-column numeric data frame occupies approximately: $$ \text{size} \approx 8 \times 10^7 \times 20 = 1.6 \times 10^9 \ \text{bytes} \approx 1.49 \ \text{GB} $$ > A 100-million-row, 50-column data frame: $$ \text{size} \approx 8 \times 10^8 \times 50 = 4 \times 10^{10} \ \text{bytes} \approx 37.25 \ \text{GB} $$ This justifies the migration to **SparkR / `sparklyr`** for "Big Data" projects. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: R-Package Trade-offs (Common 7/14-Mark Question) > [!IMPORTANT] > The examiner frequently asks: *"Compare and contrast `data.table`, `dplyr`, and `sparklyr` for Big Data projects in R."* Use this matrix as your model answer scaffold. | Dimension | Base R `data.frame` | `data.table` | `dplyr` | `sparklyr` / SparkR | |---|---|---|---|---| | Max Practical Size | $\leq 2$ GB RAM | $\leq 32$ GB RAM (radix sort) | $\leq 16$ GB RAM | **Petabyte-scale** (HDFS/S3) | | Syntax Verbosity | High | Low (concise) | Medium (pipes) | Medium (dplyr-style pipes) | | Execution Mode | Eager, in-memory | Eager, in-memory | Eager, in-memory | **Lazy, distributed** | | Parallelism | None | Multi-threaded (OpenMP) | Single-threaded | **Cluster-wide** | | Speed (1 GB `group_by`) | ~30 s | ~1 s | ~5 s | ~0.5 s (overhead) | | ML Algorithms | Limited | Limited | Limited | **Full Spark MLlib** | | Best Use | Prototyping | Production tabular | Teaching & pipelines | True Big Data | ## 3.3 Derivation: Sample Size Formula for Pilot Modeling in R When an R project begins with a sampled subset of a massive dataset, the **minimum sample size** to estimate the population mean with confidence level $1-\alpha$ and margin of error $E$ is: ### Step 1 — Define Variables - $Z$ = critical value from the standard normal (e.g., $Z = 1.96$ for 95 % confidence). - $\sigma$ = population standard deviation (estimated from a pilot sample). - $E$ = desired margin of error. ### Step 2 — Start from the Confidence Interval $$ P\!\left( \left\vert \bar{X} - \mu \right\vert \leq E \right) = 1 - \alpha $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the Standard Error $$ E = Z \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $n$ $$ \sqrt{n} = \frac{Z \sigma}{E} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = \left( \frac{Z \sigma}{E} \right)^2 = \frac{Z^2 \sigma^2}{E^2} $$ ### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example Suppose $\sigma = 50$, $E = 5$, $Z = 1.96$: $$ n = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (50)^2}{(5)^2} = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 2500}{25} = 384.16 $$ Round up: $n = 385$ — at least **385 records** are needed in the R pilot sample. ## 3.4 End-to-End Project Skeleton (R + Spark) > This is the kind of "design a Big Data project using R" question worth 14 marks. ```r # ---- 1. LIBRARIES ---- library(sparklyr) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) # ---- 2. CONNECT TO SPARK ---- sc <- spark_connect(master = "yarn", spark_home = "/opt/spark") # ---- 3. INGEST FROM HDFS ---- raw_sdf <- spark_read_csv(sc, name = "logs", path = "hdfs:///data/web_logs/2024/") # ---- 4. TRANSFORM ---- clean_sdf <- raw_sdf %>% filter(status_code == 200) %>% mutate(timestamp = cast(timestamp AS "timestamp")) %>% group_by(user_id) %>% summarise(visits = n(), avg_latency = mean(latency_ms)) # ---- 5. COLLECT TO LOCAL R (SAMPLE) FOR VISUALIZATION ---- local_df <- clean_sdf %>% sdf_sample(0.01) %>% collect() # ---- 6. VISUALIZE ---- ggplot(local_df, aes(x = visits, y = avg_latency)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4) + labs(title = "User Engagement vs Latency", x = "Visits", y = "Avg Latency (ms)") # ---- 7. DISCONNECT ---- spark_disconnect(sc) ``` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture Flow — R-driven Big Data Project The following Mermaid block renders the **complete functional pipeline** of an R-based Big Data project — from data source to final report. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Data Sources
HDFS, S3, RDBMS, APIs, Logs] --> B[Ingestion Layer
sparklyr, fread, jsonlite] B --> C[Distributed Storage
HDFS, Parquet, Delta Lake] C --> D[Processing Engine
Apache Spark] D --> E[Transformation Layer
dplyr, data.table, Spark SQL] E --> F[Modeling Layer
sparklyr ml_*, caret, h2o] F --> G[Local R Session
collect, sdf_sample] G --> H[Visualization Layer
ggplot2, plotly, shiny] H --> I[Reporting Layer
rmarkdown, knitr, Shiny Dashboard] I --> J[Business Decision
Stakeholder] D -.Lazy DAG.-> K[DAG Optimizer
Catalyst] K -.Executes only on action.- D style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Project Lifecycle — KTU-aligned Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Problem
Definition] --> P2[Data
Acquisition] P2 --> P3[Exploratory Data
Analysis] P3 --> P4[Feature
Engineering] P4 --> P5[Model
Training] P5 --> P6[Model
Evaluation] P6 --> P7{Performance
Acceptable?} P7 -- No --> P4 P7 -- Yes --> P8[Deployment
Shiny/REST API] P8 --> P9[Monitoring &
Reporting] P9 --> P3 style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000 style P7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000 style P8 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 R-Package Decision Tree (Which Tool to Use?) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Does dataset fit
in single RAM?} Q1 -- Yes, less than 1 GB --> R1[Use base R data.frame] Q1 -- Yes, 1 to 32 GB --> Q2{Need speed and
concise syntax?} Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use data.table] Q2 -- No, prefer readability --> R3[Use dplyr] Q1 -- No, greater than 32 GB --> Q3{Distributed cluster
available?} Q3 -- Yes --> R4[Use sparklyr / SparkR] Q3 -- No --> R5[Use chunked fread
with disk-backed ff] style R1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style R2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style R3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style R4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,color:#000 style R5 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]** **Q1. What is R, and why is it preferred for Big Data analytics projects? List any two R packages used for distributed computing.** **Model Answer:** R is an open-source, interpreted programming language and environment specifically designed for statistical computing, data analysis, and graphical visualization. It is preferred for Big Data analytics because of: 1. **Rich ecosystem of packages** for every stage of the data pipeline (ingestion, wrangling, modeling, visualization). 2. **Seamless integration** with distributed frameworks such as Hadoop (via `RHadoop`) and Spark (via `sparklyr` / `SparkR`). 3. **Reproducible research** capability through `rmarkdown` and `knitr`. 4. **Active community** with thousands of contributed packages on CRAN and Bioconductor. **Two R packages for distributed computing:** - `sparklyr` — provides a `dplyr`-compatible interface to Apache Spark. - `RHadoop` — collection of packages (`rmr2`, `rhdfs`, `plyrmr`) to run MapReduce jobs from R. > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]** **Q2. Differentiate between `read.csv()` and `data.table::fread()` in R for reading large datasets.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | `read.csv()` (Base R) | `data.table::fread()` | |---|---|---| | Speed | Slow | **10–100× faster** (uses `mmap` and parallel I/O) | | Memory | Loads full file into RAM | Supports **chunked reading** via `chunksize` | | Type Inference | Conservative | Automatic and accurate | | Best For | Small CSVs (< 100 MB) | **Large tabular data** (GB-scale) | ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Design R-based Big Data Project for Twitter Sentiment Analysis **(a) [7 Marks]** Design the architecture of a Big Data project using R to perform real-time sentiment analysis on 1 million tweets per day. Identify the components of the data pipeline, the R packages to be used, and the role of distributed storage. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write the R code (or pseudocode) for: (i) connecting to a Spark cluster, (ii) ingesting tweet data from HDFS, (iii) cleaning and tokenizing the text, and (iv) computing a sentiment score using the `tidytext` package. Include a flowchart in your explanation. **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture:** The project requires a distributed pipeline because 1 million tweets/day is approximately **20–50 GB of raw JSON**, far beyond single-machine R limits. **Components:** 1. **Data Source** — Twitter Streaming API (`rtweet` package) or saved JSON files. 2. **Ingestion** — Kafka → HDFS / S3 (parquet format). 3. **Distributed Storage** — HDFS or Amazon S3 stores raw and processed tweets. 4. **Processing Engine** — Apache Spark (via `sparklyr`) executes the heavy text-cleaning on a YARN cluster. 5. **R Analytics Layer** — A local or edge R session connects to Spark, performs tokenization, removes stop words, joins with `tidytext::get_sentiments("bing")`, and computes sentiment. 6. **Visualization** — `ggplot2` for trend plots, `shiny` for an interactive dashboard. 7. **Reporting** — `rmarkdown` generates a daily HTML report. **R Packages:** `rtweet`, `sparklyr`, `dplyr`, `tidytext`, `textdata`, `ggplot2`, `shiny`, `rmarkdown`. **Role of Distributed Storage:** - HDFS provides **fault tolerance** (3× replication by default). - **Parquet** columnar format reduces I/O by 70–90 % for analytical queries. - Spark reads only the columns it needs — a major optimization for text analysis where most columns are ignored. **(b) R Code:** ```r # (i) Connect to Spark library(sparklyr); library(dplyr); library(tidytext) sc <- spark_connect(master = "yarn", spark_home = "/opt/spark") # (ii) Ingest from HDFS tweets_sdf <- spark_read_json(sc, name = "tweets", path = "hdfs:///data/twitter/2024/") # (iii) Clean and tokenize (in Spark via sparklyr) clean_sdf <- tweets_sdf %>% filter(!is.na(text), lang == "en") %>% mutate(clean_text = regexp_replace(text, "http\\S+", "")) %>% select(id, clean_text) # Bring a manageable sample to local R local_tweets <- clean_sdf %>% sdf_sample(0.10) %>% collect() tidy_tweets <- local_tweets %>% unnest_tokens(word, clean_text) %>% anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") # (iv) Sentiment scoring sentiment_df <- tidy_tweets %>% inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word") %>% count(id, sentiment) %>% pivot_wider(names_from = sentiment, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>% mutate(score = positive - negative) # Visualize library(ggplot2) ggplot(sentiment_df, aes(x = score)) + geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "steelblue") + labs(title = "Daily Tweet Sentiment Distribution", x = "Score", y = "Count") spark_disconnect(sc) ``` **Flowchart:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Twitter API] --> B[Kafka] B --> C[HDFS Parquet] C --> D[Spark via sparklyr] D --> E[Tokenize + Stop-word Removal] E --> F[Bing Sentiment Join] F --> G[Local R Scoring] G --> H[ggplot2 / Shiny] style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Log Analytics with R and Hadoop **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of `RHadoop` (`rmr2`, `rhdfs`, `plyrmr`) in Big Data projects. Compare it with `sparklyr` in terms of execution model, performance, and ease of use. **(b) [7 Marks]** Design a complete R-based project to analyze 500 GB of web server logs stored in HDFS. Show how MapReduce functions are written in R using `rmr2`. Include formulas for memory estimation. **Model Solution:** **(a) RHadoop vs sparklyr:** `RHadoop` is a collection of R packages developed by Revolution Analytics to interface R with **Hadoop**: - **`rhdfs`** — provides R functions to manage HDFS (read, write, list, delete files). - **`rmr2`** — allows R users to write **MapReduce jobs** in pure R syntax. - **`plyrmr`** — a higher-level interface built on `rmr2` that mimics `plyr` verbs (`mapred`, `bind_rows`). | Dimension | `RHadoop` (rmr2) | `sparklyr` | |---|---|---| | Execution Model | **MapReduce (disk-based, two-stage)** | **DAG-based, in-memory (Spark)** | | Iterative ML | Poor (writes to disk between iterations) | **Excellent** (caches in RAM) | | Speed | Slow for iterative algorithms | **10–100× faster** for iterative ML | | Ease of Use | Moderate (verbose) | **Easy (dplyr-style verbs)** | | Latency | High (job startup overhead) | **Low (REPL-friendly)** | | Best For | Batch ETL, one-pass analytics | **ML, streaming, interactive analytics** | | Community Status | Maintenance mode | **Actively developed** | **(b) Project Design — Web Log Analytics:** ```r # Memory estimation n_rows <- 5e9 # 5 billion log entries n_cols <- 8 # ip, timestamp, method, url, status, size, referer, ua mem_bytes <- 8 * n_rows * n_cols mem_gb <- mem_bytes / (1024^3) # mem_gb ≈ 298 GB — must use distributed framework # MapReduce in R using rmr2 library(rmr2); library(rhdfs) # Mapper: emits (status_code, 1) map_status <- function(k, lines) { parts <- strsplit(lines, " ")[[1]] status <- parts[9] # HTTP status field keyval(status, 1) } # Reducer: sums counts reduce_status <- function(status, counts) { keyval(status, sum(counts)) } # Submit job hdfs.init() result <- from.dfs( mapreduce(input = "/logs/raw/2024/", input.format = "text", map = map_status, reduce = reduce_status, output = "/logs/output/status_counts") ) # Inspect head(result$key, 20) ``` **Pipeline Architecture:** ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Web Servers] --> B[Flume / Sqoop] B --> C[HDFS log storage] C --> D[rhdfs reads] D --> E[rmr2 MapReduce] E --> F[Status Code Aggregation] F --> G[Local R Visualization] G --> H[Shiny Dashboard] style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose Marks Here:** > 1. **Forgetting `spark_disconnect(sc)`** — common in 7-mark code questions. Always close the Spark connection at the end of the script. > 2. **Confusing `sdf_collect()` vs `collect()`** — `sdf_collect()` pulls the *entire* Spark DataFrame to R; this **defeats the purpose** of distributed computing. Use `sdf_sample()` first. > 3. **Writing `read.csv()` for "Big Data"** — examiners deduct marks if a student uses base R for GB-scale data. Always invoke `fread()` or Spark. > 4. **Missing the "Business Question"** — a Big Data project answer that does not begin with a clearly stated business/research objective loses 2 marks automatically. > 5. **Skipping the architecture diagram** — in 14-mark design questions, a flowchart is **mandatory** and carries 2–3 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):** - **R is a language + environment**; it is *interpreted*, *functional*, and *object-oriented*. - **Memory rule**: numeric `data.frame` of $n$ rows × $k$ columns ≈ $8nk$ bytes. - **Three R-Big-Data bridges**: `RHadoop` (MapReduce), `sparklyr` / `SparkR` (Spark DAG), `ff` / `bigmemory` (disk-backed matrices). - **`sparklyr` verbs** are translated to **Spark SQL** by the Catalyst optimizer. - **`sdf_collect()`** brings data to R; **never** call it on a full-scale DataFrame. - **Lazy evaluation in Spark** means nothing executes until an **action** (`collect`, `write`, `count`) is called. - **`data.table::fread()`** is the recommended replacement for `read.csv()` in Big Data contexts. - **Sampling formula** for pilot studies: $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \sigma^2}{E^2}$. - **Five stages of an R project**: Ingest → Transform → Model → Visualize → Report. - **`rmarkdown` + `knitr`** = reproducible research — a unique strength of R. - **HDFS replication** default = 3 → fault tolerance. - **Parquet** columnar format reduces I/O by ~80 % versus CSV. - **`shiny`** turns R analytics into interactive web apps without HTML/JS knowledge. - **Key packages to remember by name**: `dplyr`, `data.table`, `sparklyr`, `SparkR`, `rmr2`, `rhdfs`, `ggplot2`, `shiny`, `rmarkdown`, `tidytext`, `caret`, `h2o`. - **KTU favourite comparison**: `data.table` vs `dplyr` vs `sparklyr` — practice the matrix in Section 3.2. - **Always draw a flowchart** in 14-mark design questions. - **Always state a business problem** before writing code. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the 5 R Big-Data Packages**: **"R-H-S-D-S"** → `R`mr2, `H`dfs, `S`parklyr, `D`plyr, `S`parkR.

topics.title: Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the KTU 2024 B.Tech (Data Science / CSE) scheme, **Python** for Big Data Processing refers to the ecosystem of Python libraries, data structures, and computational paradigms used to **ingest, cleanse, transform, analyze, and visualize** large-scale structured and semi-structured datasets. The core stack comprises **NumPy** (numerical computing), **Pandas** (tabular data manipulation), **Matplotlib/Seaborn** (visualization), and the foundational Python 3.x interpreter features that support vectorized, high-performance, in-memory data operations as a precursor to distributed computing frameworks like **Hadoop** and **Spark (PySpark)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes Python as the *gateway language* to Big Data — students must master array broadcasting, DataFrame semantics, aggregation logic, and basic exploratory data analysis (EDA) workflows before progressing to PySpark in subsequent modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **giant warehouse** full of mixed-up delivery boxes (raw data). Python is the **manager's toolkit** that lets you:

| Real-World Analogy | Python Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A super-fast conveyor belt that sorts boxes by size & weight in one go | **NumPy arrays** (vectorized math, no loops) |
| A smart spreadsheet that auto-sorts, filters, and joins multiple sheets | **Pandas DataFrame** |
| A digital dashboard showing bar charts and heatmaps of the warehouse activity | **Matplotlib / Seaborn** |
| The warehouse rulebook ("treat missing boxes as 0, ignore duplicates") | **Data preprocessing / cleaning functions** |

**In one line:** *Python is the universal translator between raw Big Data and human-understandable insight.*

## 1.3 Why Python for Big Data? (KTU Board-Expected Justification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars Examiners Love to Quote:**
> 1. **Readability** — Python's syntax closely mirrors English pseudocode, reducing development time.
> 2. **Rich Ecosystem** — Over **350,000+** PyPI packages, with NumPy/Pandas being the de-facto standard for data work.
> 3. **Interoperability** — Native bridges to **C/C++**, **R**, **Java (Py4J → Spark)**, and **SQL engines** make it the *lingua franca* of data engineering.

## 1.4 Key Python Constructs for Big Data (Quick Glossary)

- **Vectorization** — Performing operations on entire arrays without explicit `for` loops, leveraging low-level C optimizations.
- **Broadcasting** — NumPy's mechanism for arithmetic between arrays of *different but compatible* shapes.
- **DataFrame** — A 2-D labeled, size-mutable, potentially heterogeneous tabular structure with aligned axes.
- **Series** — A 1-D labeled array capable of holding any data type.
- **GroupBy** — The *Split–Apply–Combine* paradigm for aggregate analytics.
- **Missing Data Sentinel** — `NaN` (Not a Number), following the IEEE 754 floating-point standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 NumPy — The Numerical Backbone

**NumPy (Numerical Python)** provides the `ndarray` (N-dimensional array), which is a homogeneous, contiguous block of memory. Operations on `ndarray`s execute at compiled C speed, making Python viable for numerical work on Big Data chunks.

### 2.1.1 Core Attributes of an ndarray
- `ndarray.shape` → Tuple describing dimensions, e.g., `(rows, columns)`.
- `ndarray.dtype` → Data type of elements (`int64`, `float64`, `complex128`).
- `ndarray.ndim` → Number of axes (1-D, 2-D, 3-D, …).
- `ndarray.size` → Total element count.
- `ndarray.itemsize` → Bytes per element.

### 2.1.2 Broadcasting Rules (HIGH-YIELD ⭐)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two arrays are broadcast-compatible iff, when comparing their shapes from the *trailing* dimension backward, each pair is either equal, or one of them is 1.** The array with dimension size 1 is virtually *stretched* to match the other — no actual memory copy is made.

**Generalized shape alignment rule:**

$$\text{broadcast}(A, B) \text{ is valid } \iff \forall i: \left( \text{dim}_A^{(i)} = \text{dim}_B^{(i)} \right) \lor \left( \text{dim}_A^{(i)} = 1 \right) \lor \left( \text{dim}_B^{(i)} = 1 \right)$$

### 2.1.3 Common Mathematical Aggregations

NumPy reductions operate along a specified `axis`:

- `np.sum(A, axis=0)` → Column-wise sum (collapses rows).
- `np.sum(A, axis=1)` → Row-wise sum (collapses columns).
- `np.mean`, `np.std`, `np.var`, `np.min`, `np.max`, `np.median` → Statistical reductions.

## 2.2 Pandas — Tabular Data Mastery

**Pandas** is built *on top* of NumPy and adds labeled axes, heterogeneous column types, and missing-data handling.

### 2.2.1 The Two Primary Data Structures

| Structure | Dimensionality | Mutability | Homogeneity | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Series** | 1-D (labeled) | Value-mutable, size-fixed | Homogeneous | Time-series, single-column ops |
| **DataFrame** | 2-D (labeled rows + columns) | Value- & column-mutable | Heterogeneous per column | Tabular data, EDA, ETL |

### 2.2.2 The Split–Apply–Combine Paradigm (GroupBy)

This is the analytical heart of Pandas (and a KTU-favorite topic):

1. **Split** the DataFrame into groups based on one or more keys.
2. **Apply** a function (aggregate, transform, filter) to each group independently.
3. **Combine** the results back into a unified DataFrame.

**Mathematical abstraction:**

$$G = \text{GroupBy}(D, k) \;\Rightarrow\; R = \text{Combine}\big(\{\phi(D_g) \mid g \in G\}\big)$$

where $D$ is the dataset, $k$ is the key function, $D_g$ is the subgroup, and $\phi$ is the aggregation function (e.g., `mean`, `sum`).

### 2.2.3 Missing Data — The NaN Convention

Pandas uses `NaN` (a special float) to mark missing values. The expression:

$$x_{\text{clean}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(x_i \neq \text{NaN})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(x_i \neq \text{NaN})}$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function, defines **NaN-aware mean** — Pandas computes it automatically when `skipna=True` (default).

## 2.3 Matplotlib / Seaborn — The Visualization Layer

- **Matplotlib** provides the low-level 2-D plotting primitives (`plt.plot`, `plt.scatter`, `plt.hist`).
- **Seaborn** is built on Matplotlib and offers statistically-oriented, aesthetically-tuned high-level functions (`sns.heatmap`, `sns.boxplot`, `sns.pairplot`).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this table — it consolidates every formula likely tested in the University Exam.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Broadcasting validity | $\dim_A^{(i)} = \dim_B^{(i)}$ OR one of them is 1 | Align from trailing axis |
| 2 | Vectorized sum (no axis) | $\sum_{i,j} A_{ij}$ | Scalar output |
| 3 | Column-wise mean (axis=0) | $\bar{c}_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{ij}$ | One value per column |
| 4 | Row-wise mean (axis=1) | $\bar{r}_i = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} A_{ij}$ | One value per row |
| 5 | Population variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$ | `np.var` (ddof=0) |
| 6 | Sample variance | $s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | `np.var` (ddof=1) |
| 7 | Population std dev | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | `np.std` (ddof=0) |
| 8 | Sample std dev | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ | `np.std` (ddof=1) |
| 9 | Z-score normalization | $z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Feature scaling |
| 10 | Min-Max scaling | $x'_i = \frac{x_i - \min(X)}{\max(X) - \min(X)}$ | Output range $[0,1]$ |
| 11 | Pearson correlation | $r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Range $[-1, +1]$ |
| 12 | GroupBy aggregation | $R = \text{Combine}\big(\{\phi(D_g)\}\big)$ | Split–Apply–Combine |
| 13 | Missing-data mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(x_i \neq \text{NaN})}{\sum \mathbb{1}(x_i \neq \text{NaN})}$ | `skipna=True` |
| 14 | Memory per array | $\text{bytes} = n \times \text{itemsize}$ | Contiguous block |
| 15 | Time complexity (vectorized) | $O(n)$ element-wise, no Python loop | C-level dispatch |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vectorized Sum vs. Naïve Python Loop

**Problem:** Compute the sum of all elements in a list/array of $N$ numbers.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Naïve Python approach (slow):**

$$S_{\text{naive}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \quad \text{where each iteration goes through Python interpreter.}$$

Time complexity: $O(N)$ Python-level operations → very slow due to interpreter overhead.

**NumPy vectorized approach (fast):**

$$S_{\text{vec}} = \mathbf{1}^\top \mathbf{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \quad \text{where the entire sum dispatches to optimized C code.}$$

The operation $\mathbf{1}^\top \mathbf{x}$ (a vector of ones transposed, multiplied by $\mathbf{x}$) is dispatched as a single BLAS-level call, so the per-element Python overhead is **eliminated**.

**Speedup demonstration (worked numerical example):**

Let $N = 10^7$, $x_i = i$ for all $i$.

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} i = \frac{N(N+1)}{2} = \frac{10^7 \cdot (10^7+1)}{2} = \frac{10^7 \cdot 10000001}{2}$$

$$\Rightarrow S = 50{,}000{,}005{,}000{,}000$$

Both methods return the same scalar; the vectorized version completes ~50–100× faster because the loop body executes in **C, not Python**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Broadcasting for 2-D Matrix Normalization

**Problem:** Given matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, subtract the column mean and divide by the column standard deviation to produce a Z-score-normalized matrix $Z$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the column means.**

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} A_{ij} \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

This yields a row vector $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times n}$ (shape $(1, n)$).

**Step 2: Compute the column standard deviations.**

$$\sigma_j = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m-1} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (A_{ij} - \mu_j)^2}$$

This yields a row vector $\boldsymbol{\sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times n}$ (shape $(1, n)$).

**Step 3: Apply the broadcasting rule.**

Because the shapes are:

$$\text{shape}(A) = (m, n), \quad \text{shape}(\boldsymbol{\mu}) = (1, n), \quad \text{shape}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}) = (1, n)$$

Trailing dimensions are $(n) = (n)$ — equal, so broadcasting is valid. The $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ vectors are *virtually stretched* along the $m$-axis to match $A$.

**Step 4: Compute the Z-scores element-wise.**

$$Z_{ij} = \frac{A_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$$

In NumPy:

$$Z = (A - \mu) \;/\; \sigma$$

**Step 5: Verification (numerical toy example).**

Let

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 10 \\ 2 & 20 \\ 3 & 30 \end{pmatrix}$$

Then

$$\mu_1 = \frac{1+2+3}{3} = 2, \quad \mu_2 = \frac{10+20+30}{3} = 20$$

$$\sigma_1 = \sqrt{\frac{(1-2)^2 + (2-2)^2 + (3-2)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2}} = 1$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sqrt{\frac{(10-20)^2 + (20-20)^2 + (30-20)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{2}} = 10$$

Therefore,

$$Z = \begin{pmatrix} (1-2)/1 & (10-20)/10 \\ (2-2)/1 & (20-20)/10 \\ (3-2)/1 & (30-20)/10 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Sanity check:** Column-wise mean of $Z$ is $(0, 0)$ ✓ and column-wise std is $(1, 1)$ ✓. The Z-score transformation is correct.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — GroupBy Aggregation (Analytical Form)

**Problem:** Given a sales DataFrame with columns `Region`, `Product`, `Revenue`, compute the **total revenue per Region**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the dataset.**

Let $D = \{(r_k, p_k, v_k) \mid k = 1, 2, \dots, N\}$ where $r_k$ is the region, $p_k$ the product, and $v_k$ the revenue of the $k$-th record.

**Step 2: Define the partition.**

For each unique region $R_j$ in the set $\mathcal{R} = \{R_1, R_2, \dots, R_m\}$, define the subset:

$$D_j = \{k \in \{1, \dots, N\} \mid r_k = R_j\}$$

**Step 3: Apply the aggregation.**

$$\text{TotalRevenue}(R_j) = \sum_{k \in D_j} v_k = \sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cdot \mathbb{1}(r_k = R_j)$$

**Step 4: Combine into the result table.**

| Region | TotalRevenue |
|---|---|
| $R_1$ | $\sum_{k \in D_1} v_k$ |
| $R_2$ | $\sum_{k \in D_2} v_k$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $R_m$ | $\sum_{k \in D_m} v_k$ |

In Pandas, the entire operation collapses into a single readable expression:

$$\text{result} = D.\text{groupby}(\text{'Region'})[\text{'Revenue'}].\text{sum}()$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Handling Missing Data (Imputation by Mean)

**Problem:** A Series $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)$ has $K$ entries marked as `NaN`. Derive the mean-imputed replacement value.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the non-missing count.**

$$n_{\text{valid}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(x_i \neq \text{NaN}) = N - K$$

**Step 2: Compute the mean over valid entries.**

$$\bar{x}_{\text{valid}} = \frac{1}{n_{\text{valid}}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(x_i \neq \text{NaN})$$

**Step 3: Replace each NaN with the mean.**

$$x_i^{\text{filled}} = \begin{cases} x_i & \text{if } x_i \neq \text{NaN} \\ \bar{x}_{\text{valid}} & \text{if } x_i = \text{NaN} \end{cases}$$

**Worked numerical example.**

Let $x = (10, \text{NaN}, 20, \text{NaN}, 30)$.

$$n_{\text{valid}} = 3, \quad \bar{x}_{\text{valid}} = \frac{10 + 20 + 30}{3} = 20$$

$$x^{\text{filled}} = (10, 20, 20, 20, 30)$$

Pandas implementation:

$$\text{df}[\text{'col'}].\text{fillna}(\text{df}[\text{'col'}].\text{mean}())$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Memory Footprint of an ndarray

**Problem:** Show that NumPy arrays are more memory-efficient than Python lists.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**For a Python list of $N$ floats:**

- Each Python `float` object: $\approx 24$ bytes (object header) $+ 8$ bytes (value) $+ 8$ bytes (pointer) = $\mathbf{40}$ bytes per element (CPython 3.11, 64-bit).
- Total: $40N$ bytes, plus the list's own pointer array of $8N$ bytes → roughly $48N$ bytes.

**For a NumPy `float64` array of $N$ elements:**

- Each element: **8 bytes** (raw `double`), stored contiguously.
- Total: $8N$ bytes, plus a small fixed header ($\approx 128$ bytes).

**Memory ratio:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{48N}{8N} = 6 \times$$

**Conclusion:** NumPy uses **~6× less memory** and provides **~50–100× faster** element-wise operations — a critical factor when working with Big Data chunks that must fit in RAM.

## 3.6 Python Code: All Derivations in Action

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# --- Derivation 1: Vectorized sum ---
x = np.arange(1, 10_000_001)               # 1 .. 10^7
S = np.sum(x)                              # vectorized, C-level
print("Vectorized sum:", S)
# Expected: 50000005000000

# --- Derivation 2: Z-score normalization via broadcasting ---
A = np.array([[1, 10],
              [2, 20],
              [3, 30]], dtype=float)
mu    = A.mean(axis=0)                     # shape (2,)
sigma = A.std(axis=0, ddof=1)              # shape (2,)
Z     = (A - mu) / sigma                   # broadcasting: (3,2) - (2,)
print("Z-matrix:\n", Z)

# --- Derivation 3: GroupBy aggregation ---
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Region":  ["N", "S", "N", "S", "E", "E", "N"],
    "Product": ["P1","P1","P2","P2","P1","P2","P1"],
    "Revenue": [100, 150, 200, 130, 90, 170, 110]
})
totals = df.groupby("Region")["Revenue"].sum()
print("Total revenue per region:\n", totals)

# --- Derivation 4: Missing-data imputation ---
s = pd.Series([10, np.nan, 20, np.nan, 30])
s_filled = s.fillna(s.mean())
print("Imputed series:\n", s_filled)

# --- Derivation 5: Memory footprint demonstration ---
import sys
py_list = list(range(1_000_000))
np_arr  = np.arange(1_000_000)
print("List bytes:", sys.getsizeof(py_list) + 1_000_000 * 28)
print("Array bytes:", np_arr.nbytes)       # ~8 MB vs ~28 MB for list
```

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]

**Differentiate between a Python list and a NumPy ndarray in terms of memory layout and execution speed. Why is this difference critical for Big Data processing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Python List | NumPy ndarray |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory layout** | Non-contiguous; each element is a separate Python object with a pointer (≈ 28–40 B per element). | Contiguous block of homogeneous data (e.g., 8 B per `float64`). |
| **Execution speed** | Element-wise operations require Python-level loops, which are slow due to interpreter overhead. | Vectorized operations dispatch to optimized C/BLAS routines — typically 50–100× faster. |
| **Big Data relevance** | Impractical for millions of elements due to memory bloat and slow loops. | **Essential** — fits larger chunks in RAM and processes them in C-speed, enabling in-memory analytics on large datasets. |

*(Award full 3 marks for clear mention of contiguity, vectorization, and Big Data impact.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain NumPy broadcasting with an example. State the broadcasting rule clearly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rule:** *Two arrays are broadcast-compatible if, when comparing their shapes from the trailing dimension backward, each dimension is either equal or one of them is 1. The size-1 dimension is virtually stretched to match the other — no actual memory copy occurs.*

**Example:**

$$A \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}, \quad b \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 3}$$

Adding $A + b$ broadcasts $b$ across all 3 rows:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 20 & 30 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 11 & 22 & 33 \\ 14 & 25 & 36 \\ 17 & 28 & 39 \end{pmatrix}$$

This enables **loop-free, C-level** arithmetic — a critical performance boost in data pipelines.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Explain the Pandas DataFrame and Series data structures. With a suitable code example, demonstrate the Split–Apply–Combine paradigm using `groupby()`. Also, plot a bar chart of the aggregated result using Matplotlib.**

**Model Answer:**

**DataFrame (7 marks breakdown):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A DataFrame is a 2-D, labeled, size-mutable tabular structure with potentially heterogeneous column types. It has row labels (index) and column labels.
- **Series (1 mark):** A 1-D labeled array; building block of a DataFrame (each column is a Series).
- **Construction (1 mark):** Can be built from dicts, lists, NumPy arrays, CSV files, SQL queries.

**Split–Apply–Combine with code (3 marks):**

```python
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Sample sales data
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Region":  ["North","South","North","South","East","East","North","West"],
    "Quarter": ["Q1","Q1","Q2","Q2","Q1","Q2","Q3","Q3"],
    "Revenue": [120, 150, 200, 130,  90, 170, 110, 160]
})

# 1. SPLIT   : Group by Region
# 2. APPLY   : Sum the Revenue
# 3. COMBINE : Aggregate result
agg = df.groupby("Region")["Revenue"].sum().reset_index()
print(agg)

# Bar chart
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.bar(agg["Region"], agg["Revenue"], color=["#1f77b4","#ff7f0e","#2ca02c","#d62728"])
plt.title("Total Revenue by Region")
plt.xlabel("Region")
plt.ylabel("Revenue")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Expected output table:**

| Region | Revenue |
|---|---|
| East   | 260 |
| North  | 430 |
| South  | 280 |
| West   | 160 |

**Explanation (1 mark):** The `groupby('Region')` call internally partitions the rows into 4 groups; `.sum()` computes the per-group total; `.reset_index()` flattens the result back into a regular DataFrame for plotting.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**With a worked numerical example, explain how NumPy broadcasting is used to perform Z-score normalization on a 2-D dataset. Show all five derivation steps and the broadcasting compatibility check.**

**Model Answer (full 7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the dataset:**
Let $A$ be a $3 \times 2$ matrix representing, e.g., [Height, Weight] of 3 patients.

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 160 & 60 \\ 170 & 70 \\ 180 & 80 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Compute column means (axis=0):**

$$\mu_1 = \frac{160+170+180}{3} = 170, \quad \mu_2 = \frac{60+70+80}{3} = 70$$

So $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (170,\; 70)$, shape $(2,)$.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Compute column std (ddof=1):**

$$\sigma_1 = \sqrt{\frac{(160-170)^2 + (170-170)^2 + (180-170)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{2}} = 10$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sqrt{\frac{(60-70)^2 + (70-70)^2 + (80-70)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{2}} = 10$$

So $\boldsymbol{\sigma} = (10,\; 10)$, shape $(2,)$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Broadcasting check:**
Shapes: $A$ is $(3, 2)$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ are $(2,)$. Trailing dims both = 2 → compatible. Vectors are virtually stretched to $(3, 2)$.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Apply formula and verify:**

$$Z = (A - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \;/\; \boldsymbol{\sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} (160-170)/10 & (60-70)/10 \\ (170-170)/10 & (70-70)/10 \\ (180-170)/10 & (80-70)/10 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Verification:** Column means of $Z$ = $(0, 0)$ ✓ and column std = $(1, 1)$ ✓.

**NumPy implementation:**

```python
import numpy as np
A = np.array([[160, 60], [170, 70], [180, 80]], dtype=float)
mu    = A.mean(axis=0)
sigma = A.std(axis=0, ddof=1)
Z     = (A - mu) / sigma
print(Z)
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the concept of missing data in Pandas. With a worked example, derive the mean-imputation formula and show how to apply it. Discuss the difference between `dropna()` and `fillna()`.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Missing data in Pandas (2 marks):** Pandas uses `NaN` (IEEE-754 Not-a-Number, a special float) to mark missing values in numeric Series and DataFrames. For non-numeric data, `None` (Python's `NoneType`) may also be used; `NaN` and `None` are largely interchangeable in Pandas.

**Mean-imputation derivation (2.5 marks):**

Given $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)$ with $K$ NaN entries:

$$n_{\text{valid}} = N - K, \quad \bar{x}_{\text{valid}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(x_i \neq \text{NaN})}{n_{\text{valid}}}$$

$$x_i^{\text{filled}} = \begin{cases} x_i & \text{if } x_i \neq \text{NaN} \\ \bar{x}_{\text{valid}} & \text{if } x_i = \text{NaN} \end{cases}$$

**Worked numerical example (1.5 marks):**
$x = (10, \text{NaN}, 20, \text{NaN}, 30)$

$$n_{\text{valid}} = 3, \quad \bar{x} = \frac{10+20+30}{3} = 20$$

$$x^{\text{filled}} = (10, 20, 20, 20, 30)$$

**`dropna()` vs `fillna()` (1 mark):**

| Method | Effect | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| `dropna()` | **Removes** rows/columns containing NaN. | When missingness is small (<5%) and random. |
| `fillna(v)` | **Replaces** NaN with value `v` (mean, median, 0, ffill, bfill). | When data is precious and the dataset must retain its size. |

**Code:**

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
s = pd.Series([10, np.nan, 20, np.nan, 30])
mean_val = s.mean()                          # 20.0
filled   = s.fillna(mean_val)                # (10, 20, 20, 20, 30)
cleaned  = s.dropna()                        # (10, 20, 30) — 3 rows
print(filled, "\n", cleaned)
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `axis=` in reductions** (1–2 marks lost): `df.sum()` sums *all* values (a scalar). For column-wise sum, you MUST write `df.sum(axis=0)`. Mixing up axis conventions is the #1 deduction cause.
> 2. **Confusing `ddof=0` (population) and `ddof=1` (sample) variance/std** (1 mark lost): When the question says *"sample standard deviation"*, use `ddof=1`; for *"population"*, use `ddof=0` (the NumPy default).
> 3. **Skipping the broadcasting-shape check in Z-score derivations** (1.5 marks lost): Examiners award marks specifically for stating *"trailing dimensions match: $(2,)$ and $(2,)$, hence broadcast-compatible."*
> 4. **Using Python loops where vectorization is expected** (1 mark lost): Always prefer `df.mean()` over manual `for` loops — the entire point of NumPy/Pandas is to *eliminate* Python loops.
> 5. **Not labeling axes in Matplotlib plots** (0.5 mark lost): Every plot MUST have `plt.xlabel`, `plt.ylabel`, and `plt.title` — KTU deducts marks for unlabeled figures.
> 6. **In GroupBy questions, forgetting to reset the index** (0.5 mark lost): After `groupby().sum()`, the result has the group key as the index; use `.reset_index()` before plotting/merging.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this before the exam!**

- ⭐ **Python = gateway language** to Big Data; PySpark and Dask are Python-fronted distributed systems.
- ⭐ **NumPy ndarray** is **contiguous, homogeneous, C-backed** → 6× less memory, ~50–100× faster than Python lists.
- ⭐ **Broadcasting rule:** Trailing dimensions must be *equal or one of them must be 1*; size-1 dims are virtually stretched.
- ⭐ **`axis=0` ⇒ collapse rows (column-wise result); `axis=1` ⇒ collapse columns (row-wise result).**
- ⭐ **`np.var` default ddof=0 (population); set `ddof=1` for sample variance/std.**
- ⭐ **Pandas DataFrame** = 2-D labeled, heterogeneous; **Series** = 1-D labeled, homogeneous.
- ⭐ **GroupBy** = *Split → Apply → Combine*; nearly every aggregation question reduces to this pattern.
- ⭐ **NaN-aware aggregation:** `skipna=True` (default) excludes NaN; `skipna=False` propagates NaN.
- ⭐ **Z-score:** $z_i = (x_i - \mu) / \sigma$ — used for outlier detection and ML feature scaling.
- ⭐ **Min-Max:** $x'_i = (x_i - \min)/(\max - \min)$ — bounds output to $[0,1]$.
- ⭐ **Pearson correlation** $r \in [-1, +1]$; `df.corr()` computes the full correlation matrix.
- ⭐ **Matplotlib** is the *foundation*; **Seaborn** sits on top for statistical plots (`heatmap`, `boxplot`, `pairplot`).
- ⭐ **Always label plot axes and titles** — KTU deducts marks for unlabeled figures.
- ⭐ **Vectorization > loops** — any Python `for` loop over a large array is an anti-pattern in Big Data work.
- ⭐ **Memory formula:** `bytes = n × itemsize` for ndarray; Python list needs ~6× more.
- ⭐ **Time complexity:** NumPy vectorized ops are $O(n)$ but at C-speed, not Python-speed.
- ⭐ **Big Data handoff:** A Pandas DataFrame can be exported to PySpark via `spark.createDataFrame(pandas_df)` — this is the *bridge to distributed computing* in subsequent modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Java

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java (in context of Big Data Processing):** Java is a high-level, class-based, object-oriented programming language designed to have as few implementation dependencies as possible. In the KTU 2024 Big Data Processing syllabus (Module 4), Java is studied as the **foundational language of the Hadoop ecosystem**, since Hadoop's core components (HDFS, MapReduce, YARN) are written in Java and expose their APIs primarily through Java classes and interfaces.

Java follows the **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** paradigm, achieved through compilation into platform-neutral **bytecode** executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**.

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit — compiler + tools + JRE |
| **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment — JVM + libraries |
| **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine — executes bytecode |
| **Bytecode** | Intermediate, platform-independent instruction set |
| **Class File** | `.class` file containing bytecode for a single class |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Universal Translator":** Imagine you write a letter in English, and a magical translator (the JVM) can instantly read it aloud in Malayalam, Hindi, French, or any language — without you ever re-writing the letter. That letter is **bytecode**, and the JVM is the **translator** living inside every device. This is why Hadoop (built in Java) can run on any commodity cluster — Linux servers, Windows machines, or macOS laptops — without rewriting code.

## 1.3 Why Java for Big Data?

Big Data frameworks lean heavily on Java because:

1. **Hadoop is JVM-native** — MapReduce jobs are typically written as Java classes implementing `Mapper` and `Reducer` interfaces.
2. **Strong multithreading** — essential for parallel data processing (splitting, shuffling, reducing).
3. **Robust memory management** — automatic garbage collection prevents crashes in long-running cluster jobs.
4. **Rich Collections API** — convenient for handling key-value pairs in MapReduce.
5. **Cross-platform** — same JAR can run on heterogeneous cluster nodes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Features of Java (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **Simple** — no pointers, no operator overloading, no multiple inheritance of classes.
2. **Object-Oriented** — everything is wrapped inside `class` and `object`.
3. **Platform Independent** — compiled to bytecode, run by JVM.
4. **Secured** — no explicit pointer; runs inside sandbox.
5. **Robust** — strong type checking, exception handling, garbage collection.
6. **Multithreaded** — `Thread` class and `Runnable` interface for concurrency.
7. **High Performance** — JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler converts bytecode to native code at runtime.
8. **Distributed** — RMI and EJB facilitate distributed computing (basis for Hadoop RPC).

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of OOP

| Pillar | Meaning | Big Data Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Encapsulation** | Binding data + methods in a single unit; data hiding via `private` | Wrapping a `Mapper` class with input/output logic |
| **Inheritance** | Acquiring properties of parent class via `extends` | Custom `Mapper` extending `Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable>` |
| **Polymorphism** | Same name, different behaviour (overloading/overriding) | Multiple `reduce()` overloads in different reducers |

The other two supporting concepts are **Abstraction** (hiding implementation via abstract classes/interfaces) and **Association** (has-a relationship).

## 2.3 Data Types — The Foundation Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Java has **8 primitive data types**. Memorise these — they appear in every KTU Java question.

| Type | Size | Default | Range / Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | 1 byte | 0 | $-128$ to $127$ |
| `short` | 2 bytes | 0 | $-32\,768$ to $32\,767$ |
| `int` | 4 bytes | 0 | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ |
| `long` | 8 bytes | 0L | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ |
| `float` | 4 bytes | 0.0f | $\approx \pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ (7 digits) |
| `double` | 8 bytes | 0.0d | $\approx \pm 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ (15 digits) |
| `char` | 2 bytes | `'\u0000'` | Unicode `$0$ to $65\,535$` |
| `boolean` | 1 bit (JVM-dependent) | `false` | `true` / `false` |

## 2.4 Java for MapReduce — The Critical Interface

The Hadoop `Mapper` and `Reducer` interfaces have generic type parameters:

$$
\text{Mapper}\langle K_{in}, V_{in}, K_{out}, V_{out} \rangle
$$
$$
\text{Reducer}\langle K_{in}, V_{in}, K_{out}, V_{out} \rangle
$$

For the classic **WordCount** example:

| Parameter | Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $K_{in}$ (Mapper) | `LongWritable` | Byte offset of line in file |
| $V_{in}$ (Mapper) | `Text` | One line of input |
| $K_{out}$ (Mapper) | `Text` | The word |
| $V_{out}$ (Mapper) | `IntWritable` | Count `1` |
| $K_{in}$ (Reducer) | `Text` | The word |
| $V_{in}$ (Reducer) | `IntWritable` | Iterable of counts |
| $K_{out}$ (Reducer) | `Text` | The word |
| $V_{out}$ (Reducer) | `IntWritable` | Total count |

## 2.5 Access Modifiers — Visibility Matrix

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass | Other Packages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | Yes | No | No | No |
| (default) | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| `protected` | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| `public` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

## 2.6 Exception Hierarchy (Must-Know)

$$
\text{Throwable} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Error} & \text{(JVM level, cannot handle — StackOverflow, OutOfMemory)} \\
\text{Exception} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{IOException (Checked)} \\
\text{ClassNotFoundException (Checked)} \\
\text{RuntimeException (Unchecked)} \rightarrow \text{NullPointer, ArrayIndexOutOfBounds, Arithmetic}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Checked exceptions** must be either caught or declared with `throws`. **Unchecked exceptions** are subclasses of `RuntimeException` and the compiler does not force handling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Syntax Breakdown & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Anatomy of a Java Program — Line-by-Line

Consider a minimal class:

```java
public class HelloBigData {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, Big Data!");
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step decoding:**

| Component | Role | Why it is required |
|---|---|---|
| `public` | Access modifier | JVM must be able to call `main` from outside the class |
| `class HelloBigData` | Class declaration | Java requires all code to live inside a class |
| `static` | Belongs to class, not object | JVM calls `main` **before** any object is created |
| `void` | Return type | `main` returns nothing to the OS |
| `main` | Method name | Hard-coded entry point recognised by JVM |
| `String[] args` | Command-line arguments | Used in Hadoop to read paths like `args[0]`, `args[1]` |
| `System.out.println` | Print to standard output | Used by Hadoop to log job progress |

## 3.2 Compilation and Execution Pipeline

**Step 1:** Source code is written in a `.java` file.

**Step 2:** `javac` (Java compiler) compiles it:

$$
\text{HelloBigData.java} \xrightarrow{\text{javac}} \text{HelloBigData.class (bytecode)}
$$

**Step 3:** `java` launcher invokes the JVM.

**Step 4:** **Class Loader Subsystem** loads the `.class` file into method area.

**Step 5:** **Bytecode Verifier** checks for illegal code (stack overflow, type mismatch).

**Step 6:** **Execution Engine** interprets bytecode line-by-line, or JIT-compiles hot code to native instructions.

**Step 7:** Garbage Collector (GC) reclaims unused memory from the **Heap**.

## 3.3 Object-Oriented Breakdown — WordCount Mapper

The classic MapReduce WordCount Mapper, fully derived:

```java
import java.io.IOException;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.*;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Mapper;

public class WordCountMapper extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {

    private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1);
    private Text word = new Text();

    @Override
    protected void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {

        String line = value.toString();
        String[] tokens = line.split("\\s+");

        for (String token : tokens) {
            word.set(token.toLowerCase());
            context.write(word, one);   // emit <word, 1>
        }
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

1. **Inheritance** — Class extends `Mapper<K1,V1,K2,V2>` to inherit framework behaviour.
2. **Reusable objects** — `one` and `word` are declared `private` (Encapsulation) to avoid creating millions of objects.
3. **`@Override` annotation** — Polymorphism; replacing parent's `map()`.
4. **`key`** is the byte offset (unused here, but required by signature).
5. **`value`** is one line; split using regex `\\s+` (one or more whitespace).
6. **`Context.write()`** emits intermediate `(word, 1)` key-value pairs to the shuffle phase.

## 3.4 The WordCount Reducer

```java
public class WordCountReducer extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {

    @Override
    protected void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {

        int sum = 0;
        for (IntWritable val : values) {
            sum += val.get();
        }
        context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum));
    }
}
```

**Logical derivation:**

1. **Reducer** receives all values for a single key (grouped by Hadoop's shuffle).
2. Sum them by **iterating** the `Iterable`.
3. Write final `(word, total_count)` to HDFS.

## 3.5 Exception Handling — Try-Catch-Finally Derivation

```java
try {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("input.txt");
    fr.read();
} catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
    System.out.println("File missing: " + e.getMessage());
} catch (IOException e) {
    System.out.println("Read error: " + e.getMessage());
} finally {
    System.out.println("Cleanup executed");
}
```

**Logic flow:**

- `try` block contains code that **may throw** a checked exception.
- `catch` blocks are evaluated top-to-bottom; the **most specific** exception type must come first (subclass before superclass).
- `finally` block **always executes** (even on exception), used for releasing resources (closing files, sockets).

## 3.6 Collections Framework — Hierarchy

$$
\text{Collection} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{List} \rightarrow \text{ArrayList, LinkedList, Vector} \\
\text{Set} \rightarrow \text{HashSet, LinkedHashSet, TreeSet} \\
\text{Queue} \rightarrow \text{PriorityQueue, ArrayDeque}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Map (separate hierarchy)} \rightarrow \text{HashMap, Hashtable, TreeMap, LinkedHashMap}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Big Data relevance:** In MapReduce drivers, `Configuration` and `Job` objects behave like a `Map<String,String>`. Understanding `HashMap` internals (hashing, bucket collision) helps when studying how HDFS Namenode stores file metadata in memory.

## 3.7 Memory Areas in JVM

| Area | Stores | Thread-scope |
|---|---|---|
| **Method Area** | Class metadata, static variables, constant pool | Shared |
| **Heap** | Objects and instance variables | Shared (GC happens here) |
| **Stack** | Method calls, local variables, references | Per-thread |
| **PC Register** | Address of current instruction | Per-thread |
| **Native Method Stack** | Native (C/C++) method calls | Per-thread |

**Derivation of memory size** for an object:

$$
\text{Object Size} = \text{Header (12-16 bytes)} + \text{Instance Variables} + \text{Padding}
$$

For an object of class `Text` (which wraps a `char[]` and `int length`):

$$
\text{Size} \approx 12 + 4 + (\text{array header} = 16) + 2 \cdot \text{length} \approx 32 + 2n \text{ bytes}
$$

where $n$ is the number of characters in the string.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JVM Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classA[Java Source File .java] --> classB[Javac Compiler]
    classB --> classC[Bytecode .class File]
    classC --> classD[Class Loader Subsystem]
    classD --> classE[Bytecode Verifier]
    classE --> classF[Method Area]
    classE --> classG[Heap Memory]
    classE --> classH[JVM Stack]
    classF --> classI[Execution Engine]
    classG --> classI
    classH --> classI
    classI --> classJ[Interpreter]
    classI --> classK[JIT Compiler]
    classI --> classL[Garbage Collector]
    classL --> classG
    classI --> classM[Native Method Interface JNI]
    classM --> classN[Native Method Libraries]
```

## 4.2 OOP Concepts — Relationship Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cls1[Class] -- instantiate --> obj1[Object]
    cls1 -- extends --> cls2[Subclass]
    cls2 -- inherits --> cls1
    cls1 -- implements --> intf1[Interface]
    cls2 -- implements --> intf1
    cls1 -- private data --> enc1[Encapsulation]
    cls1 -- abstract method --> abs1[Abstraction]
    cls1 -- same name diff signature --> pol1[Polymorphism Overloading]
    cls2 -- override method --> pol2[Polymorphism Overriding]
```

## 4.3 MapReduce Data Flow with Java Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[HDFS Input Block] --> splA[InputSplit]
    splA --> mapA[Java Mapper Class]
    mapA --> shufA[Shuffle and Sort]
    shufA --> redA[Java Reducer Class]
    redA --> outA[HDFS Output]
    drvA[Java Driver Class] --> mapA
    drvA --> redA
    drvA --> cfgA[Job and Configuration]
```

## 4.4 Java Exception Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    thr1[Throwable] --> err1[Error]
    thr1 --> exc1[Exception]
    err1 --> err2[OutOfMemoryError]
    err1 --> err3[StackOverflowError]
    exc1 --> io1[IOException]
    exc1 --> cnf1[ClassNotFoundException]
    exc1 --> run1[RuntimeException]
    io1 --> fnf1[FileNotFoundException]
    run1 --> npe1[NullPointerException]
    run1 --> aob1[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    run1 --> arith1[ArithmeticException]
```

## 4.5 Collections Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    col1[Collection] --> lst1[List]
    col1 --> st1[Set]
    col1 --> qu1[Queue]
    lst1 --> arr1[ArrayList]
    lst1 --> lnk1[LinkedList]
    lst1 --> vec1[Vector]
    st1 --> hst1[HashSet]
    st1 --> tst1[TreeSet]
    qu1 --> pqu1[PriorityQueue]
    mp1[Map] --> hmp1[HashMap]
    mp1 --> htb1[Hashtable]
    mp1 --> tmp1[TreeMap]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the WORA principle of Java. How is it achieved technically?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
WORA — "Write Once, Run Anywhere" — means Java source code, once compiled, can run on any operating system without recompilation. (1 mark)
It is achieved by compiling `.java` source into intermediate **bytecode** (not native machine code) using `javac`. (1 mark)
This bytecode is then executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**, which is platform-specific but is available for every OS, thus abstracting the hardware. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between `abstract class` and `interface` in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Methods | Can have both abstract and concrete | Only abstract (pre-Java 8); default/static allowed in Java 8+ |
| Variables | Any (final, non-final, static, non-static) | Only `public static final` (constants) |
| Inheritance | `extends` (single) | `implements` (multiple) |
| Constructor | Has constructor | No constructor |
| Use | Partial implementation template | Pure contract / capability |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the four main features of Java that make it suitable for Big Data processing. List the steps in the Java program execution pipeline with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to implement a Mapper class for the classic WordCount problem. Show the complete class with appropriate imports, explain each line, and mention the type parameters used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Features of Java for Big Data (7 marks)**

1. **Platform Independence (WORA)** — The same compiled JAR can run on heterogeneous Hadoop cluster nodes. (1 mark)
2. **Multithreading** — `java.lang.Thread` and `java.util.concurrent` allow parallel processing of splits; essential for MapReduce. (1 mark)
3. **Robust Memory Management** — Automatic garbage collection prevents memory leaks in long-running YARN application masters. (1 mark)
4. **Rich Collections API** — `HashMap`, `ArrayList` simplify handling of large key-value lists. (1 mark)
5. **Strong Type Checking** — Catches errors at compile time, critical when processing terabytes of data. (1 mark)

**Execution pipeline (4-step derivation, 1 mark each step):**
- Step 1: `.java` source file written by programmer.
- Step 2: `javac` compiler produces `.class` bytecode.
- Step 3: `java` launcher starts JVM, Class Loader loads `.class`.
- Step 4: Bytecode Verifier checks safety, Execution Engine (Interpreter + JIT) executes.
- (Diagrammatic representation: 1 mark)

**Part (b) — WordCount Mapper Program (7 marks)**

```java
import java.io.IOException;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.*;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Mapper;

public class WCMapper extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {
    private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1);
    private Text word = new Text();

    @Override
    public void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        String[] tokens = value.toString().split("\\s+");
        for (String t : tokens) {
            word.set(t);
            context.write(word, one);
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation (line-by-line, 7 marks distributed):**
- `import` statements — bring Hadoop's serializable types and `Mapper` base class. (1 mark)
- `extends Mapper<K1,V1,K2,V2>` — generic type parameters: `LongWritable` (input key = byte offset), `Text` (input value = line), `Text` (output key = word), `IntWritable` (output value = count 1). (2 marks)
- Static `IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1)` — reuse the same object to minimise GC pressure. (1 mark)
- `@Override map(...)` — polymorphism; Hadoop framework calls this for every input record. (1 mark)
- `split("\\s+")` — tokenise on whitespace; `context.write()` emits `(word, 1)`. (2 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Object-Oriented Programming. Discuss any four OOP principles with Java code snippets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is exception handling in Java? Explain the try-catch-finally mechanism with an example. Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — OOP Principles (7 marks)**

OOP is a paradigm that organises software design around **objects** — entities bundling data (attributes) and behaviour (methods). Java is a pure OOP language (except for primitives).

The four core principles (7 marks — 1.75 each):

1. **Encapsulation** — Wrapping data and methods in a class, restricting direct access:
   ```java
   class Student {
       private String name;
       public void setName(String n) { this.name = n; }
       public String getName() { return name; }
   }
   ```

2. **Inheritance** — `extends` keyword acquires parent properties:
   ```java
   class GradStudent extends Student {
       private String specialization;
   }
   ```

3. **Polymorphism** — Method overloading (compile-time) and overriding (run-time):
   ```java
   class MathOp {
       int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; }
       double add(double a, double b) { return a+b; } // overloading
   }
   ```

4. **Abstraction** — Hiding implementation via `abstract class` or `interface`:
   ```java
   abstract class Shape {
       abstract double area();
   }
   class Circle extends Shape {
       double r;
       double area() { return 3.14 * r * r; }
   }
   ```

**Part (b) — Exception Handling (7 marks)**

Exception handling is a mechanism to handle runtime errors gracefully, preventing abrupt termination. (1 mark)

**try-catch-finally example (3 marks):**
```java
try {
    int a = 10 / 0;
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero");
} finally {
    System.out.println("Always runs");
}
```

- `try` — code that may throw exception.
- `catch` — handles specific exception.
- `finally` — cleanup, executes always.

**Difference between checked and unchecked (3 marks):**

| Feature | Checked | Unchecked |
|---|---|---|
| Compile-time check | Yes | No |
| Subclass of | `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `RuntimeException` |
| Example | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException` |
| Handling | Must `try-catch` or `throws` | Optional |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `throws IOException, InterruptedException`** in `map()` and `reduce()` methods — costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. Using `int` and `String` instead of Hadoop's `IntWritable` and `Text` — Hadoop serialisation will fail; loses **1 mark**.
> 3. Confusing `abstract class` vs `interface` — examiners specifically test this; write the **difference table**, not a vague paragraph.
> 4. In exception questions, students often write the **most generic `Exception`** first — wrong; specific subclass must come first.
> 5. Do not confuse **`Overloading` (compile-time, same class)** with **`Overriding` (run-time, parent-child)** — 1 mark deduction each occurrence.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Java is **platform-independent** because of bytecode + JVM (WORA principle).
- **JDK > JRE > JVM** — remember the hierarchy.
- Eight **primitive data types**: `byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean`.
- Four OOP pillars: **Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction**.
- `abstract class` vs `interface` — interfaces support multiple implementation, abstract classes support partial implementation.
- Hadoop `Mapper<K1,V1,K2,V2>` and `Reducer<K1,V1,K2,V2>` are **generic interfaces** — must override `map()` / `reduce()`.
- Hadoop uses **Writable types** (`Text`, `IntWritable`, `LongWritable`) — NOT `String` or `int`.
- `main` method signature is fixed: `public static void main(String[] args)`.
- `static` members belong to the class; `this` refers to current object instance.
- **Checked exceptions** must be declared with `throws` or caught; **unchecked** (RuntimeException) are optional.
- JVM memory: **Heap (shared, GC)** vs **Stack (per-thread, method calls)**.
- `final` keyword: variable = constant, method = cannot override, class = cannot extend.
- `super()` must be the **first line** in a constructor when calling parent constructor.
- `String` is **immutable** in Java — every modification creates a new object.
- `==` compares references; `.equals()` compares values — classic trap in MapReduce keys!
- `ArrayList` is faster for random access; `LinkedList` is faster for insertions/deletions.
- **Garbage Collection** is automatic; objects become eligible when no live reference exists.
- Java is **pass-by-value** (even for object references — the reference is copied).
- `StringBuilder` (non-synchronized, faster) vs `StringBuffer` (synchronized, thread-safe).
- Hadoop Driver class uses `Job`, `Configuration`, `FileInputFormat`, `FileOutputFormat`.
- `Job.waitForCompletion(true)` submits and monitors the MapReduce job.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestion on Project Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestion on Project Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **PBCDT504 – Big Data Processing (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the phrase *"Suggestion on Project Topics"* refers to a curated set of **mini-project / capstone project problem statements** that allow students to demonstrate integrated competency across the **5 Vs of Big Data** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) using the **Hadoop / Spark / NoSQL / Stream Processing / Cloud** stack that was introduced across Modules 1–4.

A "Big Data Project Topic" in the KTU sense is therefore **not** a single algorithm or a single tool. It is an **end-to-end data pipeline** comprising:

$$
\text{Project} \;=\; \text{Data Ingestion} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Storage} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Processing} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Analytics/ML} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Visualization}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examination-Ready Line):**
> A Big Data project topic is an applied engineering problem that **justifies the use of distributed computing** (i.e., the dataset or velocity genuinely exceeds the capability of a single-node RDBMS or Pandas script) and that **implements at least one component each from storage (HDFS/NoSQL), processing (MapReduce/Spark), and analytics (MLlib/streaming/SQL-on-Hadoop).**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"**
>
> Imagine a single cook trying to prepare 10,000 meals in 1 hour. No matter how skilled, he fails. Now imagine a **head chef (master/YARN)** distributing recipes (jobs) to **100 line cooks (worker nodes)**, each chopping (map), cooking (reduce), and plating (output). The recipes are stored in a **pantry (HDFS)** with multiple shelves (replication). The menu is huge and varied — some dishes are pre-ordered (batch), some are live orders (streaming).
>
> **Big Data project topics are the "menus" you must serve using this kitchen.** The challenge is to pick a menu (problem) whose scale actually requires the kitchen — not a menu a single cook could finish.

### The "Why" of Suggesting Topics

KTU Module 4 deliberately concludes with project suggestions because:

1. The student has already learned **HDFS, MapReduce, YARN, HBase, Hive, Pig, Spark, Spark SQL, Spark MLlib, Spark Streaming, Kafka, Flume, Sqoop, NoSQL, and Cloud platforms** in Modules 1–3.
2. The university wants the student to **integrate**, not just memorize.
3. Real interview shortlists and placements test **end-to-end thinking**, not tool trivia.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (COs) for PBCDT504 explicitly map project execution to **CO4 and CO5** — *"Apply distributed processing techniques to solve real-world problems"* and *"Design and evaluate analytics pipelines on Big Data platforms."*

---

## 1.3 The 5 Vs as a Selection Filter

Every **strong** project topic in this course must touch **at least 3 of the 5 Vs**:

| V | Property | Example Triggering Question |
|---|----------|------------------------------|
| **Volume** | Terabytes+ | Can it fit on a laptop? If yes, the topic is too small. |
| **Velocity** | Streaming / real-time | Does the data arrive continuously (tweets, IoT, logs)? |
| **Variety** | Structured + Unstructured + Semi-structured | Is the data only CSV? If yes, scope is limited. |
| **Veracity** | Noise / missing / dirty | Is there a cleaning + quality module? |
| **Value** | Business insight / ML | Does it produce a model, dashboard, or KPI? |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Picking a topic that can be solved in **Pandas on a 50 MB CSV**. This does **not** qualify as a Big Data project under KTU evaluation. The dataset or velocity must justify Hadoop/Spark.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Architectural Categories of KTU Big Data Projects
