Based on the tool coverage of Modules 1–4, every valid project topic falls into **one of six categories**. Memorizing this taxonomy is the single highest-yield preparation move for the ESE viva and project review.

### Category A — Batch Analytics Projects (Hadoop + Hive/Spark)
- **Stack:** HDFS, MapReduce, Hive, Spark Core, Spark SQL.
- **Use when:** You have a **large historical dataset** (e.g., 50 GB+ of logs, sales, tweets).
- **Why pick it:** Easiest to implement; HDFS + Hive alone is sufficient.

### Category B — Streaming Analytics Projects (Kafka + Spark Streaming / Flume)
- **Stack:** Kafka / Flume → Spark Streaming / Structured Streaming → HBase / Cassandra.
- **Use when:** The data is **real-time** (tweets, IoT sensors, clickstream).
- **Why pick it:** Differentiator — shows you understand **velocity**.

### Category C — NoSQL + Hybrid Storage Projects
- **Stack:** HBase / Cassandra / MongoDB + HDFS + Spark.
- **Use when:** Data is **semi-structured or sparse** and you need **low-latency random reads/writes**.

### Category D — Machine Learning on Big Data Projects
- **Stack:** Spark MLlib / ML + Spark SQL + HDFS.
- **Use when:** You want a **predictive model** (recommender, classifier, clustering) on a large dataset.

### Category E — ETL / Data Lakehouse / Data Warehouse Migration
- **Stack:** Sqoop, Flume, Spark, Hive, Delta Lake / Iceberg.
- **Use when:** You want to show **ingestion + transformation + serving**.

### Category F — Cloud-Native Big Data Projects
- **Stack:** AWS EMR / Azure HDInsight / GCP Dataproc + S3/ADLS + Spark + serverless.
- **Use when:** You want a **deployment-focused** project.

---

## 2.2 KTU 2024 "Project Topic Selection" Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the most exam-relevant condensed view of Module 4. Reproduce it on a single revision page.**

| \# | Selection Criterion | Minimum Acceptable Bar | Ideal Bar |
|----|---------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Dataset Size** | $\geq 5 \text{ GB}$ | $\geq 50 \text{ GB}$ or unbounded stream |
| 2 | **Tool Coverage** | Storage + Processing + Analytics (3 layers) | All 5 layers (Add ingestion + visualization) |
| 3 | **5 V Justification** | Volume or Velocity | Volume + Velocity + Variety |
| 4 | **Novelty** | Re-implementation of a tutorial | Novel domain / novel preprocessing / novel feature engineering |
| 5 | **Reproducibility** | Code + dataset URL in report | Docker / docker-compose + GitHub README + screenshots |
| 6 | **Evaluation Metrics** | Accuracy / MAE / RMSE | Throughput, latency, scalability, *and* ML metric |
| 7 | **Visualization** | Console output | Grafana / Power BI / Tableau / Plotly Dash |

---

## 2.3 The "Project Architecture Triangle" — Engineering Utility

Every Big Data project must be **justified** on three engineering axes:

$$
\text{Justification} = f(\text{Scale}, \text{Speed}, \text{Cost})
$$

where:

$$\text{Scale} = \frac{\text{Dataset Size}}{\text{Single-Node RAM}} \quad (\text{should} \gg 1)$$

$$\text{Speed} = \frac{\text{Time}_{\text{Spark Cluster}}}{\text{Time}_{\text{Pandas}}} \quad (\text{should} < 1)$$

$$\text{Cost} = (\text{EC2 instances} \times \text{hours}) + \text{Storage GB} \times \text{monthly rate}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The same triangle is what a solutions architect presents to a client (e.g., Flipkart, Swiggy, banking) to justify migrating from a monolith to a Spark-on-EMR cluster. KTU examiners reward students who **quantify** this triangle in their project synopsis.

---

## 2.4 Logical Steps for Choosing a Topic (Why & How)

1. **Identify a domain you understand** (healthcare, agriculture, finance, social media, transport).
2. **Find a publicly available dataset** that is genuinely large — check **Kaggle Datasets**, **UCI ML**, **data.gov**, **Twitter API**, **Google BigQuery public datasets**, **AWS Open Data Registry**.
3. **Map the 5 Vs** to the dataset.
4. **Sketch the pipeline** on paper — ingestion, storage, processing, analytics, output.
5. **Verify tool coverage** — every layer of the pipeline must use a tool from Modules 1–3.
6. **Define measurable outcomes** — accuracy, F1, RMSE, throughput (records/sec), latency (ms).
7. **Risk analysis** — what happens if Spark fails mid-job? (Idempotency, checkpointing.)

> [!TIP]
> **Why these 7 steps?** KTU project evaluation rubric (CO5) explicitly tests: *Problem Definition (10%)*, *Tool Mapping (20%)*, *Implementation (40%)*, *Results & Analysis (20%)*, *Viva (10%)*. Each step above maps directly to a rubric line item.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Project Topic Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Project Topics" is a **design-oriented** topic (not a numerical one), the "derivation" here is the **derivation of a complete, defensible project synopsis** from a raw domain idea. We will demonstrate the full logical chain for **two flagship topics** that are extremely high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE and project reviews.

---

## 3.1 Topic Derivation A — "Real-Time Twitter Sentiment Analytics for Crisis Management"

### Step 1 — Domain Identification
- **Domain:** Disaster management / public health / brand monitoring.
- **Real-world trigger:** During COVID-19, Kerala State Disaster Management Authority (KSDMA) monitored Twitter for panic signals. A KTU student can recreate a **miniature** version of this.

### Step 2 — Dataset Sourcing
- **Source:** Twitter API v2 (Filtered Stream endpoint) — keyword filter on `#KeralaFloods`, `#COVID`, or `#ChennaiRains`.
- **Volume check:** During a 24-hour crisis, $\geq 500{,}000$ tweets easily exceed 2 GB raw / day.
- **Velocity:** Tweets arrive at $\sim 100$ to $\sim 5{,}000$ tweets/sec — clearly **real-time**.

### Step 3 — 5 V Mapping

$$V_{\text{total}} = \text{Volume} + \text{Velocity} + \text{Variety} + \text{Veracity} + \text{Value}$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 2\text{GB/day} \;+\; \text{real-time} \;+\; \{\text{text, emoji, image URLs, hashtags}\} \;+\; \{\text{spam, bots, duplicates}\} \;+\; \{\text{location heatmap, sentiment KPI}\}
$$

All 5 Vs are satisfied. The topic is **defensible**.

### Step 4 — Pipeline Sketch (Architecture)

$$
\text{Twitter API} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Kafka producer}}\; \text{Kafka topic} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Spark Structured Streaming}}\; \text{Sentiment Model (MLlib)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{HBase (live dashboard)}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{(batch branch)}
$$

$$
\text{Kafka} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Sqoop/HDFS}}\; \text{HDFS} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Hive}}\; \text{Aggregated daily reports (Tableau)}
$$

### Step 5 — Tool-to-Layer Mapping

| Layer | Tool (from Module 1–3) | Justification |
|-------|------------------------|---------------|
| Ingestion | **Kafka producer (Python `tweepy`)** | Decouples source from processing |
| Storage | **HDFS + HBase** | HDFS for batch, HBase for low-latency random reads |
| Processing | **Spark Structured Streaming** | Handles unbounded tables natively |
| Analytics | **Spark MLlib Logistic Regression / Naive Bayes** | Trained on labelled sentiment corpus |
| Serving | **Plotly Dash / Grafana** | Real-time dashboard |

### Step 6 — Measurable Outcomes

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{N_{\text{tweets processed}}}{\Delta t_{\text{wall-clock}}} \quad [\text{tweets/sec}]
$$

$$
\text{Latency} = t_{\text{tweet arrival}} - t_{\text{dashboard update}} \quad [\text{ms}]
$$

$$
\text{F1}_{\text{sentiment}} = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this topic is high-yield for KTU ESE:** It touches **Kafka, Spark Streaming, Spark MLlib, HBase, HDFS, Hive, and a dashboard tool** — i.e., nearly the entire Module 1–3 syllabus. The examiner cannot ask a question you cannot map to a known tool.

---

## 3.2 Topic Derivation B — "Predictive Maintenance of Industrial IoT Sensors Using Spark MLlib"

### Step 1 — Domain
- **Domain:** Smart Manufacturing / Industry 4.0 (NASSCOM-relevant for Kerala's electronics & rubber industries).

### Step 2 — Dataset
- **Source:** **NASA Turbofan Engine Degradation Simulation (C-MAPSS)** or **AWS IoT Analytics Kaggle dataset**.
- **Size:**  $\sim 4.7$ GB across all 4 sub-datasets, with **time-series** of 21 sensors × 100–300 engines.

### Step 3 — 5 V Mapping
- **Volume:** $\sim 4.7$ GB, with **millions** of multivariate time-series points → justifies Spark.
- **Velocity:** Optional Flume/Kafka simulation of streaming sensor feed.
- **Variety:** Sensor IDs (categorical), numeric readings, operational settings.
- **Veracity:** Sensor noise, missing readings, drift.
- **Value:** Predict **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** — a directly monetizable KPI in factories.

### Step 4 — Pipeline

$$
\text{CSV/Parquet on HDFS} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Spark SQL clean}}\; \text{Feature engineering (sliding window)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{MLlib GBT / RandomForest}}\; \text{RUL prediction} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Tableau dashboard}
$$

### Step 5 — Mathematical Justification of "Why Spark"

The parallel speedup on $n$ worker nodes (Amdahl's Law) is:

$$
S(n) = \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{n}}
$$

where $f$ is the **serial fraction** (driver-side code, e.g., data loading, final aggregation). For a typical Spark ML job, $f \approx 0.05$, so:

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.2375} = \frac{1}{0.2875} \approx 3.48\times
$$

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.11875} = \frac{1}{0.16875} \approx 5.93\times
$$

This is the **quantitative proof** that the topic justifies Spark over Pandas.

### Step 6 — Evaluation

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
$$

$$
\text{R}^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}
$$

A good project target: **RMSE $< 20$ cycles** and **R² $> 0.80$** on the FD001 sub-dataset (literature baseline).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — 10 Ready-Made Project Topics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most useful revision artifact for Module 4.** For each topic, note the **5 V that dominates** and the **primary tool from Module 1–3** it stresses. In the ESE viva, examiners frequently ask *"Why this tool and not X?"* — the 5 V column gives your one-line answer.

| \# | Project Title | Domain | Dominant V | Primary Tool (Module) | Dataset Hint |
|----|---------------|--------|------------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Real-Time Twitter Sentiment Analytics for Crisis Mgmt | Social | Velo+Variety | Spark Streaming + Kafka (M2) | Twitter API v2 |
| 2 | Predictive Maintenance of Industrial IoT Sensors | Manufacturing | Volume+Veracity | Spark MLlib (M3) | NASA C-MAPSS |
| 3 | Crime Hotspot Detection Using Chicago Crime Data | Public Safety | Volume+Value | Spark SQL + Hive (M1,M3) | Chicago Data Portal (1.5 GB) |
| 4 | Movie Recommender System Using ALS on MovieLens 25M | Entertainment | Volume | Spark MLlib ALS (M3) | MovieLens 25M |
| 5 | Log Anomaly Detection Using Flume + Spark Streaming | DevOps | Velocity | Flume + Spark (M2) | HDFS log dumps / Loghub |
| 6 | Crop Yield Prediction Using Weather + Soil Big Data | Agriculture | Variety+Veracity | Spark ML + Hive (M1,M3) | data.gov.in + IMD |
| 7 | Real-Time Cab Fare Prediction Using NYC Taxi Stream | Transport | Velocity+Value | Spark Structured Streaming (M3) | NYC TLC (Parquet, ~30 GB) |
| 8 | Hospital Readmission Prediction Using Spark ML on MIMIC-III | Healthcare | Volume+Veracity | Spark MLlib (M3) | MIMIC-III (6 GB+) |
| 9 | Customer Churn Analytics for Telecom Using Hive + Spark | Telecom | Volume+Value | Hive + Spark SQL (M1,M3) | IBM Telco + Kaggle 5G |
| 10 | Real-Time Stock Movement Prediction Using Kafka + Spark Streaming | Finance | Velocity+Veracity | Kafka + Spark Streaming (M2) | Alpha Vantage / Yahoo Finance API |

---

## 3.4 Pitfalls in Project Topic Selection (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

A topic is **rejected** by KTU evaluators if **any** of the following holds:

$$
\text{Topic Valid} \iff \bigwedge_{i=1}^{5} C_i
$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &: \text{Size}(\text{dataset}) \geq 1\text{ GB} \\
C_2 &: \text{Velocity} = \text{True} \;\lor\; \text{Volume} \geq 10\text{ GB} \\
C_3 &: \text{Tools used} \geq 3 \text{ from Modules 1--3} \\
C_4 &: \text{Output} = \text{Model} \;\lor\; \text{Dashboard} \;\lor\; \text{KPI} \\
C_5 &: \text{Reproducible} = \text{True} \;(\text{README + dataset + code})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Engineering Utility:** This 5-condition check is a **portable rubric**. A final-year B.Tech student defending a project can use it in any panel review — not just KTU.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Project Topic Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Domain Idea] --> B{Dataset Size >= 1 GB?}
    B -- No --> C[Reject: Too Small for Big Data]
    B -- Yes --> D{Real-Time or > 10 GB?}
    D -- No --> E[Reject: Use Pandas Instead]
    D -- Yes --> F{How Many Big Data Tools Will Be Used?}
    F -- 1 or 2 --> G[Reject: Not a Big Data Project]
    F -- 3 or More --> H{Defensible Output Model KPI or Dashboard?}
    H -- No --> I[Reject: No Measurable Outcome]
    H -- Yes --> J[Valid KTU Big Data Project Topic]
    J --> K[Category: Batch]
    J --> L[Category: Streaming]
    J --> M[Category: ML on Big Data]
    J --> N[Category: NoSQL Hybrid]
    J --> O[Category: Cloud Native]
    J --> P[Category: ETL / Data Lake]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for the exam:** Every rejection node is a **frequently-asked "What is not a Big Data project?" question**. Memorize the four rejection conditions — they appear almost verbatim in viva.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid — Reference End-to-End Architecture (Streaming + ML)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1[Twitter API] --> K1[Kafka Topic: raw_tweets]
    SRC2[IoT Sensors] --> K2[Kafka Topic: sensor_data]
    FS[Flume Source: App Logs] --> K3[Kafka Topic: app_logs]
    K1 --> SS[Spark Structured Streaming]
    K2 --> SS
    K3 --> SS
    SS --> SQ1[Spark SQL: Clean + Parse JSON]
    SQ1 --> ML[Spark MLlib: Sentiment or RUL Model]
    ML --> HDB[HBase: Real-Time Predictions]
    HDB --> DASH[Grafana Dashboard]
    SQ1 --> HDFS[HDFS: Parquet Archive]
    HDFS --> HV[Hive: Aggregated Reports]
    HV --> TBL[Tableau Daily KPI]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this diagram in the exam:** This single block diagram is your **answer to "Draw and explain the architecture of a typical Big Data project."** Practice drawing it from memory on a blank sheet in under 3 minutes. Label every arrow with the protocol (HTTP, Kafka, JDBC, ODBC).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid — Module 4 Topic-to-Tool Coverage Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Module 1: Hadoop HDFS and MapReduce] --> P1[Category A: Batch Analytics]
    M1 --> P5[Category E: ETL Projects]
    M2[Module 2: Spark and NoSQL] --> P2[Category B: Streaming Analytics]
    M2 --> P3[Category C: NoSQL Hybrid]
    M3[Module 3: Spark MLlib and Cloud] --> P4[Category D: ML on Big Data]
    M3 --> P6[Category F: Cloud Native]
    P1 --> VIVA[Module 4: Project Topics and Viva]
    P2 --> VIVA
    P3 --> VIVA
    P4 --> VIVA
    P5 --> VIVA
    P6 --> VIVA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every project topic is the **integration node** of tools from Modules 1–3. The viva and ESE both test this integration, not the tools in isolation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`
**"What are the characteristics that qualify a problem as a 'Big Data' project? List any four with one-line justifications."**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Ready):**
A problem qualifies as a Big Data project when at least one of the **5 Vs** is dominant and the dataset / processing requirement exceeds single-node capability. The four key characteristics are:

1. **Volume** — Dataset size in terabytes, exceeding the RAM/disk of one machine.
2. **Velocity** — Data arrives in real time (streaming), e.g., tweets, IoT.
3. **Variety** — Data is structured + semi-structured + unstructured.
4. **Veracity** — Data has noise, missing values, or quality issues requiring cleaning.
*(Value, the fifth, refers to the business insight extracted.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`
**"Differentiate between a Batch Analytics Big Data project and a Streaming Analytics Big Data project. Give one example tool for each."**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Ready):**

| Aspect | Batch Analytics | Streaming Analytics |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Data nature | Finite, historical | Unbounded, real-time |
| Latency | Minutes to hours | Milliseconds to seconds |
| Tool example | **Apache Spark Core / Hive** | **Spark Structured Streaming / Kafka Streams** |
| Storage | HDFS | Kafka + HBase / Cassandra |
| Use case | Annual sales report | Live fraud detection |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### `Question A (14 Marks)`

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`**

#### (a) **"Suggest a Big Data project topic suitable for a final-year B.Tech project. Clearly state the problem definition, objectives, and the 5 V analysis of the chosen dataset. (7 Marks)"**

**Model Solution:**

**Project Title:** *"Real-Time Sentiment Analytics of Public Transport Complaints for Smart City Initiative using Kafka and Spark Streaming"*

**Problem Definition:**
With the rise of smart-city dashboards in Kerala (KSRTC, Kochi Metro), there is a need to monitor citizen complaints from Twitter and Reddit in real time, classify them by sentiment and topic, and surface the top complaints to the operations team within seconds — not hours.

**Objectives:**
1. Ingest live tweet stream via **Twitter API v2 Filtered Stream** into a Kafka topic.
2. Perform **text cleaning and tokenization** in Spark Structured Streaming.
3. Apply a **pre-trained Spark MLlib sentiment model** (Logistic Regression on SST-2 corpus).
4. Persist live results into **HBase** and display on a **Grafana dashboard**.
5. Generate **end-of-day aggregated Hive reports** for management review.

**5 V Analysis (Answer Key):**

| V | Justification |
|---|---------------|
| Volume | 50,000+ tweets/day → $\sim 500$ MB/day raw JSON |
| Velocity | Continuous stream at $\sim 5$ tweets/sec peak |
| Variety | Text + emoji + hashtags + URLs + metadata JSON |
| Veracity | Spam, bot, multilingual, sarcasm noise |
| Value | Live complaint heatmap → actionable city operations |

**Rubric Mapping:** Problem (2) + Objectives (2) + 5 V (3) = **7 marks**.

---

#### (b) **"For the project proposed in (a), draw and explain the complete end-to-end architecture. Identify the tool used at each layer and justify the choice. (7 Marks)"**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (as drawn from Section 4.2 above):**

$$
\text{Twitter API} \;\xrightarrow{\text{HTTPS}}\; \text{Kafka Producer (tweepy)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Kafka Topic} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Spark Structured Streaming}}\; \text{Spark MLlib Model} \;\rightarrow\; \text{HBase} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Grafana}
$$

**Layer-by-Layer Justification:**

| Layer | Tool | Why this tool and not Pandas/PostgreSQL? |
|-------|------|------------------------------------------|
| Ingestion | **Kafka** | Decouples source from processing; handles burst rate |
| Storage (stream) | **HBase** | Low-latency random read/write on wide-column NoSQL |
| Storage (batch) | **HDFS** | Cheap, fault-tolerant, scales to PB |
| Processing | **Spark Structured Streaming** | Unifies batch + stream API; micro-batch engine |
| Analytics | **Spark MLlib** | Distributed model inference; same cluster as ETL |
| Serving | **Grafana / Plotly Dash** | Real-time push via WebSocket |
| Reporting | **Hive + Tableau** | SQL-on-Hadoop for non-technical stakeholders |

**Quantitative Justification (Amdahl's Law):**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{8}} \approx 5.93\times
$$

i.e., on 8 nodes, Spark runs the inference loop nearly **6× faster** than a single-node Pandas script — proving the Big Data choice is justified, not arbitrary.

**Rubric Mapping:** Architecture diagram (3) + Tool mapping (2) + Justification (2) = **7 marks**.

---

### `Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice`

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`**

#### (a) **"List the six major categories of Big Data project topics suitable for PBCDT504. For each, give one example project title and the dominant Big Data tool it stresses. (7 Marks)"**

**Model Solution:**

| \# | Category | Example Project Title | Dominant Tool |
|---|----------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Batch Analytics** | Crime Hotspot Analysis of Chicago Crime Data | **Hive + Spark SQL** |
| 2 | **Streaming Analytics** | Real-Time Cab Fare Prediction Using NYC Taxi Stream | **Spark Structured Streaming** |
| 3 | **NoSQL + Hybrid Storage** | Health Record Lookup Service Using HBase on MIMIC-III | **HBase + HDFS** |
| 4 | **ML on Big Data** | Movie Recommender Using ALS on MovieLens 25M | **Spark MLlib (ALS)** |
| 5 | **ETL / Data Lakehouse** | Data Lakehouse for Retail Using Sqoop + Spark + Delta Lake | **Sqoop + Spark + Delta** |
| 6 | **Cloud-Native Big Data** | Serverless Log Analytics on AWS Lambda + S3 + Athena | **AWS EMR + S3** |

**Rubric Mapping:** 6 categories × 1 mark + 1 mark for any one example expanded = **7 marks**.

---

#### (b) **"Compare Hadoop MapReduce and Apache Spark as the processing engine for a Big Data project. Which is more suitable for an iterative ML project, and why? (7 Marks)"**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Hadoop MapReduce | Apache Spark |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Execution Model** | Disk-based, two-stage (Map + Reduce) | In-memory DAG, multi-stage |
| **Iterative Job Performance** | Poor — writes to HDFS between iterations | Excellent — caches RDDs/DataFrames in memory |
| **Ease of Use** | Verbose Java code | High-level APIs in Python, Scala, R, Java |
| **Built-in ML / SQL / Streaming** | None — separate Mahout, Hive, Storm | Yes — MLlib, Spark SQL, Structured Streaming |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Task re-execution on HDFS re-read | RDD lineage + checkpointing |
| **Latency** | Minutes | Seconds (micro-batch) or ms (Continuous Processing) |

**Suitability for Iterative ML:**

Gradient descent in Spark MLlib iterates as:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)})
$$

For $T = 100$ iterations on 1 TB:

- **MapReduce cost** = $T \times$ (HDFS read + HDFS write + shuffle) per iteration.
- **Spark cost** = $1 \times$ (initial HDFS read) $+ (T-1) \times$ in-memory update.

$$
\frac{\text{Time}_{\text{MapReduce}}}{\text{Time}_{\text{Spark}}} \approx \frac{T \cdot (R_{\text{HDFS read}} + W_{\text{HDFS write}})}{R_{\text{HDFS read}} + (T-1) \cdot R_{\text{RAM}}}
$$

Since $R_{\text{RAM}} \gg R_{\text{HDFS read}}$, the ratio is large (often 10–100×). Therefore, **Spark is more suitable for iterative ML** projects.

**Rubric Mapping:** Comparison table (3) + Suitability reasoning (2) + Quantitative justification (2) = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Picking a small dataset (< 1 GB).** The single most common project-rejection reason in KTU panel reviews.
> 2. **Single-tool projects.** A project that only uses Spark MLlib (no HDFS, no Hive, no Kafka) will be marked down for "incomplete tool coverage."
> 3. **No 5 V justification.** Stating the topic is Big Data *without* naming which V is dominant.
> 4. **No quantitative metric.** A project with only a confusion matrix and no throughput / latency / scalability measurement scores low on the *Results* rubric.
> 5. **Copy-paste from a Medium tutorial.** Examiners cross-check GitHub commits and originality. Always add **at least one novel preprocessing step or feature engineering trick** to differentiate.
> 6. **Viva trap: "Why not Pandas?"** — You **must** be ready to answer this in one sentence (single-node RAM limit, or real-time velocity).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Page):**
>
> - [ ] A Big Data project must satisfy **at least one of the 5 Vs at scale**.
> - [ ] Every project needs **$\geq 3$ tools from Modules 1–3** covering storage + processing + analytics.
> - [ ] Dataset size **$\geq 1$ GB minimum** (ideal: 10 GB+ or unbounded stream).
> - [ ] Project must produce a **measurable artifact**: model (RMSE/F1), dashboard (latency), or KPI (throughput).
> - [ ] Six valid categories: **Batch, Streaming, NoSQL Hybrid, ML, ETL/Lakehouse, Cloud-Native**.
> - [ ] Always justify tool choice with **Amdahl's Law** or a back-of-envelope scaling calculation.
> - [ ] Always include a **reproducibility artifact**: GitHub README + dataset link + screenshots.
> - [ ] Viva must answer: *"Why this tool, why not Pandas / RDBMS / single-node?"*
> - [ ] Avoid the "**single-tutorial copy-paste**" trap — add at least one novel module.
> - [ ] Reference the **Kerala-specific** domains (KSDMA, KSRTC, Kerala PSC exam analytics, rubber/coconut price analytics) for higher viva impact.
> - [ ] **Defense template for any project**: Title → Problem → 5 V → Architecture → Tool map → Metrics → Risk → Reproducibility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Log File Analysis: Collect log files from web servers or applications and analyze them to extract useful information like error rates, traffic patterns, and popular pages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Log File Analysis: Collect log files from web servers or applications and analyze them to extract useful information like error rates, traffic patterns, and popular pages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Log File Analysis** is the systematic process of collecting, parsing, transforming, aggregating, and interpreting machine-generated log records (also called *event logs*, *audit trails*, or *request logs*) emitted by web servers, application servers, operating systems, databases, and network devices, in order to derive actionable operational, security, and business intelligence metrics such as **error rates**, **traffic patterns**, **popular pages (top-N resources)**, **latency distributions**, and **user behavior signatures**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCDT504 – Big Data Processing, Module 4)**, log file analysis is positioned as a canonical *Volume + Velocity + Variety* Big Data use-case, typically executed over distributed storage (HDFS) using batch tools (Hadoop MapReduce, Apache Pig, Apache Hive) or streaming engines (Apache Spark Streaming, Flink, Kafka + Logstash).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** A web-server log line is a *semi-structured* record (typically Common Log Format – CLF, or Combined Log Format). Each line is an independent event, but the file as a whole is *unstructured* text. The first engineering task is therefore **schema imposition** (extracting fields) before any analytics is meaningful.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The CCTV Footage of the Internet)

Imagine a shopping mall that has **thousands of entry/exit gates**. Every gate has a security guard who writes one line in a register every time a customer walks in:

> *"10:42 AM | Gate 3 | Mr. A | Browsed shoes, then billing counter, then exited Gate 1"*

Individually, each line is meaningless. But if you stack **1 million such lines per day** and a data analyst reads them, the analyst can answer:

| Business Question | What the Analyst Computes |
|---|---|
| How busy is the mall? | Traffic patterns (entries per hour) |
| Which shop is most visited? | Popular pages (top-N URLs) |
| Were there any incidents? | Error rate (4xx/5xx status counts) |
| When do we need more staff? | Peak-hour detection (temporal aggregation) |

**Log file analysis is exactly this** — the mall register is your `access.log` file, and the analyst is your MapReduce / Spark / Pig job.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Industry Baseline)

The following metrics are universally adopted and must be memorized for KTU exams:

- **Error Rate** — fraction (or percentage) of HTTP responses with status $\ge 400$
- **Requests Per Second (RPS)** — throughput indicator
- **Bytes Served** — bandwidth utilization
- **Unique Visitors** — distinct client IPs (approximation)
- **Top-N Pages** — most frequently requested URIs
- **p50 / p95 / p99 Latency** — response-time percentiles

> [!NOTE]
> The de-facto standard file analyzed is the **Apache/Nginx `access.log`** (Combined Log Format). A sample line is:
>
> `127.0.0.1 - frank [10/Oct/2023:13:55:36 -0700] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326 "http://example.com" "Mozilla/5.0"`
>
> The **9 fields** are: client-IP, ident, user, timestamp, request-line, status, bytes, referer, user-agent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Log Analysis Pipeline

A production-grade log analysis pipeline (whether implemented in Hadoop, Spark, or ELK) decomposes into **five ordered stages**. This is the canonical answer expected for "explain log analysis pipeline" type questions.

**Stage 1 — Ingestion (Collection)**
- Logs are pulled from distributed servers using agents like **Flume, Filebeat, Logstash, Fluentd**, or simply via `rsync`/`scp`.
- Critical design property: ingestion must be **idempotent** and **at-least-once** to survive crashes.

**Stage 2 — Storage (Landing Zone)**
- Raw logs are dumped into HDFS, S3, or HBase.
- Common directory convention: `/raw_logs/yyyy/mm/dd/<server_hostname>.log`.
- Files are typically stored as **line-delimited JSON** or **plain text** — never parsed/structured at this stage (preserves the *single source of truth*).

**Stage 3 — Parsing & Schema Imposition**
- A `Mapper` / Spark `flatMap` / Pig `PigStorage` / Hive `RegexSerDe` extracts the 9 CLF fields using a **regular expression**.
- The canonical CLF regex:
  `^(\\S+) (\\S+) (\\S+) \\[([^\\]]+)\\] "(\\S+) (\\S+) (\\S+)" (\\d{3}) (\\d+|-) "([^"]*)" "([^"]*)"$`

**Stage 4 — Aggregation & Analytics**
- MapReduce / Spark computes the four canonical outputs:
  1. **Hits per URL** → top-N popular pages
  2. **Status-code distribution** → error rate
  3. **Hits per time-bucket** → traffic patterns
  4. **Bytes per URL** → bandwidth hotspots

**Stage 5 — Visualization / Action**
- Output is pushed to dashboards (Grafana, Kibana) or triggers alerts (PagerDuty, email).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|
| Ingestion | Decouples log generation from log processing → fault tolerance |
| Raw Storage | Reprocessable; if parser regex has a bug, fix the job — not the data |
| Parsing | Converts *unstructured text* into *structured tuples* that SQL/MR can reason about |
| Aggregation | Reduces TB-scale logs into KB-scale summaries (the *value extraction* step) |
| Visualization | Humans do not read 10⁹ lines; we read a chart |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When asked *"why not just `grep` the log?"*, answer: `grep` is **O(n) per query** with no parallelism, no aggregation, and no historical view. MapReduce/Spark provides **horizontal scalability, fault tolerance, and built-in aggregation primitives** — exactly the properties Big Data demands.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the units; partial credit in KTU valuations is often lost on wrong units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Error Rate (\%)} \quad & ER = \frac{N_{4xx} + N_{5xx}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
\textbf{2. Success Rate (\%)} \quad & SR = \frac{N_{2xx} + N_{3xx}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
\textbf{3. Requests Per Second} \quad & RPS = \frac{N_{total}}{T_{window}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{4. Average Bytes per Request} \quad & \bar{B} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} B_i}{N} \\[6pt]
\textbf{5. Bandwidth (Window)} \quad & BW = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} B_i}{T_{window}} \quad \text{[bytes/sec]} \\[6pt]
\textbf{6. p-th Percentile Latency} \quad & L_p = \min \{ x : F(x) \ge p/100 \} \\[6pt]
\textbf{7. Moving Average (Traffic)} \quad & MA_t = \alpha \cdot x_t + (1-\alpha) \cdot MA_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \alpha \le 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary Conditions & Units Table**

| Symbol | Meaning | Valid Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N_{4xx}$, $N_{5xx}$ | Client / server error count | $\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ |
| $N_{total}$ | Total HTTP requests | $\mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ |
| $T_{window}$ | Observation window | seconds (s) |
| $B_i$ | Bytes in response $i$ | Bytes |
| $F(x)$ | Empirical CDF of latency | $[0,1]$ |
| $\alpha$ | Smoothing factor (EMA) | $(0,1]$, dimensionless |
| $L_p$ | Latency at percentile $p$ | milliseconds (ms) |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always state units** (e.g., "RPS = 1200 req/s" not just "RPS = 1200"). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units in numerical answers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Log file analysis is not an academic exercise — it powers:

- **SRE / DevOps**: SLA monitoring (Google's *SRE Book* mandates error budget tracking)
- **Cybersecurity**: Intrusion detection (sudden spike of `POST /admin` from one IP)
- **Business Analytics**: Conversion funnel, A/B test measurement
- **Capacity Planning**: Predict server provisioning needs from RPS growth
- **CDN Optimization**: Cache-hit ratio analysis (popular pages → aggressive caching)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Error Rate, RPS, and Top-N Computation

Consider the following **synthetic 6-line log snippet** (Combined Log Format, abridged to 6 fields for clarity):

```
10.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2023:10:00:01] "GET /home"     200 1024
10.0.0.2 - - [10/Oct/2023:10:00:02] "GET /home"     200 1024
10.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2023:10:00:03] "GET /about"    200 512
10.0.0.3 - - [10/Oct/2023:10:00:04] "GET /missing"  404 200
10.0.0.4 - - [10/Oct/2023:10:00:05] "POST /api"     500 0
10.0.0.2 - - [10/Oct/2023:10:00:06] "GET /home"     200 1024
```

**Step 1 — Tally the total request count $N_{total}$.**

$$
N_{total} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6
$$

*Logic:* Each line is exactly one HTTP transaction. We count lines.

**Step 2 — Classify by HTTP status class.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{2xx} &= |\{200, 200, 200, 200\}| = 4 \\
N_{3xx} &= 0 \\
N_{4xx} &= |\{404\}| = 1 \\
N_{5xx} &= |\{500\}| = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Status codes 200–299 are successes, 300–399 are redirects, 400–499 are client errors, 500–599 are server errors. We tag each line and bucket it.

**Step 3 — Compute the error rate $ER$ (Formula 1).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{4xx} + N_{5xx} &= 1 + 1 = 2 \\
ER &= \frac{2}{6} \times 100 = 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Two of six requests failed. That is a 33.33% error rate — well above the 1% industry SLO. Action: page the on-call.

**Step 4 — Compute the success rate $SR$ (Formula 2).**

$$
SR = \frac{4 + 0}{6} \times 100 = 66.67\%
$$

*Logic:* By definition $ER + SR + 3xx\% = 100\%$, so $33.33 + 66.67 = 100$. ✓ (sanity check passes).

**Step 5 — Compute RPS (Formula 3).**

The first request is at `10:00:01` and the last is at `10:00:06`. The window spans:

$$
T_{window} = 6 - 1 = 5 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
RPS = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2 \text{ req/s}
$$

*Logic:* RPS = total events divided by elapsed time. The 5-second window contains 6 events.

**Step 6 — Compute average bytes per request $\bar{B}$ (Formula 4).**

$$
\sum B_i = 1024 + 1024 + 512 + 200 + 0 + 1024 = 3784 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\bar{B} = \frac{3784}{6} \approx 630.67 \text{ bytes/req}
$$

*Logic:* Sum the byte field (column 7) of every line, then divide by $N_{total}$.

**Step 7 — Compute bandwidth (Formula 5).**

$$
BW = \frac{3784}{5} = 756.8 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 6.05 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 8 — Determine the Top-N popular pages (URL frequency table).**

Group by URL and count:

| URL | Count | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| `/home` | 3 | 🥇 1 |
| `/about` | 1 | 🥈 2 |
| `/missing` | 1 | 🥉 3 (tie) |
| `/api` | 1 | 🥉 3 (tie) |

*Logic:* The Mapper emits `(URL, 1)` per line; the Reducer sums the 1s. This is the canonical **WordCount** MapReduce pattern applied to URLs.

**Step 9 — Traffic Pattern (Hits per minute bucket).**

Within our 5-second window, all 6 events fall in the `[10:00, 10:01)` bucket, so $hits_{10:00} = 6$. A real MapReduce job would output `(hour_bucket, count)` tuples, which are then plotted as a time series.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapper vs Reducer Responsibility

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners frequently ask: *"In a MapReduce log analysis job, what does the Mapper do and what does the Reducer do?"* Use the matrix below as the model answer.

| Operation | Mapper Output (Intermediate Key-Value) | Reducer Aggregation |
|---|---|---|
| Error rate | `(status_class, 1)` | Sum → `N_{2xx}, N_{4xx}, N_{5xx}` |
| Popular pages (Top-N) | `(url, 1)` | Sum → URL frequency, then sort desc |
| Traffic per hour | `(hour_bucket, 1)` | Sum → hits per hour |
| Bandwidth | `(url, bytes)` | Sum → bytes per URL |
| Unique visitors | `(client_ip, 1)` | Distinct count (use a `Set` or HBase `HyperLogLog`) |
| p95 Latency | `(url, latency_ms)` | Sort, then pick the 95th-percentile element |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Exponential Moving Average (EMA) for Traffic Smoothing

A web server reports the following RPS every minute: `[10, 40, 30, 50, 20]`. Using $\alpha = 0.5$ and $MA_0 = 10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MA_1 &= 0.5 \times 10 + 0.5 \times 10 = 10.0 \\
MA_2 &= 0.5 \times 40 + 0.5 \times 10 = 25.0 \\
MA_3 &= 0.5 \times 30 + 0.5 \times 25 = 27.5 \\
MA_4 &= 0.5 \times 50 + 0.5 \times 27.5 = 38.75 \\
MA_5 &= 0.5 \times 20 + 0.5 \times 38.75 = 29.375
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* EMA dampens single-minute spikes (the value 40 is "averaged down" to a 25 forecast). A larger $\alpha$ reacts faster; a smaller $\alpha$ smooths more.

### 3.4 Worked Example — p95 Latency Calculation

Latencies (ms) for 10 requests: `[50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130, 1000]`.

**Step A — Sort ascending** (already sorted).

**Step B — Compute the rank index for p95.** For $n=10$ and $p=95$:

$$
\text{rank} = \lceil p/100 \times n \rceil = \lceil 0.95 \times 10 \rceil = \lceil 9.5 \rceil = 10
$$

**Step C — Pick the 10th element.**

$$
L_{95} = 1000 \text{ ms}
$$

*Logic:* 95% of requests are faster than 1 second — the one outlier drags the SLO breach. A p99 would surface the same anomaly; p50 would hide it. This is why **percentile-based SLOs are superior to averages**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — End-to-End Log Analysis Architecture (Hadoop / Spark Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Web Server Farm]
        ws1[Web Server 1\naccess.log]
        ws2[Web Server 2\naccess.log]
        ws3[Web Server 3\naccess.log]
    end

    agent1[Flume Agent\nSource: tail]
    agent2[Flume Agent\nSource: tail]
    agent3[Flume Agent\nSource: tail]

    hdfsRaw[(HDFS\n/raw_logs/yyyy/mm/dd)]

    mr1[MapReduce Job 1\nParse + Filter]
    mr2[MapReduce Job 2\nAggregate by URL]
    mr3[MapReduce Job 3\nAggregate by Hour]
    mr4[MapReduce Job 4\nStatus Code Count]

    hdfsOut[(HDFS\n/analytics/output)]

    hiveTbl[(Hive Table\nlog_analytics)]
    pigScript[Pig Script\ngroup by url]

    dash[Grafana Dashboard\nTop Pages, Errors, RPS]

    ws1 --> agent1
    ws2 --> agent2
    ws3 --> agent3
    agent1 --> hdfsRaw
    agent2 --> hdfsRaw
    agent3 --> hdfsRaw
    hdfsRaw --> mr1
    mr1 --> mr2
    mr1 --> mr3
    mr1 --> mr4
    mr2 --> hdfsOut
    mr3 --> hdfsOut
    mr4 --> hdfsOut
    hdfsOut --> hiveTbl
    hdfsOut --> pigScript
    hiveTbl --> dash
    pigScript --> dash
```

**How to read the diagram:** Logs flow left-to-right. The web servers emit raw text → Flume tails them → HDFS stores the raw zone → four MapReduce jobs parse and aggregate → the structured output is loaded into Hive (SQL queries) and processed by Pig (dataflow scripts) → a Grafana dashboard visualizes Top-N pages, error rates, and traffic curves.

### 4.2 Diagram B — MapReduce Data Flow for "Error Rate" Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Line 1\nGET /home 200] --> mapA
    inputB[Line 2\nGET /home 200] --> mapA
    inputC[Line 3\nGET /missing 404] --> mapB
    inputD[Line 4\nPOST /api 500] --> mapC
    inputE[Line 5\nGET /home 200] --> mapA

    mapA[Mapper\nEmit: 2xx, 1]
    mapB[Mapper\nEmit: 4xx, 1]
    mapC[Mapper\nEmit: 5xx, 1]

    shuffle{Shuffle\nand Sort\nby key}

    red2xx[Reducer\nkey=2xx\nsum = 3]
    red4xx[Reducer\nkey=4xx\nsum = 1]
    red5xx[Reducer\nkey=5xx\nsum = 1]

    finalOut[/N_2xx = 3\nN_4xx = 1\nN_5xx = 1\nN_total = 5\nER = 40 percent/]

    inputA --> mapA
    mapA --> shuffle
    mapB --> shuffle
    mapC --> shuffle
    shuffle --> red2xx
    shuffle --> red4xx
    shuffle --> red5xx
    red2xx --> finalOut
    red4xx --> finalOut
    red5xx --> finalOut
```

**Reading guide:** The **Mapper** is *stateless* and only emits `(status_class, 1)`. The **Shuffle & Sort** phase groups by key. The **Reducer** sums. Finally, a tiny driver program (or a downstream Hive query) computes $ER$ from the three counts.

### 4.3 Diagram C — Spark RDD / DataFrame Pipeline (Streaming Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    kafkaTopic[Kafka Topic\nraw-web-logs]
    sparkStream[Spark Streaming\nDirect Stream]
    dstream[Discretized Stream\nDStream of lines]
    parsed[Parsed Log\nDataFrame schema]
    filterErr[Filter\nstatus = 4xx or 5xx]
    groupUrl[GroupBy url\ncount]
    window[Windowed Aggregation\n1 minute tumbling]
    sink1[HDFS Parquet\ntop_pages]
    sink2[HDFS Parquet\nerror_counts]
    sink3[HDFS Parquet\ntraffic_per_min]

    kafkaTopic --> sparkStream
    sparkStream --> dstream
    dstream --> parsed
    parsed --> filterErr
    parsed --> groupUrl
    parsed --> window
    filterErr --> sink2
    groupUrl --> sink1
    window --> sink3
```

**Note:** Spark Streaming adds a **windowing** primitive (e.g., 1-minute tumbling windows) that MapReduce cannot express naturally. This is why modern stacks prefer Spark over vanilla MapReduce for log analytics.

### 4.4 Diagram D — The Five-Stage Pipeline (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stage 1\nIngestion] --> A2[Stage 2\nRaw Storage HDFS]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3\nParsing Schema]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4\nAggregation]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5\nVisualization]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define log file analysis. List any four metrics that can be extracted from web server logs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Log file analysis is the process of collecting, parsing, and interpreting machine-generated log records emitted by web servers, applications, and OS subsystems to derive operational and business metrics.

Four commonly extracted metrics:

1. **Error rate** — percentage of HTTP responses with status $\ge 400$
2. **Requests per second (RPS)** — server throughput
3. **Top-N popular pages** — most frequently requested URIs
4. **Bytes served / Bandwidth** — data volume per time window

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for 4 metrics = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the Common Log Format (CLF)? Name its fields.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CLF is a standardized text format used by Apache/Nginx web servers where each line is a single HTTP request. Its **7 fields** (extended to 9 in *Combined Log Format*) are:

1. `client_ip` — remote host
2. `ident` — RFC 1413 identity (usually `-`)
3. `user` — authenticated user (or `-`)
4. `timestamp` — request time in `[day/Mon/year:hh:mm:ss zone]`
5. `request` — `"METHOD URI HTTP/version"`
6. `status` — HTTP status code
7. `bytes` — response size in bytes (or `-` if zero)
8. `referer` — *(combined only)* referring URL
9. `user_agent` — *(combined only)* browser string

*(0.5 mark for "what is CLF" + 2.5 marks for listing fields = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 questions carry internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the **end-to-end pipeline** for performing log file analysis on a large web-server farm using Hadoop. In your answer, clearly explain the role of each stage and the data flow. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given the following 8-line log excerpt, compute (i) the error rate, (ii) the requests per second over a 7-second window, and (iii) the top-2 most popular pages. Show all steps. *(7 marks)*

```
10.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2023:12:00:01] "GET /home"    200 1024
10.0.0.2 - - [10/Oct/2023:12:00:02] "GET /home"    200 1024
10.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2023:12:00:03] "GET /about"   200 512
10.0.0.3 - - [10/Oct/2023:12:00:04] "GET /home"    200 1024
10.0.0.4 - - [10/Oct/2023:12:00:05] "GET /admin"   403 200
10.0.0.5 - - [10/Oct/2023:12:00:06] "POST /api"    500 0
10.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2023:12:00:07] "GET /about"   200 512
10.0.0.2 - - [10/Oct/2023:12:00:08] "GET /home"    404 200
```

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) The Log Analysis Pipeline (7 marks):**

The pipeline has **five stages**:

**Stage 1 — Ingestion (1 mark):** Multiple web servers each generate an `access.log` file. A **Flume agent** (or Filebeat/Logstash) running on each server *tails* the log file and ships each new line to a central collector. Flume uses a *source–channel–sink* model. The collector forwards to HDFS. The design property of ingestion is **at-least-once delivery** with idempotency so that retries on failure do not duplicate data.

**Stage 2 — Raw Storage (1 mark):** Incoming lines are appended to HDFS files in a date-partitioned directory layout, e.g. `/raw_logs/2023/10/10/server01.log`. The data is stored **as-is, line-delimited, in plain text**. Storing raw is non-negotiable: if a downstream parser has a bug, we simply re-run the job against the raw zone; the original is preserved.

**Stage 3 — Parsing & Schema Imposition (2 marks):** A **MapReduce job** reads the raw zone line by line. The Mapper applies the CLF regex to extract the 7 (or 9) fields and emits a structured tuple, e.g. `(timestamp, client_ip, method, url, status, bytes)`. This step is the boundary between *unstructured text* and *structured records*. A regex like `^(\S+) \S+ \S+ \[([^\]]+)\] "(\S+) (\S+) \S+" (\d{3}) (\d+|-)` suffices for the first 7 fields.

**Stage 4 — Aggregation & Analytics (2 marks):** Subsequent MapReduce (or Pig/Hive) jobs aggregate the parsed records:
- **Reducer 1** groups by URL and counts → popular pages.
- **Reducer 2** groups by status code class and counts → error rate.
- **Reducer 3** groups by hour bucket and counts → traffic pattern.
- **Reducer 4** groups by URL and sums bytes → bandwidth hotspot.

**Stage 5 — Visualization / Action (1 mark):** The structured output is loaded into a Hive external table. SQL queries (e.g., `SELECT url, COUNT(*) FROM log GROUP BY url ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10`) and BI tools like **Grafana/Kibana/Tableau** render dashboards. Alerts (PagerDuty) can be wired to threshold rules (e.g., `ER > 1%` for 5 minutes).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total request count** (1 mark):

$$
N_{total} = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Status-code classification** (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{2xx} &= |\{200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200\}| = 6 \\
N_{4xx} &= |\{403, 404\}| = 2 \\
N_{5xx} &= |\{500\}| = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — (i) Error rate** (2 marks):

$$
N_{err} = N_{4xx} + N_{5xx} = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

$$
ER = \frac{3}{8} \times 100 = 37.5\%
$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Requests per second** (1 mark):

Window spans from `12:00:01` to `12:00:08`, so $T_{window} = 8 - 1 = 7$ seconds.

$$
RPS = \frac{8}{7} \approx 1.143 \text{ req/s}
$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Top-2 popular pages** (2 marks):

| URL | Count | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| `/home` | 4 | 🥇 1 |
| `/about` | 2 | 🥈 2 |
| `/admin` | 1 | 3 |
| `/api` | 1 | 3 |

**Answer:** Top-2 = `/home` (4 hits) and `/about` (2 hits). Error rate = **37.5%**, RPS = **1.143 req/s**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain how **MapReduce is used to compute the top-N most popular pages from web-server logs**. Provide the Mapper logic, Reducer logic, and a sample intermediate key-value trace for a 5-line input. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages of using Apache Spark over MapReduce** for log file analysis. How does Spark Streaming extend this to real-time log monitoring? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Top-N Popular Pages via MapReduce (7 marks):**

**Mapper Logic (2 marks):** For each line in the input, the Mapper:
1. Applies the CLF regex to extract the URL field.
2. Emits the key-value pair `(url, 1)`.

```java
// Pseudocode (Java MapReduce style)
public void map(LongWritable offset, Text line, Context ctx) {
    String[] f = line.toString().split(" ");
    String url = f[3]; // simplified extraction
    ctx.write(new Text(url), new IntWritable(1));
}
```

**Reducer Logic (2 marks):** The Reducer receives `(url, [1, 1, 1, ...])` and sums the values. The output is `(url, total_count)`. A secondary sort or a `TreeMap` of size N then selects the top-N.

```java
public void reduce(Text url, Iterable<IntWritable> counts, Context ctx) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (IntWritable c : counts) sum += c.get();
    ctx.write(url, new IntWritable(sum));
}
```

**Sample Trace (3 marks)** for input lines `[/home, /home, /about, /home, /home]`:

| Phase | Output |
|---|---|
| Mapper | `(/home,1), (/home,1), (/about,1), (/home,1), (/home,1)` |
| Shuffle & Sort | Group by key: `[ /home → 1,1,1,1 ]`, `[ /about → 1 ]` |
| Reducer | `(/home, 4), (/about, 1)` |
| Top-N sort | `(/home, 4) 🥇, (/about, 1) 🥈` |

**Optimization Note (bonus, 0.5 mark):** A single MapReduce job gives frequencies, but the top-N sort requires a second pass. Alternatively, the Reducer can use an in-memory `TreeMap<Long, List<String>>` of capacity N to produce the top-N *during* the first job — avoiding a second stage.

---

**(b) Spark vs MapReduce for Log Analysis (7 marks):**

**Advantages of Spark (4 marks):**

1. **In-memory computation:** Spark keeps intermediate data in RAM (RDD lineage) instead of writing to HDFS between stages. For iterative log analysis (e.g., sessionization), this is 10–100× faster than MapReduce, which materializes every map/reduce output to disk.

2. **Richer API:** MapReduce forces `map` + `reduce` only. Spark offers `filter`, `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `window`, `mapPartitions`, and DataFrame/SQL operations — making complex analyses (e.g., p95 latency, funnel analysis) trivial.

3. **Lazy evaluation + DAG optimizer:** Spark builds a directed acyclic graph of transformations and optimizes it (predicate pushdown, column pruning) before execution. MapReduce has a rigid two-stage template.

4. **Multi-language support:** Scala, Python (PySpark), Java, R — lowering the barrier for analysts.

**Spark Streaming for Real-Time Log Monitoring (3 marks):**

Spark Streaming introduces the **DStream** (Discretized Stream) abstraction — a sequence of RDDs where each RDD represents a micro-batch (typically 0.5–10 seconds) of incoming log lines.

- **Source:** Kafka, Flume, Kinesis, or socket.
- **Transformations:** `parsedStream.filter(_.status >= 400)` gives a live stream of error lines.
- **Windowed aggregations:** `parsedStream.window(Minutes(1)).groupByKey().count()` computes hits-per-minute continuously.
- **Sink:** Output is written to HDFS, a database, or pushed to a dashboard via WebSockets.

This enables **real-time SLO monitoring** — a 1% error-rate alert fires within seconds rather than after a 24-hour MapReduce batch.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students lose marks on log-analysis questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** — RPS without "req/s" loses 0.5 mark. Always write `RPS = 1.14 req/s`.
> 2. **Confusing `4xx` and `5xx`** — `4xx` is *client* error (browser fault), `5xx` is *server* error (your fault). Both count toward error rate but mean different ops actions.
> 3. **Saying "MapReduce is faster than Spark"** — it is *not*. Spark is in-memory; MapReduce spills to disk. This is a guaranteed 0 if written in exams.
> 4. **Forgetting the "raw storage" stage** — the 2-stage storage pattern (raw zone + curated zone) is what makes log analysis *reprocessable*. Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing the CLF regex without explaining its groups** — examiners want to see you understand the *capturing groups*, not just paste a regex.
> 6. **Not distinguishing *Hits* vs *Unique Visitors*** — `COUNT(*)` is hits; `COUNT(DISTINCT client_ip)` is visitors. They differ by an order of magnitude.
> 7. **Omitting the Shuffle & Sort phase** in MapReduce diagrams — it is the heart of the framework and earns 1–2 marks by itself.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Log file analysis = **collect → parse → aggregate → visualize** machine-generated text.
- ✅ Canonical input format = **Apache/Nginx Combined Log Format (CLF)** with **9 fields**.
- ✅ Five-stage pipeline: **Ingestion → Raw Storage → Parsing → Aggregation → Visualization**.
- ✅ **Flume / Filebeat / Logstash** are ingestion tools; **HDFS** is the raw storage layer; **MapReduce / Spark / Pig / Hive** are the processing engines; **Grafana / Kibana** are dashboards.
- ✅ **Error Rate** = $(N_{4xx} + N_{5xx}) / N_{total} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **RPS** = $N_{total} / T_{window}$ (units: req/s).
- ✅ **Top-N pages** are computed by the canonical WordCount MapReduce pattern on the URL field.
- ✅ **Traffic pattern** = time-bucketed hit count (per minute / per hour / per day).
- ✅ **Bytes served** = sum of the byte column, optionally per URL.
- ✅ **p95 / p99 latency** is preferred over averages for SLO definition.
- ✅ MapReduce is **disk-bound, two-stage, batch**; Spark is **in-memory, DAG, multi-operator, supports streaming**.
- ✅ Always store the **raw zone** separately from the curated zone to enable reprocessing.
- ✅ **Schema imposition** (regex parsing) is the critical boundary between unstructured text and structured analytics.
- ✅ Common metrics to memorize: ER, RPS, bandwidth, top-N URLs, unique visitors, p50/p95/p99 latency.
- ✅ For real-time log analysis, use **Spark Streaming / Flink / Kafka Streams** instead of batch MapReduce.
- ✅ **Idempotency** and **at-least-once delivery** are non-negotiable design properties of the ingestion layer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Data: Collect tweets or social media posts and perform sentiment analysis to understand public opinion on a particular topic or product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT504 - BIG DATA PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Data: Collect tweets or social media posts and perform sentiment analysis to understand public opinion on a particular topic or product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)** is a sub-field of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Text Mining** that systematically identifies, extracts, quantifies, and studies affective states and subjective information from textual data. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, when applied to social media streams (Twitter/X, Facebook, Instagram, Reddit), it transforms unstructured high-velocity big data streams into structured polarity labels: **Positive**, **Negative**, or **Neutral**.

Formally, given a corpus of documents $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n\}$ and a target entity $e$ (e.g., a product, political party, or event), the sentiment function is:

$$S : d_i \rightarrow \{+1, 0, -1\} \quad \text{or} \quad S : d_i \rightarrow [-\alpha, +\alpha]$$

where the output is a categorical polarity class or a continuous sentiment intensity score bounded by a normalization constant $\alpha$ (commonly $\alpha = 1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The topic explicitly demands (a) **data collection** from a live API and (b) **analytical processing** to derive public opinion. Both stages carry independent marks in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Associated Formal Definitions

| **Term** | **Rigorous Definition** |
|---|---|
| **Polarity** | The directional orientation (positive / negative) of an opinion expressed in text. |
| **Subjectivity** | A measure of whether a sentence expresses a personal opinion ($s \to 1$) or factual statement ($s \to 0$). |
| **Token** | A single meaningful linguistic unit (word, hashtag, emoji) after splitting. |
| **Corpus** | The complete aggregated set of social media posts under study. |
| **Lexicon** | A pre-built dictionary mapping words to sentiment scores (e.g., VADER, Bing Liu). |
| **Stop Words** | High-frequency function words (is, the, at) carrying negligible sentiment weight. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Stadium Thermometer"**
> Imagine walking into a massive cricket stadium in Kochi. You cannot interview all 50,000 fans individually, but you can sample reactions: cheers, boos, silence, hashtags on posters, chants. By aggregating these **signals** you estimate the *collective mood* of the crowd toward a player. **Sentiment Analysis is the digital equivalent of that stadium thermometer** — it reads millions of "cheers and boos" (tweets/posts) and converts them into a numerical public-opinion score for a brand, film, or policy.

- **Social media post** = a fan's verbal reaction in the stadium.
- **Hashtag / emoji** = a placard held up by the fan.
- **Sentiment polarity** = the crowd's net cheer-vs-boo meter.
- **Aggregation dashboard** = the final "mood score" displayed on the Jumbotron.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is a "Big Data" Problem

Social media data exhibits the canonical **5 V's of Big Data**, making it unsuitable for traditional RDBMS-based analysis:

| **V** | **Manifestation in Twitter Data** |
|---|---|
| **Volume** | 500+ million tweets/day, ~6,000 tweets/second during peak events. |
| **Velocity** | Real-time streaming — opinions shift within minutes (e.g., during a product launch). |
| **Variety** | Text, emojis, hashtags, URLs, images, videos, multiple languages. |
| **Veracity** | Heavy presence of sarcasm, slang, spam bots, and noisy tokens. |
| **Value** | Extracted sentiment drives business decisions worth millions in revenue. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is precisely why KTU 2024 places this topic inside the **Big Data Processing** course — sentiment analysis at social media scale mandates distributed frameworks (Hadoop, Spark Streaming) and not a single-machine script.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The end-to-end workflow is decomposed into **six logical stages**, each justified by a specific engineering need:

**Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Collection Layer)**
- **Why:** No analysis is possible without raw text.
- **How:** Use OAuth-authenticated REST/Streaming APIs. Twitter API v2 provides the `recent search` and `filtered stream` endpoints. For KTU lab work, the `Tweepy` library in Python wraps these endpoints.
- **Output:** Raw JSON payloads containing `text`, `lang`, `created_at`, `user`, `retweet_count`, etc.

**Stage 2 — Storage in a Distributed File System**
- **Why:** Volume exceeds local disk; we need horizontal scalability.
- **How:** Stream ingestion into **HDFS** (Hadoop) or **S3/ADLS** (cloud). Format: JSON / Parquet / Avro for columnar efficiency.
- **Why Parquet:** Predicate pushdown reduces I/O when filtering by date or language.

**Stage 3 — Preprocessing (Text Normalization)**
- **Why:** Raw tweets contain noise that destroys classifier accuracy.
- **Operations applied (in order):**
  1. **Lowercasing** — reduces vocabulary size.
  2. **URL stripping** — `https://t.co/xyz` carries zero sentiment.
  3. **Mention / Hashtag symbol removal** — keep the word but drop `@` and `#`.
  4. **Emoji-to-text conversion** — `:)` → `smiling_face`, mapped via the Unicode emoji lexicon.
  5. **Negation handling** — `not good` ≠ `good`. The token `not` flips the polarity of the next adjective.
  6. **Tokenization** — split sentences into word tokens.
  7. **Stop-word removal** (contextual — sometimes retained for negation).
  8. **Stemming / Lemmatization** — `running`, `ran`, `runs` → `run`.

**Stage 4 — Feature Engineering**
- **Why:** ML models and lexicons need numerical input.
- **Techniques:**
  - **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** — term frequency vector.
  - **TF-IDF weighting** — penalizes globally common terms.
  - **Word Embeddings** — Word2Vec, GloVe, or contextual BERT vectors of dimension $d \in \{50, 100, 300\}$.

**Stage 5 — Sentiment Classification Engine**
- **Why:** This is the analytical heart.
- **Two principal paradigms:**
  - **Lexicon-based (rule-driven):** VADER, TextBlob, AFINN. Each lexicon word $w$ has a pre-assigned valence $v(w) \in [-1, +1]$. Aggregate via summation/averaging.
  - **Machine Learning (data-driven):** Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression, LSTM, BERT. Learns $P(\text{class} \mid \text{features})$ from labeled training data.

**Stage 6 — Aggregation, Visualization & Decision Support**
- Aggregate per-post polarity into **time-series, geo-spatial heatmaps, and word clouds**.
- Compute **Net Sentiment Score (NSS)** and **Sentiment Velocity** (rate of change).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examination-critical for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Range / Unit** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{t,d} = tf(t,d)$ | Term frequency of term $t$ in doc $d$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | BoW representation |
| 2 | $idf(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{df_t}$ | Inverse document frequency; $N$ = total docs, $df_t$ = docs containing $t$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | TF-IDF weighting |
| 3 | $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ | Final TF-IDF weight | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Feature vector |
| 4 | $S_{pol} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} v(w_i)}{\sqrt{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} v(w_i)\right)^2 + \alpha}}$ | VADER compound score, $\alpha = 15$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Lexicon polarity |
| 5 | $P(c \mid x) = \dfrac{P(x \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(x)}$ | Bayes posterior for class $c$ given features $x$ | $[0, 1]$ | Naive Bayes |
| 6 | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$ | MAP estimate (class independence assumed) | class label | NB classifier |
| 7 | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions | $[0, 1]$ | Evaluation |
| 8 | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives | $[0, 1]$ | Evaluation |
| 9 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | $[0, 1]$ | Evaluation |
| 10 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness | $[0, 1]$ | Evaluation |
| 11 | $\text{NSS} = \dfrac{\#\text{Positive} - \#\text{Negative}}{\#\text{Total}}$ | Net Sentiment Score (normalized) | $[-1, +1]$ | Final report |
| 12 | $\text{Macro-}F_1 = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} F_{1,j}$ | Average per-class F1 ($k$ = classes) | $[0, 1]$ | Multi-class eval |

**Boundary Conditions & Engineering Limits:**

- $idf(t) = 0$ when $df_t = N$ (term appears in every document → no discriminative power).
- VADER compound score is **bounded** in $[-1, +1]$ by the additive normalization $\alpha = 15$.
- Naive Bayes assumes **conditional independence** of features — violated by n-grams; mitigated by SVM/BERT.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Sentiment Pipeline

| **Domain** | **Application** | **Decision Impact** |
|---|---|---|
| **Brand Management** | Track product launch reactions | Real-time PR crisis response |
| **Finance** | Predict stock movement from tweet sentiment | Algorithmic trading signals |
| **Politics** | Gauge voter mood before elections | Campaign strategy adjustment |
| **Healthcare** | Detect population-level anxiety/depression signals | Early-warning public health |
| **Film Industry** | Predict box-office from trailer sentiment | Marketing budget reallocation |
| **Customer Service** | Auto-triage angry tweets to priority queue | SLA compliance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Contextual Note:** The 14-mark ESE question on this topic almost always asks for the *complete architecture* — collection, storage, preprocessing, model, and visualization — **not** a single isolated algorithm. Memorize the pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — VADER Compound Score Normalization

The VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner) lexicon assigns each sentiment-bearing word a valence $v(w_i) \in [-4, +4]$. The raw sum $x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v(w_i)$ is unbounded; it must be squashed into the unit interval $[-1, +1]$.

**Step 1 — Sum the valences of all lexicon words in the text.**

$$x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v(w_i)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the additive normalization with smoothing constant $\alpha = 15$ (empirically tuned by Hutto & Gilbert, 2014).**

$$c = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + \alpha}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the boundary behaviour:**

- If $x = 0$ (neutral sentence): $c = \dfrac{0}{\sqrt{0 + 15}} = 0$ ✓
- If $x \to +\infty$: $c = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{x^2(1 + \alpha/x^2)}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0}} = +1$ ✓
- If $x \to -\infty$: similarly $c \to -1$ ✓

**Step 4 — The $\alpha$ constant controls sensitivity.**

A small $\alpha$ → score saturates quickly (over-sensitive). A large $\alpha$ → score stays near zero (under-sensitive). The value $\alpha = 15$ is the empirical sweet spot for English micro-text.

### 3.2 Derivation II — TF-IDF Weight from First Principles

**Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF).**
Number of occurrences of term $t$ in document $d$:

$$tf(t, d) = \sum_{w \in d} \mathbb{1}[w = t]$$

**Step 2 — Document Frequency (DF).**
Number of documents in the corpus that contain term $t$:

$$df_t = \sum_{d \in D} \mathbb{1}[t \in d]$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF).**
The IDF is the logarithm of the inverse fraction of documents containing $t$:

$$idf(t) = \log \frac{N}{df_t}$$

**Why the logarithm?** A linear IDF would over-penalize common words (e.g., "the" with $df_t \approx N$). The log compresses the dynamic range. The base of the log is conventionally $e$ (natural log) or $10$.

**Step 4 — Final Weight (TF-IDF).**

$$w_{t,d} = tf(t, d) \cdot \log \frac{N}{df_t}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Let corpus $D$ contain $N = 100$ tweets. Term $t$ = "excellent" appears $tf = 3$ times in tweet $d_1$, and appears in $df_t = 5$ documents of the corpus.

- $idf(\text{excellent}) = \log \dfrac{100}{5} = \log 20 \approx 2.996$
- $w_{\text{excellent}, d_1} = 3 \times 2.996 = 8.987$

> **Interpretation:** A high TF-IDF value means the term is **frequent in this specific document but rare in the corpus** — exactly the property we want for sentiment-bearing adjectives.

### 3.3 Derivation III — F1-Score from a Confusion Matrix

A sentiment classifier outputs one of three classes: Positive (P), Negative (N), Neutral (U). For the **Positive** class:

**Step 1 — Define the four cardinalities from the confusion matrix.**

- $TP$ = True Positive = tweets correctly labelled Positive.
- $FP$ = False Positive = tweets wrongly labelled Positive (actually N or U).
- $FN$ = False Negative = Positive tweets missed by the classifier.
- $TN$ = True Negative = correctly rejected as non-Positive.

**Step 2 — Precision (Positive Predictive Value).**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Step 3 — Recall (True Positive Rate / Sensitivity).**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of P and R).**
The arithmetic mean $\frac{P+R}{2}$ is misleading when one metric is tiny. The harmonic mean penalizes imbalance:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

Derivation of the harmonic mean form:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} = \frac{2PR}{P + R}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**

A Naive Bayes sentiment classifier is evaluated on a held-out test set of 100 tweets. The confusion matrix for the Positive class is:

$$TP = 50, \quad FP = 10, \quad FN = 5, \quad TN = 35$$

Substituting:

- $P = \dfrac{50}{50 + 10} = \dfrac{50}{60} = 0.8333$
- $R = \dfrac{50}{50 + 5} = \dfrac{50}{55} = 0.9091$
- $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \times 0.8333 \times 0.9091}{0.8333 + 0.9091} = \dfrac{1.5150}{1.7424} \approx 0.8695$

- $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{50 + 35}{50 + 35 + 10 + 5} = \dfrac{85}{100} = 0.85$

> **Interpretation:** The classifier achieves 85% overall accuracy, but the F1 of 0.87 is the **more honest metric** for the Positive class because it balances both false alarms (FP) and missed detections (FN).

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Naive Bayes Posterior for Sentiment

For a tweet represented by feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ (e.g., word-presence indicators), the posterior probability of class $c \in \{\text{Pos, Neg, Neu}\}$ is given by Bayes' theorem:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Assuming **conditional independence** of features given the class (the "naive" assumption):

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

Therefore:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

The Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) decision rule picks the class with the highest posterior. Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across classes, it is dropped:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c) \right]$$

In practice, products of small probabilities underflow; we work in log-space:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c) \right]$$

> **Engineering Reality Check:** The naive independence assumption is mathematically violated by bigrams and contextual phrases (e.g., "not bad" is positive despite "bad" being negative). This is why modern pipelines fall back to **LSTMs** or **transformer-based BERT**, which model context explicitly.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Sentiment Methods

| **Criterion** | **Lexicon (VADER / TextBlob)** | **Classical ML (NB / SVM)** | **Deep Learning (LSTM / BERT)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Training data required | **No** (uses pre-built lexicon) | Yes (thousands of labelled tweets) | Yes (hundreds of thousands) |
| Handles sarcasm | **No** (fails badly) | Limited | **Yes** (with context) |
| Computational cost | **Very low** (milliseconds) | Low | **High** (GPU needed) |
| Domain adaptation | Difficult (lexicon biased to general English) | Easy (retrain on new domain) | Easy (fine-tune) |
| Interpretability | **High** (each word score visible) | Medium | **Low** (black box) |
| KTU lab suitability | **Highly suitable** (no GPU) | Suitable | Usually skipped in B.Tech labs |
| Typical accuracy (Twitter) | 70 – 80% | 78 – 85% | 88 – 95% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** For the 14-mark question, students who **compare and justify** the choice of method (with reference to the above matrix) consistently score higher than those who merely describe one method.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SM[Social Media Platforms Twitter, Facebook, Reddit]
    API[REST and Streaming API with OAuth]
    RAW[Raw JSON Payloads]
    HDFS[Distributed Storage HDFS or S3]
    CLEAN[Text Preprocessing Pipeline]
    TOKEN[Tokenization and Normalization]
    STOP[Stopword Removal and Lemmatization]
    FEAT[Feature Extraction TF-IDF and Embeddings]
    LEX[Lexicon Engine VADER and TextBlob]
    ML[ML Classifier Naive Bayes and SVM]
    DL[Deep Model BERT and LSTM]
    POL[Polarity Aggregator]
    POS[Positive Bucket]
    NEG[Negative Bucket]
    NEU[Neutral Bucket]
    VIZ[Visualization Dashboard]
    DEC[Business Decision Output]

    SM --> API
    API --> RAW
    RAW --> HDFS
    HDFS --> CLEAN
    CLEAN --> TOKEN
    TOKEN --> STOP
    STOP --> FEAT
    FEAT --> LEX
    FEAT --> ML
    FEAT --> DL
    LEX --> POL
    ML --> POL
    DL --> POL
    POL --> POS
    POL --> NEG
    POL --> NEU
    POS --> VIZ
    NEG --> VIZ
    NEU --> VIZ
    VIZ --> DEC
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- `SM` is the external data source.
- `API` enforces rate-limits and authentication.
- `HDFS` is the system-of-record; all downstream stages are idempotent against it.
- `FEAT` fans out into **three parallel analytical engines** — a deliberate design choice allowing ensemble voting.
- `POL` is the **single point of fusion** where lexicon, ML, and DL outputs are reconciled.
- `VIZ` feeds `DEC` — closing the loop to business value.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Layer View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph L1 [Layer 1 Data Ingestion]
        L1A[Tweepy Stream Listener]
        L1B[Kafka Topic]
    end
    subgraph L2 [Layer 2 Storage]
        L2A[HDFS Raw Zone]
        L2B[Parquet Curated Zone]
    end
    subgraph L3 [Layer 3 Processing on Spark]
        L3A[Spark Streaming Job]
        L3B[Preprocessing UDFs]
        L3C[Feature Pipeline]
    end
    subgraph L4 [Layer 4 Analytics and ML]
        L4A[Sentiment Scorer]
        L4B[Model Registry]
    end
    subgraph L5 [Layer 5 Serving and Visualization]
        L5A[REST API Flask]
        L5B[Dashboard Grafana or Kibana]
        L5C[Alert Engine]
    end
    L1A --> L1B
    L1B --> L2A
    L2A --> L2B
    L2B --> L3A
    L3A --> L3B
    L3B --> L3C
    L3C --> L4A
    L4B --> L4A
    L4A --> L5A
    L5A --> L5B
    L5A --> L5C
```

**Architectural Rationale:**

- **Layer 1** decouples the API rate-limiter from the processing engine via **Kafka** (absorbs traffic spikes).
- **Layer 2** uses a **multi-zone** HDFS layout: raw (immutable) vs. curated (Parquet, schema-enforced).
- **Layer 3** is where **Spark Streaming** provides fault-tolerant, exactly-once semantics via checkpointing.
- **Layer 4** separates the scorer from the model registry → enables A/B testing of lexicon vs. ML models.
- **Layer 5** exposes results via REST and pushes real-time alerts when NSS drops below a threshold (e.g., $-0.5$).

### 4.3 Decision Flow Inside the Polarity Aggregator

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Incoming Tweet Text]
    B{Compound Score c from VADER}
    C{c greater than or equal to 0.05}
    D{c less than or equal to minus 0.05}
    E[Label: POSITIVE]
    F[Label: NEGATIVE]
    G[Label: NEUTRAL]
    H[Update Counters]
    I[Push to Dashboard]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -->|Yes| E
    C -->|No| D
    D -->|Yes| F
    D -->|No| G
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

**Threshold Interpretation:**

- $c \geq +0.05 \rightarrow$ Positive
- $c \leq -0.05 \rightarrow$ Negative
- $-0.05 < c < +0.05 \rightarrow$ Neutral

> **Why $\pm 0.05$?** These are the empirically validated VADER thresholds that optimize agreement with human raters on social media data.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define **Sentiment Analysis**. List any **four challenges** specific to performing sentiment analysis on Twitter data.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Sentiment Analysis, also called Opinion Mining, is a Natural Language Processing (NLP) technique used to computationally identify, extract, and classify the emotional tone — Positive, Negative, or Neutral — expressed in a piece of text. When applied to Twitter data, it enables real-time measurement of public opinion at scale.

**Four Challenges (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Use of slang, abbreviations, and informal grammar** — tweets rarely follow formal English; e.g., "luv", "gr8", "OMG".
2. **Sarcasm and irony** — "Oh great, another update. Just what I needed." is negative despite the word "great".
3. **Emojis and emoticons** — `:)` or `😂` carry strong sentiment but are non-ASCII symbols.
4. **Mixed-language (code-mixed) content** — Indian tweets frequently blend English with Malayalam/Hindi.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Differentiate between **lexicon-based** and **machine learning-based** approaches to sentiment analysis. Give **one example tool** for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Lexicon-Based** | **Machine Learning-Based** |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Uses a pre-built dictionary of word-sentiment scores. | Learns sentiment patterns from labelled training data. |
| Training | **No training required.** | Requires thousands of labelled examples. |
| Example Tool | **VADER** (Valence Aware Dictionary) or **TextBlob**. | **Naive Bayes / SVM / BERT classifier.** |
| Handles Sarcasm | Poor. | Moderate to good (with sufficient data). |
| Speed | Very fast. | Moderate (training) to fast (inference). |
| Adaptability | Domain-porting is hard. | Easily retrained for new domains. |

*(Full 3 marks awarded for clear, tabulated comparison with valid examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Sub-parts (a) and (b) of the chosen question are **compulsory**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Architecture & Pipeline Design*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end architecture** of a big-data system that collects tweets using the Twitter Streaming API, stores them in HDFS, preprocesses them using Apache Spark, and performs sentiment classification. Clearly label each component and state the data format at every interface.

**Model Answer — Sub-part (a):**

The complete architecture consists of **five sequential layers**, each with a specific data format and engineering purpose.

**Layer 1 — Data Acquisition (1 mark):**
A Python-based listener using the `Tweepy` library opens a persistent HTTPS connection to the Twitter API v2 `filtered stream` endpoint. Bearer-token OAuth 2.0 authentication is enforced. Search rules specify keywords (e.g., `iPhone 16 -is:retweet lang:en`). The output is a stream of newline-delimited JSON objects, one per tweet.

**Layer 2 — Ingestion & Buffering (1 mark):**
The raw JSON stream is published to a **Kafka topic** named `tweets_raw` with a partition key derived from `user_id` (ensures per-user ordering). Kafka acts as a buffer that absorbs traffic bursts and decouples producers from consumers.

**Layer 3 — Distributed Storage (1 mark):**
A **Spark Streaming** job consumes the Kafka topic using `spark-sql-kafka` connector. Tweets are written to HDFS in two zones:
- `hdfs:///raw/tweets/YYYY/MM/DD/` — original JSON, write-once.
- `hdfs:///curated/tweets/` — **Parquet** format, partitioned by `date` and `lang`.

**Layer 4 — Preprocessing & Feature Engineering (2 marks):**
